example_name: DoorDash CCPA Settlement with California AG
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, collected extensive personal information from California users, including location data, purchase history, and device identifiers. The company shared this data with third-party analytics and advertising partners without proper disclosures in its privacy notice. When consumers submitted deletion requests, DoorDash did not consistently notify its service providers or downstream third parties to whom the data had been sold or shared to effectuate the deletions, leading to retention of consumer data beyond what was permitted.
process_and_outcome: Following an investigation, DoorDash settled with the California AG in 2022, paying $375,000 in civil penalties and committing to enhanced deletion request handling procedures, including notifications to service providers and third parties, along with two years of compliance monitoring.
source_rule: notify any service providers or contractors to delete the consumer’s personal information from their records, and notify all third parties to whom the business has sold or shared the personal information to delete the consumer’s personal information unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort.

example_background: CMT Digital, doing business as Instacart, a grocery delivery service, failed to process verified consumer requests to delete personal information. Between 2018 and 2022, the company collected sensitive data such as delivery addresses and order histories from millions of California consumers. Upon receiving deletion requests, Instacart did not direct its service providers, including data analytics firms and advertising partners, to delete the information, nor did it notify third parties to whom the data had been sold or shared, resulting in ongoing data retention.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated the first enforcement action under CCPA in 2024, fining Instacart $1.6 million. The company was ordered to implement systems for proper deletion notifications and comply with ongoing reporting requirements.

When California consumers exercised their CCPA rights to delete their information, Gushcloud did not notify its service providers or the third parties that had purchased the data to remove it from their records, leading to persistent data usage.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the California Attorney General secured a $1.5 million settlement, requiring Gushcloud to overhaul its data deletion processes, including mandatory notifications to downstream recipients, and to maintain detailed records of compliance efforts.

The company's privacy controls did not include steps to notify these service providers or third-party buyers of personal information when consumers requested deletion, resulting in data lingering in external databases.
process_and_outcome: Settled in 2023 for $325,000 with the California AG, Farmers agreed to update its systems for deletion notifications and employee training programs.

This was uncovered in the California AG's 2022 enforcement sweep, highlighting Gushcloud's lack of opt-out links and inadequate response to consumer rights requests under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: In August 2022, Gushcloud signed an assurance of discontinuance, committing to CCPA compliance, including proper deletion handling, opt-out implementation, and employee training. No fine was levied.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, processed vast amounts of personal information from California consumers, including names, addresses, phone numbers, payment details, and order histories. The company shared consumer data with third-party advertising networks and analytics providers without implementing a functional 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on its website or app, as required by CCPA. Additionally, DoorDash did not honor universal opt-out signals and failed to provide consumers with accessible methods to exercise their rights under the law. This issue persisted from the CCPA's effective date in 2020, impacting users who placed orders through the service and were unaware their data was being sold for marketing purposes.
process_and_outcome: Following an investigation by the California Attorney General in 2022, DoorDash settled by agreeing to an Assurance of Discontinuation. The company committed to adding a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, recognizing GPC and other opt-out signals, enhancing privacy notices, and conducting annual audits. No fine was levied in this case.

example_background: Activision Blizzard, a video game publisher, collected player data including usernames, gameplay metrics, chat logs, and purchase records for games like Call of Duty and World of Warcraft. The company shared this data with third parties for analytics and advertising without proper opt-out mechanisms or honoring deletion requests, extending beyond necessary service provision.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General reached a $1.25 million settlement, mandating improved notice, opt-out processes, data minimization, and annual compliance certifications.

process_and_outcome: Settled a class action and FTC charges in 2017 for $17 million initially, followed by a 2023 CCPA-related settlement for additional $2.5 million, requiring data deletion protocols and caps on retention periods.

example_name: Microsoft Azure CCPA Data Deletion Practices
example_background: Microsoft Azure serves as a cloud service provider to thousands of businesses, handling personal information in storage, analytics, and AI services. Under CCPA, Microsoft established a 'right to be forgotten' workflow in its Data Protection Addendum, where upon receiving deletion instructions from a business customer, Azure automatically purges data from active systems, backups, and logs after a 30-day recovery window. The process includes notifying subcontractors like content delivery networks if they accessed the data. Microsoft rejected direct consumer requests, directing them to the controlling business, and implemented proportionate effort assessments to avoid impossible deletions in distributed systems. This was applied in high-volume scenarios, such as e-commerce platforms processing bulk deletions.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's compliance was validated through SOC 2 audits and CCPA-specific assessments in 2021-2024, with no regulatory penalties. Customer case studies highlighted 99.9% deletion success rates.
source_rule: delete personal information about the consumer collected, used, processed, or retained by the service provider or the contractor. The service provider or contractor shall notify any service providers, contractors, or third parties who may have accessed personal information from or through the service provider or contractor, unless the information was accessed at the direction of the business, to delete the consumer’s personal information unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. A service provider or contractor shall not be required to comply with a deletion request submitted by the consumer directly to the service provider or contractor to the extent that the service provider or contractor has collected, used, processed, or retained the consumer’s personal information in its role as a service provider or contractor to the business.

example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, collected viewing habits, device IDs, IP addresses, and demographic data from millions of California consumers via its TVs and apps, sharing this with third-party advertisers and data aggregators. The company did not provide accessible deletion request portals and failed to delete data from service providers like Nielsen and comScore after consumer requests. Retained data was used in ongoing ad profiles, with Vizio's contractors continuing access despite instructions. This stemmed from inadequate data mapping and lack of notification protocols to downstream parties.
process_and_outcome: California AG settled in 2022 for $2.2 million plus injunctive relief, requiring Vizio to overhaul its data deletion systems, notify all recipients of consumer data, and conduct triennial audits.

Salesforce limits direct consumer interactions and uses data minimization to reduce retention scopes. Annual penetration testing verified propagation to subcontractors.

This occurred without adequate notice to users about the data practices, limiting consumer control over their information.

example_background: Facebook operated a feature called 'Tag Suggestions' from 2011 to 2015 that analyzed facial images uploaded by users to generate biometric identifiers without obtaining user consent prior to creating or using those identifiers. This involved scanning millions of photos to create facial recognition templates stored in a database. Users were not informed about the collection of this data, and the system operated automatically upon photo uploads. The practice came to light through a class action lawsuit filed in Illinois federal court, highlighting concerns over unauthorized biometric data handling in electronic communications and photo-sharing platforms.
process_and_outcome: A class action lawsuit was certified, leading to a $650 million settlement approved by the court in 2021. Facebook agreed to pay damages to Illinois residents affected by the feature, delete existing facial recognition data, and suspend the tool for Illinois users. The company also faced ongoing monitoring and compliance requirements.

process_and_outcome: California's Attorney General settled with Clearview AI in 2022 for $30 million, with additional injunctive relief requiring the company to stop selling to private entities in the state and delete data of California residents. The settlement included civil penalties and mandated compliance reforms.

This approach was developed to align with privacy laws amid increasing scrutiny over device and cloud data access.
process_and_outcome: Apple voluntarily enhanced its encryption protocols, resulting in zero reported breaches related to unauthorized access to iCloud communications. The company has publicly reported denying certain government requests due to inability to access encrypted data, demonstrating sustained adherence.

example_name: California AG v. Sephora Data Practices
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, collected extensive personal information from California consumers through its online platform, mobile app, and in-store services. The company shared data including purchase history, skin type, and product preferences with third-party vendors for targeted advertising without providing clear notices or opt-out mechanisms to consumers. This occurred during a period when the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) had recently taken effect in January 2020, requiring businesses to disclose data sharing practices and honor consumer rights. Sephora's practices involved automated decision-making profiles and involved over 100 million data points annually from California users, raising questions about transparency in data processing for commercial purposes.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General initiated an enforcement action in 2022, resulting in Sephora agreeing to a settlement that included implementing a comprehensive privacy program, training for employees, biennial audits, and public disclosure of its data practices. No monetary penalty was imposed as part of the resolution.

example_background: Flo Health, a women's health app, collected sensitive health data from millions of users, including menstrual cycle information, symptoms, and ovulation predictions. In 2019, the company shared this data with third parties like Google Analytics and Facebook for analytics and advertising without explicit user consent or adequate disclosure. The data sharing involved raw user inputs and derived health inferences, affecting users across the US, including California residents. This practice continued post-CCPA enactment, during a time when apps were navigating emerging privacy requirements for health data.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission investigated and settled with Flo in 2021, prohibiting future sharing of sensitive health data without affirmative consent and requiring data deletion from third parties where possible. Flo agreed to privacy program reforms but no fine was levied.

example_background: Sirius XM Radio Inc., a satellite radio and streaming service provider, collected personal information from California subscribers such as names, addresses, payment information, listening habits, vehicle data, and geolocation through its apps and services. The company's privacy policy and notices did not disclose consumers' rights to request correction of inaccurate personal information or provide details on how to exercise CCPA rights like access and deletion. This affected millions of California users whose data was used for personalized content and advertising, with the company failing to update notices post-CCPA implementation in 2020.

example_name: Apple App Store CCPA Compliance Notice
example_background: Apple Inc., through its App Store serving California consumers, collects personal information including Apple IDs, purchase history, device identifiers, location data, and app usage analytics. In response to CCPA requirements, Apple updated its App Store privacy notices and developer guidelines to explicitly disclose consumers' rights to request correction of inaccurate personal information. The notices detail how users can access an online portal to submit correction requests, describing the process, timelines, and verification methods. This implementation covers data from app downloads, in-app purchases, and subscriptions, ensuring millions of California users are informed via prominent links in privacy policies and account settings.
process_and_outcome: Apple proactively integrated CCPA rights into its ecosystem without enforcement action. The company publishes annual privacy reports demonstrating handling of thousands of consumer requests, including corrections, with high fulfillment rates and transparent reporting on its privacy website.

The company implemented a centralized privacy dashboard accessible via account settings, prominently disclosing rights to request correction of inaccurate personal information alongside access, deletion, and opt-out options. Detailed explanations cover data categories, request submission methods (online form, email, phone), response timelines (45 days), and appeal processes, integrated into privacy statements for all consumer-facing products.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's compliance framework has handled over 100,000 CCPA requests annually with reported 95% fulfillment rates. The company publishes transparency reports and undergoes third-party audits, maintaining adherence without penalties.

The retailer's website privacy policy lacked clear disclosures about consumers' CCPA rights to request correction of inaccurate personal information, with no dedicated section, links, or descriptions of the request process despite collecting data through e-commerce, in-store apps, and marketing emails affecting tens of millions of transactions annually.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, as part of ongoing enforcement, the California Privacy Protection Agency noted Home Depot among companies updating notices post-notice of violations. Home Depot implemented policy revisions, a consumer request portal, and training programs to address disclosure gaps.

process_and_outcome: BMW maintains a CCPA-compliant program with annual privacy training and audits, reporting effective handling of consumer requests without regulatory penalties.

example_name: California AG Action Against Activision Blizzard
example_background: Activision Blizzard, the video game publisher behind titles like Call of Duty and World of Warcraft, collected vast amounts of personal data from players, including usernames, in-game behaviors, communication logs, and linked account details. Consumers reported issues with inaccurate data, such as incorrect age verifications affecting content access or erroneous email associations leading to account lockouts. The company did not have robust mechanisms to handle correction requests promptly, despite CCPA mandates requiring businesses to correct inaccurate personal information based on the data's nature and processing purposes like game personalization and anti-cheat systems. This was part of early 2021 enforcement sweeps.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General issued a notice in March 2021, leading to a settlement where Activision Blizzard agreed to no penalties but implemented a CCPA compliance program. This included dedicated portals for correction requests, 45-day response guarantees, biannual audits, and public reporting on request volumes.
source_rule: (8) Comply with a legal obligation.1798.106.Consumers’ Right to Correct Inaccurate Personal Information (a) A consumer shall have the right to request a business that maintains inaccurate personal information about the consumer to correct that inaccurate personal information, taking into account the nature of the personal information and the purposes of the processing of the personal information.

Zoom initially processed some requests but faced challenges in scaling for accurate corrections, especially for AI-generated data, prompting regulatory review on compliance with the right to correct under expanded privacy laws.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement action, resulting in Zoom agreeing to a $1.2 million settlement for various CCPA/CPRA violations, including inadequate correction processes. example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount service, collected sensitive health data including pharmacy records, claims data, and personal identifiers from California consumers. It shared this data with advertising platforms like Facebook and Google without proper notice. The privacy policy did not disclose the categories of personal information collected such as health and biometric data, sources including partner pharmacies, purposes like targeted advertising, or third parties involved. Consumers requesting access were not provided specifics on data collected about them, limiting transparency.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission investigated and settled in 2023, imposing a $1.5 million civil penalty, prohibiting sharing of health data for ads, requiring detailed disclosures, and mandating a comprehensive privacy program with consumer request fulfillment.

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Data Correction Settlement
example_background: CMT Digital, LLC, doing business as Gatos Studios, operated mobile gaming apps that collected personal information from millions of California consumers, including device identifiers, IP addresses, and behavioral data. The company failed to process consumer requests submitted through the California Attorney General's CCPA portal to delete or correct personal information held in consumer profiles. Between January 2020 and 2022, CMT received multiple verifiable consumer requests via the official portal but did not verify the consumers' identities adequately or take steps to update inaccurate data or delete information as requested, retaining the data for advertising purposes instead. This occurred amid growing regulatory scrutiny on mobile app developers' handling of CCPA rights requests.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated following consumer complaints to the CCPA portal. In February 2023, CMT agreed to a $1.25 million settlement, deleted all California consumer data in its possession, ceased collecting CCPA-covered data, and implemented processes for handling future rights requests including correction requests.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_name: DoorDash CCPA Verification and Correction Failures
example_background: DoorDash, Inc., a food delivery platform, collected extensive personal information from California users including names, addresses, payment details, order histories, and location data. The company received numerous verifiable consumer requests to access, delete, and correct personal information but implemented verification processes that were overly burdensome, such as requiring government ID uploads for basic corrections, and failed to correct inaccurate profile data like delivery addresses or dietary preferences as directed. From 2020 to 2022, DoorDash processed only a fraction of requests adequately, often denying them due to 'verification failures' while retaining inaccurate data for marketing and service personalization.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General launched an enforcement action in 2023. DoorDash settled for $3.75 million in August 2024, agreed to streamline verification for corrections, update privacy notices, and delete data from inactive accounts upon correction requests.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_background: Ticketmaster, a live events ticketing platform owned by Live Nation, collected personal information from California consumers including purchase histories, payment details, and event preferences. The company faced complaints for not honoring verifiable requests to correct inaccurate data in user accounts, such as wrong email addresses or payment methods, and for delaying responses beyond the 45-day statutory period. From 2020 onward, Ticketmaster's verification process involved complex identity proofs disproportionate to the request, failing to apply commercially reasonable efforts to propagate corrections across marketing and sales databases.
process_and_outcome: California AG enforcement action resulted in a January 2024 settlement of $5.75 million. Ticketmaster committed to enhancing its consumer request portal, simplifying verification for corrections, and auditing compliance annually.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_background: Apple Inc., a technology company offering devices, services, and an app ecosystem, collects personal information including Apple ID details, purchase histories, location data, and health metrics via iCloud and Health app. Apple established a dedicated privacy portal at privacy.apple.com where California consumers can submit verifiable requests to access, delete, or correct personal information. The company uses methods like email verification and device-based authentication to confirm identities and then corrects inaccurate data such as profile names, addresses, or linked payment methods across its ecosystem with commercially reasonable efforts, propagating changes to associated services.
process_and_outcome: Apple voluntarily implemented the portal prior to full CCPA regulations in 2023, processed millions of requests successfully, and received positive feedback in California AG comment periods. No enforcement actions were taken against Apple for CCPA rights handling.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_background: Microsoft Corporation collects personal information through its cloud services, Office suite, Azure, and advertising platforms, including account details, usage data, and billing information from California users. The company developed the Microsoft Privacy Dashboard and account.microsoft.com/privacy portals enabling consumers to view, download, delete, or correct personal data held across Microsoft services. For correction requests, Microsoft verifies via account login and multi-factor authentication, then updates inaccurate information like contact details or subscription preferences enterprise-wide using automated and manual processes to ensure consistency.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's program has handled over 100 million global privacy requests annually with high compliance rates, audited internally and by third parties. It served as a model in CCPA regulation rulemaking, with no penalties issued.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_background: Mozilla Corporation, developer of Firefox browser and Pocket service, collects personal information such as browsing telemetry, account details, and sync data from California users. Mozilla provides a CCPA-specific privacy portal allowing consumers to submit verifiable requests to correct inaccurate personal information, with verification through email confirmation and account access. Upon verification, Mozilla corrects data like sync preferences or email addresses and deletes associated inaccurate records across servers, applying commercially reasonable efforts including database synchronization.
process_and_outcome: Implemented ahead of deadlines, Mozilla's portal processed requests efficiently, featured in privacy advocacy reports as a best practice. No regulatory investigations or fines related to CCPA corrections.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_background: LinkedIn, a professional networking platform owned by Microsoft, collects profile data, connection histories, and job search behaviors from California members. The platform offers a data privacy dashboard where verified users can edit and correct personal information directly, with changes propagated to recommendations, ads, and search indexes. Verification occurs via login and security checks, fulfilling CCPA requirements by enabling direct corrections without additional requests in many cases.
process_and_outcome: LinkedIn integrated CCPA tools into its platform, handling requests seamlessly and publicly reporting compliance metrics. Cited positively in regulatory guidance documents.
source_rule: (c) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request to correct inaccurate personal information shall use commercially reasonable efforts to correct the inaccurate personal information as directed by the consumer, pursuant to Section 1798.130 and regulations adopted pursuant to paragraph (7) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185.

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Fine by California AG
example_background: CMT Digital, doing business as Guts & Glory Holdings, operated a website offering cash for personal information. It collected names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and financial data directly from consumers and sold this data to third parties. The site failed to provide disclosures on categories of personal information collected, sources (direct user submission), purposes (selling for commercial gain), categories of buyers, and did not notify users of CCPA rights to request their specific information or details on sales and to whom.
process_and_outcome: In January 2024, the California AG announced the first-ever CCPA penalty of $1.2 million against CMT Digital for failing to register as a data broker and lacking required privacy notices.
source_rule: (1) The categories of personal information it has collected about consumers.(2) The categories of sources from which the personal information is collected.(3) The business or commercial purpose for collecting, selling, or sharing personal information.(4) The categories of third parties to whom the business discloses personal information.(5) That a consumer has the right to request the specific pieces ofpersonal information the business has collected about that consumer.1798.115.Consumers’ Right to Know What Personal Information is Sold or Shared and to Whom (a) A consumer shall have the right to request that a business that sells or shares the consumer’s personal information, or that discloses it for a business purpose, disclose to that consumer:
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100, § 1798.115

example_background: Adidas America collected consumer data including names, demographics, purchase history, browsing activity, and fitness metrics via its websites and apps. Data came from user accounts, cookies, pixels, and device signals. It shared with affiliates, vendors, and ad networks for personalization and marketing. Initial notices lacked specificity on categories, sources, sharing purposes, third-party categories, and consumer rights notices for requesting specific data or sales/sharing details.
process_and_outcome: June 2022 settlement for $2 million; Adidas enhanced privacy notices, streamlined rights requests, and agreed to training and audits.

relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100, § 1798.115

example_name: CPRA Notice at Collection Requirements Compliance by Best Buy
example_background: Best Buy updated its privacy notices post-CCPA/CPRA to include a 'Notice at Collection' detailing 10+ categories of personal information collected (e.g., identifiers, commercial info, geolocation), sources (online, in-store, affiliates), business/commercial purposes (sales, marketing, analytics), third-party categories (service providers, affiliates, ad partners), and explicit statements on consumer rights to request specific pieces of data and disclosures on sharing/sales.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary implementation avoided enforcement; recognized in industry reports for comprehensive disclosures enhancing consumer control.
source_rule: (1) The categories of personal information it has collected about consumers.(2) The categories of sources from which the personal information is collected.(3) The business or commercial purpose for collecting, selling, or sharing personal information.(4) The categories of third parties to whom the business discloses personal information.(5) That a consumer has the right to request the specific pieces ofpersonal information the business has collected about that consumer.1798.115.Consumers’ Right to Know What Personal Information is Sold or Shared and to Whom (a) A consumer shall have the right to request that a business that sells or shares the consumer’s personal information, or that discloses it for a business purpose, disclose to that consumer:
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)/California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100, § 1798.115

example_background: Gusto, a payroll and human resources platform processing data for numerous California-based businesses, collected personal information from employees and contractors, including Social Security numbers, wages, bank details, and demographic data. The company shared this information with affiliates, service providers, and partners for business purposes like payroll processing and benefits administration. Gusto did not disclose to consumers, upon verifiable requests, the required details under CCPA regarding categories of personal information disclosed for business purposes, the categories of recipients, and the specific business purposes, as mandated. Its privacy notices and request response templates omitted these specifics, and the company did not maintain sufficient internal processes to compile and provide this information accurately.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the California Privacy Protection Agency secured a $10 million settlement, the largest CCPA penalty to date, mandating Gusto to revise disclosures, automate request handling, retain records for 24 months, and submit compliance monitoring reports.
source_rule: (b) A business that sells or shares personal information about a consumer, or that discloses a consumer’s personal information for a business purpose, shall disclose, pursuant to paragraph (4) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.130, the information specified in subdivision (a) to the consumer upon receipt of a verifiable consumer request from the consumer.

example_name: Microsoft CCPA Data Subject Rights Portal
example_background: Microsoft, operating services like Azure, Office 365, and advertising platforms affecting California consumers, collects and processes personal data including usage telemetry, contact info, and behavioral signals. It discloses data for business purposes to partners and affiliates. Through its Account Privacy Dashboard and Data Subject Request portal, Microsoft provides, upon verifiable requests, specifics on personal information sold/shared (e.g., advertising IDs), categories of third parties (e.g., ad networks), and purposes (e.g., personalization), with downloadable reports and 24-month retention. Privacy statements detail these disclosures transparently.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft has complied without major CCPA enforcement; annual transparency reports and third-party audits confirm effective request fulfillment rates exceeding 95%, supporting ongoing operations without fines.
source_rule: (b) A business that sells or shares personal information about a consumer, or that discloses a consumer’s personal information for a business purpose, shall disclose, pursuant to paragraph (4) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.130, the information specified in subdivision (a) to the consumer upon receipt of a verifiable consumer request from the consumer.

example_background: Walmart, with vast California retail presence, collects shopper data via Walmart+ memberships, e-commerce, and in-store apps, including purchase histories, locations, preferences, and device info. It shares data for business purposes with suppliers, advertisers, and logistics partners. Walmart's privacy hub responds to verifiable requests by disclosing exact categories shared (e.g., inferred interests), recipient types (e.g., service providers), and purposes (e.g., supply chain optimization), with GPC opt-out integration and detailed fulfillment notices.
process_and_outcome: Implemented enterprise-wide CCPA program post-2020, passing regulatory reviews and self-reported 100% request compliance in 2023 privacy report, avoiding enforcement.
source_rule: (b) A business that sells or shares personal information about a consumer, or that discloses a consumer’s personal information for a business purpose, shall disclose, pursuant to paragraph (4) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.130, the information specified in subdivision (a) to the consumer upon receipt of a verifiable consumer request from the consumer.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, gathered extensive personal information from users such as names, contact details, locations, order histories, and payment information. The company disclosed this data to service providers and partners for operational purposes and shared it with advertising networks for targeted marketing without adequately disclosing the categories of personal information sold or shared, or providing an opt-out mechanism prominently on its website. This occurred during the period leading up to the 2022 enforcement actions.
process_and_outcome: Following an investigation by the California Attorney General, DoorDash settled in August 2022 for $375,000. The settlement required DoorDash to enhance its privacy disclosures, implement a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link, conduct annual compliance audits, and provide employee training on CCPA requirements.

example_background: Gatorade, owned by PepsiCo, operated a website and app collecting consumer personal information including identifiers, geolocation data, and inferences about preferences through interactive features. The company shared this information with third parties for advertising without making required disclosures in its privacy notice about the specific categories shared or maintaining a functional 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link as mandated. This was identified during the California AG's 2022-2023 enforcement efforts targeting major brands.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General announced a settlement in October 2023 where Gatorade agreed to pay $100,000 and undertake compliance measures including updating privacy policies, adding required links, employee training, and two years of compliance monitoring reports.
source_rule: (c) A business that sells or shares consumers’ personal information, or that discloses consumers’ personal information for a business purpose, shall disclose, pursuant to subparagraph (C) of paragraph (5) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.130:

example_background: Meta Platforms, operating Facebook and Instagram, collected vast amounts of California consumers' personal information including identifiers, commercial information, internet activity, geolocation, and inferences. The company shared this data with numerous third parties for business and advertising purposes without providing the required privacy notice disclosures detailing categories of personal information shared and categories of recipients, and without a clear opt-out link for selling or sharing.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency filed its first standalone enforcement action in January 2024. An administrative proceeding is ongoing, with Meta contesting the allegations. Proposed penalties could reach $1.7 billion based on violation counts.
source_rule: (c) A business that sells or shares consumers’ personal information, or that discloses consumers’ personal information for a business purpose, shall disclose, pursuant to subparagraph (C) of paragraph (5) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.130:

example_name: TikTok CPPA Fine for Children's Privacy
example_background: TikTok collected and shared personal information of children under 16, including video watch history, search terms, precise location, and device identifiers, with third parties for business purposes without parental consent. The platform disclosed data to analytics and advertising partners without the mandated CCPA/CPRA disclosures in its privacy policy regarding categories shared.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA approved a $1.6 million penalty against TikTok for violations related to children's data handling under CCPA/CPRA. TikTok agreed to compliance reforms including enhanced age verification and data deletion processes.
source_rule: (c) A business that sells or shares consumers’ personal information, or that discloses consumers’ personal information for a business purpose, shall disclose, pursuant to subparagraph (C) of paragraph (5) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.130:

example_background: Gogo, a provider of in-flight internet services, gathered personal information from California passengers including names, email addresses, IP addresses, device information, and browsing activity during Wi-Fi usage on airplanes. The company disclosed categories such as identifiers, internet activity, and geolocation data to third-party advertisers and analytics providers for business purposes including ad targeting and network optimization. Prior to 2022, Gogo's privacy notice did not disclose the specific categories of personal information it shared for business purposes, only mentioning general data sharing without the required level of detail mandated by CCPA for transparency to consumers.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General reached a $30,000 settlement with Gogo in 2022. Gogo agreed to update its notices to specify disclosed categories, implement privacy controls, and cease certain dark patterns in its data practices.
source_rule: (2) The category or categories of consumers’ personal information it has disclosed for a business purpose, or if the business has not disclosed consumers’ personal information for a business purpose, it shall disclose that fact.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.130

example_background: Zoom Video Communications collected personal information from California users such as names, email addresses, IP addresses, device information, and meeting participation data through its video conferencing platform. The company disclosed this information to third parties including analytics providers and service vendors for business purposes like usage analytics and platform improvements. In its privacy notices prior to corrective actions, Zoom did not explicitly list the categories of personal information disclosed for business purposes, providing instead broad descriptions of data sharing that did not meet CCPA specificity requirements.
process_and_outcome: $100,000 settlement approved by CPPA in 2024. Zoom updated its privacy policy to include detailed disclosures of shared categories, enhanced employee training, and committed to ongoing compliance monitoring.
source_rule: (2) The category or categories of consumers’ personal information it has disclosed for a business purpose, or if the business has not disclosed consumers’ personal information for a business purpose, it shall disclose that fact.

The company's CCPA-specific privacy statement details categories disclosed for business purposes, such as identifiers and internet activity shared with affiliates, vendors, and partners for advertising, research, and service delivery. These disclosures have been prominently featured since CCPA's inception, with regular updates and a dedicated 'California Resident Privacy Notice' section.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft has maintained compliance without enforcement actions, earning recognition in privacy benchmarks like those from the Electronic Frontier Foundation for transparent disclosures.

Its 'California Privacy Disclosure' explicitly lists categories of personal information disclosed for business purposes to third parties like advertisers and measurement providers, with details on categories such as 'Purchase and consumption history' and 'Internet or other electronic network activity.' These notices are integrated into privacy policies and updated frequently.
process_and_outcome: Google faced early scrutiny but implemented robust disclosures, avoiding major CCPA fines and receiving positive mentions in regulatory compliance reviews.

example_background: Gillette, a personal care products company under Procter & Gamble, operated websites that gathered personal information from California consumers such as browsing activity, device identifiers, and purchase data. The privacy policy failed to disclose that certain categories of this data were sold or shared with advertising partners for retargeting and measurement. This lack of transparency persisted even after CCPA's effective date, preventing consumers from exercising their rights to know about data sales and to opt out.
process_and_outcome: Following a 2022 enforcement action by the California Attorney General, Gillette settled by paying $1.5 million and agreeing to update its privacy practices, including clear disclosures of sold or shared data categories and streamlined opt-out processes across its sites.

example_background: Mobilize, a tech platform facilitating event organization and mobilization, collected personal data from California residents including names, emails, phone numbers, and political preferences. Its privacy policy did not disclose the categories of personal information shared with third parties for advertising and analytics, despite engaging in practices that qualified as 'sharing' under CCPA definitions. This was identified during a routine compliance check by state regulators.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency issued the first-ever CCPA citation in 2024, fining Mobilize $1.2 million for multiple violations including inadequate disclosures, leading to mandated policy revisions and a compliance roadmap.

example_name: VIZIO Smart TV Data Sharing Settlement
example_background: VIZIO, a manufacturer of smart televisions, equipped its devices with automatic content recognition software that collected viewing habits, demographics, and device data from California consumers without clear notice. The company sold this data to third parties for advertising while its privacy policy omitted specifics on the categories of personal information involved in these transactions, affecting millions of users.
process_and_outcome: In a 2023 class action settlement tied to CCPA enforcement, VIZIO paid $2.6 million and agreed to enhance disclosures stating exact data categories sold, provide opt-out options, and delete previously collected data.
source_rule: (1) The category or categories of consumers’ personal information it has sold or shared, or if the business has not sold or shared consumers’ personal information, it shall disclose that fact.

example_background: Mozilla, the organization behind Firefox browser, publishes a detailed privacy notice that categorically states it does not sell personal information collected from California users, such as browsing history, IP addresses, and device information. The policy further explains limited sharing practices with service providers and provides opt-out mechanisms where applicable, serving as a model for transparency in the tech sector.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla's practices have undergone third-party audits with positive findings, and it has avoided enforcement actions, demonstrating sustained adherence through regular policy updates and public commitments to privacy.

example_name: California AG Sweep Against Home Depot
example_background: Home Depot, a large home improvement retailer, operated e-commerce sites collecting consumer data like purchase history, search queries, and contact information. Its privacy policy did not specify categories of personal information sold or shared with ad tech vendors using cookies and pixels for retargeting, despite these practices qualifying under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: As part of a 2022 enforcement initiative, Home Depot settled for $375,000, agreeing to implement detailed disclosures, a global opt-out link, and monitoring for compliance.
source_rule: (1) The category or categories of consumers’ personal information it has sold or shared, or if the business has not sold or shared consumers’ personal information, it shall disclose that fact.

example_name: California AG Action Against Gaze Tracking Companies
example_background: In a novel enforcement action, the California Attorney General targeted three companies—X-Mode, Outlogic, and Adsquare—that purchased precise geolocation data from various apps and websites. These third parties received location data originally collected and sold by first-party businesses without consumer knowledge of the resale chain. The companies then aggregated and resold this data to clients including data brokers and advertisers, bypassing requirements for explicit notice and opt-out opportunities for California consumers whose data was involved.
process_and_outcome: Announced in August 2024, the AG secured settlements totaling over $1.2 million. Each company agreed to cease collecting or selling California geolocation data without proper CCPA compliance, implement deletion protocols, and provide transparency reports.
source_rule: (d) A third party shall not sell or share personal information about a consumer that has been sold to, or shared with, the third party by a business unless the consumer has received explicit notice and is provided an opportunity to exercise the right to opt-out pursuant to Section 1798.120.

example_background: CMG Brands, operating beauty and wellness brands like Physicians Formula, shared personal information collected from California consumers with third-party service providers for advertising and data enrichment. These third parties resold or shared the data further without the company ensuring consumers received explicit prior notice or an opt-out option, contravening disclosure obligations for downstream data flows.
process_and_outcome: California AG imposed a $1.275 million penalty in 2024, with CMG agreeing to program-wide remediation including updated privacy notices and vendor contracts enforcing CCPA compliance.
source_rule: (d) A third party shall not sell or share personal information about a consumer that has been sold to, or shared with, the third party by a business unless the consumer has received explicit notice and is provided an opportunity to exercise the right to opt-out pursuant to Section 1798.120.

example_background: Walmart, a large retailer with significant California operations, established a vendor compliance program requiring all third parties receiving consumer data to adhere to CCPA opt-out signals and provide explicit notices. When data is shared for analytics or marketing, Walmart ensures downstream recipients are contractually bound to honor consumer directives, with regular audits to verify notice and opt-out availability.
process_and_outcome: Walmart's program was highlighted in its 2023 privacy report and has not faced CCPA enforcement, serving as an example in regulatory guidance for scalable third-party management.
source_rule: (d) A third party shall not sell or share personal information about a consumer that has been sold to, or shared with, the third party by a business unless the consumer has received explicit notice and is provided an opportunity to exercise the right to opt-out pursuant to Section 1798.120.

example_name: Mozilla CCPA Do Not Sell/Do Not Share Implementation
example_background: Mozilla, operator of Firefox, implemented prominent 'Do Not Share' links across its properties, automatically honoring Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals to prevent sharing personal data with third parties for cross-context behavioral advertising. Explicit notices inform users when data may be shared and provide opt-out options propagated to all recipients.
process_and_outcome: Recognized by the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) in guidance documents as best practice. Mozilla received no violations and contributes to open-source privacy tools.

example_name: Home Depot CCPA Vendor Data Sharing Audit
example_background: Home Depot conducted enterprise-wide audits of third-party data sharing, implementing contractual requirements for vendors to provide opt-out mechanisms and notices when reselling or resharing consumer data obtained from Home Depot, such as purchase and loyalty program information.
process_and_outcome: The proactive measures were detailed in Home Depot's privacy disclosures and have positioned the company favorably in regulatory reviews, avoiding enforcement.
source_rule: (d) A third party shall not sell or share personal information about a consumer that has been sold to, or shared with, the third party by a business unless the consumer has received explicit notice and is provided an opportunity to exercise the right to opt-out pursuant to Section 1798.120.

example_name: CMT Digital Period Tracking App Settlement
example_background: CMT Digital developed 'My Period Tracker,' an app that gathered sensitive personal health data on menstrual cycles from users and transmitted it to third parties including Facebook for advertising without informing users of these purposes or providing controls to limit disclosures of such sensitive information.
process_and_outcome: The FTC reached a 2021 settlement banning the sharing of reproductive health data with advertisers, requiring privacy assessments, and mandating notices about data uses and consumer rights to limit sensitive data handling.
source_rule: a consumer’s sensitive personal information for purposes other than those specified in this subdivision shall provide notice to consumers, pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 1798.135, that this information may be used, or disclosed to a service provider or contractor, for additional, specified purposes and that consumers have the right to limit the use or disclosure of their sensitive personal information.

example_background: Google updated its privacy policy and controls in response to CCPA and CPRA, providing detailed notices on its privacy hub about the collection and use of sensitive personal information such as precise location, racial/ethnic data from ad personalization, and health inferences. Users receive options to limit uses for advertising and disclosures to service providers for specified additional purposes.
process_and_outcome: Google avoided major enforcement actions by proactively implementing granular controls like 'sensitive settings' in ad personalization, demonstrating adherence through annual transparency reports and third-party audits.
source_rule: a consumer’s sensitive personal information for purposes other than those specified in this subdivision shall provide notice to consumers, pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 1798.135, that this information may be used, or disclosed to a service provider or contractor, for additional, specified purposes and that consumers have the right to limit the use or disclosure of their sensitive personal information.

example_background: Meta (Facebook) enhanced its data practices post-CCPA/CPRA by introducing 'Off-Facebook Activity' tools and sensitive ad categories restrictions, notifying users about uses of sensitive personal information like health, religion, and sexual orientation inferred from activities, and offering limits on use for advertising purposes beyond core platform services.
process_and_outcome: Following privacy audits and settlements in other jurisdictions, Meta implemented these features, achieving compliance recognition and reducing complaint volumes related to sensitive data notices.
source_rule: a consumer’s sensitive personal information for purposes other than those specified in this subdivision shall provide notice to consumers, pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 1798.135, that this information may be used, or disclosed to a service provider or contractor, for additional, specified purposes and that consumers have the right to limit the use or disclosure of their sensitive personal information.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, collected and processed personal information from California residents through its app and website. The company engaged in activities that involved sharing personal information with third parties for cross-context behavioral advertising purposes. Consumers submitted opt-out requests through Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals and other mechanisms, but DoorDash continued to share the data despite these directives. The platform's privacy policy outlined data practices, yet implementation gaps led to ongoing sharing post-opt-out. This occurred amid growing awareness of CCPA rights following the 2023 amendments that introduced the opt-out of sharing provision.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement action in 2024, resulting in the first-ever CCPA fine of $100,000 against DoorDash. The agency cited failure to honor opt-out signals as the basis. DoorDash agreed to update its systems for GPC recognition and data sharing practices.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_name: CPPA Citation Against Los Angeles Times
example_background: The Los Angeles Times operated an online news platform that collected personal information from users, including identifiers and browsing behavior. The publication shared this data with advertising networks for targeted ads, which qualified as sharing under CCPA. When California consumers activated opt-out preferences via browser signals like GPC, the Times' systems did not consistently process these requests, leading to continued data sharing. This issue surfaced during routine compliance reviews by state authorities focused on media companies' digital practices.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA issued a formal citation and $9,000 fine to the Los Angeles Times for inadequate opt-out mechanisms. The newspaper was required to implement technical fixes and conduct internal audits to ensure future compliance.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_name: Outrigger Media CCPA Fine by California AG
example_background: Outrigger Media operated as an advertising technology firm that facilitated the sale and sharing of consumer personal information, including precise geolocation data from mobile apps. The company transacted with data brokers without implementing mechanisms to process consumer opt-out requests under CCPA. California residents' data was shared without regard for directives to cease sales or sharing, affecting millions through connected apps. This was part of broader scrutiny on ad tech firms' data monetization.
process_and_outcome: In August 2023, the California Attorney General secured a $3.5 million settlement with Outrigger Media, marking one of the first CCPA-specific penalties. The firm was mandated to cease unauthorized data sales and enhance opt-out processes.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5, requiring apps to obtain explicit user permission before tracking and sharing identifiers like the Advertising ID (IDFA) across apps and websites for advertising. This framework directly supported CCPA opt-out rights by enabling users to direct businesses not to share data for cross-context behavioral advertising. Apple's implementation included system-level controls and GPC signal recognition, applied globally but particularly benefiting California users.
process_and_outcome: The feature led to widespread adoption, with many apps reporting reduced tracking rates. Apple faced no CCPA penalties and received praise from privacy advocates for facilitating consumer rights.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_name: CPPA Action Against GTM Payload LLC
example_background: GTM Payload LLC provided Google Tag Manager plugins to websites, enabling the transfer of personal information to third-party analytics and ad platforms. The service ignored Global Privacy Control (GPC) opt-out signals from California users, resulting in continued sharing of browsing and device data despite consumer directives. This affected numerous websites using the plugin.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA issued a $9,000 citation to GTM Payload, requiring updates to recognize and honor GPC signals across its services.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_background: Mozilla, through its Firefox browser and advocacy efforts, developed and promoted the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal, a standardized opt-out mechanism for consumers to direct businesses not to sell or share their personal information. The company integrated GPC into Firefox and provided resources for websites to comply, demonstrating proactive support for CCPA rights across its ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla's efforts contributed to industry-wide adoption, with no enforcement actions against it. The initiative was recognized in CPPA regulations as a compliant opt-out method.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, collected viewing habits and other personal data from California consumers via its SmartCast platform and sold or shared it with third parties for advertising without adequate opt-out options. The company tracked users across devices and failed to provide easy mechanisms for residents to exercise their rights under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: In February 2023, the California Attorney General settled with Vizio for $2.2 million, requiring prominent opt-out notices, data deletion capabilities, and annual privacy audits.
source_rule: 1798.120.Consumers’ Right to Opt Out of Sale or Sharing of Personal Information (a) (1) A consumer shall have the right, at any time, to direct a business that sells or shares personal information about the consumer to third parties not to sell or share the consumer’s personal information. This right may be referred to as the right to opt out of sale or sharing.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform serving millions of California users, sold or shared consumer personal information such as order histories, delivery addresses, and device identifiers with advertising partners without adequately honoring consumer opt-out requests submitted through the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal or providing clear mechanisms for consumers to direct the company not to engage in such activities. The platform's data practices involved extensive sharing for targeted advertising, impacting users who expected their data handling to align with privacy expectations.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the California Attorney General secured a $375,000 settlement with DoorDash, requiring implementation of GPC recognition across all properties, maintenance of an up-to-date list of service providers and third parties receiving personal information, and submittal of compliance reports along with annual privacy training for relevant staff.

example_name: TikTok COPPA and CCPA Minors Investigation
example_background: TikTok, the popular short-video app, collected personal information from users under 16 without obtaining verifiable parental consent and continued to share such data for advertising purposes even after awareness of user ages through account settings and behavioral data. The platform's algorithms and features enabled extensive data sharing with third parties, affecting millions of minor users in California.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the FTC imposed a $5.7 million penalty for COPPA violations, with additional CCPA scrutiny leading to enhanced age gates, default private settings for minors, and prohibitions on sharing minors' data without consent; California AG coordinated in ongoing compliance oversight.

example_background: Mozilla integrated the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal into its Firefox browser, automatically sending opt-out signals with every request to websites, enabling consumers to direct businesses not to sell or share their personal information. This browser-level enforcement ensured that participating sites received and processed these signals without requiring additional user action.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla's efforts contributed to CCPA compliance across the web ecosystem, with no enforcement actions against it and recognition from California regulators for advancing standardized opt-out mechanisms; adoption grew to support millions of users.

example_background: Meta (formerly Facebook) did not consistently recognize and act on Global Privacy Control opt-out signals across its platforms including Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, continuing to share user personal information for advertising purposes despite receiving these consumer directions through supported browsers.
process_and_outcome: California AG enforcement in 2024 resulted in a settlement requiring Meta to fully implement GPC across all properties, pay penalties as part of broader actions, and provide regular compliance reports.

example_background: Grokster, operator of the Muslim Pro app with millions of users, collected precise geolocation data classified as sensitive personal information. The app shared this data with data brokers and advertising networks for purposes unrelated to the core prayer time notification service, such as location-based ad targeting and profiling. Users were not provided with a straightforward way to limit these disclosures, and the privacy notices did not clearly delineate uses necessary for expected services versus additional commercial exploitation. This practice persisted over years, affecting a large user base seeking religious services, with data sales generating revenue streams.
process_and_outcome: The California AG settled with Grokster in 2022 for $200,000, mandating cessation of unauthorized geolocation sharing, deletion of collected data, and implementation of controls allowing users to limit sensitive data use. The company also faced additional FTC scrutiny.

example_name: Apple's App Tracking Transparency and Sensitive Data Limits
example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework and extended it with Intelligent Tracking Prevention, alongside privacy nutrition labels in the App Store. For apps collecting sensitive personal information like health data, precise location, or biometrics, Apple requires developers to obtain explicit user consent before tracking across apps or websites and provides toggles in iOS settings for limiting sensitive data sharing. Users can direct restrictions via system-wide privacy dashboards, ensuring uses are confined to expected services like navigation or fitness tracking, with disclosures minimized.
process_and_outcome: Apple's implementation has been recognized in regulatory comments and industry analyses as aligning with CPRA requirements, enabling millions of opt-outs and reducing unauthorized sensitive data use across the ecosystem. No enforcement actions have targeted Apple for these features.

example_background: CM.com, a global conversation platform, conducted a third-party audit under the California Voluntary Regulatory Sandbox and implemented controls for sensitive personal information, including government identifiers and precise geolocation used in messaging services. The company established user portals allowing directives to limit data use to service performance, such as communication delivery, and integrated GPC signal support to automate restrictions on disclosures for advertising.
process_and_outcome: Received certification in 2023 as one of the first under the sandbox, demonstrating adherence through documented processes, with ongoing monitoring and no violations noted.

example_background: L'Oréal collected precise geolocation and biometric data via AR beauty apps for virtual try-ons, disclosing to third parties for analytics beyond service expectations. No direct controls existed for users to limit these uses prior to enforcement.
process_and_outcome: 2022 settlement for $750,000, with mandates for sensitive data controls, GPC integration, and audits.

example_background: Pandora, a music streaming service, collected personal information from California listeners including listening habits, search queries, device data, and IP addresses. The company shared this data with third-party advertisers and analytics firms for personalization and ad targeting. In the period following the CCPA's effective date and leading into CPRA amendments, Pandora's website lacked a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link and did not provide notice in its privacy policy that California consumers' data was subject to sale or sharing with opt-out rights available.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated enforcement in 2024, resulting in a $100,000 settlement. Pandora was required to add compliant opt-out links, update privacy notices, and ensure global opt-out tools align with CCPA/CPRA requirements.
source_rule: (b) A business that sells consumers’ personal information to, or shares it with, third parties shall provide notice to consumers, pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 1798.135, that this information may be sold or shared and that consumers have the “right to opt out” of the sale or sharing of their personal information.

example_background: Facebook (now Meta) tracked California users' activities across its platforms and third-party sites via Pixel technology, collecting data like browsing history, purchases, and interests. This data was shared with advertisers and partners. In June 2020, during CCPA audits, Facebook lacked a functional 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link for California residents despite engaging in data sales/sharing.
process_and_outcome: California AG publicly cited Facebook among six companies in June 2020 for CCPA shortcomings, prompting Facebook to add the required link and update notices. No monetary penalty at that stage but served as formal notice.

example_background: Citrix, a workspace software provider, collected California consumers' personal data including IP addresses, device info, and usage patterns through its websites. This data was shared with third-party vendors for advertising without providing notice of sharing or an opt-out link as required under CCPA/CPRA. The issue persisted post-CCPA effective date into 2023.
process_and_outcome: CPPA announced a $1.1 million settlement in 2024, requiring Citrix to implement compliant disclosures, opt-out functionality, and employee training programs.

example_name: The New York Times CCPA Compliance Implementation
example_background: The New York Times, operating digital news platforms with significant California readership, discloses in its privacy policy the sharing of personal information such as reading habits, subscriptions, and device data with third parties for advertising and analytics. It provides a prominent 'Your Privacy Choices' section with a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Information' opt-out link, detailing categories of data shared and purposes, fully aligned with CCPA notice requirements.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; recognized in privacy reports as a positive example of transparent practices, enhancing consumer trust.
source_rule: (b) A business that sells consumers’ personal information to, or shares it with, third parties shall provide notice to consumers, pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 1798.135, that this information may be sold or shared and that consumers have the “right to opt out” of the sale or sharing of their personal information.

example_background: Epic Games, the developer of Fortnite, collected personal information from users through its online services without obtaining verifiable parental consent for children under 13 years of age. The game allowed children to engage in chat functions, make purchases, and share data, with the company failing to implement adequate age screening or parental verification processes. This occurred over several years prior to the action, affecting millions of young users whose data was used for behavioral advertising and other purposes without authorization.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission investigated the practices, leading to a settlement in December 2022. Epic Games agreed to pay $520 million, including $275 million in redress to the FTC and $245 million in refunds to affected users, and implement system-wide changes for age-appropriate experiences and parental consent mechanisms.

example_background: Meta's Facebook platform designed features such as friend suggestions, contact uploads, and people-you-may-know tools that collected persistent identifiers and other personal data from users under 13 without notifying parents or obtaining verifiable consent. This data collection persisted even after users indicated they were under 13 during sign-up attempts, affecting child users since at least 2014.
process_and_outcome: The FTC's investigation resulted in a $5.1 billion settlement in 2019, with specific COPPA violations contributing to findings of systemic privacy failures. Meta was mandated to overhaul child data practices, appoint compliance leaders, and submit to independent audits.

example_name: California AG Enforcement Against Data Brokers
example_background: In 2023, the California Attorney General investigated data brokers such as Outlogic and others for selling precise geolocation data of California residents, including potentially minors, without opt-in consent. The brokers purchased mobile location data from apps and SDKs, aggregating it into profiles sold to advertisers and others, disregarding age verification despite knowing datasets included children.
process_and_outcome: The AG secured settlements totaling millions, including $1.25 million from Outlogic. Brokers agreed to cease selling raw location data, implement age screening, and obtain opt-in consent before sharing sensitive data under CCPA/CPRA.

process_and_outcome: Settled with New York AG in 2023 for $2.5 million, part of broader child privacy actions. The company committed to COPPA-compliant design changes and parental tools.

example_name: Common Sense Media Report on EdTech COPPA Compliance
example_background: Educational technology companies like Google Classroom and Zoom for Education implemented age-appropriate defaults and parental consent portals for K-12 users. They blocked personalized ad tracking for verified under-13 accounts and provided school administrators with data control tools, handling student data responsibly during remote learning surges.
process_and_outcome: Recognized in 2021 Common Sense Privacy report for strong practices, avoiding enforcement actions. Companies maintained compliance through annual audits and transparency reports.

process_and_outcome: Following 2023 FTC COPPA sweep, Microsoft demonstrated proactive compliance, earning praise in enforcement reports and avoiding penalties through certified processes.
