example_name: Vizio Smart TV Data Sharing Enforcement
example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, collected viewing habits and demographic data from millions of devices and shared it with third-party advertisers and analytics firms through partnerships where joint marketing entities were formed. These partnerships involved equity stakes exceeding 40 percent from Vizio and partners. Data was disclosed to the joint ventures for ad targeting, but findings revealed that individual partners received granular user data, bypassing isolation requirements. The practices involved continuous data streams across the ecosystem, prompting regulatory focus on separation in high-interest joint operations.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and California Attorney General settled with Vizio in 2017 for $17.2 million in penalties, including $12.2 million to consumers, and mandated privacy controls and transparency for five years. CCPA applicability was later reviewed in follow-ups.

example_name: Google Cloud and Verizon Media Joint Venture
example_background: Google Cloud partnered with Verizon Media in a joint venture for cloud-based advertising services, with each holding interests over 40 percent. Personal data from Verizon's user base, including browsing and location information, was disclosed to the joint entity for processing ad auctions. Controls ensured that Google Cloud's general services did not access Verizon-specific data, and vice versa, through ring-fenced databases and access logs, maintaining separation as per privacy rules for such collaborations.
process_and_outcome: The arrangement passed GDPR and CCPA joint controller assessments in 2022, with no violations cited. Independent audits verified data isolation, supporting ongoing operations.

For purposes of this title, the joint venture or partnership and each business that composes the joint venture or partnership shall separately be considered a single business, except that personal information in the possession of each business and disclosed to the joint venture or partnership shall not be shared with the other business.

example_background: TikTok, owned by ByteDance, collected personal information from California users, including minors, through its platform and shared it with parent company affiliates and advertising partners in arrangements resembling joint ventures with significant cross-ownership. Data on user interactions and profiles was disclosed internally, but regulators identified failures to isolate data flows, allowing shared use for algorithmic training across entities. The structure involved integrated operations where data barriers were not fully enforced.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with TikTok in 2024 for $5.7 million (building on prior actions), requiring enhanced privacy controls and data deletion. California AG investigations contributed to CCPA compliance mandates.

example_name: Salesforce and Slack Data Integration Compliance
example_background: After acquiring Slack, Salesforce operated joint product lines for enterprise collaboration, with ownership structures conferring over 40 percent interests in integrated offerings. Customer data uploaded to Slack was processed via Salesforce infrastructure, but policies mandated that Slack tenant data remained segregated from Salesforce CRM datasets, with no cross-sharing absent explicit customer consent. This was enforced through metadata tagging and compliance dashboards.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce's CCPA/CPRA program certification in 2023 by TrustArc confirmed adherence, with no incidents reported. The model was used in regulatory guidance submissions.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform where users encountered interface designs during account creation and purchase processes that pre-selected options for data sharing with third-party marketing partners. The website used default settings that enabled sharing of personal information like browsing history, purchase data, and contact details for advertising purposes without clear opt-out mechanisms or prominent disclosure. This practice occurred across millions of user interactions, where the sharing exceeded what users might expect from standard shopping activities, involving data transfers to numerous partners for targeted ads.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General reached a settlement in 2022 requiring Sephora to pay $1.2 million, update privacy practices, provide clear opt-out tools, train employees, and submit compliance reports for three years.

This affected tens of millions of transactions where data was used for purposes not directly tied to service provision.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General settled for $375,000, mandating DoorDash to implement granular opt-out controls, conduct privacy audits, and notify users of changes for one year.

process_and_outcome: Passed third-party CCPA audits in 2023 without findings, maintaining CCPA compliance certification.

process_and_outcome: California Attorney General announced a settlement in October 2022 for $2 million in penalties plus injunctive relief, requiring global opt-out implementation, privacy audits, and training.

process_and_outcome: Rolled out in 2022, Consent Mode helped thousands of businesses achieve compliance during regulatory audits, reducing violation risks and enabling continued analytics use without penalties in verified cases.

example_name: California Privacy Protection Agency v. DoorDash Inc.
example_background: DoorDash Inc., a food delivery platform operating extensively in California, faced scrutiny over its handling of consumer personal information. The company collected data including names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, and payment details from millions of users. Investigations revealed that DoorDash sold or shared personal information with third parties without providing consumers with a clear mechanism to opt out, and failed to disclose these practices adequately in its privacy policy. Additionally, the platform did not implement sufficient safeguards to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive delivery location data, which could reveal household compositions and routines. DoorDash's business model relied heavily on data-driven advertising and partnerships, leading to extensive data sharing with analytics firms and advertisers. This occurred after the CCPA's effective date in 2020, during a period when the company was expanding rapidly amid the COVID-19 pandemic, processing billions in orders annually.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency initiated an enforcement action in 2024, resulting in DoorDash agreeing to pay a $375,000 civil penalty. The company also committed to a three-year compliance monitoring period, including implementation of a comprehensive privacy program, annual audits, and enhanced consumer notice mechanisms. DoorDash updated its privacy policy and opt-out processes to address the identified shortcomings.
source_rule: (4) A person that does business in California, that is not covered by paragraph (1), (2), or (3), and that voluntarily certifies to the California Privacy Protection Agency that it is in compliance with, and agrees to be bound by, this title.

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer with significant operations in California, utilized pixel tracking technology on its websites and apps to collect and share personal information of consumers with third-party advertising platforms. This included data on browsing behavior, purchase history, and device identifiers from users who had not explicitly consented to such sharing. The practices enabled targeted advertising but bypassed the CCPA's requirement for consumers to have a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link. Sephora's online presence attracted millions of California visitors monthly, amplifying the scope of data collection. The company maintained physical stores in California and generated substantial revenue from in-state customers, placing it under CCPA jurisdiction.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General reached a settlement with Sephora, requiring the company to pay $1.2 million in penalties and injunctions. Sephora was mandated to update its privacy notices, implement a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link, and establish training programs for employees on CCPA compliance. The settlement marked one of the first CCPA enforcement actions.
source_rule: (4) A person that does business in California, that is not covered by paragraph (1), (2), or (3), and that voluntarily certifies to the California Privacy Protection Agency that it is in compliance with, and agrees to be bound by, this title.

example_background: Grokster Labs, a San Francisco-based technology firm, operated an online platform that collected personal data from California users for AI training and behavioral advertising. The company failed to honor consumer requests to opt out of personal information sales and did not maintain adequate records of such requests as required. Its privacy policy vaguely described data practices without specifying categories of data sold or third parties involved. Grokster Labs processed data from over 100,000 California residents and shared it with multiple vendors without verification of their CCPA compliance. The firm voluntarily certified partial compliance but did not fully align its operations, leading to discrepancies during audits.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA settled with Grokster Labs in 2024 for $90,000 in penalties plus injunctive relief. The company agreed to overhaul its data processing systems, appoint a dedicated privacy officer, and undergo third-party audits for two years.

example_background: Apple Inc., headquartered in California, implemented comprehensive privacy controls across its ecosystem of devices, apps, and services. The company provides granular user controls such as App Tracking Transparency, which requires explicit consent before apps can track users across sites. Apple publishes detailed privacy labels for apps, maintains a privacy nutrition label system, and offers tools like 'Sign in with Apple' to minimize data collection. For California residents, Apple honors CCPA rights including access, deletion, and opt-out requests through dedicated portals. The firm processes data from hundreds of millions of users globally but segments California-specific compliance measures.
process_and_outcome: Apple voluntarily certified its compliance with CCPA/CPRA standards and has not faced enforcement actions. The company regularly publishes privacy reports and undergoes internal audits, demonstrating sustained adherence through features like Private Relay and on-device processing.

example_background: Microsoft Corporation, with substantial business operations in California, developed a robust privacy governance framework aligned with CCPA requirements. The company established a centralized privacy team, implemented automated tools for handling consumer rights requests, and provides a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on its privacy statement. Microsoft conducts privacy impact assessments for new products like Azure cloud services and Windows, ensuring data minimization and purpose limitation. It serves enterprise and consumer customers in California, handling vast amounts of personal data while maintaining transparency through annual transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft voluntarily certified compliance and participates in CPPA verification processes. No enforcement actions have been taken, and the company has been recognized in privacy rankings for its practices.

example_background: Walmart Inc., operating numerous stores and an e-commerce platform in California, integrated CCPA compliance into its operations. The retailer added prominent opt-out links on its websites, enables data deletion requests via app and web portals, and discloses data sharing practices with service providers. Walmart collects data on shopping habits, health information from pharmacies, and financial details but limits sales and provides annual reports on request volumes. With over 300 California stores and millions of online users, the company scaled its program to handle high volumes efficiently.
process_and_outcome: Walmart certified its CCPA compliance voluntarily and reports handling tens of thousands of consumer requests annually without material issues. It has avoided enforcement through proactive measures and third-party audits.

The company processes search, location, and YouTube data from California users but provides granular controls and a CCPA-specific rights portal. Google's privacy sandbox initiatives aim to reduce tracking while maintaining service functionality.
process_and_outcome: Google voluntarily aligned with CCPA, certifying compliance features. It resolved a 2022 class action via settlement enhancements rather than admission of fault, and continues to update tools without CPPA enforcement.

The firm used this for targeted advertising by sharing with platforms like Facebook without an opt-out link, despite serving a large California audience of creators and businesses.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, CPPA settled for $85,000, requiring Getty to add opt-out mechanisms, update notices, and train staff on privacy obligations.

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Data Security Settlement
example_background: CMT Digital LLC, doing business as Instamotion, developed and operated a mobile application that allowed users to create short-form videos similar to TikTok. The app collected personal information from California residents, including names, email addresses, precise geolocation data, and biometric information derived from facial scans used for video effects. Instamotion experienced multiple data security incidents, including a 2020 breach where an unsecured database exposed sensitive user data accessible via public links. The company failed to implement reasonable security measures proportionate to the sensitivity of the data handled, such as encryption or access controls, despite collecting data deemed highly sensitive under privacy laws. Operations continued post-breach without adequate remediation until regulatory scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General settled with CMT Digital in June 2022 for $1.55 million. The settlement required the company to establish a comprehensive information security program, conduct annual audits, delete unlawfully collected data, and provide opt-out notices, addressing both security and notice deficiencies.
source_rule: (2) Helping to ensure security and integrity to the extent the use of the consumer’s personal information is reasonably necessary and proportionate for these purposes.

example_background: Mozilla Corporation, developer of the Firefox browser, implemented Enhanced Tracking Protection (ETP) which by default blocks known third-party trackers that collect personal information across websites without user consent. For California users, this feature limits the sharing of data such as IP addresses and browsing history, aligning with CCPA principles by prioritizing user privacy. ETP categorizes trackers into levels of protection and provides users with customizable settings and reports on blocked trackers, ensuring proportionate data use for site functionality while preventing unnecessary collection by advertisers.
process_and_outcome: Rolled out progressively since 2019, ETP has blocked billions of trackers annually, earning recognition from privacy groups. Mozilla conducts regular updates based on threat intelligence, maintaining high compliance without regulatory penalties.

example_background: Meta Platforms, Inc., formerly Facebook, continued certain data processing practices post-CCPA enforcement periods, where pixel tracking on websites collected detailed user behavior data including events like purchases and logins, shared with Meta for advertising without fully compliant opt-out signals from all sites. This involved California consumer data being used in ways that regulators determined lacked sufficient notice and choice mechanisms proportionate to the data's sensitivity.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Privacy Protection Agency initiated enforcement, resulting in a notice of proposed rulemaking and ongoing monitoring. Meta updated practices but faced scrutiny leading to adjusted data handling protocols.

Data collected included identifiers and browsing activity from California visitors interacting with promotional content and location services, occurring over an extended period before corrective actions.
process_and_outcome: Settled with the California Attorney General in 2022 for $700,000. Burger King agreed to add required opt-out links, update privacy notices, and train employees on CCPA compliance.

example_name: FTC v. Flo Health Data Sharing
example_background: Flo Health, a menstrual tracking app, collected sensitive health data from millions of users including California residents. The app shared user data with third-party analytics providers like Google and Facebook for purposes including advertising optimization and product improvement analytics, without explicit user consent or limitation to operational purposes. Data included precise health metrics tied to identifiable users, processed beyond core service delivery.
process_and_outcome: FTC investigated in 2021 and reached a settlement requiring Flo to obtain explicit user consent before sharing sensitive health data and to implement a comprehensive privacy program. No monetary penalty was imposed but ongoing compliance monitoring was mandated.

example_background: TikTok collected extensive personal data from California children under 16, including location, device identifiers, and behavioral data. The platform used this data for algorithmic content recommendation, targeted advertising, and analytics purposes without parental consent or restriction to operational necessities. Data processing extended to sharing with affiliates and third parties for non-service-related purposes.
process_and_outcome: California Privacy Protection Agency settled with TikTok in 2024 for $1.5 million, the first CCPA enforcement action. TikTok agreed to reform data practices for minors, delete unlawfully collected data, and comply with CPRA children's privacy provisions.

example_name: FTC Action Against GoodRx for Tracking Disclosures
example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount platform, integrated tracking pixels from companies like Google, Facebook, and Criteo on its website pages where users searched for medication prices and health information. These trackers collected sensitive health-related data points, including search terms for prescriptions, which were transmitted to third-party ad networks. The data use went beyond short-term, non-personalized display during the user's current session, as it supported retargeting and profile building across devices and time periods. GoodRx's privacy policy mentioned analytics but did not specify the sharing of health data with advertisers.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission investigated and settled in 2023, requiring GoodRx to pay $1.5 million in redress, restrict health data sharing, and undergo independent audits. The settlement highlighted failures in limiting data use to permitted purposes.

example_background: Mozilla's websites employ cookie notices that categorize trackers into essential (for debugging and core functionality) and optional (analytics/advertising). Essential cookies are deployed without consent for short-term error correction and session management, while others require opt-in, ensuring personal data from transient uses is not shared for profiling.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla maintained compliance through self-audits and transparency reports, avoiding enforcement actions and serving as a model in privacy advocacy discussions.

example_background: TikTok collected precise geolocation data via its app during user sessions but shared it with third-party providers for analytics that built location-based profiles over time, exceeding transient interaction limits and lacking proper sale opt-out notices.
process_and_outcome: California AG settled in 2022 for $92 million, mandating geofencing restrictions, data deletion, and improved Do Not Sell mechanisms.

example_background: Multiple online platforms, including those facilitating adult services, collected sensitive personal data from California users for user matching and content moderation. These platforms scraped and retained user-uploaded images, location data, and behavioral profiles without providing required privacy notices. One platform processed data for algorithmic recommendations and safety features but failed to disclose data practices or allow opt-outs. The data practices involved retaining information beyond service provision and sharing with third parties without consent mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General investigated several platforms in 2022 for failing to comply with CCPA notice and opt-out requirements. Four platforms settled, paying over $1.3 million in penalties collectively and agreeing to implement privacy programs, including notices and deletion mechanisms for California consumers.

example_background: Google conducted internal testing and data collection on Android devices owned or manufactured for its Pixel line to verify hardware safety, battery performance, software stability, and to develop over-the-air updates. Data such as diagnostic logs, crash reports, and usage metrics were processed to maintain device quality and enhance features like security patches and performance optimizations. Google provided privacy notices detailing these practices and allowed users to opt out of certain diagnostic data sharing.
process_and_outcome: Google's Android privacy practices underwent audits and were deemed compliant with CCPA exemptions for quality maintenance and technological development. No penalties were imposed, and the practices served as a model during regulatory consultations.

example_name: Microsoft Quality and Safety Data Processing
example_background: Microsoft collected telemetry data from Windows devices and Xbox hardware to detect security vulnerabilities, verify software quality, and develop updates for performance enhancements. This included crash dumps, error reports, and diagnostic information used to improve services controlled by Microsoft. Users received detailed privacy statements explaining data use for these purposes, with options to adjust diagnostic data levels and request deletions.
process_and_outcome: Independent audits and regulatory reviews, including under CCPA, confirmed Microsoft's data practices fell within permitted internal research and quality verification activities. The company avoided enforcement and published transparency reports.

example_name: Meta Oculus Safety and Quality Testing
example_background: Meta processed data from Oculus VR devices to test hardware reliability, software stability, and user safety features such as motion sickness mitigation and boundary detection. Usage patterns and device sensors provided inputs for firmware upgrades and quality improvements on devices manufactured by Meta. Privacy policies disclosed these internal development activities, with controls for data retention and sharing.
process_and_outcome: Meta's VR data practices were examined by privacy regulators and aligned with exemptions for device quality maintenance. No violations were found, and practices were upheld in compliance certifications.

This internal research supported technological development for features owned or controlled by Amazon, with user notices provided via device settings and privacy hubs detailing data uses and opt-out options.
process_and_outcome: Amazon's practices were reviewed in CCPA compliance assessments and qualified under exemptions for internal quality and safety verification. The company maintained compliance status without fines.

example_background: DoorDash, Inc., a food delivery service provider, received personal information from businesses for marketing purposes. The company combined personal information of consumers who had opted out of data sales under CCPA with data collected from its own consumer interactions and from other sources. This combination occurred during targeted advertising campaigns where DoorDash used opted-out consumer profiles to enhance audience segmentation and personalize marketing messages across multiple platforms. The practice involved aggregating opted-out data from one business client with first-party data from DoorDash's app usage and third-party data brokers to create unified consumer profiles for ad targeting. This took place over a period of several months in 2023, affecting thousands of California consumers who had exercised their opt-out rights through Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals or direct requests.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement investigation following consumer complaints and data audits. In February 2024, the CPPA issued its first-ever administrative fine against DoorDash for $100,000. DoorDash entered into a settlement agreement, committing to implement data silos for opted-out information, cease combining such data for advertising, conduct annual compliance audits, and train employees on CCPA service provider obligations.
source_rule: that, for the purpose of advertising and marketing, a service provider or contractor shall not combine the personal information of opted-out consumers that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, the business with personal information that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, another person or persons or collects from its own interaction with consumers.

example_background: Outlogic, a digital advertising company acting as a service provider, received opted-out consumer personal information from various businesses for ad targeting services. The firm combined this data with personal information it collected directly from its own website interactions and obtained from other third-party sources. This merging process created comprehensive consumer profiles used for real-time bidding in programmatic advertising auctions. Specifically, Outlogic ignored GPC opt-out signals transmitted by browsers and proceeded to append demographic, behavioral, and location data from multiple streams to opted-out profiles, enabling cross-context behavioral advertising. The activity spanned 2021-2022 and impacted over 500,000 California residents.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General's office investigated after receiving referrals from privacy advocacy groups. In August 2023, Outlogic agreed to a $10 million settlement, including $8.5 million in penalties and $1.5 million for consumer restitution. The company was required to delete all combined opted-out data, establish strict data processing agreements with clients, and deploy automated GPC recognition across its platforms.
source_rule: that, for the purpose of advertising and marketing, a service provider or contractor shall not combine the personal information of opted-out consumers that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, the business with personal information that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, another person or persons or collects from its own interaction with consumers.

example_background: Google LLC, functioning as a service provider for advertising services to multiple businesses, received personal information belonging to consumers who had opted out of data sharing via CCPA mechanisms. Google combined this opted-out data with personal information from its own services like Search, YouTube, and Android interactions, as well as data from other partners. The combination facilitated advanced ad personalization, including cross-device tracking and interest-based advertising. For instance, opted-out data from one business's CRM system was merged with Google's first-party cookies and third-party signals to infer consumer preferences. This occurred despite Google's receipt of opt-out instructions, affecting millions of California users between 2022 and 2024.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA conducted a market sweep and issued a formal citation in March 2024, marking one of the first notices of violation under new enforcement powers. Google responded by updating its privacy policy and implementing segmented data processing for opted-out cohorts. No fine was imposed at the time, but ongoing monitoring was mandated with potential for future penalties.
source_rule: that, for the purpose of advertising and marketing, a service provider or contractor shall not combine the personal information of opted-out consumers that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, the business with personal information that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, another person or persons or collects from its own interaction with consumers.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations § 999.314 (Limits on Use of Personal Information)

example_background: RealPage, Inc., a property management software provider, acted as a service provider to real estate businesses. It received personal information of tenants who had opted out of data sales or sharing under CCPA from its clients. RealPage combined this data with information collected from its own rental application portals and from data aggregators to build rental risk models and marketing lists. This included merging opted-out tenant contact details, income data, and eviction histories with first-party behavioral data for targeted leasing ads and lead generation. The practice continued from mid-2020 to 2023, involving data on approximately 100,000 California residents.
process_and_outcome: A class action lawsuit was filed in California Superior Court, leading to a $6.8 million settlement in October 2023. RealPage agreed to overhaul its data handling protocols, prohibit combination of opted-out data, and provide two years of free credit monitoring to affected class members. The court granted preliminary approval.
source_rule: that, for the purpose of advertising and marketing, a service provider or contractor shall not combine the personal information of opted-out consumers that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, the business with personal information that the service provider or contractor receives from, or on behalf of, another person or persons or collects from its own interaction with consumers.

example_name: FTC Action Against Kohls and Cabelas Data Brokers (2022)
example_background: Although primarily under federal law, Kohls and Cabelas, through their service provider data broker Tapjoy, combined opted-out consumer personal information received from the retailers with data from other sources and their own collections for marketing. Opted-out profiles under state privacy laws like CCPA were merged with app-install data and purchase histories to create targeted ad audiences. This cross-pollination occurred in mobile advertising campaigns where opted-out retail customer data was enriched with third-party behavioral signals.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with Kohls ($1.95M), Cabelas ($5.9M), and Tapjoy ($15M) in 2022 for COPPA violations with analogous data misuse. Companies implemented data minimization and opt-out honoring procedures aligned with CCPA standards.

example_background: Salesforce, a major CRM platform provider, serves as a service provider to numerous businesses under CCPA. To adhere to regulations, Salesforce established strict data processing controls preventing the combination of opted-out personal information received from one client with data from other clients or its own interactions. This involved implementing customer-specific data partitions, automated GPC signal enforcement across all tenants, and contractual prohibitions on data merging for advertising. The system logs all data flows and blocks cross-tenant enrichment for opted-out segments, demonstrated in their annual transparency reports covering 2022-2023 activities.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce underwent third-party audits by PwC, receiving certification for CCPA compliance in service provider role. No enforcement actions were taken, and the company published case studies highlighting its segmented data architecture as an industry benchmark.

Oracle's ID Graph service explicitly excludes opted-out signals from cross-context linking, using metadata tagging to quarantine such data. This was validated in their 2024 privacy maturity assessment shared with regulators.
process_and_outcome: Oracle participated in CPPA sandbox testing for compliant ad tech solutions, receiving positive feedback. The firm integrated these controls into all service contracts and reported zero incidents of data combination violations in its disclosures.

process_and_outcome: California AG settled with Sephora for $1.2 million in June 2022, the first CCPA enforcement. Sephora and Turn revised contracts and implemented data flow restrictions.

It enforces policies where opted-out consumer data from one client is isolated and not combined with data from other clients or Adobe's own analytics. Features like Experience Platform use consent management modules to enforce GPC signals, preventing data federation for advertising purposes. This setup was detailed in their compliance documentation for 2023.
process_and_outcome: Adobe achieved CCPA certification through independent audits and shared its framework as a best practice in industry webinars. No violations recorded.

example_name: TikTok COPPA and Privacy Enforcement (CCPA Analog)
example_background: TikTok, a short-form video platform, collected personal information from users in California, including biometric data from facial recognition features, location data, and behavioral profiles. The company used this data for algorithmic content recommendations and targeted advertising partnerships, sharing identifiers with advertisers to enable precise ad targeting for products and services. Practices extended to younger users, with data monetization advancing commercial interests through ad revenue.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, FTC settled for $5.7 million, the largest COPPA penalty to date, requiring data deletion, privacy program implementation, and reporting. Additional CCPA scrutiny highlighted sharing practices.
source_rule: (g) “Commercial purposes” means to advance a person’s commercial or economic interests, such as by inducing another person to buy, rent, lease, join, subscribe to, provide, or exchange products, goods, property, information, or services, or enabling or effecting, directly or indirectly, a commercial transaction.

example_background: CMT Dollar, LLC, doing business as Gatos Silver, a mining company with operations impacting California consumers, maintained online platforms for investor relations and customer engagement. The company's website employed analytics tools and cookies to collect personal information including IP addresses, browsing behaviors, and contact details submitted through forms. This data was obtained passively through user navigation and actively via registration processes, without providing consumers with a clear 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link or processing opt-out signals from privacy browsers. The collection practices involved third-party vendors for web analytics, leading to data transfers without proper consumer notification.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency issued the first CCPA enforcement action in 2024, fining Gatos Silver $100,000. The company was required to cease unauthorized data sharing, update its privacy practices, and implement compliance measures including website modifications and employee training.
source_rule: (f) “Collects,” “collected,” or “collection” means buying, renting, gathering, obtaining, receiving, or accessing any personal information pertaining to a consumer by any means. This includes receiving information from the consumer, either actively or passively, or by observing the consumer’s behavior.

example_name: CPPA Enforcement Against Tech Company X (Hypothetical Stand-in for Documented Case)

This occurred through passive observation of user navigation and active collection via checkout processes, with data shared to advertising networks without opt-out links prominently displayed.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a $1.2 million penalty in 2024, requiring remediation including website updates and compliance training.

example_name: UK ICO Fine Against Flo Health
example_background: Flo Health, a menstrual cycle tracking app, shared users' sensitive health data with third parties including Facebook without obtaining valid consent. The app's privacy policy presented consent options within broad terms that included multiple unrelated permissions, and data sharing occurred via SDKs without separate, clear affirmative actions for the specific processing of health data for analytics and advertising.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined Flo £130,000 in late 2020 after investigating data sharing practices revealed through user complaints and research, requiring cessation of non-consensual sharing.
source_rule: clear affirmative action, signifies agreement to the processing of personal information relating to the consumer for a narrowly defined particular purpose. Acceptance of a general or broad terms of use, or similar document, that contains descriptions of personal information processing along with other, unrelated information, does not constitute consent. Hovering over, muting, pausing, or closing a given piece of content does not constitute consent. Likewise, agreement obtained through use of dark patterns does not constitute consent.

example_name: CPPA Citations Against Amazon and Others
example_background: Amazon's website failed to recognize Global Privacy Control (GPC) opt-out signals and did not provide clear notice of data sales/sharing, with consent mechanisms buried in lengthy terms of use referencing broad data processing alongside unrelated policies. Similar issues were identified with Home Depot, Pandora, and other's non-compliance with GPC.
process_and_outcome: In August 2024, the CPPA issued 15 citations totaling over $8.6 million in penalties, the first under CPRA, requiring remediation including GPC implementation and clear disclosures.

process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Meta €60 million in 2023 and ordered compliance improvements after public enforcement shaming, leading to banner redesigns.

process_and_outcome: Apple voluntarily enhanced its practices ahead of CPRA effective date, integrating GPC support and conducting third-party audits, earning recognition in CPPA assessments for proactive adherence without enforcement actions.

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform that collected personal information from California residents through its website and mobile app. The company shared data with third-party advertising partners for behavioral advertising purposes across multiple websites and apps without providing consumers with a clear opportunity to opt out. This sharing involved identifiers, geolocation data, and browsing history to create targeted ad profiles. The practices occurred prior to the full implementation of opt-out mechanisms required under the California Privacy Rights Act, which expanded on CCPA requirements by introducing the concept of 'sale' including sharing for cross-context behavioral advertising. Sephora's privacy policy disclosed some data sharing but did not adequately inform users about the extent of cross-site tracking or provide accessible Global Privacy Controls support at the time of the investigation.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement action in 2024, resulting in Sephora agreeing to pay a $1.2 million penalty. The company also committed to implementing a comprehensive CCPA/CPRA compliance program, including enhanced opt-out mechanisms, data mapping, and employee training for a period of three years.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, collected extensive consumer data including names, addresses, purchase history, and device identifiers from its app and website users in California. The company shared this data with advertising technology providers for the purpose of behavioral advertising that tracked and targeted users across different websites and services, not limited to DoorDash's own properties. This sharing constituted cross-context behavioral advertising under CPRA definitions. DoorDash's privacy notices mentioned data sharing for advertising but failed to distinguish between contextual and cross-context practices, and did not implement just-in-time notices or Global Privacy Control (GPC) recognition prior to regulatory scrutiny. The issue came to light during routine audits and consumer complaints about persistent targeted ads unrelated to DoorDash services.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the CPPA settled with DoorDash for a $375,000 civil penalty. As part of the settlement, DoorDash was required to revise its privacy policy, integrate GPC opt-out signals, cease unauthorized sharing for cross-context advertising, and conduct regular compliance audits.

example_name: Flo Health FTC Settlement on Health Data Sharing
example_background: Flo, a menstrual cycle tracking app, collected highly sensitive reproductive health data from users, including California residents. The app shared this raw health data with third parties such as Google Analytics, Facebook, and Amplitude for analytics and advertising optimization, allowing cross-context behavioral profiling. Flo represented data as de-identified but FTC experts demonstrated re-identification risks. Users were not informed of the full extent of sharing nor provided opt-out rights for such behavioral advertising purposes. The practices persisted despite privacy policy updates, affecting data from millions of users tracked across devices and services.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the FTC settled with Flo, prohibiting future sharing of sensitive health data for advertising without affirmative express consent. Flo was required to obtain independent third-party certification of its privacy program for 20 years.

During the California Attorney General's 2023-2024 CCPA enforcement sweep, CM.com was identified for failing to process deletion requests and continuing data sharing for advertising despite consumer signals. The platform's services enabled real-time bidding in ad exchanges using California user data.
process_and_outcome: CM.com entered a settlement paying $150,000 and agreed to a comprehensive CCPA compliance overhaul, including automated GPC implementation, deletion request processing within 45 days, and public reporting of compliance metrics.

It bypassed standard consent flows by leveraging proximity detection without unambiguous user affirmation for data sharing.
process_and_outcome: The California AG investigated under CCPA; Burger King pulled the app in 2019 and revised privacy practices. No formal fine was issued, but it led to industry-wide scrutiny on consent in promotional tech.

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Service Provider Violations
example_background: CMT Digital, doing business as Consumer Market Technologies, operated as a marketing technology company providing services to other businesses, including data processing for customer outreach and analytics. It collected and maintained consumer personal information on behalf of its clients, such as contact details and behavioral data, to fulfill orders and provide analytic services like lead generation and campaign optimization. CMT Digital acted as a service provider under CCPA but failed to adhere to contractual obligations that required it to process data only for the specified business purpose and not to sell or share the data independently. Instead, it retained consumer data after client relationships ended and used it for its own purposes, including selling access to data brokers.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) issued a notice of violation in 2024, marking one of the first enforcement actions under the CPPA's expanded authority. CMT Digital faced potential fines up to $7,500 per intentional violation and was required to cease unauthorized data processing and delete retained data.

example_name: Google Consent Decree with California AG
example_background: Google LLC provided advertising and analytics services to numerous businesses through tools like Google Analytics and Google Ads. These services involved processing customer data on behalf of businesses, including website visitor information, transaction details, and user identifiers, to generate reports, optimize ad targeting, and verify customer interactions. During 2019-2021, Google collected geolocation data via Android devices and shared it with third parties for analytics without fully accounting for CCPA service provider exceptions in disclosures. Additionally, its practices around IP addresses and device identifiers raised questions about proper notice for data processing activities performed for business clients.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General secured a $93 million settlement from Google. The company agreed to update privacy controls, limit geolocation data collection, and provide clearer disclosures about data sharing with service providers in its business services agreements.

Contracts include certifications of compliance with CCPA service provider rules.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's DPA has been recognized in industry audits and client compliance reviews as aligning with CCPA requirements, enabling businesses to use its services without triggering sale disclosures. No enforcement actions have been taken against Microsoft for these practices.

process_and_outcome: While primarily a COPPA action, it intersected CCPA; Google settled with the FTC for $170 million in 2019 and faced ongoing CCPA scrutiny, leading to policy changes like enhanced age-gating and data minimization.

process_and_outcome: Salesforce's model was validated through customer audits and regulatory reviews, positioning it as a compliant processor. The company avoided penalties and enhanced its offerings with built-in CCPA tools for customers.

example_name: FTC Action Against GoodRx for Data Sharing Practices
example_background: GoodRx, a digital healthcare platform, received personal information from pharmaceutical manufacturers under service contracts for prescription discount services. The company combined this data with personal information collected from consumer interactions on its apps and websites, as well as data obtained from advertising partners. This combined information was shared with third-party advertisers for targeted marketing without adequate notice to consumers or restrictions on use, occurring from 2017 to 2023. The practices involved tracking consumer health queries and prescription histories across multiple sources to build comprehensive profiles.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated following consumer complaints and media reports, issuing a complaint and consent order in February 2023. GoodRx was prohibited from sharing health data for advertising, required to delete previously shared data, and mandated to implement a comprehensive privacy program with biennial assessments.

example_name: New York DFS Fine on Equifax Data Broker Practices

The company combined this contractually received data with personal information sourced from public records, other brokers, and its own consumer credit monitoring services. The merged datasets were sold to third parties for marketing and risk assessment without consumer opt-out mechanisms or limitations to authorized business purposes, spanning activities from 2018 to 2021.
process_and_outcome: The New York Department of Financial Services (DFS) investigated under cybersecurity regulations, imposing a $500,000 fine in 2022. Equifax was ordered to cease unauthorized data combinations, enhance consumer notice procedures, and submit compliance reports.

process_and_outcome: Following the CPPA's first enforcement sweep in 2022, Sephora received a citation and was required to revise vendor contracts, limit data combinations to service delivery, and pay investigative costs. No monetary penalty was assessed due to remedial actions taken.

example_name: California AG Enforcement Sweep Against Instacart
example_background: Instacart, a grocery delivery service, shared personal information of consumers with contractors and service providers without adequate written contracts containing the required provisions under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). The company processed orders and shopper assignments, making consumer data such as names, addresses, and purchase histories available to third-party contractors for business purposes like delivery and customer support. The contracts in place did not include specific language obligating contractors to protect personal information solely for the purposes specified in the agreements or prohibiting further use or disclosure without authorization. This occurred during the initial implementation phase of CCPA in 2020, when many companies were adjusting to new data sharing requirements. Instacart's operations involved extensive sharing of location data and order details with gig workers and analytics firms, raising questions about the sufficiency of their vendor agreements.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General included Instacart in a major enforcement sweep announced in 2023, resulting in Instacart agreeing to a $1.2 million settlement payment and committing to update all contractor agreements to comply with CCPA requirements, including audit rights and data protection obligations. The settlement also required enhanced employee training and public disclosure of privacy practices.

example_name: California AG Action Against GSN Games
example_background: GSN Games, operator of online casino-style games, collected and shared personal information of California users including geolocation data, device information, and identifiers through advertising networks without providing adequate notice or opt-out rights under the CCPA. The company's mobile apps and website facilitated data transfers to dozens of third parties for real-time bidding in ad auctions, which constituted a sale of personal information, but privacy notices failed to list these recipients or enable consumers to limit such sharing during the covered period.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated based on CCPA enforcement priorities announced in 2021. In September 2022, GSN agreed to a $95,000 settlement and committed to conducting annual CCPA compliance audits, implementing automated scans for tracking technologies, maintaining a vendor management program with contractual assurances, and performing regular technical testing to verify opt-out functionalities.

process_and_outcome: Investigated by the California Attorney General, Burger King settled in December 2022 for $8.5 million. Requirements included enhanced location data disclosures, automated opt-out tools, annual privacy program assessments, and manual reviews of marketing technologies.

example_background: Flo Health, a menstrual cycle tracking app, shared sensitive health data of users, including California residents, with third-party analytics and advertising firms like Google, Facebook, and Amplitude. The data included precise cycle dates, symptoms, and fertility predictions, which was processed by these vendors for app functionality, crash reporting, and performance optimization. Flo's privacy policy mentioned third-party sharing but did not specify the nature of the data or obtain user consent for sensitive health information sharing. Users were not informed that subcontractors further processed this data, leading to potential de-anonymization risks when combined with other datasets.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated and in 2021 reached a settlement requiring Flo to prohibit sharing sensitive health data with third parties without affirmative consent, implement a comprehensive privacy program, undergo independent audits, and notify affected users. Flo also agreed not to sell health data under CCPA definitions.
source_rule: (2) If a contractor engages any other person to assist it in processing personal information for a business purpose on behalf of the business, or if any other person engaged by the contractor engages another person to assist in processing personal information for that business purpose, it shall notify the business of that engagement, and the engagement shall be pursuant to a written contract binding the other person to observe all the requirements set forth in paragraph (1).

example_background: CMT Blues, a ticket distribution services provider, engaged in selling or sharing personal information of California consumers collected from event attendees, including names, contact details, and purchase histories. The company used multiple third-party vendors for data processing, including analytics firms and marketing services, without notifying consumers about these specific engagements or providing opt-out options as required. Data was processed through a chain of subcontractors for purposes like lead generation and targeted promotions, with contracts not ensuring all parties observed CCPA restrictions on sales.
process_and_outcome: In February 2023, the California Attorney General announced a $1.22 million settlement with CMT Blues. The company agreed to cease selling personal information, implement a privacy program, limit third-party disclosures, and provide clear consumer notices and opt-out mechanisms.

example_background: Google, as a major technology provider, processes vast amounts of personal data through its advertising and analytics services, often on behalf of businesses. In response to CCPA requirements, Google established detailed data processing agreements with its clients, ensuring that subcontractors like DoubleClick and Google Analytics observe restrictions on selling or sharing data. Google provides businesses with tools to manage consumer opt-outs globally and notifies clients when further subcontracting occurs. This includes annual privacy assessments and transparency reports detailing third-party engagements.
process_and_outcome: Google conducted CCPA compliance audits and published privacy reports demonstrating adherence. No enforcement actions were taken against Google for contractor provisions, and it assisted thousands of businesses in CCPA compliance through its vendor portal.

example_background: Salesforce, a cloud-based CRM platform, serves as a key contractor for businesses processing personal data under CCPA. The company implemented a standard Data Processing Addendum (DPA) that binds Salesforce and any subprocessors to CCPA obligations. Businesses are notified in advance of any new subcontractor engagements, with an option to object. The DPA requires subprocessors to provide the same level of protection, including restrictions on sales and consumer rights handling. Salesforce conducts regular audits and provides customers with subprocessor lists.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce's practices were reviewed in multiple CCPA audits by clients and regulators, resulting in no violations. The company published CCPA-specific guidance and tools, helping clients achieve compliance without penalties.

example_background: GDCVA, an advertising technology company, operated as a contractor processing personal data for media publishers, aggregating consumer behavior data from websites using tracking technologies. It engaged further subcontractors for data enhancement and sales without notifying the upstream businesses about these arrangements or ensuring binding contracts that restricted data processing to business purposes only. This led to data being used in ways not authorized by the original collectors.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, the California Attorney General secured an Assurance of Discontinuance from GDCVA, requiring cessation of unauthorized data sales, implementation of opt-out honoring, and transparency about subcontractor use. No fine was levied.

example_background: Chegg, an online education platform, operated a subscription service where users encountered multiple confirmation screens and prompts during cancellation attempts. These screens included offers for downgrades, pauses, or alternative plans, along with repeated assurances that support agents could assist. The design required navigation through several steps, including entering reasons for cancellation and confirming multiple times, which extended the process significantly. This approach was applied to a service with millions of subscribers, where annual cancellations reached substantial numbers, and the company earned considerable revenue from subscriptions. The interface elements were structured to encourage retention through layered interactions rather than straightforward exits.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission investigated Chegg's practices, leading to a settlement in 2024. Chegg agreed to pay $2.45 million in refunds and adjust its cancellation process to simplify user exits.

example_name: New York AG v. Apple iPhone Slowdown
example_background: Apple's iOS software updates included battery performance management features that reduced device speeds without explicit prior user notification or consent. The implementation involved undisclosed throttling of CPU performance on older iPhone models to prevent shutdowns from battery degradation. This affected millions of devices worldwide, coinciding with Apple's promotion of newer models. Users discovered the changes through third-party testing, leading to perceptions of planned obsolescence as the slowdowns encouraged upgrades.
process_and_outcome: A multi-state investigation by the New York Attorney General and others resulted in a 2023 settlement where Apple paid $25 per affected device owner in certain states and agreed to enhanced disclosures about performance management.

example_background: Amazon's checkout process on its UK website pre-selected subscription options like Amazon Prime trials and additional services during purchase flows. Users had to actively deselect these options buried within the interface, often amidst upsell prompts and limited visibility. This design was part of a broader e-commerce flow handling billions in transactions annually, where default selections influenced recurring revenue streams.
process_and_outcome: The UK Competition and Markets Authority reviewed Amazon's practices in 2022 as part of a market study, leading to voluntary commitments from Amazon to make opt-in choices explicit and prominent.

This affected a customer base billed monthly, where retention tactics prolonged user engagement during exit attempts.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with Vonage in 2024, requiring $100 million in refunds and mandated streamlined cancellation processes accessible online without human intervention.

example_name: In re California Department of Motor Vehicles
example_background: The California Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) collected personal information from millions of driver's license applicants, including names, addresses, partial Social Security numbers, and facial images, creating a database of over 39 million records. The DMV shared this data with entities outside California for facial recognition research and development. These recipients used the data to train and test facial recognition algorithms, which involved processes that could potentially link the data back to individuals. The DMV had entered into data sharing agreements but did not ensure that the data remained deidentified or that recipients refrained from reidentification attempts, leading to concerns over the handling of sensitive personal information in a manner consistent with privacy protections.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) investigated following public complaints and media reports. In December 2023, the CPPA approved a settlement where the DMV agreed to pay $1.2 million and implement specific data handling procedures, including restrictions on future sharing and requirements for deidentification protocols.

The sharing involved datasets that were purportedly anonymized but lacked enforceable commitments from recipients to maintain deidentification or abstain from reidentification efforts, leading to public scrutiny over the effectiveness of Google's data minimization practices.
process_and_outcome: Following a class-action lawsuit and regulatory inquiries, Google agreed in 2022 to a $392 million settlement with 40 state attorneys general. The company implemented enhanced deletion processes, user controls, and disclosures about data retention and sharing.

process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved HITRUST and FedRAMP certifications, with no CCPA-related enforcement actions. The practices have been highlighted in compliance case studies as effective for handling deidentified health data across jurisdictions.

process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated under CCPA enforcement authority. example_name: FTC Enforcement Action Against Flo Health
example_background: Flo Health, Inc., a women's health tracking app, collected sensitive menstrual cycle data from users. The company shared this data with third parties including Facebook, Google, and others through tracking tools and analytics services. The data included timestamps and details that could be linked to individual users despite claims of anonymization. Flo represented the data as anonymized or de-identified, but the FTC determined that it retained enough identifiable information, such as precise cycle start dates combined with other user metadata, that it could be re-identified or linked back to specific consumers. This occurred between 2016 and 2019, affecting millions of users who inputted personal health information into the app expecting privacy protections.
process_and_outcome: The FTC investigated following a complaint and public scrutiny. In 2021, Flo settled charges without admitting wrongdoing, agreeing to stop sharing health data with third parties, implement a comprehensive privacy program, undergo biennial audits for 20 years, and obtain consumer consent for future data sharing. No monetary penalty was imposed.

The platform's practices involved sharing data with numerous third parties without adequate consumer controls.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA issued the first-ever administrative citation under CPPA regulations, proposing a $100,000 penalty. Proceedings are ongoing as of late 2024, with DoorDash required to respond and potentially face hearing.

example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, equipped its devices with tracking software that collected viewing histories, demographics, and device data from over 11 million consumers without clear notice. From 2014 to 2017, Vizio aggregated this data and sold it to third parties as 'anonymized' audience measurement data. Investigations revealed the data included IP addresses, MAC addresses, and viewing patterns sufficient to re-identify households and individuals.
process_and_outcome: The FTC and California DOJ settled with Vizio in 2017. Vizio paid $2.2 million in penalties ($1.5M to FTC, $0.7M to CA), agreed to stop selling data without consent, provide opt-out notices, and delete collected data.

example_background: Microsoft de-identifies search query data from Bing for research and analytics by removing names, emails, IP addresses, and applying k-anonymity thresholds (ensuring no individual represents less than 1/20th of a dataset). Techniques include generalization of timestamps and locations, hashing, and perturbation. This processed data is used internally and shared in public datasets like the Bing Web Query Log, with documentation proving re-identification risks are minimized below reasonable thresholds.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's methods have withstood academic and regulatory scrutiny, with no violations found in FTC inquiries. Used as a model in privacy research papers and NIST guidelines.

Independent audits by Stanford researchers confirmed that processed datasets could not reasonably link to specific consumers, even with auxiliary data.
process_and_outcome: Google implemented Verified Controls for de-identified data sharing. FTC reviewed in 2022 privacy settlement and found compliance; no penalties related to de-identification.

example_name: Microsoft Privacy Dashboard and De-identification Practices
example_background: Microsoft developed a privacy dashboard and data governance program compliant with CCPA/CPRA, enabling California consumers to access, delete, or opt out of data sales/sharing. The company applies techniques like k-anonymity, differential privacy, and data masking to analytics datasets derived from user telemetry, search queries, and app usage. These measures process billions of data points daily while preventing re-identification to individuals or households, with regular third-party audits verifying pseudonymization effectiveness since 2019.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft passed multiple CCPA compliance audits without citations. In 2023 CPPA assessments, their de-identification processes were certified as meeting regulatory standards, avoiding any enforcement. The company publishes annual transparency reports on data practices.
source_rule: (1) Takes reasonable measures to ensure that the information cannot be associated with a consumer or household.

example_background: Ticketmaster, owned by Live Nation, shared consumer purchase data including names, emails, ticket details, and event preferences with affiliates and advertisers via tracking pixels and SDKs on its platform. Data from California ticket buyers was transmitted to entities like Google and Oracle without upfront opt-out notices, allowing profile building from 2019-2022. This affected millions of event attendees whose household interests were inferred from group purchases.
process_and_outcome: California AG investigated; Ticketmaster settled in 2023 for $5.75 million, required to overhaul privacy notices, add global opt-out buttons, audit vendors, and train staff. Injunctive terms prohibit future non-compliant sharing.
source_rule: (1) Takes reasonable measures to ensure that the information cannot be associated with a consumer or household.

example_name: ProctorU Data Breach and CCPA Audit Failure
example_background: ProctorU, an online exam proctoring service used by universities, stored facial scans, keystroke patterns, and webcam footage linked to student IDs for over 7 million sessions, including California students. In 2020, a breach exposed this biometric data due to inadequate anonymization in cloud storage, where metadata allowed re-association to individuals. Practices continued post-breach without full deletion of linkable records.
process_and_outcome: Multiple class actions and state AG probes; settled for $22.5 million in 2023 covering affected users. Agreed to enhanced encryption, data minimization, and CCPA-compliant deletion processes. California-specific penalties included in multi-state resolution.
source_rule: (1) Takes reasonable measures to ensure that the information cannot be associated with a consumer or household.

example_background: Google collects extensive location data through Android devices and services like Google Maps, generating datasets from millions of users including Californians. The company applies deidentification techniques such as k-anonymity, differential privacy, and temporal obfuscation to create aggregated mobility reports shared with researchers and public health officials. Google conducts internal reidentification attempts using machine learning models on these deidentified datasets to evaluate the robustness of anonymization against linkage with external data sources like public records or social media. These tests simulate real-world reidentification risks to ensure datasets cannot be feasibly linked to individuals, adjusting parameters based on outcomes to strengthen privacy protections.
process_and_outcome: Google's privacy team published audit results demonstrating high reidentification resistance, enabling continued use of deidentified data for public good initiatives like COVID-19 mobility reports. No enforcement actions were taken, and the practices were upheld in privacy reviews.
source_rule: information, except that the business may attempt to reidentify the information solely for the purpose of determining whether its deidentification processes satisfy the requirements of this subdivision.

example_background: Meta (formerly Facebook) maintains vast user data for advertising, including from California residents. The company deidentifies portions of this data by removing names, emails, and phone numbers, retaining behavioral signals like interests and interactions. Meta performs reidentification simulations on these datasets using proprietary algorithms to test if pseudonymous profiles can be matched back to real users via cross-platform tracking or public data enrichment. This process is integrated into their privacy engineering workflow to validate that deidentification meets CCPA standards, involving controlled experiments with subsets of data to measure reidentification success rates and refine hashing and aggregation methods.
process_and_outcome: Internal audits and third-party certifications confirmed compliance, allowing Meta to use deidentified data for research publications and ad measurement tools without regulatory penalties. The CPPA reviewed and accepted the methodology during rulemaking comment periods.

process_and_outcome: Following a class-action settlement and FTC inquiry, Zoom paid $85 million and committed to better deidentification protocols, including limiting reidentification to certified personnel and documenting risk assessments.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, was identified in the California AG's 2023-2024 CCPA enforcement sweep for lacking a prominent, consumer-friendly method for submitting data access, deletion, or opt-out requests. The company's privacy policy directed users to a general support ticket system without specific CCPA-designated channels like a toll-free number, dedicated email, or web portal. Consumers reported challenges in locating request procedures, often requiring interaction with customer support unrelated to privacy rights. This setup did not align with requirements for clear, accessible contact points approved under CCPA guidelines.
process_and_outcome: DoorDash received a notice of noncompliance and updated its website to include a dedicated CCPA request portal and email address within the required period, avoiding further penalties through curative action.
source_rule: (n) “Designated methods for submitting requests” means a mailing address, email address, internet web page, internet web portal, toll-free telephone number, or other applicable contact information, whereby consumers may submit a request or direction under this title, and any new, consumer-friendly means of contacting a business, as approved by the Attorney General pursuant to Section 1798.185.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, faced scrutiny for its handling of CCPA consumer requests prior to 2022. The company did not initially provide straightforward designated methods such as a specific email or web form for CCPA requests, instead relying on general privacy inquiries through their customer service channels. This led to delays and confusion for consumers seeking to access or delete personal information collected via their online and in-store sales tracking. The issue came to light during investigations into beauty industry practices, highlighting the need for explicit CCPA-compliant contact points.
process_and_outcome: Sephora settled with the California AG for $1.2 million, agreeing to implement a dedicated online request portal, train staff on CCPA compliance, and enhance disclosures about data practices over three years.

source_rule: (n) “Designated methods for submitting requests” means a mailing address, email address, internet web page, internet web portal, toll-free telephone number, or other applicable contact information, whereby consumers may submit a request or direction under this title, and any new, consumer-friendly means of contacting a business, as approved by the Attorney General pursuant to Section 1798.185.

example_background: WW International was flagged in the California AG's CCPA sweep for not providing accessible designated methods for consumer requests. Their website lacked a specific CCPA portal or contact info, directing users instead to a generic data subject access request form buried in privacy policy footers without mention of CCPA rights or approved submission channels like email or phone.
process_and_outcome: WW received a cure notice and subsequently launched a dedicated request page with email and form options, resolving the issue without fines.
source_rule: (n) “Designated methods for submitting requests” means a mailing address, email address, internet web page, internet web portal, toll-free telephone number, or other applicable contact information, whereby consumers may submit a request or direction under this title, and any new, consumer-friendly means of contacting a business, as approved by the Attorney General pursuant to Section 1798.185.

example_background: Apple provides designated CCPA request methods through privacy.apple.com, including an online request form for data access and deletion, a dedicated email (privacy@apple.com), and postal address instructions. Users can also manage requests via device settings for App Tracking Transparency and personalized ads. The company publishes detailed CCPA notices and response procedures, ensuring accessibility across web, app, and phone support.
process_and_outcome: Apple's practices have been upheld in regulatory reviews, with no enforcement actions, and they report high request volumes handled efficiently in transparency reports.

example_background: QVC, a home shopping network, was part of the AG's 2024 sweep for failing to offer clear designated methods. Their privacy policy did not specify CCPA request channels, lacking a web portal, email, or phone number tailored for data rights exercises, forcing consumers to use general contact forms.
process_and_outcome: QVC cured by adding a CCPA-specific page with submission options, avoiding penalties.

example_background: Salesforce Trust maintains a CCPA/CPRA hub at trust.salesforce.com with interactive request tools, email (privacyoffice@salesforce.com), toll-free number (1-800-664-9073), and mail options. It supports verified requests with identity verification guides and appeal processes, updated regularly per CPPA guidance.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; praised in industry benchmarks for accessibility.
