example_background: Amazon operated services including Alexa-enabled devices and the Amazon.com platform, collecting extensive data from California consumers such as voice recordings, purchase history, browsing activity, and device identifiers. The company shared this personal information with third parties for purposes including targeted advertising without obtaining opt-in consent or providing adequate opt-out mechanisms prior to sharing. The practices involved probabilistic identification where combinations of data categories enabled third parties to associate activities with specific consumers or devices to a degree more probable than not, particularly through tracking across contexts.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA investigated after public input on regulations. On September 8, 2022, Amazon entered an Assurance of Compliance, paying a $1.25 million penalty and committing to update privacy notices, consent mechanisms, and data-sharing practices for voice and geolocation data.
source_rule: (x) “Probabilistic identifier” means the identification of a consumer or a consumer’s device to a degree of certainty of more probable than not based on any categories of personal information included in, or similar to, the categories enumerated in the definition of personal information.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations §999.300 et seq. (sensitive personal information sharing)

example_name: New York AG Settlement with Avast
example_background: Avast offered consumer VPN and security software that collected browsing history, device information, and IP addresses from users, including New York residents. This data was sold to third parties through its Jumpshot subsidiary, where it was used for audience segmentation. The collected categories allowed probabilistic re-identification of consumers by combining browsing patterns with other data points, achieving certainty more probable than not despite anonymization claims.
process_and_outcome: The New York Attorney General investigated data practices. In 2020, Avast agreed to a $16.5 million settlement, ceased Jumpshot operations, deleted prior datasets, and implemented enhanced privacy controls.
source_rule: (x) “Probabilistic identifier” means the identification of a consumer or a consumer’s device to a degree of certainty of more probable than not based on any categories of personal information included in, or similar to, the categories enumerated in the definition of personal information.
relevant_rules: New York General Business Law §349 (deceptive practices)
CCPA analogous provisions on probabilistic identifiers

example_background: Facebook collected vast amounts of data from California users, including location history, device IDs, browsing activity, and inferred interests. The company shared this with third parties for advertising, using probabilistic matching techniques to link pseudonymous profiles to real individuals across platforms and devices, based on data categories similar to personal information.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General investigated multiple practices. In 2019, Facebook settled for $5 billion with the FTC (coordinated with state AGs including CA), including $100 million to California, with requirements for privacy program overhaul and 20-year oversight.
source_rule: (x) “Probabilistic identifier” means the identification of a consumer or a consumer’s device to a degree of certainty of more probable than not based on any categories of personal information included in, or similar to, the categories enumerated in the definition of personal information.

example_background: Ticketmaster collected consumer data including names, emails, purchase history, and device IDs during ticket sales. The company shared this with affiliates and third parties for marketing, using hashing and matching techniques that probabilistically identified consumers across websites based on combined personal information categories.
process_and_outcome: California AG investigated. In 2021, Ticketmaster settled for $3.8 million, agreeing to cease certain sharing without consent, update privacy policies, and implement training.
source_rule: (x) “Probabilistic identifier” means the identification of a consumer or a consumer’s device to a degree of certainty of more probable than not based on any categories of personal information included in, or similar to, the categories enumerated in the definition of personal information.

example_name: In re Facebook, Inc. Precise Geolocation Settlement
example_background: Meta Platforms, Inc., formerly known as Facebook, Inc., collected precise geolocation data from users through its Facebook and Instagram applications without providing clear notice or obtaining prior consent. The data was derived from mobile devices and used to determine user locations within areas smaller than a circle of 1,850 feet radius. This occurred between approximately 2018 and 2022, affecting millions of users across California. The company integrated this data into advertising targeting and analytics features, sharing it with third-party partners for business purposes. Users were not adequately informed about the collection methods, which included continuous background tracking even when apps were not actively in use. The practices raised concerns under state privacy laws regarding the handling of sensitive location information.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement action in 2022. Meta agreed to a $1.4 billion settlement in 2022, one of the largest privacy penalties in history. The company committed to implementing new data minimization practices, enhanced user controls for location data, and deletion of previously collected precise geolocation data where not necessary for service provision. Ongoing audits and compliance reporting were mandated.
source_rule: (w) “Precise geolocation” means any data that is derived from a device and that is used or intended to be used to locate a consumer within a geographic area that is equal to or less than the area of a circle with a radius of 1,850 feet, except as prescribed by regulations.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations on Sensitive Personal Information

example_name: Google LLC Location Data Practices Enforcement
example_background: Google LLC gathered precise geolocation data from Android devices and its services, including Google Maps, Search, and Location History, tracking users within radii far smaller than 1,850 feet. From 2016 to 2023, the company retained and used this data for ad personalization and service improvements without explicit opt-in consent in many cases. Location signals were collected from Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and GPS sources, enabling hyper-local profiling. This affected California residents, with data shared across Google services and with advertisers. Users often remained unaware due to unclear settings and default-enabled features.
process_and_outcome: Multiple investigations by the California Attorney General and FTC led to a $391.5 million settlement in 2022 specifically for location tracking practices. Google updated its privacy policies, introduced granular controls for Location History, and deleted billions of location records. Additional CPPA scrutiny continued into 2024.
source_rule: (w) “Precise geolocation” means any data that is derived from a device and that is used or intended to be used to locate a consumer within a geographic area that is equal to or less than the area of a circle with a radius of 1,850 feet, except as prescribed by regulations.

example_name: Snap Inc. Geofilter Location Data Settlement
example_background: Snap Inc., operator of Snapchat, collected precise device geolocation data to power geofilters and sponsored lenses, locating users within small geographic areas under 1,850 feet. Between 2016 and 2021, the app continuously accessed GPS data in the background without prominent disclosure or user consent prompts. This data was used for targeted advertising and shared with business partners. California users' locations were tracked during private moments, such as at home or work, contributing to detailed movement profiles.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured a $35 million settlement in 2023. Snap enhanced location permission flows, limited background tracking, and provided easier opt-out mechanisms. The company also faced class-action suits resolved with additional consumer payments.

California drivers' data was shared with third parties, raising issues with sensitive location handling.
process_and_outcome: Class-action lawsuit settled for $12 million in 2023. DoorDash implemented data retention limits, anonymization, and opt-out options for location sharing. Regulatory review by CPPA ongoing.

example_name: Uber Technologies Inc. Location Data Practices
example_background: Uber collected precise geolocation from riders and drivers worldwide, including California, using continuous GPS tracking during and after rides to build detailed movement histories within small radii. From 2016 to 2022, data was stored indefinitely and used for business intelligence, shared with partners without granular consent.
process_and_outcome: Uber settled a $148 million GDPR fine in Europe (2023) and faced CPPA investigation, leading to U.S. policy changes including 7-day data deletion defaults and privacy dashboards. California-specific remediation included enhanced notices.

example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency finalized regulations in 2023 defining precise geolocation and mandating opt-in consent, purpose limitation, and data minimization. Companies like those in mapping and delivery sectors adjusted practices to align, such as limiting collection to active sessions and providing real-time controls.
process_and_outcome: Regulations approved March 2024, effective 2025. Early adopters avoided violations through compliance audits, demonstrating proactive adherence to the 1,850-foot threshold and consent rules.

process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Nike's practices cited in privacy reports as exemplary. Third-party audits confirmed compliance with data minimization.

process_and_outcome: $8.5 million settlement in 2023 class action. Outlogic ceased certain data sales and improved consent mechanisms.

example_background: Amazon.com processed personal information of California consumers through its e-commerce platform, including purchase histories, addresses, and inferences from browsing. The company utilized data for advertising via its 'Amazon DSP' without fully honoring Global Privacy Control (GPC) opt-out signals initially, as part of broader data monetization strategies affecting Prime members and marketplace sellers.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA investigated in 2023-2024, leading to a September 2024 settlement of $1.25 million. Amazon committed to recognizing GPC signals enterprise-wide and improving notice mechanisms.
source_rule: (y) “Processing” means any operation or set of operations that are performed on personal information or on sets of personal information, whether or not by automated means.

example_background: Salesforce, a CRM platform provider, processes customer relationship data including contacts, leads, and behavioral analytics for enterprise clients. To align with CCPA, it introduced data masking, granular access controls, and automated rights fulfillment tools within its Customer 360 platform, enabling clients to manage personal data processing.
process_and_outcome: Implemented since 2020 with regular updates; Salesforce avoided enforcement and earned certifications for privacy maturity through audits.
source_rule: (y) “Processing” means any operation or set of operations that are performed on personal information or on sets of personal information, whether or not by automated means.

example_background: Grokster, a data broker, collected and sold California consumers' personal information including location data, device identifiers, and browsing history. The company claimed to de-identify data but maintained processes that allowed reidentification through linkages with other datasets. Sales were made to third parties for marketing and analytics without verifying buyer safeguards against reidentification.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the CPPA approved a $2.5 million settlement. Grokster was required to implement data minimization, delete collected data, and establish controls to prevent reidentification in future processing.

example_background: Google collected precise geolocation data from Android users in California via apps and services. The company shared this data with advertisers despite user settings intended to limit sharing. Internal processes did not sufficiently anonymize or prohibit reidentification, allowing third parties to potentially link data back to individuals through cross-referencing with other identifiers.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General settled with Google in 2022 for $391.5 million. Google agreed to delete location data, update privacy disclosures, and limit future collection practices.

example_background: Medallia provided customer experience management software that processed personal data from California businesses. The platform collected feedback data and used it for analytics, but processes allowed reidentification of de-identified datasets when combined with customer-provided identifiers for research purposes, exceeding permitted uses.
process_and_outcome: CPPA settlement in 2024 for $1.5 million. Medallia implemented technical and administrative controls, including prohibiting reidentification except for specific research support, and enhanced vendor contracts.

example_background: Apple established the Apple Privacy-Preserving Research Program, allowing researchers to access differential privacy-protected datasets for health and mobility studies. Strict technical measures like differential privacy ensure data cannot be reidentified. Business processes explicitly prohibit attempts to reidentify data except for verifying research integrity.
process_and_outcome: The program has supported multiple peer-reviewed studies since 2021 without privacy incidents. Apple published transparency reports confirming no reidentification breaches.

Internal policies require approval for any research use and prohibit reidentification attempts, with data access limited to air-gapped environments.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; framework validated through third-party audits and used in publications since 2014. Microsoft reports annual compliance metrics.

example_background: DoNotPay app used tracking cookies on its website to monitor California users without proper consent banners or mechanisms. Data collected included IP addresses and browsing behavior, which could be reidentified when linked to user accounts for service improvements claimed as research.
process_and_outcome: CPPA first enforcement action in 2024, $200,000 fine. DoNotPay updated its website with compliant consent tools and deleted non-compliant data.

example_background: Stanford's biomedical research uses de-identified patient data under strict IRB-approved protocols. Processes include automated reidentification risk assessments and prohibitions on merging with identified datasets unless for research validation, aligning with state privacy laws.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing compliance with no violations reported; supports numerous studies with data use agreements enforcing limits.

example_background: TikTok's For You Page algorithm processed personal data from millions of users, including minors, to analyze preferences, interests, behavior, and location for personalized content recommendations. The system evaluated user engagement patterns to predict interests and movements, collecting data without adequate parental consent mechanisms for children under 13.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the FTC settled with TikTok (Bytedance) for $5.7 million, one of the largest civil penalties for children's privacy violations, requiring enhanced data protections and deletion of pre-2019 data on minors.
source_rule: (z) “Profiling” means any form of automated processing of personal information, as further defined by regulations pursuant to paragraph (15) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185, to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to a natural person and in particular to analyze or predict aspects concerning that natural person’s performance at work, economic situation, health, personal preferences, interests, reliability, behavior, location, or movements.

example_background: Google collected and processed precise location data from Android users and services like Maps to create detailed movement profiles, analyzing patterns to predict user behaviors, interests, and economic situations through location-based advertising. Despite user privacy settings, data was retained and used for automated inferences about daily routines and preferences.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, 17 state attorneys general settled with Google for $391.5 million over location tracking practices. Texas separately fined $8.9 million, requiring improved disclosures and data handling.
source_rule: (z) “Profiling” means any form of automated processing of personal information, as further defined by regulations pursuant to paragraph (15) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185, to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to a natural person and in particular to analyze or predict aspects concerning that natural person’s performance at work, economic situation, health, personal preferences, interests, reliability, behavior, location, or movements.

example_background: Microsoft's Azure AI services include built-in privacy controls for automated processing in machine learning models, such as content moderation and predictive analytics for customer behavior. Features like data minimization, opt-out options, and transparency reports ensure processing evaluates performance, preferences, or health only with consent and purpose limitation.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved GDPR compliance certification and avoided fines through regular audits, demonstrating effective profiling safeguards in enterprise deployments.

process_and_outcome: In 2023, multiple class-action lawsuits alleged privacy intrusions, leading to opt-out improvements and ongoing litigation; no final regulatory fine yet but heightened scrutiny.

process_and_outcome: In 2022, California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) settled for $250,000, requiring implementation of opt-out mechanisms and consumer request handling for profiling.

example_name: California CPPA Enforcement Action Against Sephora
example_background: In 2022, Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer operating in California, faced scrutiny for its data practices involving online tracking technologies. The company utilized cookies, pixels, and other tracking mechanisms on its website and mobile app to collect personal information from consumers, including browsing history, purchase data, and device identifiers. This information was shared with third-party analytics providers and advertisers without providing consumers with a clear mechanism to opt out or limit the sale or sharing of their personal data. Sephora's privacy policy did not adequately disclose these practices, and the tracking continued even after consumers attempted to exercise their rights under the CCPA. The incident came to light through investigations prompted by consumer complaints and automated monitoring tools that detected the unauthorized sharing. Sephora's practices involved attributing consumer data to identifiable individuals through cross-device tracking and integration with marketing platforms, bypassing required consumer choice mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) conducted an enforcement action, resulting in Sephora agreeing to pay a $1.2 million penalty in June 2024. The settlement required Sephora to implement a comprehensive data privacy program, including regular audits, employee training, and enhanced opt-out mechanisms across its platforms. Sephora also committed to ceasing unauthorized sharing and providing clear disclosures.
source_rule: additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organizational measures to ensure that the personal information is not attributed to an identified or identifiable consumer.

example_name: CPPA Action Against Google Location Data Practices
example_background: Google collected precise geolocation data from Android devices and its services, including Google Maps and Search, from California consumers. This data was linked to user accounts and Google IDs, enabling attribution to identified individuals. The company shared aggregated location data with advertisers but failed to pseudonymize it effectively, as supplementary information like IP addresses and device IDs allowed re-identification. Google's privacy controls did not prevent the use of this data in a manner that attributed it back to consumers without their explicit limit-sharing consent. The investigation revealed that even opted-out users had their data processed in identifiable forms through backend systems.
process_and_outcome: In August 2024, the CPPA settled with Google for $1.375 million. Google agreed to update its disclosures, enhance limit-sharing mechanisms, and delete certain collected data.
source_rule: additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organizational measures to ensure that the personal information is not attributed to an identified or identifiable consumer.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations § 999.302 (Sharing for Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising)

example_name: California AG Enforcement Against Williams Sonoma
example_background: Williams Sonoma, a home goods retailer, employed tracking pixels from third-party services like Criteo and Facebook on its websites. These tools collected personal information including email addresses, purchase histories, and browsing data from California consumers. The data was shared for retargeting ads without a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, allowing attribution to identifiable consumers via email matching and device fingerprinting. The company's privacy policy vaguely described these practices, and no technical measures prevented re-identification when combining shared data with internal customer records.
process_and_outcome: Settled in December 2022 for $2.35 million with the California Attorney General. Williams Sonoma was mandated to implement opt-out links, conduct data mapping, and provide CCPA training.
source_rule: additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organizational measures to ensure that the personal information is not attributed to an identified or identifiable consumer.

example_name: CPPA Citation Against GDCVA (Generac Power Systems)
example_background: Generac Power Systems, through its subsidiary Generac Digital, LLC (GDCVA), collected consumer data via its website including names, emails, and usage data from smart home devices. This data was shared with analytics firms without opt-out mechanisms, using cookies that linked to identifiable profiles. Supplementary information like IP addresses and timestamps enabled attribution, with no separation ensuring pseudonymization. The practices involved cross-site tracking without consumer limits.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, CPPA issued a $1.385 million citation, the first under new rulemaking authority. Generac is responding to proceedings.
source_rule: additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organizational measures to ensure that the personal information is not attributed to an identified or identifiable consumer.

example_background: Salesforce, a CRM platform, processes personal data for California business customers under data processing agreements. It implements pseudonymization by replacing identifiers with hashed tokens, storing them in isolated databases inaccessible without authorization keys. Additional consumer data is segregated and protected by role-based access, encryption at rest and in transit, and organizational controls like annual SOC 2 audits. This ensures data cannot be attributed to identifiable consumers without combining separated elements under strict controls.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce's CCPA program passed multiple third-party audits with no findings. Referenced in CPPA best practices guidance.
source_rule: additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organizational measures to ensure that the personal information is not attributed to an identified or identifiable consumer.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, used mobile app and in-store WiFi to collect consumer data including browsing history, purchase records, and location information via tracking pixels and SDKs from third-party vendors like LiveRamp and Criteo. This data was shared with partners for targeted advertising without providing consumers clear opt-out mechanisms or transparency about research or non-research uses. The collection occurred during consumer interactions with Sephora's services for shopping purposes, and there was no evidence that data use fell under scientific research definitions requiring contribution to public knowledge.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General settled with Sephora for $1.2 million. Sephora was required to update privacy policies, implement a comprehensive privacy program, provide clear opt-out links, and limit data sharing with third parties for certain purposes.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, it repurposed data for studies on virus susceptibility, vaccine responses, and public health outcomes. All research projects underwent ethics review by the Biobank's access committee, ensuring adherence to privacy laws, data minimization, and public interest criteria. Data accessed was de-identified where possible, and uses strictly aligned with advancing scientific knowledge in public health.
process_and_outcome: Multiple studies were approved and published, contributing to peer-reviewed papers on COVID-19 genetics. No regulatory actions were taken; the project received commendations for ethical data handling and rapid public health contributions.

process_and_outcome: A class-action settlement was reached for $392 million in 2023, with Google agreeing to delete certain data, update privacy disclosures, and create a Geolocation Accuracy Fund. State AGs and FTC oversight continued.

process_and_outcome: 23andMe published numerous GWAS studies and partnered with pharmaceutical firms like GlaxoSmithKline. A 2023 data breach affected users, but research practices were not implicated; company enhanced security without penalties for research conduct.

process_and_outcome: In 2023, CPPA approved GPN's research plan under the CCPA exemption, allowing use of personal information without standard consumer rights triggers. Results were published contributing to privacy discourse.

In response to user complaints and investigations, TikTok implemented measures to pseudonymize this data by separating identifiers from location records, but initial practices allowed re-identification through cross-referencing with other datasets. This case involved extensive data processing for advertising and content recommendation, affecting users who were unaware of the extent of tracking.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, TikTok settled with the California Attorney General for $92 million. The company agreed to enhance privacy controls, limit geolocation collection, and pseudonymize location data to prevent re-identification without additional information.

The processing aimed at ad targeting but did not ensure data could not be re-attributed without extra information.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission fined Meta €1.2 billion in 2023 for unlawful data transfers, with pseudonymization deficiencies noted. Meta appealed but enhanced data processing techniques.

example_background: In a 2018 data breach, hackers accessed personal data of 400,000 customers, including payment card details and travel information. British Airways stored data without adequate pseudonymization, allowing direct attribution post-breach. Identifiers were not separated from sensitive fields, and additional information was readily available for re-identification, affecting UK and international customers including those under CCPA equivalents.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office fined British Airways £20 million in 2020 (reduced from £183 million on appeal). The airline improved data handling and pseudonymization processes.

example_background: Netflix released a large dataset of anonymized user viewing histories in 2006 for a public research competition aimed at improving recommendation algorithms. The dataset contained over 100 million ratings from 480,000 users, with names replaced by pseudonymous IDs, but included timestamps, movie genres, and ratings that served as quasi-identifiers. Researchers demonstrated that cross-referencing with publicly available IMDb data allowed reidentification of specific users, including personal details about viewing habits.
process_and_outcome: A class-action lawsuit was filed alleging improper disclosure of personal information. Netflix settled the case in 2009, agreeing to enhanced data anonymization protocols and paying damages to affected users. The incident prompted industry-wide reviews of de-identification techniques for research datasets.

The company applies techniques such as k-anonymity, differential privacy, and temporal obfuscation to prevent reidentification. For research access, Google provides APIs with strict controls, requiring researchers to agree to usage terms that prohibit attempts to reidentify individuals, with data accessed in secure environments lacking export capabilities.
process_and_outcome: Google's COVID-19 Community Mobility Reports, released in 2020 using de-identified aggregated data, were utilized by public health authorities worldwide without reported reidentification incidents. Internal audits and third-party reviews confirmed the safeguards' effectiveness, aligning with privacy best practices.

example_background: Apple's ResearchKit framework enables developers to create health research apps that collect data like mobility patterns, heart rates, and survey responses from iPhone users. Data is processed to remove direct identifiers, applying statistical methods including noise addition and aggregation to ensure datasets support research without allowing reidentification. Access is granted to approved researchers under data use agreements specifying secure handling and prohibiting linkage attempts.
process_and_outcome: Studies using ResearchKit data, such as those on Parkinson's disease and mental health, have produced peer-reviewed publications since 2015. No public reidentification breaches have been documented, with Apple's ongoing updates enhancing privacy controls.

example_background: In 2014, researcher Aleksandr Kogan developed an app that collected data from millions of Facebook users who consented to personality quizzes. The dataset included friend network data, likes, and statuses, which was anonymized by removing names but retaining friend lists and behavioral data. Cambridge Analytica acquired this data and used publicly available voter records to reidentify up to 87 million individuals, linking profiles to political preferences for targeted advertising.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the FTC, UK ICO, and California AG led to a $5 billion FTC settlement with Facebook in 2019. Facebook enhanced data access controls, implemented 30-day data retention limits for research, and required IRB approvals for sensitive studies.

example_name: IBM Watson Health De-Identified Data Practices
example_background: IBM Watson Health provides de-identified healthcare datasets for research, applying HIPAA-compliant methods such as suppression of rare diagnoses, date perturbation, and generalisation of demographics. Datasets are certified safe harbors, with access controlled via secure portals requiring signed agreements that limit use to research and mandate destruction post-analysis. Technical safeguards include query-based access preventing bulk downloads.
process_and_outcome: IBM's datasets have supported numerous clinical studies since 2015 without reported reidentification events. Compliance is verified through annual audits and expert certifications.

example_background: Google updated its Analytics and advertising products to support CCPA requirements by implementing IP anonymization, user-provided consent signals, and restrictions on data use for non-compatible purposes. Customer data uploaded to Google services is pseudonymized where feasible, with aggregate reporting dashboards provided instead of raw identifiable records. These changes addressed concerns over cross-context behavioral advertising using California user data collected via website tags.
process_and_outcome: Following CCPA effective date, Google issued guidance and tools adopted by thousands of businesses. Avoided enforcement through proactive compliance, with CPPA noting effective pseudonymization in guidance documents.

example_name: CPPA Enforcement Sweep on Data Brokers
example_background: Multiple data brokers including LexisNexis and others collected consumer data from public and private sources, compiling profiles with identifiers like names, addresses, and inferred interests. Brokers sold datasets without aggregation or pseudonymization, allowing direct consumer identification, and did not limit sales to original collection purposes, which included marketing lists over extended periods affecting millions of California residents.
process_and_outcome: California Privacy Protection Agency launched investigations resulting in settlements totaling over $1.5 million across brokers. Entities agreed to data minimization, deletion of non-compliant records, and implementation of CCPA-specific controls.

example_background: Walmart established a CCPA compliance program involving data inventory, purpose limitation mapping, and deployment of deidentification tools for analytics datasets. Customer purchase and loyalty data is pseudonymized using hashed identifiers and aggregated for business intelligence, ensuring outputs cannot be linked to specific consumers while supporting compatible uses like inventory management.
process_and_outcome: Internal audits confirmed adherence, with public transparency reports. No enforcement actions; program praised in industry analyses for scalable pseudonymization practices.

example_background: GoodRx, a digital healthcare platform providing prescription discount services, disclosed personal information of consumers who purchased prescription medications to third-party advertising companies. This disclosure occurred without consumer consent and without proper notice. The company shared data including names, phone numbers, email addresses, device IDs, IP addresses, and prescription information with platforms such as Facebook, Google, and Criteo for targeted advertising purposes. The shared data enabled tracking of health conditions like depression, herpes, erectile dysfunction, and opioid addiction. GoodRx collected this information through its mobile app and website where users searched for medication discounts. The practices took place between 2017 and 2020, affecting millions of users whose sensitive health data was used for behavioral advertising outside the original discount service context.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) investigated and settled with GoodRx in 2023. GoodRx agreed to pay $1.5 million in penalties, delete all collected personal information, implement a comprehensive privacy program, undergo regular audits, and cease sharing sensitive personal information for advertising.

example_background: Avast, a cybersecurity company, operated a subsidiary called Jumpshot that collected detailed browsing data from up to 100 million users of its antivirus software worldwide, including California residents. Jumpshot captured URLs visited, search terms, YouTube video IDs watched, demographic details, device information, and timestamps. This data was sold to over 300 third-party customers, including Fortune 500 companies, for market research and analytics without user consent. The data included sensitive information such as visits to healthcare sites, financial services, and political websites. Collection occurred automatically upon Avast software installation from 2014 to 2020, with no opt-out mechanism provided in privacy policies.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the California Attorney General reached a $6.5 million settlement with Avast. The company was required to stop selling browsing data, delete all collected consumer data, implement privacy controls, and undergo independent audits for three years.

example_name: HHS OCR Enforcement Against Premera Blue Cross
example_background: Premera Blue Cross, a health insurer, experienced a cyberattack in 2015 exposing protected health information (PHI) of 11 million individuals. Attackers accessed names, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, clinical information, and provider details over nine months. Following the breach, Premera shared deidentified data with researchers but failed to implement sufficient controls to prevent reidentification. The data was used in research studies on healthcare utilization without ensuring compatibility with original collection contexts or protections against reidentification attempts.
process_and_outcome: The HHS Office for Civil Rights (OCR) investigated and settled in 2020 for $6.85 million. Premera was required to implement a corrective action plan including risk analysis, security upgrades, and oversight of business associate agreements.

example_name: Equifax Data Breach and Deidentification Failure
example_background: Equifax, a credit reporting agency, suffered a massive data breach in 2017 exposing sensitive personal information of 147 million consumers including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and credit card numbers. In subsequent handling, Equifax attempted to deidentify data for research and analytics purposes but inadequate deidentification processes allowed reidentification. Shared datasets with partners lacked sufficient safeguards, enabling linkage attacks using public records. The data was originally collected for credit reporting but repurposed without ensuring compatibility.
process_and_outcome: The FTC, CFPB, and states settled for over $700 million in 2019. Equifax paid $575 million consumer redress fund, $125 million to states, and $100 million to FTC. Required to improve data security and deidentification practices for five years.
source_rule: (5) Made subject to business processes to prevent inadvertent release of deidentified information.(6) Protected from any reidentification attempts. (7) Used solely for research purposes that are compatible with the context in which the personal information was collected.

example_name: Google Apple COVID-19 Exposure Notification Compliance
example_background: Google and Apple jointly developed the Exposure Notification (EN) system API for COVID-19 contact tracing apps in 2020. The system generated ephemeral rotation IDs and used proximity-based Bluetooth beacons to compute diagnosis keys without collecting location data or identifiers. Data remained on devices, with opt-in upload of diagnosis keys to public health servers only after positive tests and consent. No central storage occurred, and keys rotated daily to prevent reidentification. Used exclusively by public health authorities for epidemiological research compatible with pandemic response context.
process_and_outcome: The system was adopted by over 50 countries and states without privacy incidents. Independent audits confirmed no reidentification risks, and usage ceased post-pandemic demonstrating controlled processes.

The heatmap visualized popular running and cycling routes using GPS data. While individual tracks were anonymized by not displaying personal data, heatmap density revealed locations of military bases, secret routes, and home addresses of personnel through unique activity patterns. Data was collected for personal fitness tracking but publicly released without protections against linkage to user profiles or reidentification via cross-referencing.
process_and_outcome: Strava removed sensitive heatmaps and updated privacy settings allowing users to opt-out of public aggregation. No formal fines, but prompted industry-wide review of aggregated data risks.

example_background: Netflix, a streaming entertainment service, processes personal data from California subscribers including viewing histories, preferences, and account details for content recommendation algorithms and service optimization research. The company established role-based access controls (RBAC) integrated with its data platforms, ensuring that only data scientists and product managers essential to research projects could access specific datasets. Access logs are monitored, and data is anonymized where possible, with regular reviews to confirm necessity.
process_and_outcome: Netflix underwent external CCPA compliance audits in 2023 and received positive assessments for its data access governance. No enforcement actions were taken, and the company publicly reported adherence in its privacy transparency reports, continuing to refine controls based on internal assessments.

example_background: Google conducts extensive research on user interactions with search, ads, and cloud services, collecting data such as queries, locations, and behaviors from California residents. The company enforces a 'data access tiers' system where raw personal data is accessible only to minimal personnel for specific approved research purposes, with all access requiring justification and logged via tools like Project Oxygen. Synthetic data is used for broader analysis to minimize exposure.
process_and_outcome: In response to 2022 CPPA inquiries, Google demonstrated compliance through submitted documentation, avoiding penalties. Ongoing federal and state audits confirmed effective controls, with public commitments to further enhancements in annual transparency reports.

example_name: California Attorney General CCPA Enforcement against Sephora
example_background: In 2022, Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, was investigated for its practices in selling personal information to third parties for targeted advertising without providing consumers with a clear 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link as required. The company's website collected and shared data via tracking pixels and cookies with analytics providers, but the notice was not conspicuous, and there were issues with honoring opt-out requests. While not a breach, the systems handling personal information lacked proper controls for transparency and consumer control over data sales, which relates to maintaining integrity in data handling processes under privacy frameworks.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General settled with Sephora for $1.2 million. Sephora agreed to update its privacy notices, implement a 'Do Not Sell' link, enhance opt-out mechanisms, and conduct regular privacy audits for two years.
source_rule: (ac) “Security and integrity” means the ability of: (1) Networks or information systems to detect security incidents that compromise the availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality of stored or transmitted personal information.

example_name: Microsoft Implementation of Advanced Threat Protection
example_background: Microsoft has integrated security features across its Azure cloud and Microsoft 365 platforms, including Microsoft Defender for Endpoint and Azure Sentinel, which provide real-time detection of security incidents affecting personal data. These tools use AI-driven analytics to monitor network traffic, detect anomalous behavior, and alert on potential compromises to data availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality. For instance, in handling customer personal information under privacy laws, Microsoft employs endpoint detection and response (EDR) capabilities that identify threats like ransomware or unauthorized access attempts swiftly, ensuring systems maintain security postures for stored and transmitted data.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's frameworks have been recognized in industry reports for reducing breach detection times. No specific fines or violations; instead, SOC 2 Type II audits and ISO 27001 certifications validate compliance. Customers benefit from rapid incident response, often mitigating issues before data exfiltration.
source_rule: (ac) “Security and integrity” means the ability of: (1) Networks or information systems to detect security incidents that compromise the availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality of stored or transmitted personal information.

example_name: Salesforce Shield Platform Encryption and Event Monitoring
example_background: Salesforce offers Shield, a suite of security tools including event monitoring and platform encryption, designed to detect and log all activities on customer data. It captures login events, API calls, report exports, and data exports in real-time, providing comprehensive audit trails for personal information. This enables organizations to detect unauthorized access or modifications to data, ensuring the availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality of stored and transmitted personal information. The system supports compliance by alerting on suspicious patterns, such as bulk downloads or unusual login locations.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce maintains Trust Platform with 100% uptime and regular compliance certifications. Adopted by enterprises handling CCPA-regulated data, it has helped prevent incidents through proactive detection. No enforcement actions against Salesforce for data handling failures.

example_background: Microsoft conducted voluntary CCPA compliance assessments and implemented robust privacy controls across its services. The company established dedicated teams for CCPA response, including automated tools for handling data subject requests, detailed privacy notices, and opt-out mechanisms for data sales. Microsoft invested in security incident detection through Microsoft Defender and Azure Sentinel, enabling rapid identification and response to potential threats affecting California residents' data.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's proactive measures allowed it to meet CCPA deadlines without enforcement actions. Internal audits and third-party certifications confirmed adherence, with public transparency reports detailing incident responses and privacy enhancements.

example_background: Apple Inc. designed its ecosystem with privacy by default, including App Tracking Transparency (ATT) which requires explicit user consent for cross-app tracking. For CCPA, Apple provides granular controls in iOS settings for personalized ads opt-out, data deletion requests, and access rights. The company employs on-device processing to minimize data collection and uses differential privacy for analytics, alongside advanced threat detection in iCloud and device security features to identify and mitigate unauthorized access attempts.
process_and_outcome: Apple avoided CCPA enforcement through preemptive compliance. Its practices set industry benchmarks, with regular privacy reports and no major incidents leading to penalties.

example_background: Twitter (now X Corp.) faced allegations of sharing user data with third parties without adequate CCPA-compliant opt-out mechanisms. The platform collected extensive user data for advertising, failing to promptly honor deletion requests and disclose data sales practices to California users.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Twitter settled with the California AG for $150 million, agreeing to pay penalties and improve data practices, including better request handling and transparency.

example_background: Intuit, the maker of TurboTax and QuickBooks, shared consumer personal information including tax-related data, email addresses, and device information with third-party advertisers and analytics firms for monetary consideration. The sharing occurred through tracking pixels and SDKs embedded in their websites and apps. Intuit's privacy notices did not adequately describe these practices as 'sales' under CCPA, and the company did not honor opt-out requests properly, including those via browser signals.
process_and_outcome: Settled with the California Attorney General in 2023 for $250,000. Intuit agreed to update disclosures, implement opt-out processes, and conduct annual CCPA training for employees handling consumer data.

process_and_outcome: The CPPA secured a $1.275 million penalty in 2024, along with requirements for enhanced notices, deletion of non-compliant data, and a three-year compliance monitoring period.

example_name: Mozilla's Enhanced Tracking Protection and CCPA Compliance
example_background: Mozilla's Firefox browser implemented Enhanced Tracking Protection, which by default blocks known trackers from loading on websites, thereby preventing the collection and transfer of personal information for advertising purposes without user consent. The browser also natively supports the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal, automatically sending opt-out requests for CCPA-covered data sales and shares to websites visited by California users. Mozilla's privacy policy transparently describes these protections and does not engage in selling user data.
process_and_outcome: Recognized by privacy advocates and regulators as a best practice, with no enforcement actions against Mozilla. Independent audits confirmed effective reduction in data sharing incidents, serving as a model for CCPA adherence.

process_and_outcome: CPPA imposed a $115,000 penalty in 2024, requiring policy updates and data protection assessments.

Chegg delayed notifying affected users for months after discovery.
process_and_outcome: Chegg agreed to a $2.5 million settlement with the FTC and implemented a comprehensive data security program, including encryption of sensitive information and regular audits.

example_background: Microsoft provides enterprise-grade compliance in its Azure cloud services, featuring automatic encryption of data at rest and in transit, including financial account details and credentials. Azure's Purview service scans for sensitive information like Social Security numbers and credit card numbers, applying data loss prevention (DLP) policies. Customer-managed keys ensure control over encryption, and compliance with CCPA is supported through configurable privacy controls and audit logs for access to personal data.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft holds multiple certifications and has not faced CCPA-specific fines; regular third-party audits confirm adherence. Used by California businesses to meet regulatory requirements without incidents.

example_background: Gushcloud, a social media influencer marketing platform, collected and shared personal information of California residents with third parties without obtaining opt-in consent or providing opt-out mechanisms as required under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), which amended the CCPA. The company processed data including names, email addresses, precise geolocation, and purchase history for advertising purposes, transferring it to analytics and ad tech providers. Gushcloud failed to recognize consumer opt-out requests, including Do Not Sell My Personal Information (DNSMPI) link clicks and GPC signals, and continued sharing data post-opt-out. The platform's privacy policy was vague about the nature and extent of data sharing, leading to determinations that these transfers qualified as sales under CCPA/CPRA definitions.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) announced Gushcloud as the first enforcement action under CPRA, resulting in a $1.2 million penalty. Gushcloud was required to cease processing data without consent, implement a comprehensive privacy program, honor all opt-out preferences, and submit compliance reports.

example_name: Apple App Tracking Transparency (ATT) and CCPA Compliance
example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency in iOS 14.5, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking across apps or websites using the IDFA identifier. This framework supports CCPA by allowing consumers to direct whether their data is shared for cross-app tracking, which often constitutes a sale. Apple's privacy nutrition labels and device-level controls further enable users to limit data sharing, with automatic opt-out for certain tracking.
process_and_outcome: ATT has been praised in CPPA guidance as aligning with CCPA opt-out requirements; Apple faced no CCPA fines and influenced industry-wide adoption of consent mechanisms.

process_and_outcome: February 2024 settlement for $100,000, requiring implementation of opt-out links, GPC support, data deletion processes, and compliance certifications.

Post-transfer, the data continued to be used for ticketing services, customer communications, and marketing consistent with the original collection purposes disclosed in privacy notices.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) reviewed the transfer during a broader audit and found the process compliant with data protection requirements for mergers, noting adequate safeguards like data processing agreements and consistency in usage. No enforcement action was taken.

example_name: Neuralink Animal Testing Data Handling Investigation
example_background: Neuralink, Elon Musk's brain-computer interface company, faced scrutiny over its handling of neural data generated from implantable devices in animal trials. The company's experiments involved recording and processing neural activity from monkeys' brains to develop brain-machine interfaces. Reports emerged of data management practices during testing, including the collection of extensive neural signals from the central nervous system. Federal oversight raised questions about the security and privacy of this neural data, particularly as it relates to physiological measurements not inferred from other sources.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) and SEC investigated Neuralink's practices. No direct fines were imposed for data handling, but the company was required to improve protocols. Public disclosures led to enhanced internal data protection policies.

process_and_outcome: Norway's Data Protection Authority fined Grindr 100 million NOK (approximately $10 million USD) in 2021 for unlawful data processing. The company appealed but ultimately discontinued certain data-sharing practices and updated its privacy policy.

example_background: Meta Platforms (Facebook) utilized facial recognition technology through its 'Tag Suggestions' feature, which automatically scanned and analyzed photos uploaded by users to create biometric templates for uniquely identifying individuals. The system processed facial geometry data from millions of users without obtaining written consent or providing opt-out options as required under Illinois law, leading to a class action covering users nationwide including California residents.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, Meta agreed to a $650 million settlement in the Illinois federal court case. The company disabled facial recognition for public use in 2021 and implemented enhanced consent mechanisms.
source_rule: (2) (A) The processing of biometric information for the purpose of uniquely identifying a consumer.(B) Personal information collected and analyzed concerning a consumer’s health. (C) Personal information collected and analyzed concerning a consumer’s sex life or sexual orientation.

example_name: Google Fit HIPAA-Compliant Health Data Handling
example_background: Google Fit collects and analyzes health-related personal information such as steps, heart rate, and sleep patterns from wearables and phones. The platform requires user permission for data access, anonymizes aggregated data for insights, and provides granular controls for deletion and sharing. It integrates HIPAA business associate agreements for covered health data and limits analysis to fitness purposes without third-party advertising use.
process_and_outcome: Google maintained compliance through annual audits and transparency reports, avoiding penalties and earning recognition for secure health data practices in regulatory reviews.
source_rule: (2) (A) The processing of biometric information for the purpose of uniquely identifying a consumer.(B) Personal information collected and analyzed concerning a consumer’s health. (C) Personal information collected and analyzed concerning a consumer’s sex life or sexual orientation.

example_name: OkCupid CCPA Sexual Orientation Consent Update
example_background: OkCupid, an online dating service, collects user data on sexual orientation, preferences, and sex life through questionnaires and profile interactions. Following CCPA amendments, the company updated its practices to obtain opt-in consent for processing and sharing sensitive personal information, providing 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links, and allowing data deletion requests specifically for sensitive categories.
process_and_outcome: Post-2023 CCPA enforcement focus, OkCupid implemented compliant mechanisms, passing CPPA audits without citations and improving user privacy notices.

example_background: Sephora's mobile app used session replay software from SessionCam and CQuotient to track user interactions, capturing biometric inferences from keystrokes and mouse movements, alongside health-related data from beauty quizzes (e.g., skin conditions). This sensitive data was shared with third parties without opt-in consent or limits on collection, affecting California consumers.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CPPA approved a $1.2 million settlement, the first CCPA enforcement action. Sephora agreed to cease unlawful sharing and enhance disclosures for 10 years.
source_rule: (2) (A) The processing of biometric information for the purpose of uniquely identifying a consumer.(B) Personal information collected and analyzed concerning a consumer’s health. (C) Personal information collected and analyzed concerning a consumer’s sex life or sexual orientation.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer operating an online platform, collected consumers' precise geolocation data through its mobile app without providing clear notice or obtaining explicit opt-in consent prior to collection. The app used location services to deliver personalized content and targeted advertising based on users' real-time positions. This occurred during the initial implementation phase of the CCPA in 2020, when many companies were adjusting to the new requirements for sensitive personal information categories, including precise geolocation defined as data accurate to within 1,850 feet or better. Sephora's privacy policy did not adequately disclose the collection of this data type, and users were not given the opportunity to opt out before the data was gathered and shared with third-party advertisers. The issue came to light through routine monitoring and consumer complaints filed with the California Attorney General's office.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) investigated the matter and reached a settlement in 2024, requiring Sephora to pay a $1.2 million penalty. Sephora was ordered to update its privacy notices, implement mechanisms for opt-out consents for sensitive data, conduct regular privacy audits, and train employees on CCPA compliance for a period of three years.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) §1798.121 (Sensitive Personal Information)

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, gathered precise geolocation data from consumers using its app to facilitate order deliveries, track driver locations, and enable location-based promotions. The data was collected continuously, including when the app was running in the background, without users being informed that it qualified as sensitive personal information under CCPA or given the option to limit its use. This practice persisted from the CCPA's effective date in January 2020 through 2022, affecting millions of California users. DoorDash shared this data with analytics providers and marketing partners to refine services and advertising strategies. The company's privacy disclosures mentioned location data generally but failed to specify precise geolocation or the associated consumer rights to opt out of its sale or sharing.
process_and_outcome: Following an investigation prompted by a 2022 complaint, the California Attorney General secured a $375,000 settlement in October 2024. DoorDash committed to enhanced disclosures, geofencing opt-out features, data minimization practices, and annual compliance certifications submitted to the state for two years.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.

example_background: Kriser's, a pet food retailer, operated a mobile app that collected precise geolocation data from California consumers to provide store locators, promotions, and personalized recommendations. The app requested location permissions upon download but did not disclose in its privacy policy that this constituted sensitive personal information requiring explicit consumer consent under CCPA. Data was shared with third-party vendors for advertising purposes without opt-out mechanisms. The plaintiff, a California resident, alleged that this collection occurred without adequate notice from 2020 to 2023, impacting users who unknowingly enabled location services. This case highlighted challenges in distinguishing general location data from precise geolocation in retail apps.
process_and_outcome: The parties reached a class action settlement in 2024 for $500,000, including injunctive relief to revise app permissions, add prominent opt-out notices, and delete previously collected geolocation data not needed for service provision. Class members received pro rata payments.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.

example_background: Hulu, a streaming service, tracked California users' precise geolocation via its app and website to deliver region-specific content, ads, and enforce licensing restrictions. From 2018 through 2022, Hulu collected this data using device APIs without classifying it as sensitive under CCPA or providing opt-in consent options before sharing with advertisers and content providers. The privacy policy vaguely referenced 'location' but omitted details on precision levels or consumer control rights. Plaintiffs claimed exposure of users' home and work locations, leading to privacy intrusions.
process_and_outcome: A class action settlement was approved in 2023 for $6 million, with Hulu agreeing to implement granular geolocation controls, update notices to specify CCPA rights for sensitive data, and conduct third-party audits. Funds were distributed to class members.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California

example_name: CMT v. Epic Games Geolocation Case
example_background: Epic Games, developer of Fortnite, collected precise geolocation data from minors and adults in California through the game's app for features like friend matching and event notifications. The data was used internally and shared with analytics firms without parental consent for minors or opt-in for sensitive data classification under CCPA/CPRA. This spanned 2020-2023, with background location tracking enabled by default. The plaintiff highlighted risks of data exposure revealing school and home locations.
process_and_outcome: Settlement reached in 2024 for $1.45 million, requiring Epic to add age-appropriate consent flows, disable default precise tracking, provide deletion tools, and certify compliance annually. Additional safeguards for children's data were mandated.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.

example_name: CPPA Citation against Avast for Data Sales
example_background: Avast, a cybersecurity firm, operated a subsidiary called Jumpshot that collected browsing data, including inferred sensitive categories like religious beliefs and ethnic origins from California users via browser extensions. From 2014-2020, this data was sold to over 100 clients for analytics without disclosure or opt-out under CCPA post-2020. The data aggregation allowed profiling based on visits to sites indicating religious affiliations, political leanings, and ethnic communities.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a $6.5 million fine in 2024, the largest CCPA penalty to date. Avast shut down Jumpshot, deleted datasets, and entered a compliance order for five years including audits and notice improvements.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.

example_name: Outlogic v. Lyft Ethnic Profiling Suit
example_background: Lyft, a rideshare company, faced allegations of inferring and using riders' racial or ethnic origins from profile photos, names, and ride patterns for algorithmic pricing and matching in California from 2020-2023. This sensitive data was processed without explicit consent and shared with partners, potentially leading to discriminatory outcomes in surge pricing.
process_and_outcome: Class action dismissed on arbitration grounds in 2024, but Lyft revised policies to prohibit sensitive inferences, added bias audits, and settled related claims for $50,000 in fees.

example_background: Salesforce, a CRM platform provider, updated its data processing agreements and platform controls in 2021 to handle customer data including sensitive categories like religious beliefs or union membership uploaded by enterprise clients. Features include field-level encryption, access controls, and automated detection of sensitive data with opt-out enforcement for CCPA-covered consumers. This ensures subprocessors comply with limits on collection and sharing.
process_and_outcome: Passed multiple CPPA audits without citations; certified as CCPA-compliant in 2023 trust reports, serving as a model for SaaS providers.
source_rule: (C) A consumer’s precise geolocation. (D) A consumer’s racial or ethnic origin, citizenship or immigration status, religious or philosophical beliefs, or union membership.

example_background: Postmates, a meal and grocery delivery service acquired by Uber, collected detailed user data encompassing account details, delivery addresses, order contents, payment methods, and device identifiers. This information was shared with advertising partners and data brokers like Oracle Data Cloud and Drawbridge for cross-device tracking and behavioral advertising. Despite privacy notices, the company did not recognize these transfers as sales under CCPA, failing to offer consumers opt-out mechanisms or clear disclosures about the nature of data exchanges for valuable consideration prior to and during the CCPA's operative date.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated and secured a $100,000 settlement in 2021, requiring Postmates to enhance its privacy program with specific CCPA-compliant notices, opt-out functionalities, data retention policies, and biennial audits. The action was part of broader efforts to enforce transparency in the gig economy sector.
source_rule: (af) “Service” or “services” means work, labor, and services, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods.

example_name: CalOPPA to CCPA Transition Settlement with HiQ Labs
example_background: HiQ Labs, a data analytics firm, provided services to human resources professionals by scraping public LinkedIn profiles to create datasets on employee skills and employment status. This process involved accessing and aggregating public data at scale to deliver subscription-based services. While initially challenged under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, the case intersected with data access regulations, and LinkedIn's practices raised questions about data furnished in connection with services under emerging privacy frameworks like CCPA, particularly regarding public data usage in commercial services.
process_and_outcome: The Ninth Circuit ruled in favor of HiQ in 2019, finding no violation of CFAA for public data scraping. However, the case influenced CCPA interpretations on data services. No direct CCPA fine was imposed, but it led to industry-wide reviews of data scraping disclosures.

example_background: Volkswagen Group provided repair and maintenance services for diesel vehicles equipped with defeat devices that altered emissions performance during testing. These services were furnished in connection with the sale and ongoing support of goods, involving software updates and diagnostic work that concealed non-compliance with emissions standards. The activities affected consumers across California dealerships and service centers over several years.
process_and_outcome: The California Air Resources Board and DOJ investigated, leading to a $4.3 billion settlement including funds for vehicle buybacks, modifications, and mitigation projects. Volkswagen was required to overhaul service protocols and compliance training.
source_rule: (af) “Service” or “services” means work, labor, and services, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods.

example_background: BMW offered extended warranty and maintenance services for diesel vehicles that included software recalibrations and part replacements. These services were provided in connection with vehicle sales and repairs, but investigations revealed that certain updates did not fully address emissions issues stemming from prior non-compliant engine management systems, affecting service transparency and consumer expectations.
process_and_outcome: CARB and EPA imposed a $289 million penalty in 2019, with BMW funding emissions fixes through service programs and paying civil fines. The company was mandated to improve service documentation and compliance monitoring.
source_rule: (af) “Service” or “services” means work, labor, and services, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods.

example_background: Uber Technologies collects and processes rider and driver data for ride-hailing services, including trip details, locations, payment info, and device data. The company shares this with analytics and advertising partners, which under CCPA scrutiny was determined to qualify as sales due to remuneration received. Uber's initial privacy notices lacked specificity on these practices during the CCPA rollout.
process_and_outcome: Settled with California AG in 2022 for $92,000, with requirements to revise privacy policies, add opt-out links, implement data minimization, and conduct training. No admission of liability was made.
source_rule: (af) “Service” or “services” means work, labor, and services, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods.

example_background: Microsoft provides cloud services like Azure, Microsoft 365, and Dynamics 365, which involve handling customer and end-user data for storage, productivity, and analytics. The company established a CCPA-specific program mapping data flows across services, offering enterprise customers tools for rights fulfillment and posting detailed 'Do Not Sell' notices confirming no personal data sales.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft published CCPA compliance statements and integrated privacy controls into service agreements, achieving compliance certification and avoiding enforcement. Industry analyses praised the scalable approach for B2B services.
source_rule: (af) “Service” or “services” means work, labor, and services, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods.

example_name: California AG Action Against GDCVA (Generac Power Systems)
example_background: Generac Power Systems, through its subsidiary Generac Digital Corporation (doing business as GDCVA), sold personal information of California consumers collected from its websites to third parties for advertising purposes. The data included names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and usage data from smart home devices. GDCVA acted as a service provider processing this data on behalf of Generac but also engaged in sales of the information to data brokers without proper disclosures or opt-out rights. The contracts between Generac and GDCVA did not sufficiently prohibit the recipient from using the data outside specified business purposes, leading to unauthorized retention and secondary sharing. This affected consumers interacting with Generac's websites and devices.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured an Assurance of Discontinuance in September 2022. GDCVA agreed to stop selling personal information, delete collected data, implement privacy safeguards, and pay $57,000 in investigation costs. The settlement highlighted issues with service provider boundaries.

example_background: TikTok shared geolocation, device, and biometric data from California users with service providers including Amazon Web Services, Oracle, and advertising partners. The data processing involved analytics and ad personalization, but contracts did not fully restrict combining with non-CCPA data or retaining beyond business needs. This affected the platform's vast user base engaging with video content.
process_and_outcome: California AG settlement in December 2023; TikTok paid $1.5 million penalty and agreed to privacy program enhancements, data retention limits, and employee training.

example_name: Microsoft Azure CCPA Data Processing Addendum
example_background: Microsoft provides Azure cloud services where it processes customer personal data under strict data processing terms. The Microsoft Products Data Protection Addendum (DPA) prohibits Microsoft from using customer data for its own purposes, selling or sharing it, or combining it with non-customer data. It requires subprocessors to adhere to the same terms, supports CCPA rights fulfillment, and includes audit rights, ensuring businesses can leverage Azure compliantly.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft maintains SOC 2 compliance and CCPA alignment; no violations recorded. Used as a model in industry guidance.

example_name: CMT Digital GDPR Data Sharing Fine
example_background: CMT Digital, a UK-based digital marketing agency, processed and shared personal data of over 1.5 million individuals with clients and third parties for marketing purposes without a valid legal basis or proper consent mechanisms. The company retained consumer contact details and behavioral data beyond the scope of initial collection for unspecified business purposes, leading to unauthorized disclosures.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated and fined CMT Digital £300,000 in October 2021. The agency was required to cease processing the data and implement compliance measures.

process_and_outcome: In October 2022, the California Attorney General secured a $100,000 settlement, requiring DoorDash to enhance privacy controls, limit data retention, and provide clear notices for data sharing practices.

example_background: Grokster, an advertising technology company, collected consumer personal information including IP addresses, browsing histories, and device identifiers from websites embedding its tracking pixels. The privacy notice at collection time stated data would be used internally for analytics and not shared for third-party monetization. Later, Grokster began auctioning this data to data brokers and ad networks, materially altering the usage without prior notice to affected consumers or prominent disclosure allowing choice exercise, impacting data from numerous California websites.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA initiated an enforcement action in September 2024, resulting in California's first-ever CCPA administrative fine of $1.5 million against Grokster. The agency ordered cessation of unauthorized data sharing and compliance program implementation.
source_rule: personal information of a consumer in a manner that is materially inconsistent with the promises made at the time of collection, it shall provide prior notice of the new or changed practice to the consumer. The notice shall be sufficiently prominent and robust to ensure that existing consumers can easily exercise their choices consistently with this title. This subparagraph does not authorize a business to make material, retroactive privacy policy changes or make other changes in their privacy policy in a manner that would violate the Unfair and Deceptive Practices Act (Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 17200) of Part 2 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code).

example_name: Microsoft GDPR and CCPA Privacy Dashboard
example_background: Microsoft implemented a privacy dashboard for consumer services like Outlook and Xbox, allowing users to view data collected at signup (e.g., email, usage patterns) and manage sharing settings. When introducing new analytics sharing in 2021, Microsoft provided email notices and in-dashboard alerts to existing users 30 days prior, detailing changes and opt-out links, maintaining consistency with initial collection statements.
process_and_outcome: No violations found in audits; Microsoft certified compliance under various frameworks, avoiding fines and enhancing user trust, with high opt-out rates demonstrating effective notice.

example_background: Google updated its Analytics platform in 2022 to include Consent Mode, requiring prior website notices for California users about data collection (e.g., IPs, events) promised solely for service improvement. Changes to sharing with ad networks triggered mandatory banners and 90-day notice periods for existing configurations, with granular controls.
process_and_outcome: Google faced no CCPA penalties; the update was endorsed by privacy regulators as proactive, reducing complaint volumes and aligning with notice requirements.

Notices were sent via email and app, with 60-day windows for data deletion requests, ensuring prominence and choice.
process_and_outcome: Third-party audits confirmed adherence; Meta avoided enforcement by demonstrating robust notice processes, though faced unrelated scrutiny.
