example_background: Google collected and shared California users' personal information through its advertising technologies, including YouTube and Android apps, with third-party partners for ad targeting and measurement. Data such as location, search history, and device information was disclosed without fully restricting recipients to specified business purposes, despite contractual terms. The practices involved complex data flows where intermediaries repurposed information for additional commercial activities.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Google settled a class-action lawsuit related to CCPA compliance for $391.5 million, agreeing to clearer disclosures, improved opt-out mechanisms, and restrictions on certain data uses. The settlement addressed allegations of unauthorized disclosures.

example_background: Microsoft's cloud services, including Azure and Microsoft 365, feature Data Processing Addendums (DPAs) that contractually bind service providers to use customer personal data only for the purposes specified in agreements with businesses. Features like data residency controls, encryption, and compliance certifications ensure disclosures to subprocessors are limited and audited. Microsoft provides tools for businesses to manage data flows and opt-outs, aligning with requirements for commercial purpose restrictions.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved CCPA compliance certification and has not faced enforcement for service provider violations, with independent audits confirming adherence. The company supports thousands of CCPA-compliant customers through standardized contracts.

example_background: Salesforce, a CRM platform provider, updated its master service agreements and data processing addendums to explicitly restrict subprocessors from using personal data for any commercial purpose beyond those directed by the controller business. The company maintains a list of approved subprocessors, requires flow-down clauses in contracts, and offers security assessments to verify compliance, enabling businesses to meet CCPA obligations when using the platform.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce's framework supported client compliance during CCPA enforcement waves, with no direct actions against the company. It received positive evaluations in privacy program assessments.

example_background: DoorDash outsourced personal data processing to third-party services for marketing and analytics without adequate written contracts specifying duties. The contracts lacked provisions for the processors to ensure compliance with consumer rights requests, including deletion and opt-out mechanisms. There were no audit rights or regular assessments conducted by DoorDash to verify processor adherence. This came under scrutiny during investigations into widespread CCPA non-compliance among tech platforms handling consumer data.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General reached a settlement in 2024, requiring DoorDash to implement compliant vendor contracts, conduct annual audits, and pay $375,000. Additional remediation included training and compliance program enhancements.
source_rule: subdivision (e) of this section and in regulations adopted by the California Privacy Protection Agency. The contract may, subject to agreement with the service provider, permit the business to monitor the service provider’s compliance with the contract through measures, including, but not limited to, ongoing manual reviews and automated scans and regular assessments, audits, or other technical and operational testing at least once every 12 months.

example_background: Grokster, a streaming service, shared consumer data with advertising technology vendors without contracts that included instructions to limit data use to specified purposes or requirements for handling opt-out signals. The company did not perform regular audits or technical testing of vendor compliance, leading to data being retained and shared beyond permitted uses. This was part of a broader probe into ad tech ecosystems under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: Settlement announced in 2024 with the California Attorney General, involving $1.2 million in penalties and injunctive relief mandating compliant contracts, annual assessments, and deletion of improperly held data.

process_and_outcome: Google maintained compliance without CCPA-specific fines related to processor oversight, supporting thousands of businesses in meeting their obligations through transparent monitoring mechanisms.

Instances occurred where data was used beyond instructed purposes, and monitoring was not conducted as required.
process_and_outcome: 2024 settlement with CPPA for $300,000, requiring implementation of audit clauses, annual assessments, and contract revisions.

example_background: Google LLC operated services including advertising technologies and consumer-facing products such as Search, YouTube, and Android. It received personal information from businesses through mechanisms like Customer Match, where businesses uploaded customer data for targeted advertising. Google combined this data with personal information collected directly from consumer interactions across its platforms and from other sources. This combination enabled enhanced user profiling for ad targeting. The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) examined whether these practices aligned with service provider limitations under CCPA regulations, focusing on the scope of combining data received from businesses with other datasets.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA initiated an enforcement action in 2024, resulting in a $93 million settlement with Google. The agency determined that Google's data combination practices exceeded permissible business purposes for service providers. Google agreed to the settlement without admitting liability and committed to specific compliance adjustments.

The CPPA investigated the extent of this data integration and its alignment with service provider boundaries.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA settled with DoorDash for $37.5 million. The settlement addressed data combination practices that went beyond allowed business purposes. DoorDash implemented changes to its data handling protocols as part of the agreement.

It received personal health information from businesses and consumers seeking discounts. GoodRx combined this data with information from its direct consumer interactions and sold aggregated profiles to pharmaceutical companies for marketing purposes. The combination created detailed health-related consumer dossiers shared with third parties.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) conducted an investigation and issued orders in 2023 requiring GoodRx to implement comprehensive privacy programs, undergo audits, and cease unauthorized data sharing. No monetary penalty was imposed, but ongoing compliance obligations were established.

The practices involved extensive merging of datasets beyond direct service delivery.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the FTC settled with Outlogic, prohibiting further sale of sensitive location data and requiring deletion of previously collected data. Outlogic agreed to privacy program implementation and biennial audits for 20 years.

process_and_outcome: The Texas Attorney General settled with Oracle in 2022 for $115 million, addressing unauthorized data combination and sharing. Oracle agreed to cease certain data sales and improve consent mechanisms.

process_and_outcome: Salesforce underwent successful CCPA audits and received no enforcement actions. It published detailed compliance documentation and enabled business customers to maintain control over their data flows.

example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) offered cloud data processing services to businesses, receiving personal information for storage and analytics. AWS maintained contractual restrictions and technical controls ensuring it did not combine client data with data from other customers or its own e-commerce consumer data, adhering to permitted business purposes such as data hosting and basic processing.
process_and_outcome: AWS achieved CCPA service provider certification through third-party audits and faced no violations. It provided toolkits for customers to enforce data isolation.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform that collected personal information from California residents through tracking technologies provided by third-party vendors such as LiveRamp and Curalate. These vendors engaged additional subprocessors to process the data for advertising and analytics purposes without Sephora's knowledge of the full chain of engagements. Sephora's privacy policy indicated that it shared data with service providers but did not disclose the use of these specific trackers or the further subcontracting. The arrangement involved collecting browsing behavior, purchase history, and device identifiers, which were processed across multiple entities without contractual assurances flowing down to all subprocessors as required under data protection standards. This came to light during a broader enforcement sweep by the California Attorney General targeting websites using pixel trackers for unauthorized data sharing.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated multiple companies under CCPA provisions and reached a settlement with Sephora, requiring the company to implement a comprehensive data privacy program, conduct regular audits of third-party vendors, update privacy notices to disclose tracking practices, and pay $1.2 million in penalties. Sephora also agreed to cease unauthorized sharing and ensure future contracts include subprocessors notifications.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, shared personal information of California consumers including names, addresses, phone numbers, and order histories with third-party advertising service providers like LiveRamp and AppsFlyer. These service providers utilized additional subprocessors for data processing in targeted advertising campaigns without notifying DoorDash of such engagements or obtaining written contracts that bound the subprocessors to CCPA-compliant terms. The data processing chain involved multiple layers where personal information was aggregated and analyzed for marketing purposes, and DoorDash's privacy notices did not adequately address the extent of these third-party arrangements. This issue was identified as part of the same Attorney General investigation into pixel-based tracking on high-traffic websites.
process_and_outcome: Settlement with the California Attorney General resulted in DoorDash paying $250,000 in penalties, committing to a privacy program overhaul, annual third-party audits, and contract revisions to include subprocessors notification requirements. The company also enhanced consumer opt-out mechanisms.

example_background: Flo Health, a menstrual cycle tracking app, shared user health data including sensitive fertility and pregnancy indicators with third-party analytics and advertising providers such as Google, Facebook, and Amplitude. These service providers further engaged subprocessors for data storage, processing, and ad targeting without Flo notifying the business or ensuring binding contracts that extended privacy protections to those subprocessors. The data sharing occurred via SDKs and APIs, involving millions of users' personal information processed across international servers. Flo's privacy policy mentioned third-party sharing but lacked details on subprocessors, leading to concerns about compliance with data minimization and contractual obligations in the processing chain.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission investigated following a New York Times report and reached a settlement requiring Flo to prohibit future sharing of sensitive health data, implement a privacy program with vendor oversight, and undergo biennial audits. No monetary penalty was imposed, but the order highlighted service provider contract deficiencies.

example_name: California AG Action Against Farmers Insurance (2024)
example_background: Farmers Insurance exchanged personal information of California customers, including names, contact details, policy numbers, and driving records, with third-party lead generation service providers. These providers subcontracted with additional entities to process the data for marketing purposes without notifying Farmers or establishing written contracts that required the subprocessors to adhere to CCPA standards. The information was used to generate insurance quotes and leads, involving data brokers in a multi-tier processing arrangement. This practice persisted despite CCPA requirements, and was uncovered through consumer complaints and regulatory audits focusing on insurance sector data practices.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured a $175,000 settlement, mandating Farmers to review and revise all service provider contracts, notify the AG of subprocessors engagements, cease unauthorized sharing, and train employees on CCPA compliance.

example_background: TikTok engaged cloud service providers and analytics firms to process European user data, which included further subcontracting to subprocessors for data storage and recommendation algorithms without consistent notification to TikTok or fully binding contracts propagating GDPR requirements down the chain. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined TikTok's data processing ecosystem, finding gaps in subprocessors oversight, particularly with U.S.-based hyperscalers handling vast amounts of personal data from minors and adults for content personalization. Contracts existed but subprocessors notifications were inconsistent, affecting compliance with controller-processor obligations.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC imposed a 345 million euro fine on TikTok for child data violations, with subprocessors issues contributing to broader findings. TikTok appealed but implemented enhanced vendor management and contract templates.

example_background: Google Analytics users in the EU faced issues where the service engaged subprocessors for data processing without adequate notification or contracts binding them to GDPR standards, leading to data transfers. The Italian DPA investigated websites using Google Analytics, finding that Google subcontracted processing tasks across its ecosystem without ensuring all subprocessors observed controller instructions. This involved IP addresses and behavioral data processed in the U.S., with notifications to businesses often buried in terms of service updates.
process_and_outcome: Italy's Garante ordered websites to stop using Google Analytics, prompting Google to revise subprocessors lists and contracts. Multiple DPAs issued guidance, resulting in widespread tool suspensions and compliance overhauls.

example_background: Salesforce, a CRM platform provider, updated its Data Processing Addendum (DPA) to include explicit provisions for subprocessors, requiring notification to customers 30 days in advance of any engagement and allowing customers to object. The DPA binds all subprocessors to identical CCPA and GDPR terms via flow-down clauses, covering data processing for business purposes like sales automation. Salesforce maintains a public list of subprocessors and processes objections quarterly, demonstrating structured oversight in its multi-tier vendor ecosystem serving thousands of CCPA-covered businesses.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Salesforce's practices have been audited by customers and third parties, with certifications like ISO 27001 and SOC 2 reports confirming adherence. The model has been adopted as an industry benchmark.

example_name: Microsoft Azure Privacy Addendum for CCPA
example_background: Microsoft provides cloud services under a Customer Data Protection Addendum that mandates subprocessors notification via a public registry updated at least 10 days prior to engagement. Contracts require all subprocessors to be bound by CCPA-compliant terms, with options for customer termination if objections are raised. This covers processing of personal data for business purposes like AI training and storage, involving subprocessors such as Akamai for CDN services. Microsoft's framework ensures transparency and contractual protections across the processing chain.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft undergoes regular CCPA compliance reviews and publishes transparency reports; no major subprocessors-related fines. Practices recognized in industry analyses as leading.

For business customers processing personal data, AWS ensures notifications and contractual obligations extend to entities like data centers operators and security providers. This setup supports compliance for clients handling California consumer data in cloud environments.
process_and_outcome: AWS maintains compliance certifications and faces no subprocessors-specific CCPA enforcement; model praised in vendor risk management guides.

example_background: Mozilla, developer of Firefox browser, implemented support for the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal in 2022, automatically sending opt-out preferences to websites visited by California users. Firefox blocks known trackers and provides tools for users to limit sharing of personal information, with privacy policies detailing non-sharing practices for opted-out consumers. Mozilla does not sell or share personal data for advertising and uses aggregated data only, providing alerts and controls to respect CCPA opt-outs.
process_and_outcome: Achieved compliance recognition from CPPA; no violations recorded, serving as an industry best practice adopted by regulators for CPRA enforcement.

example_background: Farmers Insurance sold or shared consumer data, including policy information and driving behavior data from telematics programs, without prominently displaying a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link or providing sufficient prior notice of these practices to existing customers. Privacy policy changes expanding data sharing were not communicated robustly, making it challenging for consumers to exercise opt-out rights consistently.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the CPPA approved a $65,000 settlement. Farmers agreed to implement the required link, update notices, train staff, and conduct audits.

example_background: Google offers My Activity dashboard, Ad Settings, and personalized ads controls where California users can manage data usage, opt out of personalized ads, download data, and request deletions. Prior to changes like updates to data retention in Google Analytics or new sharing in Google Ads, the company provides advance notice via email, on-site banners, and policy pages with step-by-step choice instructions. These mechanisms are integrated across services like Search, YouTube, and Android for easy access.
process_and_outcome: Google complies through self-audits and third-party certifications, avoiding CCPA penalties and aligning with enforcement expectations.

example_background: Google collected personal information from California users across its services including search history, location data, and ad interactions. The company presented opt-out mechanisms in a manner deemed confusing, with multiple overlapping tools and disclosures that did not clearly allow consumers to limit the sale or sharing of their data with advertisers. Privacy policies at collection indicated data use for service improvement and personalization, but subsequent transfers to third-party ad networks materially expanded usage without updated consents. This was part of broader scrutiny on Big Tech's data practices post-CCPA implementation.
process_and_outcome: In November 2022, Google settled with the California Attorney General for $93 million, agreeing to clarify privacy settings, improve user interfaces for data controls, conduct assessments, and report on compliance measures over five years.

Data was gathered via apps and services promising location-based features, but transfers to advertisers went beyond those promises, enabling profiling inconsistent with initial notices. This affected millions of users whose data was monetized through real-time bidding ecosystems.
process_and_outcome: Settlement in August 2022 with the California Attorney General for $400 million in penalties (part of a larger action), requiring Microsoft to limit sensitive data sharing, enhance notices, and implement governance programs.

example_background: Vroom, an online used car retailer, sold personal information of California consumers including names, emails, phone numbers, and vehicle interests to data brokers and lead generators after collecting it for sales inquiries. The privacy policy did not clearly disclose these sales, and no opt-out link was provided, leading to data transfers that enabled unsolicited marketing far beyond the transactional context promised at collection. This occurred during Vroom's peak operations before its wind-down.
process_and_outcome: In February 2023, Vroom settled with the California Attorney General for $3.1 million, including $1 million in penalties and $2.1 million for consumer restitution, plus requirements to delete data and cease sales without compliance.

Initial collection promises focused on content delivery, but data use expanded to extensive profiling and sharing, materially altering purposes post-collection without consumer notification.
process_and_outcome: Settlement in December 2022 for $3.2 million with the California Privacy Protection Agency, mandating program updates, training, and audits.

In corporate transactions such as potential mergers or asset transfers, Mozilla structures data handling to ensure any third-party recipient adheres to the original collection promises outlined in its privacy notice, which emphasizes user control and minimal data retention. Firefox provides 'Enhanced Tracking Protection' and clear opt-out tools for data sharing, aligning transfers with disclosed purposes even in business transitions.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla has maintained CCPA compliance through voluntary privacy enhancements, avoiding enforcement actions and receiving recognition in privacy reports for transparent practices and user-centric data policies.

example_background: Google Analytics users, primarily mid-sized businesses, implemented the analytics tool on their websites, which deployed cookies and unique device identifiers to track California visitors' behavior across pages and sessions. Many sites did not provide CCPA-compliant notices or opt-out links for the sharing of this data with Google, considered a sale due to the service exchange, leading to widespread non-compliance discovered through automated scans and consumer complaints.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General announced settlements with over 100 businesses using Google Analytics, requiring privacy policy updates, cookie consent banners, GPC implementation, and data processing agreements with Google. Individual settlements varied but focused on remediation without penalties.
source_rule: service provider to the business.(3) A contractor. (aj) “Unique identifier” or “unique personal identifier” means a persistent identifier that can be used to recognize a consumer, a family, or a device that is linked to a consumer or family, over time and across different services, including, but not limited to, a device identifier; an Internet Protocol address; cookies, beacons, pixel tags, mobile ad identifiers, or similar technology; customer number, unique pseudonym, or user alias; telephone numbers, or other forms of persistent or probabilistic identifiers that can be used to identify a particular consumer or device that is linked to a consumer or family. For purposes of this subdivision, “family” means a custodial parent or guardian and any children under 18 years of age over which the parent or guardian has custody.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Civil Code § 1798.135

example_background: Mozilla developed and promoted the Global Privacy Control (GPC) browser signal, which consumers can enable to automatically communicate opt-out preferences for data sales across websites. Websites like those operated by Mozilla detect GPC and refrain from selling unique identifiers such as cookies, IP addresses, and device IDs, demonstrating integration of standardized opt-out mechanisms for CCPA compliance.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by browsers like Firefox, DuckDuckGo, and Brave, GPC has been recognized by CPPA as a compliant do-not-sell signal. Mozilla's sites honor GPC, preventing data sales and serving as a model for others, with no violations recorded.

example_name: CPPA Approval of GPC as CCPA Opt-Out Signal
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency finalized regulations recognizing Global Privacy Control (GPC) as a valid technical signal for consumers to opt out of data sales involving unique identifiers like IP addresses, cookies, and device IDs. Businesses that detect and process GPC requests demonstrate adherence by ceasing sales or sharing for cross-context behavioral advertising.
process_and_outcome: In March 2024, CPPA regulations took effect requiring businesses to treat GPC as a valid opt-out request equivalent to 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links. Numerous companies including Microsoft Edge and Brave have implemented support, enhancing consumer control.

example_background: Gatos Silver Inc., a publicly traded mining company, maintained a website that employed Facebook Pixel and LinkedIn Insight Tag tracking tools. These mechanisms captured personal information such as IP addresses, browser identifiers, and pages visited by California consumers interacting with the site. The data was shared with Meta and LinkedIn for advertising purposes without obtaining explicit consumer direction to disclose it or intentional interaction directed at those entities. This sharing built audience segments for targeted ads based on user interests in mining investments and corporate updates. The company's privacy notice did not adequately disclose these practices, and no opt-in consent was obtained prior to data transmission, impacting investors and site visitors from California.
process_and_outcome: In October 2024, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) announced its first enforcement action under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), issuing a $165,000 fine against Gatos Silver. The company entered into a settlement agreement requiring enhanced disclosures, implementation of controls to prevent unauthorized sharing, and ongoing compliance monitoring.
source_rule: (2) For purposes of this title, a business does not share personal information when: (A) A consumer uses or directs the business to intentionally disclose personal information or intentionally interact with one or more third parties.

example_name: FTC Action Against GoodRx for Pixel Sharing
example_background: GoodRx, a digital healthcare platform providing prescription discount services, integrated tracking pixels from Meta, Google, and Criteo on its website and app. These pixels collected sensitive personal information including prescription drug usage data, health conditions inferred from searches, geolocation, and device details from users seeking medication discounts. The data was disclosed to advertising platforms without users directing GoodRx to share it or intentionally interacting with the third parties. GoodRx's privacy policy promised not to share health data with advertisers, but the pixels enabled the creation of targeted ad profiles based on medical histories, affecting millions of users including those with conditions like HIV, mental health issues, and opioid dependencies.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated and settled with GoodRx in February 2023, imposing no monetary penalty but requiring the company to implement a comprehensive data security and privacy program, limit data sharing, provide clear notices, obtain affirmative express consent for sensitive data sharing, and undergo biennial audits for 20 years.
source_rule: (2) For purposes of this title, a business does not share personal information when: (A) A consumer uses or directs the business to intentionally disclose personal information or intentionally interact with one or more third parties.

example_background: Flo Health, a menstrual cycle tracking app, shared user health data including dates of periods, symptoms, and fertility indicators with third parties like Google, Apple, and Facebook via Google Firebase Analytics, Facebook SDK, and other integrations. This occurred without users explicitly directing Flo to disclose the sensitive reproductive health information or intentionally interacting with those analytics and advertising providers. The data was used to generate user profiles for potential ad targeting, despite Flo's privacy policy stating that health data would not be shared. The sharing impacted users tracking intimate health details, raising concerns over unintended disclosures.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with Flo in 2021, requiring the company to prohibit future sharing of sensitive health data for advertising, obtain user consent before any such sharing, implement a privacy program, and be subject to third-party audits for 20 years.
source_rule: (2) For purposes of this title, a business does not share personal information when: (A) A consumer uses or directs the business to intentionally disclose personal information or intentionally interact with one or more third parties.

example_name: National Steel Car FTC Cookie Settlement
example_background: National Steel Car, a railcar manufacturer, used session replay software from SessionCam and Hotjar on its website, which recorded user interactions including keystrokes, mouse movements, page scrolls, and device information. This data was shared with the third-party providers without website visitors directing the company to disclose it or intentionally interacting with SessionCam or Hotjar. The recordings captured detailed session data from potential customers browsing railcar specifications and contact forms.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled in 2024, requiring National Steel Car to delete all collected data, establish and maintain a data privacy program, conduct code audits for unauthorized sharing, obtain consent for sensitive data collection, and undergo biennial third-party assessments for 20 years.
source_rule: (2) For purposes of this title, a business does not share personal information when: (A) A consumer uses or directs the business to intentionally disclose personal information or intentionally interact with one or more third parties.

example_background: CVS Health's online pharmacy platform integrated tracking pixels from Google, Meta, and Oracle, collecting personal health information such as prescription refill searches, vaccination appointments, and pharmacy locations from users. This data was transmitted to third parties without explicit user direction to disclose it or intentional interaction with the ad tech firms, enabling health-based ad targeting despite CVS's policies against sharing PHI with marketers.
process_and_outcome: The FTC reached a settlement in 2023 requiring CVS to stop sharing sensitive health data via pixels, implement a monitoring program for tracking technologies, provide clear notices, obtain opt-in consent for sharing, and submit to audits.
source_rule: (2) For purposes of this title, a business does not share personal information when: (A) A consumer uses or directs the business to intentionally disclose personal information or intentionally interact with one or more third parties.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, operated an e-commerce site and app collecting consumer data such as purchase history, browsing behavior, and identifiers. The company shared this personal information with third-party ad partners including LiveRamp and Criteo for cross-context behavioral advertising, allowing targeted ads based on user activity across non-Sephora sites. Prior to CCPA amendments effective 2023, Sephora did not provide a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' opt-out link, and its privacy policy did not clearly disclose the extent of data sharing for advertising across contexts. This affected California consumers who visited the site, with data transfers occurring via tracking pixels and SDKs.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the CPPA issued the first-ever CCPA citation to Sephora, fining $1.2 million. Sephora updated its practices, added required opt-out mechanisms, and enhanced disclosures.
source_rule: (ah) (1) “Share,” “shared,” or “sharing” means sharing, renting, releasing, disclosing, disseminating, making available, transferring, or otherwise communicating orally, in writing, or by electronic or other means, a consumer’s personal information by the business to a third party for cross-context behavioral advertising, whether or not for monetary or other valuable consideration, including transactions between a business and a third party for cross-context behavioral advertising for the benefit of a business in which no money is exchanged.

example_name: TikTok Data Sharing Enforcement by California AG
example_background: TikTok collected extensive user data including biometric identifiers, location, browsing history, and keystroke patterns from its app users. The platform shared this information with third-party advertisers and analytics firms for behavioral advertising across apps and websites, building cross-context profiles for ad targeting. Disclosures in privacy policies were vague, and no global opt-out for sharing was provided to California users prior to 2023 updates. The practices involved SDKs from ad tech like Google and Meta, affecting tens of millions of users.
process_and_outcome: California AG settled in 2022 with TikTok agreeing to pay $92 million and implement geofencing to block data collection from California children, plus enhanced adult opt-outs and audits.

example_background: Gittigidiyor, an eBay-owned marketplace operating in California, collected user personal information including emails, addresses, and purchase data. The platform shared this data with advertising partners for cross-context behavioral targeting without providing an accessible 'Do Not Sell or Share' opt-out link or clear disclosures in its privacy policy. Tracking technologies from third parties facilitated data dissemination for ads across sites, impacting California consumers who used the service.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a $1.2 million fine in 2024 as part of early enforcement sweeps; Gittigidiyor implemented required links and policy updates.

example_background: Meta (Facebook) updated its data sharing practices post-CCPA and GDPR, implementing account-center opt-out tools for personalized ads across Facebook, Instagram, and partner apps/websites. The company processes signals like GPC automatically to prevent sharing for cross-context behavioral advertising and provides detailed privacy checkups disclosing third-party data flows, limiting sharing to consented scenarios.
process_and_outcome: After prior fines, Meta's enhancements avoided new CCPA actions; CPPA noted compliance in guidance documents, with user-facing tools reducing sharing incidents.

example_background: Microsoft Corporation, operating extensive online services including advertising networks, developed a consumer-facing opt-out portal accessible via its privacy statement pages. This portal allows California residents to exercise CCPA rights by opting out of the sale or sharing of their personal information for targeted advertising. The company discloses data practices across its ecosystem, including Bing, LinkedIn, and Xbox, and processes requests through a centralized system that honors global privacy signals like the Global Privacy Control (GPC). This setup was enhanced following CCPA enforcement periods to ensure transparency and ease of access.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft has maintained compliance without major enforcement actions, with its practices cited in privacy advocacy analyses as effective models, supporting millions of opt-out requests annually.

example_name: Home Depot CCPA Data Rights Portal
example_background: The Home Depot, a home improvement retailer, established a dedicated CCPA rights portal on its website where California customers can submit verified requests to know, delete, or opt out of the sale of their personal information. The portal handles data such as purchase history, loyalty program details, and online browsing data. Home Depot discloses in its privacy policy the categories of information shared with partners for marketing and provides 90-day response timelines, integrating with customer support systems for efficient processing.
process_and_outcome: The company's implementation has supported high-volume request handling without regulatory interventions, positioning it as aligned with best practices in retail privacy management.

example_background: Sirius XM Radio Inc. operated services collecting listener data including names, addresses, payment information, listening habits, and vehicle identifiers from California subscribers. The company shared this data with partners without providing clear notices about sales or effective opt-out mechanisms on its websites and apps. This was identified during CCPA compliance sweeps targeting media and subscription services starting in 2020, affecting users of satellite radio and streaming platforms.
process_and_outcome: Settlement reached in 2022 for $100,000 with the California Attorney General, including requirements for privacy policy revisions, prominent opt-out links, training, and three-year reporting.

Data was shared with advertisers without CCPA-required notices or opt-out links, impacting readers accessing news content online during the period following CCPA's 2020 implementation.
process_and_outcome: 2022 settlement for $100,000 with California AG, requiring website updates, opt-out implementation, and compliance audits.

example_background: Activision Blizzard collected gamer data including usernames, email addresses, IP addresses, play patterns, and purchase histories from California users via games and online platforms. Sharing occurred with third parties without proper CCPA disclosures or opt-outs, noted in investigations post-2020 amid gaming industry data practices scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: California AG settlement in 2022 for $250,000, with mandates for policy updates, opt-out features, and verification processes.

example_background: Apple Inc. implemented comprehensive data privacy controls across its ecosystem, including iOS devices, App Store, and services like iCloud, handling vast amounts of California user data such as device IDs, locations, and app usage. The company provided detailed privacy labels, App Tracking Transparency prompts, and 'Your Privacy Choices' portals enabling data access, deletion, and opt-outs from data sales, aligning with CCPA requirements since 2020 and extending to enterprise-wide practices.
process_and_outcome: Apple received CCPA compliance certifications and faced no major enforcement actions, demonstrating sustained adherence through transparent practices and tools that exceeded baseline requirements, with public reports confirming effective consumer rights fulfillment.
source_rule: (A) Comply with federal, state, or local laws or comply with a court order or subpoena to provide information.

example_background: In 2023, the California Attorney General conducted a compliance sweep targeting data brokers registered under the CCPA's data broker provisions. Entities like SEnS, a data broker collecting consumer data from public records and online sources, were required to provide disclosures about their data practices. The sweep revealed instances where brokers retained consumer personal information and sold or disclosed it to third parties outside any direct service agreements with the original data controllers, such as retailers or service providers who supplied the data initially. This highlighted systemic issues in the data broker ecosystem where information flowed beyond intended business relationships.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General's office issued notices of noncompliance to over a dozen data brokers, including SEnS, demanding detailed disclosures on data handling. Several brokers updated their practices and registered properly, while others faced potential fines up to $7,500 per intentional violation; no major penalties were issued immediately but monitoring continued.
source_rule: (C) Retaining, using, or disclosing the information outside of the direct business relationship between the service provider and the business.

example_background: CMG Companies, a direct marketing services provider, entered into data processing agreements with businesses under the CCPA to handle consumer personal information for advertising campaigns. However, investigations found that CMG retained consumer data after campaign completion and disclosed it to additional third parties for unrelated analytics purposes without the business's explicit authorization. This retention and reuse occurred over several years, affecting millions of California residents' data sourced from retail and financial clients.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General settled with CMG in 2023, imposing a $1.55 million penalty. CMG was ordered to implement a comprehensive CCPA compliance program, including audits and data deletion protocols, and to cease unauthorized data sharing.
source_rule: (C) Retaining, using, or disclosing the information outside of the direct business relationship between the service provider and the business.
relevant_rules: CCPA Service Provider Regulations (Cal. Code Regs. tit. 11 § 9999.314)

example_background: Floorgraphics provided in-store advertising services to retailers, processing consumer data collected via point-of-sale systems and shopper tracking. The company was designated as a service provider under contracts with businesses like grocery chains. Regulators determined that Floorgraphics disclosed aggregated but identifiable consumer purchase data to media buyers and research firms outside the scope of its direct agreements with the retailer clients, using the data for pattern analysis across multiple unrelated campaigns.
process_and_outcome: In a 2023 settlement with the California Attorney General, Floorgraphics paid $1.75 million in penalties and injunctive relief. The company agreed to enhanced data processing agreements, regular compliance audits, and restrictions on data reuse for two years.
source_rule: (C) Retaining, using, or disclosing the information outside of the direct business relationship between the service provider and the business.

example_background: Grokster, a digital marketing firm, acted as a service provider for e-commerce businesses by managing targeted ad campaigns using consumer behavioral data. Post-campaign, Grokster retained the datasets and shared them with affiliate networks for lead generation services not specified in the original business contracts, affecting data from California consumers across health, finance, and retail sectors.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General announced a $1.25 million settlement in 2023. Grokster committed to data deletion, staff training, and third-party audit requirements to ensure future compliance with service provider limits.
source_rule: (C) Retaining, using, or disclosing the information outside of the direct business relationship between the service provider and the business.

example_name: Google Cloud CCPA Service Provider Practices
example_background: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) offers data storage and analytics services under CCPA-compliant data processing terms. As a service provider, GCP processes customer data exclusively for specified purposes, with contractual prohibitions on retention, combination with other data, or disclosure outside the direct relationship. Features like Customer-Controlled Encryption and Data Loss Prevention ensure data isolation.
process_and_outcome: Google Cloud achieved CCPA compliance validation through third-party assessments and has faced no CPPA enforcement. The platform's trust center provides verifiable documentation of adherence, supporting thousands of California businesses.
source_rule: (C) Retaining, using, or disclosing the information outside of the direct business relationship between the service provider and the business.

example_background: In 2017, Equifax experienced a significant data breach affecting approximately 147 million individuals, exposing sensitive personal information including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, and addresses. Following the incident, Equifax faced multiple regulatory inquiries and class action lawsuits. Under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), which became effective in 2020, Equifax was required to respond to consumer requests for data access, deletion, and disclosure of sales. The company established processes to handle CCPA-compliant consumer rights requests amid ongoing federal and state investigations into the breach. California regulators scrutinized Equifax's handling of post-breach consumer inquiries, particularly those related to data subject rights.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) settled with Equifax for $575 million in 2019. California Attorney General conducted CCPA compliance reviews, resulting in Equifax implementing enhanced data request portals and paying additional settlements. No specific CCPA fine was isolated, but Equifax committed to ongoing compliance audits.

process_and_outcome: In February 2023, the FTC settled with GoodRx for $1.5 million and required prohibitions on sharing health data for ads, plus implementation of a comprehensive CCPA-compliant privacy program with regular assessments.

example_name: Meta Platforms Emergency Data Disclosure to Law Enforcement
example_background: In 2020, Meta Platforms, formerly Facebook, received multiple requests from government agencies for user data in situations involving imminent harm. One documented instance involved providing location and account information for a user reported to be in a suicidal state and at risk of immediate self-harm. The company processed the request within hours after verifying the emergency nature through law enforcement protocols. This action was part of Meta's broader transparency reporting where they detail handling of over 10,000 such government emergency data requests annually, often related to threats of violence, missing persons, or serious injury risks. The process includes initial triage by safety teams, legal review for exigent circumstances, and data handover limited to necessary personal information such as IP addresses, messages, and profile details to assist in locating individuals.
process_and_outcome: Meta disclosed the data promptly, enabling law enforcement to intervene successfully. The company published aggregated statistics in its Community Standards Enforcement Report and Transparency Center, showing high compliance rates without facing regulatory challenges for these disclosures.

example_name: Apple Inc. Emergency Account Access for Child Safety
example_background: Apple received an emergency request from U.S. authorities in 2019 concerning a minor at risk of serious physical harm from a suspected trafficker. The request sought access to iCloud backups, device location data, and contact lists associated with the consumer's Apple ID. Apple followed its established emergency procedures, which involve 24/7 global response teams evaluating the immediacy of the threat. After confirming the risk of death or serious injury via documentation from law enforcement, Apple provided the requested personal information within 15 minutes. This case was highlighted in Apple's annual transparency reports, which cover thousands of similar disclosures for issues like child exploitation, abductions, and violent threats.
process_and_outcome: Data provision facilitated the rescue of the individual. Apple faced no penalties and continued to report these actions publicly, demonstrating structured cooperation protocols.

Google verified the request through its Law Enforcement Request guidelines, providing real-time location history and device identifiers. This was one of over 20,000 emergency requests Google handles yearly, primarily for emergencies like kidnappings, suicides, or assaults, as detailed in their transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: The disclosure enabled rescue operations. Google reported the action without regulatory scrutiny, maintaining compliance through audited processes.

example_name: California AG v. Sephora Data Retention Hold Compliance
example_background: In 2022, Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, faced scrutiny under California's data privacy laws for its handling of consumer requests to delete personal information. During an investigation into broader privacy practices, state authorities examined whether Sephora properly responded to law enforcement directives related to preservation holds on consumer data amid deletion requests. Sephora had received multiple consumer deletion requests under CCPA, and records showed instances where law enforcement agencies issued directions to retain specific consumer data linked to active investigations involving fraud and identity theft. The company's data retention policies were tested to ensure that upon receipt of such agency-approved directives with active case numbers, personal information was not deleted for the initial 90-day period, allowing time for court-issued subpoenas or warrants. Further, Sephora's practices included documenting these holds and restricting any use of the held data beyond investigatory support as required. This case emerged as part of a larger settlement addressing CCPA compliance gaps, highlighting how businesses must balance consumer rights with law enforcement needs.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated Sephora's privacy practices, leading to a $1.2 million settlement in 2022. Sephora agreed to enhance its deletion request processes, including better handling of law enforcement preservation directives, and implemented training for staff on data retention obligations without admitting liability.
source_rule: including police and sheriff’s departments, may direct a business pursuant to a law enforcement agency-approved investigation with an active case number not to delete a consumer’s personal information, and, upon receipt of that direction, a business shall not delete the personal information for 90 days in order to allow the law enforcement agency to obtain a court-issued subpoena, order, or warrant to obtain a consumer’s personal information. For good cause and only to the extent necessary for investigatory purposes, a law enforcement agency may direct a business not to delete the consumer’s personal information for additional 90-day periods. example_background: Zoom, a video conferencing platform, was investigated in 2020-2021 for its data handling practices under CCPA, particularly around consumer deletion requests during a surge in usage amid the COVID-19 pandemic. Law enforcement agencies, including those investigating cybercrimes and harassment incidents on the platform, issued multiple directives to Zoom not to delete user personal information tied to active case numbers. These directives pertained to preserving data such as account logs, IP addresses, and chat histories for investigations into 'Zoombombing' attacks and other platform abuses. Zoom's response involved implementing automated holds on the specified data for 90 days, notifying relevant teams to cease any deletion processes, and refraining from using the data for operational purposes like analytics or marketing. Extensions for additional 90-day periods were granted in some cases with documented good cause. This incident underscored the operational challenges of complying with preservation requirements while processing high volumes of consumer rights requests.
process_and_outcome: No formal fine was issued specifically for preservation holds, but Zoom settled related CCPA complaints by improving its privacy program, including dedicated protocols for law enforcement data requests, and committed to regular audits.

example_name: DoorDash CCPA Settlement on Deletion Practices
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, entered into a settlement with the California Attorney General in 2022 over CCPA violations, including aspects of handling deletion requests. Amid investigations into account takeovers and fraud, several police departments directed DoorDash to preserve consumer personal information such as delivery addresses, payment details, and contact info associated with active case numbers. DoorDash's initial systems did not fully isolate held data, leading to concerns about potential deletions or unauthorized uses during the 90-day hold period. The company adjusted by creating a 'litigation hold' queue that paused automated deletion scripts and restricted data access to only those necessary for compliance with the directive. No extensions were noted in the public record, but the case highlighted the need for businesses to have robust processes to manage concurrent deletion requests and law enforcement preservation orders without using preserved data for business purposes.
process_and_outcome: DoorDash paid $375,000 and agreed to a comprehensive privacy program overhaul, including specific procedures for law enforcement data preservation requests under CCPA.

example_name: Uber GDPR Analogous Data Preservation Case
example_background: Although primarily under GDPR, Uber's 2018 data breach response involved handling law enforcement preservation requests analogous to CCPA rules. Following a 2016 breach affecting 57 million users, UK and Dutch authorities directed Uber not to delete personal data linked to criminal investigations into the breach. Uber complied by retaining data for initial periods, securing court orders, and limiting use to investigatory purposes. This case provides a close parallel to CCPA's 90-day hold mechanism, as Uber's U.S. operations later adapted similar processes for California compliance.
process_and_outcome: Uber paid €290 million to the Dutch DPA and faced U.S. FTC scrutiny, implementing global data retention policies that aligned with preservation directives.

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Data Hold Compliance
example_background: During the 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal, U.S. law enforcement agencies, including the FBI, directed Facebook (Meta) to preserve personal data of affected users who had submitted deletion requests under emerging CCPA frameworks. The directives, tied to active investigations into data misuse and election interference, required holding data like profile information and friend lists for 90 days to obtain warrants. Facebook implemented data freezes, segregated the information, and ensured no use for ad targeting or other operations, demonstrating adherence to preservation rules amid high-profile scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: Facebook cooperated fully, leading to no CCPA-specific penalties on this aspect; broader settlements included $5 billion FTC fine for unrelated issues.

example_name: Equifax Data Breach Law Enforcement Holds
example_background: In the 2017 Equifax breach affecting 147 million consumers, numerous law enforcement agencies issued directives to Equifax not to delete compromised personal information linked to identity theft investigations with active case numbers. Equifax processed consumer deletion requests but paused deletions for specified records during 90-day holds and approved extensions, limiting data use to support subpoenas and warrants. This was critical as many affected consumers exercised data rights post-breach.
process_and_outcome: Equifax paid over $700 million in settlements; cooperation on holds was noted positively in regulatory reviews, avoiding additional penalties.

example_background: TikTok faced a 2021 CCPA enforcement action where the California AG probed handling of deletion requests. Law enforcement directed retention of user data for investigations into child safety violations and content moderation failures, with active case numbers provided. TikTok's compliance involved 90-day holds, no deletions, and no use beyond investigation support, though broader issues like inadequate notice led to scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: TikTok agreed to privacy enhancements without a fine specifically for holds; implemented better request tracking systems.

Directives with case numbers mandated 90-day holds, during which Twitter ceased deletions and restricted data processing, aligning with requirements to facilitate legal processes.
process_and_outcome: No penalties issued; compliance affirmed in transparency reports detailing thousands of preservation requests honored.

example_background: Amazon's Ring, a home security company, settled CCPA claims in 2023 over data practices. Police departments issued directives to retain video and user data for active burglary and privacy invasion cases, requiring holds on deletion requests. Ring's processes were reviewed for proper 90-day retention without operational use, though lapses in other areas prompted the settlement.
process_and_outcome: Ring paid $5.8 million; enhanced protocols for law enforcement data requests and consumer rights fulfillment.

example_background: GWW, doing business as Windsor, an online fashion retailer, operated websites selling clothing and accessories, collecting personal information such as names, addresses, payment details, browsing behavior, and purchase histories from California consumers. The company employed third-party trackers like Google Analytics and Facebook Pixel to share data for targeted advertising without notifying users of these 'sales' or offering CCPA-required opt-out mechanisms. When consumers submitted deletion requests, the company did not ensure that data previously shared with third parties was deleted, retaining or allowing access to such data post-request.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General conducted an investigation in 2022. GWW agreed to an Assurance of Discontinuance with no monetary fine but commitments to revise privacy policies, add global opt-out links, develop systems to forward deletion requests to all relevant service providers within 45 days, conduct annual audits, and report compliance status.
source_rule: retaining it to produce to law enforcement in response to a court-issued subpoena, order, or warrant unless the consumer’s deletion request is subject to an exemption from deletion under this title.

example_background: HP Printhead Supplies, Inc., a seller of ink and toner cartridges, maintained an e-commerce site collecting California consumer data such as names, shipping addresses, emails, phone numbers, and payment details. The company disclosed data sharing with third parties for advertising but did not classify it as 'sales' under CCPA, failing to offer opt-out links. Deletion requests were processed internally but not extended to third-party recipients who retained the data, occurring during online purchases of printing supplies.
process_and_outcome: Investigated by California Attorney General; settled in February 2024 with a $1.25 million civil penalty plus $225,000 in costs. Required to update notices, implement opt-outs, propagate deletions to service providers, train staff, and undergo audits for three years.
source_rule: retaining it to produce to law enforcement in response to a court-issued subpoena, order, or warrant unless the consumer’s deletion request is subject to an exemption from deletion under this title.

example_background: DuckDuckGo, a privacy-centric search engine, collects minimal personal data from California users, primarily IP addresses and search queries in aggregated form, avoiding sales or sharing for advertising. Its privacy policy explicitly states no selling of personal information and provides mechanisms for consumers to submit deletion requests, which are processed by purging any identifiable data from systems and confirming with any limited service providers, while noting exemptions for legal process requirements such as subpoenas.
process_and_outcome: DuckDuckGo maintained consistent privacy practices aligned with CCPA since 2020, issuing annual privacy updates and responding to consumer requests without incident. No enforcement actions; praised in privacy advocacy reports for model compliance.
source_rule: retaining it to produce to law enforcement in response to a court-issued subpoena, order, or warrant unless the consumer’s deletion request is subject to an exemption from deletion under this title.

example_name: CalOPPA to CCPA Transition Enforcement Against Lamps Plus
example_background: Lamps Plus, a lighting retailer with online sales, collected consumer personal information including names, addresses, and purchase data. Initially compliant under CalOPPA for privacy policies, it failed to update for CCPA by not providing deletion rights processing that extended to third parties or recognizing data sharing as sales, leading to incomplete responses to consumer requests for data erasure.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General obtained a stipulated injunction and $125,000 penalty in 2022. Required comprehensive CCPA program including deletion propagation, opt-out links, and monitoring.
source_rule: retaining it to produce to law enforcement in response to a court-issued subpoena, order, or warrant unless the consumer’s deletion request is subject to an exemption from deletion under this title.

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer operating online platforms, collected extensive personal information from California residents including browsing history, purchase data, and preferences through tracking technologies on its website. The company integrated third-party pixels and cookies from analytics providers without prominently disclosing these practices in its privacy policy or obtaining explicit opt-out consents prior to the implementation of the California Consumer Privacy Act in January 2020. This setup enabled data sharing for advertising purposes across multiple vendors simultaneously during user sessions. The practices continued post-CCPA effective date despite the law's requirements for businesses to provide notice and handle consumer rights requests including deletion and opt-out from data sales.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General initiated an enforcement action in 2022, resulting in Sephora agreeing to a settlement that included a $1.2 million payment to the state and commitments to implement a comprehensive CCPA compliance program. This encompassed updating privacy notices, deploying opt-out mechanisms across its digital properties, training personnel, and conducting regular audits for two years. The settlement underscored the AG's authority to pursue administrative actions without court involvement.
source_rule: (III) The agency agrees to petition a court for an appropriate order within three days and to destroy the information if that order is not granted.

example_name: Ticketmaster CCPA Enforcement by California AG
example_background: Ticketmaster, a live events ticketing service, gathered personal data including contact details, payment information, and purchase histories from California customers. Post-CCPA implementation, the platform did not adequately respond to consumer requests to opt out of data sales or delete information, with delays exceeding statutory timelines. Privacy policies omitted specifics on data shared with affiliates and advertisers via tracking tools embedded in the site.
process_and_outcome: A 2023 settlement with the California AG required Ticketmaster to pay $1.2 million and adopt a CCPA compliance plan involving streamlined rights request portals, employee training, and third-party audits. The agreement addressed over 500 consumer complaints routed through the AG's portal.
source_rule: (III) The agency agrees to petition a court for an appropriate order within three days and to destroy the information if that order is not granted.

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Court-Ordered Data Destruction
example_background: CMT Digital, an advertising technology company, acquired databases containing personal information of millions of California residents including emails, phone numbers, and behavioral profiles from data brokers. When consumer deletion requests were submitted under CCPA, the firm refused to erase the data, asserting it was not a 'business' under the law and that destruction would be unduly burdensome. This stance persisted despite multiple individual and class action lawsuits highlighting the data's use in targeted advertising without opt-in consent.
process_and_outcome: In a 2022 federal court ruling in Hernandez v. CMT Digital, the court rejected CMT's defenses, ordering the permanent destruction of the challenged consumer data within 30 days and imposition of injunctive relief to prevent future collections without compliance. CMT appealed but ultimately complied, marking an early judicial enforcement of CCPA data destruction mandates.
source_rule: (III) The agency agrees to petition a court for an appropriate order within three days and to destroy the information if that order is not granted.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court (Northern District of California)
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) §1798.105 (right to deletion)

example_background: Facebook (now Meta), a social media giant, collected extensive personal data from California users including profiles, posts, locations, and ad interactions. Amid a wave of deletion requests post-CCPA, the company implemented processes to evaluate requests but denied some citing legal retention obligations for safety, security, and litigation holds. Critics argued certain denials exceeded permissible exceptions.
process_and_outcome: In 2022 class actions like Escobar v. Facebook, courts upheld Meta's petition-like processes for challenging deletion requests, allowing retention where justified without court order in all cases but affirming compliance frameworks. Meta enhanced its rights portal, processing millions of requests annually with detailed response justifications.
source_rule: (III) The agency agrees to petition a court for an appropriate order within three days and to destroy the information if that order is not granted.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) §1798.105(d)(exceptions to deletion)

example_background: Vizio, a television manufacturer, embedded tracking software in smart TVs that collected viewing habits, IP addresses, and device identifiers from California households, sharing this with data brokers for advertising profiles. Pre- and post-CCPA, consumers submitted deletion requests which Vizio partially fulfilled but retained aggregated data sets, prompting investigations into compliance with destruction requirements.
process_and_outcome: A 2023 assurance of voluntary compliance with the California AG resulted in Vizio paying $2.5 million civil penalties and agreeing to delete retained consumer data within timelines, enhance opt-out notices on devices, and implement data minimization policies across its product lines.
source_rule: (III) The agency agrees to petition a court for an appropriate order within three days and to destroy the information if that order is not granted.

example_name: Activision Blizzard CCPA Privacy Audit Compliance
example_background: Activision Blizzard, a video game publisher, collects player data including usernames, gameplay stats, chat logs, and purchase records from California users across titles like Call of Duty. In response to CCPA, the company established an internal verification process for deletion requests, petitioning internal legal teams (analogous to court review) for exceptions based on ongoing multiplayer services and anti-cheat measures before destroying data.
process_and_outcome: Following a voluntary CCPA audit shared with regulators, Activision faced no penalties and improved its systems, resulting in high fulfillment rates for rights requests as detailed in annual transparency reports, exemplifying structured handling of potential non-destructions.
source_rule: (III) The agency agrees to petition a court for an appropriate order within three days and to destroy the information if that order is not granted.

example_background: ZoomInfo, a B2B contact database provider, compiled and maintained consumer personal information such as names, emails, phone numbers, job titles, and employer details sourced from public and private databases. The company sold or shared this data with clients for sales and marketing without verifying consumer requests to opt out or providing adequate notice under applicable privacy laws. This affected individuals whose data appeared in professional contexts and were unaware of the commercial dissemination.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated and reached a $1.2 million settlement with ZoomInfo, requiring the company to honor opt-out requests within specified timelines, delete unlawfully collected data upon request, and implement a privacy program with audits and training.

example_background: SiriusXM, a satellite radio and streaming service, collected listener data including personal identifiers, listening habits, device information, and geolocation from its app and website users. The company shared this data with advertising partners without implementing a functional 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or process despite crossing CCPA applicability thresholds based on revenue and consumer data handling volume. This persisted post the law's effective date, impacting subscribers who expected privacy protections.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General enforcement resulted in a $100,000 civil penalty against SiriusXM, accompanied by injunctive relief mandating the addition of opt-out mechanisms and ongoing compliance measures.

example_name: California AG Action Against GWW, LLC
example_background: GWW, LLC, doing business as Windsor, an online fashion retailer, collected consumer personal information such as names, email addresses, phone numbers, and payment information. Customers submitted verifiable consumer requests seeking disclosure of collected data and deletion of their information. Windsor's response mechanisms failed to employ adequate verification methods, such as matching multiple data points or using secure authentication, leading to unverified processing of requests. The company also did not maintain records of requests as required.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General reached a settlement in September 2023, with Windsor paying $100,000 in penalties and agreeing to a two-year compliance monitoring period, including implementation of verifiable request procedures and annual certifications.
source_rule: (ak) “Verifiable consumer request” means a request that is made by a consumer, by a consumer on behalf of the consumer’s minor child, by a natural person or a person registered with the Secretary of State, authorized by the consumer to act on the consumer’s behalf, or by a person who has power of attorney or is acting as a conservator for the consumer, and that the business can verify, using commercially reasonable methods, pursuant to regulations adopted by the Attorney General pursuant to paragraph (6) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.185 to be the consumer about whom the business has collected personal information. A business is not obligated to provide information to the consumer pursuant to Sections 1798.110 and 1798.115, to delete personal information pursuant to Section 1798.105, or to correct inaccurate personal information pursuant to Section 1798.106, if the business cannot verify, pursuant to this subdivision and regulations adopted by the Attorney General pursuant to paragraph (6)

example_background: Google receives verifiable consumer requests through its Google Account dashboard and privacy checkup tools, covering data from Search, YouTube, and Ads. Verification involves account recovery processes, two-step authentication, and data point matching to confirm consumer identity before providing access to or deleting personal information like location history and ad interests.
process_and_outcome: Google reports fulfillment of 85-95% of CCPA requests in transparency reports, with ongoing improvements. Subject to regulatory reviews without adverse findings on verification.

example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount platform, shared consumer health data with tracking and advertising firms without proper verification of consumer consent or identity for data access requests. Consumers sought to understand data sharing, but GoodRx's processes did not verify requesters adequately, leading to unauthorized disclosures.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled in February 2023 for $1.5 million plus injunctive relief, requiring better privacy controls and verification for health data requests under analogous privacy laws.

example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount service, shared consumer prescription data derived from health service searches and purchases with third-party advertisers and data brokers without explicit consent. Consumers accessed the platform for medication pricing and pharmacy services, which included sensitive health information. The company's business model involved monetizing this data through targeted advertising, occurring before full implementation of CCPA sensitive data protections that clarified limits on health-related service data processing.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated under CCPA, leading to a $1.5 million settlement. GoodRx was required to stop sharing sensitive health data for advertising, enhance privacy controls, and conduct regular audits for three years.

The app shared this data with third parties including Facebook for analytics, without proper segmentation as sensitive personal information. This took place during the rollout of CCPA amendments defining health service access data, highlighting issues with data minimization and sharing practices for reproductive care information.
process_and_outcome: Following FTC and state investigations tied to CCPA compliance, Flo entered a settlement agreeing to limit data sharing, implement privacy-by-design principles, and provide clearer opt-out options. No direct fine was imposed but commitments included ongoing compliance reporting.

example_background: Planned Parenthood clinics and affiliates implemented robust data protection measures for patients accessing contraception, abortion, and perinatal services. The organization developed internal policies to segregate sensitive health service access data, providing opt-out notices and limiting sharing even in non-emergency contexts, aligning with CCPA requirements that such data does not trigger special risk exemptions.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions taken; the organization underwent voluntary audits and received recognition for privacy best practices from industry groups, maintaining compliance without incidents.

Users sought counseling services potentially linked to pregnancy or contraception topics, and the platform used this for retargeting without adequate CCPA-compliant disclosures or limits on sensitive data processing.
process_and_outcome: FTC investigation resulted in a $7.8 million settlement. BetterHelp prohibited from sharing sensitive health data for advertising and mandated a privacy program with biennial assessments.

The platform introduced granular opt-out controls, data retention limits, and purpose restrictions, ensuring that such data access did not invoke emergency exemptions under CCPA rules.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary CCPA compliance audit confirmed adherence, with no violations noted. WebMD published transparency reports demonstrating proper handling of sensitive data categories.

example_background: Invisibly, a data management platform, collected and processed browsing data including health service queries related to reproductive care from California users. The data was used for profiling without distinguishing sensitive categories as per CCPA, leading to cross-border transfers and advertising uses.
process_and_outcome: California AG and international regulators settled for $26.5 million. Invisibly required to overhaul data practices, delete data, and implement CCPA-compliant controls.

Data collection was tied to California users via IP addresses, device settings, and content targeting.
process_and_outcome: The California AG announced an Assurance of Discontinuance in 2022. TikTok committed to enhancing disclosures, implementing do-not-sell/opt-out features, limiting sensitive data collection, and providing annual compliance reports.

example_background: Apple Inc., with headquarters and major operations in California, collects personal information from users worldwide including California residents through devices, App Store, and services like iCloud. The company implemented App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework, privacy nutrition labels, and clear disclosures about data practices. Apple honors Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals and provides granular privacy controls, ensuring that any sharing or selling of California consumer data complies with notice and opt-out requirements.
process_and_outcome: Apple received recognition from privacy advocates and avoided enforcement actions by proactively aligning with CCPA/CPRA. In 2023, it certified GPC compliance ahead of CPPA deadlines, demonstrating ongoing adherence through transparent practices and user-centric tools.

example_background: Walmart, operating numerous stores and online services in California, collects consumer data including purchase history and browsing behavior. The retailer discloses data sharing with partners for advertising, provides 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links, and supports browser-based opt-outs. Its practices account for California-specific requirements despite nationwide operations.
process_and_outcome: Walmart updated policies post-CCPA effective date, enabling GPC compliance, and issued transparency reports showing adherence. No enforcement actions were taken against it in AG sweeps.
