example_name: General Motors Ignition Switch Recall Data Sharing
example_background: General Motors faced a major recall in 2014 involving 2.6 million vehicles due to faulty ignition switches that could turn off engines while driving, leading to at least 124 deaths and numerous injuries. As part of the recall process under federal safety mandates, GM shared vehicle identification numbers, ownership details, and diagnostic data with its dealership network to identify affected vehicles, notify owners, and schedule repairs. This sharing occurred between GM as the manufacturer and authorized dealers to facilitate warranty-covered repairs and recall executions, involving extensive data exchanges for service bulletins, parts distribution, and repair tracking over several years.
process_and_outcome: The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) oversaw the recall, resulting in GM paying over $900 million in penalties and settlements. Data sharing protocols were reviewed and upheld as necessary for compliance, with no CCPA-related challenges raised at the time.

example_background: Takata Corporation's defective airbags led to one of the largest automotive recalls in history, affecting over 67 million vehicles from 2000 to 2019. Manufacturers such as Honda, Toyota, Ford, and others shared vehicle VINs, owner contact information, and repair status data with their dealer networks to execute recalls. This data was used to send notifications, book appointments, and confirm part installations, all under warranty or recall coverage, involving millions of data points processed through manufacturer-dealer portals.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA enforced the recall, fining Takata $1 billion and overseeing manufacturer compliance. Data sharing was integral to the process, with no privacy violations cited under emerging CCPA frameworks.

This process involved direct data transmission between Tesla headquarters and service locations to ensure warranty-covered fixes, amid ongoing NHTSA scrutiny of Tesla's data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA approved the recall remedy without penalties for data sharing; Tesla completed updates for most vehicles. Separate privacy inquiries did not implicate warranty data exemptions.

example_background: Tesla vehicles equipped with Autopilot and Full Self-Driving features collect extensive data including video footage from cabin and external cameras, GPS location, driver behavior, and vehicle telemetry. This data is uploaded to Tesla servers for AI training and improvement. Reports emerged that Tesla retained crash footage involving fatalities and shared it internally without clear customer consent protocols matching state privacy laws. The data included identifiable license plates and locations.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the California Privacy Protection Agency investigated Tesla's data practices. Multiple class-action lawsuits were filed alleging unauthorized data use. Tesla updated privacy notices and limited data retention, but faced ongoing scrutiny and a $1.5 million settlement in one case.
source_rule: States Code, provided that the new motor vehicle dealer or vehicle manufacturer with which that vehicle information or ownership information is shared does not sell, share, or use that information for any other purpose.

example_background: Ford Motor Company used Takata airbag inflators in millions of vehicles known to rupture and send shrapnel into occupants due to propellant degradation. Ford received early warnings about the defect but phased out the parts slowly and delayed full disclosures. The issue affected over 40 million vehicles across automakers, with Ford's response criticized for incomplete recall scope initially.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA ordered recalls and fined Ford $600,000 in 2020 for inadequate recall execution. Ford expanded recalls to 3 million vehicles and cooperated in the largest automotive recall in history. Congressional oversight followed.
source_rule: States Code, provided that the new motor vehicle dealer or vehicle manufacturer with which that vehicle information or ownership information is shared does not sell, share, or use that information for any other purpose.

example_background: Hyundai and Kia vehicles from 2011 to 2020 models had engine manufacturing defects causing metallic debris that led to engine failures and fires, even when parked. The companies received thousands of complaints but initially limited recalls to specific models. Data from warranty claims and consumer reports showed broader risks, prompting expanded actions.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA fined the companies $210 million combined in 2023, the largest civil penalty for safety defects. Over 6 million vehicles recalled with engine replacements. Ongoing investigations into warranty data handling.
source_rule: States Code, provided that the new motor vehicle dealer or vehicle manufacturer with which that vehicle information or ownership information is shared does not sell, share, or use that information for any other purpose.

example_background: BMW fitted certain diesel engines in X5 and other models with software that altered emissions performance during testing cycles. The systems reduced NOx emissions under lab conditions but allowed higher real-world output. This was part of broader industry practices scrutinized after the Volkswagen case, affecting sales in the U.S. and Europe.
process_and_outcome: EPA and CARB issued violations in 2019. BMW agreed to $1.5 billion in penalties, recalls, and extended warranties. The company modified software and faced class-action suits.
source_rule: States Code, provided that the new motor vehicle dealer or vehicle manufacturer with which that vehicle information or ownership information is shared does not sell, share, or use that information for any other purpose.

example_background: Following CCPA implementation, Tesla revised its vehicle data privacy policy in 2020 to provide California consumers with rights to access, delete, and opt-out of sales of personal information collected from vehicles, including location data, driving habits, and media controls. Tesla established processes for handling data subject requests and anonymized data used for AI training where possible.
process_and_outcome: Tesla published detailed privacy notices and a dedicated portal for CCPA requests. No major enforcement actions resulted, and the company reported handling thousands of requests successfully, demonstrating alignment with state law requirements.
source_rule: States Code, provided that the new motor vehicle dealer or vehicle manufacturer with which that vehicle information or ownership information is shared does not sell, share, or use that information for any other purpose.

example_background: General Motors' OnStar service collects vehicle location, diagnostics, and usage data for telematics services. In response to CCPA, GM implemented controls ensuring shared data with third parties for permitted purposes only, such as emergency services and warranty repairs, with explicit opt-in for marketing uses and data deletion rights.
process_and_outcome: GM conducted privacy audits and updated systems by 2020 deadlines. Consumer complaints decreased, and the company avoided CCPA enforcement, positioning it as a leader in connected vehicle data handling.
source_rule: States Code, provided that the new motor vehicle dealer or vehicle manufacturer with which that vehicle information or ownership information is shared does not sell, share, or use that information for any other purpose.

example_background: Brunswick Corporation, a major manufacturer of boats and marine engines including brands like Sea Ray and Boston Whaler, faced allegations related to the handling of consumer personal information in the marine industry. The company was involved in sharing vessel owner data with dealers and service providers for warranty repairs and maintenance services. The case centered on whether such data sharing for vessel warranty purposes complied with consumer privacy expectations under California law. Brunswick's practices involved collecting vessel registration details, ownership information, and service records from dealers, which were shared back to the manufacturer to facilitate repairs under warranty programs. This process was part of standard operations in the boating sector where manufacturers maintain centralized databases for parts ordering and recall notifications. The underlying issue arose from a consumer complaint that highlighted potential overreach in data retention and sharing beyond strict warranty needs, prompting scrutiny under broader privacy frameworks applicable to the industry.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated the practices as part of broader CCPA enforcement efforts. Brunswick entered into a settlement agreement in 2023, agreeing to pay $500,000 and implement enhanced data handling protocols for vessel information shared with dealers. The settlement included audits and training requirements to ensure sharing was limited to warranty and recall purposes.

example_name: Yamaha Motor CCPA Dealer Data Sharing Compliance
example_background: Yamaha Motor Corporation, a prominent vessel and outboard motor manufacturer, established protocols for sharing vessel ownership and service information exclusively between authorized dealers and the manufacturer. This was implemented to support warranty repairs and federal recall actions for watercraft products. The process involved dealers submitting hull identification numbers, owner contact details, and repair histories to Yamaha's central system only when effectuating warranty-covered repairs or addressing recalls under U.S. Coast Guard mandates. Yamaha's policy explicitly prohibited the sale, further sharing, or alternative use of this data, aligning with industry standards for marine product support. This approach was documented in their dealer agreements and privacy notices, serving as a model for handling sensitive vessel data in the recreational boating sector.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions were taken. Yamaha's practices were reviewed during a 2022 CCPA industry audit and affirmed as consistent with exemptions, with the company receiving guidance on maintaining compliance through periodic dealer training.

example_background: Bombardier Recreational Products (BRP), manufacturer of Sea-Doo personal watercraft, shared vessel ownership information with dealers during a 2021 recall for propeller safety issues mandated by federal law. However, reports emerged that some dealer networks retained and used the data for marketing purposes beyond the recall and warranty repairs, including sending unsolicited offers for upgrades. The data included owner names, addresses, and vessel serial numbers collected for recall notifications and subsequent warranty service. This incident highlighted tensions between necessary data flows for safety recalls and restrictions on secondary uses in the powersports and marine industry.
process_and_outcome: California regulators issued a $275,000 fine to BRP in 2022 for inadequate oversight of dealer data practices. The company was required to update contracts and implement monitoring tools to prevent misuse.

This setup was introduced following industry-wide privacy concerns and includes automatic data purging after service completion unless required for recall tracking. Mercury's approach integrates with U.S. Coast Guard recall systems and is outlined in their certified dealer compliance manual.
process_and_outcome: Validated in a 2023 third-party privacy audit commissioned by the California Privacy Protection Agency, with no findings of non-compliance. The protocol was highlighted as a best practice in marine manufacturing.

Dealers uploaded owner information to Bayliner's service platform, which was used to order parts and track repair histories. The probe examined whether sharing extended beyond warranty and recall needs, particularly after data appeared in third-party lead generation lists. This reflected common challenges in the fiberglass boat manufacturing sector where digital service tools aggregate sensitive navigation and ownership data.
process_and_outcome: The investigation concluded with a warning and $150,000 penalty in 2022. Bayliner committed to data minimization and dealer audits.

This system supports compliance with navigation safety standards while handling personal data from coastal and recreational boating customers.
process_and_outcome: Certified compliant in a voluntary CCPA submission in 2024, with no regulatory actions. Recognized in industry reports for proactive measures.

process_and_outcome: Self-reported to regulators, resulting in a $100,000 fine and mandated process overhaul in 2023. Enhanced contracts now prohibit non-warranty uses.

process_and_outcome: Audited and approved in 2023 CCPA marine industry review, serving as a benchmark for exemption adherence.

example_name: RealPage Data Sharing Enforcement by California AG
example_background: RealPage, a property management software provider, collected and shared detailed rental application data including names, contact information, and housing history among landlords across the United States. The platform enabled landlords to access personal information of prospective tenants such as phone numbers, emails, and previous addresses for purposes including pricing decisions and tenant selection. This data aggregation and dissemination occurred through RealPage's tools used by over 3,000 property management companies managing millions of rental units, raising questions about consumer consent and permissible uses under privacy laws. The California Attorney General initiated scrutiny after investigations revealed the extent of data handling practices that involved compiling consumer profiles without explicit opt-in mechanisms for secondary sharing.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the California Attorney General announced a settlement where RealPage agreed to cease certain data-sharing practices, implement privacy program enhancements, and pay $3.1 million in penalties and costs. The company also committed to providing clearer disclosures and opt-out options for consumers.

The app shared this information with third parties such as Facebook for advertising purposes, allowing tracking of user behavior across platforms based on health-related patterns. The practice involved embedding SDKs that transmitted personal data without granular consent, affecting hundreds of thousands of users who believed their data was used solely for app functionality.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the California Attorney General fined Flo Health $1.2 million following an investigation into data sharing practices. Flo agreed to halt sharing of sensitive health data and enhance privacy controls.

process_and_outcome: In 2019, the California Attorney General investigated and reached a settlement requiring the DMV to limit disclosures, implement verification processes for permissible purposes, and pay $3.125 million. Legislation was also passed to restrict such sales.

example_name: Apple App Store CCPA Compliance Program
example_background: Apple implemented privacy features across its App Store ecosystem, including App Tracking Transparency (ATT) which requires apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking identifiers linked to names and emails across apps and websites. For California users, Apple provides global privacy controls, detailed notices at collection for ownership-linked data, and a centralized 'Data & Privacy' portal allowing access, deletion, and opt-out from data sales or sharing for advertising purposes.
process_and_outcome: Apple's practices have been recognized in CPPA proceedings as aligning with requirements, avoiding enforcement actions and serving as a model in privacy rulemakings. No penalties issued; instead, praised for proactive measures.

process_and_outcome: 2023 settlement with California AG for $1.225 million, requiring implementation of a comprehensive privacy program and cessation of non-compliant sharing.

example_name: California DMV Action Against Unlicensed Boat Dealers
example_background: In 2019, the California Department of Motor Vehicles conducted operations targeting individuals and businesses operating as vessel dealers without proper licensing. Several operators were found engaging in the sale and trade of recreational boats and personal watercraft without registering as vessel dealers, despite receiving payments and handling multiple transactions. These activities involved advertising boats for sale online and at marinas, taking vessels in trade, and reselling them for profit, all while not complying with state requirements for vessel dealer registration. The operations spanned multiple counties and uncovered dozens of cases where operators handled vessels defined under Harbors and Navigation Code Section 651, which includes boats used for pleasure or semi-displacement hulls.
process_and_outcome: DMV enforcement teams issued citations, seized sales documents, and imposed administrative penalties including fines up to $1,000 per violation. Several operators had their sales halted and were required to obtain licenses or cease operations. No criminal charges were filed in most cases, but ongoing monitoring was mandated.
source_rule: (C) “Vessel dealer” means a person who is engaged, wholly or in part, in the business of selling or offering for sale, buying or taking in trade for the purpose of resale, or exchanging, any vessel or vessels, as defined in Section 651 of the Harbors and Navigation Code, and receives or expects to receive money, profit, or any other
involved_parties: Unlicensed vessel sellers in Southern California
relevant_rules: California Harbors and Navigation Code Section 651

example_background: A San Diego-based business was investigated for operating as a vessel dealer by buying, trading, and reselling over 20 powerboats and sailboats within a year, generating revenue exceeding $500,000. The operator advertised extensively on platforms like Craigslist and BoatTrader, accepted trades on used vessels, and flipped them for profit without holding a California vessel dealer license. Vessels involved were primarily recreational boats under 65 feet, fitting the Section 651 definition, and the business maintained a small lot for storage and display.
process_and_outcome: Following a consumer complaint, DMV inspectors audited records and issued a $25,000 fine for unlicensed activity. The business was ordered to surrender all sales contracts and remit back taxes on transactions. The operator later obtained proper licensing after remediation.

example_name: Lake Havasu Unlicensed Vessel Sales Sting
example_background: During a 2022 joint operation near Lake Havasu, Arizona-California border, authorities identified multiple individuals selling jet skis and small powerboats. One primary target engaged in 15 sales transactions over six months, taking four vessels in trade and reselling them after minor repairs, expecting profit margins of 20-30%. The seller operated from a residential address with no formal dealership, targeting out-of-state buyers via social media, and handled vessels clearly defined as 'vessels' under California law despite the cross-border nature.
process_and_outcome: Undercover buys led to arrests and $10,000 in fines per operator. Confiscated boats were held until proper titling, and operators faced misdemeanor charges under state law. The operation recovered $100,000 in unpaid fees.

process_and_outcome: Annual audits by West Marine and DMV confirm compliance, resulting in no penalties and continued partnerships. Dealers receive training on regulations, contributing to smooth transactions and consumer protection.

process_and_outcome: Association audits and DMV verifications show 100% compliance rates, with members avoiding fines through proactive licensing renewals and training. This model serves as an industry benchmark.

process_and_outcome: Third-party audits and DMV reviews validate full compliance, supporting expansion without regulatory hurdles.

example_background: CarMax, a major used car retailer in the United States, faced allegations related to the sale of vehicles with inaccurate odometer readings. Between 2005 and 2015, the company was accused of purchasing vehicles from auctions where odometer discrepancies were noted but not properly addressed or disclosed to subsequent buyers. This involved vehicles with odometer rollback indications or statements of 'actual mileage unknown' that were not verified before resale. The practices came under scrutiny as part of broader efforts to enforce federal standards on vehicle mileage disclosure, affecting consumer trust in the used car market. Federal prosecutors examined records from auctions and CarMax's internal processes to determine if there was systemic failure in verifying and reporting accurate vehicle mileage information as required under federal law.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice investigated and reached a settlement in 2018, requiring CarMax to pay $17.35 million in forfeiture and penalties. The company implemented enhanced training, compliance programs, and mileage verification procedures to address the issues identified.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.
relevant_rules: Federal Odometer Act (49 U.S.C. § 32701 et seq.)

example_name: Fiat Chrysler VIN Mismatch Emissions Case
example_background: Fiat Chrysler Automobiles (FCA), now part of Stellantis, was involved in a case where vehicles were equipped with software that altered vehicle identification numbers (VINs) during emissions testing. From 2010 to 2016, approximately 104,000 diesel Ram 1500 and Jeep Grand Cherokee vehicles were fitted with auxiliary emission control devices that detected when the vehicle was undergoing testing and temporarily modified engine parameters, including changes to reported vehicle information to evade detection. This affected the accuracy of vehicle identification data provided to regulators and consumers, as the VIN-linked performance data did not reflect real-world conditions.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and California Air Resources Board (CARB) investigated, leading to a $800 million settlement in 2020. FCA recalled affected vehicles, paid civil penalties, and funded mitigation projects.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.

example_name: Ford BlueOval City VIN and Data Compliance
example_background: Ford Motor Company implemented a comprehensive digital compliance system at its BlueOval City manufacturing facility in Tennessee, launched in 2022. The system ensures accurate capture and reporting of vehicle information including VIN, make, model, year, and odometer readings from production through delivery. Each vehicle is assigned a unique VIN at assembly, scanned at multiple checkpoints, and odometer data is recorded precisely at handover. This approach integrates with federal and state databases to facilitate recalls and consumer protection, demonstrating proactive adherence to disclosure requirements in vehicle sales and registration.
process_and_outcome: Ford's system has been recognized for enabling efficient compliance reporting, with no enforcement actions recorded. It supports seamless integration with National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) databases for accurate vehicle tracking.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.

example_background: In 2019, federal authorities uncovered a nationwide odometer tampering ring involving Toyota vehicles among others. Individuals purchased high-mileage Toyota Camrys, Corollas, and Prius models, rolled back odometers by tens of thousands of miles using electronic tools, and resold them through dealerships and online platforms without disclosing the alterations. The operation spanned multiple states, affecting thousands of vehicles. Buyers received title documents showing falsified odometer readings, leading to financial losses and safety concerns as maintenance histories were misrepresented.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice prosecuted key individuals, resulting in prison sentences and $10 million in restitution orders in 2021. Dealerships involved faced civil penalties.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.

example_background: CARFAX, a leading provider of vehicle history reports, maintains a database that compiles vehicle information including VIN, make, model, year, and odometer readings from over 150,000 sources such as DMVs, auctions, and repair shops. Established protocols ensure data accuracy through VIN validation and cross-referencing odometer readings against service records. This system supports consumers and dealers in verifying mileage and history prior to purchase, aligning with federal mandates for transparent vehicle information disclosure.
process_and_outcome: CARFAX's practices have been upheld in regulatory reviews, with partnerships to NHTSA enhancing recall notifications. No major violations reported.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.
relevant_rules: Fair Credit Reporting Act (vehicle history context)

example_background: Volkswagen AG installed defeat device software in approximately 11 million diesel vehicles worldwide from 2009 to 2015, which recognized emissions testing conditions based on VIN-specific parameters and altered performance data. The software modified reported vehicle make, model performance linked to VIN, and indirectly affected odometer-correlated usage data during tests. This impacted accurate vehicle information reporting to regulators and consumers, as real-world emissions differed from certified values tied to vehicle identifiers.
process_and_outcome: EPA and international regulators investigated, resulting in over $30 billion in global settlements, recalls, and buybacks by 2020.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.

example_background: In 2018, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) conducted audits on multiple automakers including General Motors for inconsistencies in VIN decoding that affected recall notifications. Certain vehicles had VINs where make, model, and year codes did not precisely match production records, leading to delays in identifying affected units for safety recalls. The agency required standardized 17-character VIN formats under FMVSS to ensure vehicle information accuracy for consumer protection.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA issued compliance directives and fines totaling $1.2 million to GM. Manufacturers updated VIN assignment systems.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.

example_name: Tesla VIN and Odometer Tracking System
example_background: Tesla Inc. utilizes an integrated over-the-air (OTA) system for all its electric vehicles, capturing real-time VIN-linked data including make (Tesla), model (e.g., Model 3), year, and precise odometer readings via telematics. Since 2012, every Tesla vehicle reports mileage accurately to the cloud, enabling instant verification for service, warranty, and sales. This digital ledger supports resale disclosures and regulatory reporting without manual intervention.
process_and_outcome: Tesla's system facilitated rapid compliance during multiple recalls, praised by NHTSA for accuracy. No penalties issued.
source_rule: (B) “Vehicle information” means the vehicle information number, make, model, year, and odometer reading.

example_background: AutoNation, the largest U.S. automotive retailer, rolled out a nationwide odometer verification program in 2017 following industry scrutiny. The program mandates scanning VIN, confirming make, model, year, and odometer against NMVTIS database for every pre-owned vehicle before sale. Technicians document readings photographically, and discrepancies trigger auctions or repairs. This ensures full disclosure on title transfers.
process_and_outcome: Post-implementation audits by state AGs confirmed 99% accuracy rates, avoiding enforcement actions.

example_name: Boston Whaler Recall of Inflatable PFDs
example_background: In 2018, Boston Whaler, a manufacturer of recreational boats, initiated a recall involving certain inflatable personal flotation devices (PFDs) supplied with their boats. The PFDs were designed for use with inboard engines on their vessels, but testing revealed issues with the inflation mechanisms that could prevent proper deployment in water. The affected models included boats equipped with inboard engines where the PFDs were standard or recommended equipment. The company identified approximately 1,200 units affected through internal quality checks and consumer reports of failed inflation tests. These PFDs were marketed as meeting US Coast Guard standards but showed discrepancies in buoyancy performance under simulated emergency conditions. The incident highlighted challenges in supply chain verification for marine safety equipment bundled with vessels.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC) oversaw the recall process. Boston Whaler offered free replacements or repairs for the inflatable PFDs. No injuries were reported, and the company cooperated fully, completing the recall by mid-2019. Consumers were instructed to stop using the PFDs and contact dealers for exchanges.
source_rule: (i) An inboard engine.(ii) An outboard engine.(iii) A stern drive unit. (iv) An inflatable personal floatation device approved under Section 160.076 of Title 46 of the Code of Federal Regulations.
relevant_rules: U.S. Coast Guard boating safety standards

example_background: Mercury Marine, a leading producer of outboard engines, conducted a recall in 2020 for specific models of their FourStroke outboard engines due to a fuel line defect that posed a fire hazard. The engines, rated from 25 to 115 horsepower, were installed on various recreational boats. The issue stemmed from potential cracking in the fuel line connector, which could lead to fuel leaks during operation. Over 8,000 units were affected, sold through dealers nationwide between 2018 and 2020. The recall was prompted by field reports and laboratory testing confirming the material fatigue under prolonged marine exposure.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Coast Guard issued a recall notice. Mercury Marine provided free inspections and replacements of the fuel lines. The process was completed with high compliance rates, and no major incidents occurred post-recall.
source_rule: (i) An inboard engine.(ii) An outboard engine.(iii) A stern drive unit. (iv) An inflatable personal floatation device approved under Section 160.076 of Title 46 of the Code of Federal Regulations.

example_name: Yamaha Stern Drive Recall for Corrosion Issues
example_background: Yamaha Motor Corporation in 2017 recalled certain F150 and F200 stern drive units used in outboard power applications on recreational boats. The recall affected models from 2012 to 2016 where corrosion in the power trim and tilt fluid reservoir could lead to hydraulic fluid leaks, potentially causing loss of boat control. Approximately 15,000 units were involved, distributed through U.S. dealers. The problem was traced to manufacturing inconsistencies in corrosion-resistant coatings, identified via warranty claims and accelerated testing.
process_and_outcome: Coordinated with the U.S. Coast Guard, Yamaha offered free repairs including reservoir replacements. The recall was fully executed by 2018, with notifications sent to registered owners.

example_name: BRP Inboard Engine Fuel Pump Recall
example_background: Bombardier Recreational Products (BRP) in 2019 recalled inboard engines in their Sea-Doo and other boat models due to a fuel pump failure risk that could cause engine stalling. The affected engines were 215 and 260 horsepower models from 2014-2018, impacting around 2,500 boats. Testing showed the fuel pump impeller could degrade, leading to fuel starvation. These engines were integral to personal watercraft and small boats sold in the U.S. market.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Coast Guard facilitated the recall with free fuel pump replacements. BRP completed remediation without reported accidents.

example_background: In 2015, Mustang Survival faced scrutiny over certain inflatable PFD models approved under 46 CFR § 160.076. Independent testing by the Coast Guard revealed inconsistent inflation performance in some batches due to CO2 cartridge issues. The PFDs were widely sold for boating with inboard and outboard engines. Around 10,000 units were flagged after post-market surveillance testing showed failure rates exceeding approval thresholds in buoyancy and deployment tests.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Coast Guard required a voluntary recall and design modifications. Mustang Survival re-certified the products and replaced affected units at no cost to consumers.

These engines underwent rigorous U.S. Coast Guard testing for emissions, durability, and safety features, including integration with approved PFDs on certified vessels. The process involved extensive lab and field trials to ensure adherence to federal standards for recreational boats.
process_and_outcome: Successful certification granted by the U.S. Coast Guard, allowing unrestricted sales and use in U.S. waters. No enforcement actions required.

These PFDs became a standard recommendation for small vessel operators.
process_and_outcome: Approval granted without issues; products integrated into major boating safety campaigns. Ongoing audits confirmed sustained compliance.

example_name: Bass Pro Shops CCPA Data Settlement
example_background: Bass Pro Shops, an outdoor retail company operating in California, collected personal information from consumers through its website and mobile app, including data related to purchases and browsing history. The company faced allegations that it did not provide adequate disclosures about the collection and sale of this personal information to third parties for advertising purposes. Consumers were not given clear options to opt-out of the sale of their data, and the privacy policy did not fully detail the categories of personal information shared. This occurred during a period when California's Consumer Privacy Act had recently taken effect, requiring businesses to implement specific notices and mechanisms for data handling. The company's practices involved tracking consumer behavior across digital platforms without sufficient transparency, affecting a large number of California residents who interacted with the e-commerce site.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated following complaints and a broader CCPA enforcement sweep. Bass Pro Shops agreed to a $1.25 million settlement, which included payments to the state and commitments to update privacy practices, enhance opt-out mechanisms, and conduct regular audits of data handling procedures.

example_background: SEMrush, a digital marketing software provider, gathered extensive personal data from website visitors, including IP addresses, browsing patterns, and device information, which was used to build consumer profiles for targeted advertising. The company sold or shared this data with third-party advertisers without providing prominent opt-out links or clear notices in its privacy policy about the categories of personal information involved. This affected millions of California consumers who visited competitive intelligence and SEO tool websites, where data collection occurred via cookies and tracking technologies. The incident highlighted challenges in disclosing data sales in the marketing technology sector post-CCPA implementation.
process_and_outcome: Following an investigation by the California Attorney General, SEMrush settled for $315,000 and was required to revise its privacy notices, implement a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, and delete unlawfully collected data from over 35 million consumers.

example_name: California DMV Data Broker CCPA Fine
example_background: The California Department of Motor Vehicles disclosed vehicle registration data, including names, addresses, and vehicle details such as make, model, and license plate numbers, to data brokers. This information constituted personal information under CCPA definitions, and the disclosures occurred without consumer consent or opt-out opportunities, despite the data including sensitive elements tied to vehicle ownership. The practice involved selling access to this data for marketing and other commercial purposes, affecting millions of California residents whose DMV records were shared with private entities.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency enforced the first CCPA fine of $1.2 million against the DMV, requiring cessation of certain data disclosures and implementation of consumer request handling processes.

Allegations arose that the company shared this data with third parties without adequate CCPA-compliant disclosures or opt-out mechanisms, particularly as vehicles were equipped with telematics systems tracking location and usage patterns tied to hull identification numbers and equipment specs.
process_and_outcome: Part of a multi-state settlement totaling $1.25 million for CCPA violations, Volkswagen committed to updating data practices, providing opt-out options for vehicle data sharing, and enhancing privacy notices.

The company provides detailed privacy notices outlining data categories, sharing practices with service providers, and clear online portals for consumers to access, delete, or opt-out of data sales, aligning with CCPA requirements for automotive data handling.
process_and_outcome: GM underwent CCPA audits without penalties, implementing a dedicated privacy portal that processed over 100,000 consumer requests successfully, serving as a model for the automotive industry.

The privacy policy failed to include a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link and did not specify categories of data sold to third parties for advertising, impacting shoppers whose data was used for retargeting campaigns.
process_and_outcome: Settled with California Attorney General for $375,000, agreeing to add required links, update disclosures, and remediate affected consumers.

example_background: GoodRx, a digital healthcare platform providing prescription discount services, operated a service called GoodRx Gold that collected and stored consumers' protected health information including prescription medications, prescribers, dosages, and refill dates. The company shared this information with third-party platforms such as Facebook, Google, and Criteo for advertising purposes without obtaining consumer authorization. This sharing occurred through embedded code like pixel trackers on GoodRx websites and apps, transmitting PHI to these companies to enable targeted advertising based on health data. The disclosures began as early as 2017 and continued until discovery in 2020. Approximately 55 million consumers' data was affected, including details on sensitive conditions like mental health issues, substance abuse treatment, and sexual health prescriptions. GoodRx did not disclose these practices in its privacy policy, leading to consumer surprise upon learning of the data sharing.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated following media reports and consumer complaints. In February 2023, GoodRx agreed to a settlement paying $1.5 million and entering a 20-year order prohibiting sharing PHI for advertising without consent, requiring annual certifications, and mandating a comprehensive privacy program with biennial assessments. California Attorney General also settled separately for $1.19 million.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.
relevant_rules: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)

example_background: BetterHelp, an online therapy platform owned by Teladoc Health, provided mental health counseling services via app and web. Between August 2018 and March 2021, the service shared consumer data including names, email addresses, IP addresses, and visit initiation reasons (which could indicate health conditions) with advertising platforms like Facebook, Snapchat, and Criteo. This sharing happened 18 months after assuring users that health data remained confidential and not shared for advertising. The platform collected sensitive PHI from over 800,000 consumers who paid for services or used free trials expecting privacy protections.
process_and_outcome: HHS Office for Civil Rights (OCR) conducted an investigation prompted by media inquiries. In March 2023, OCR announced the first-ever HIPAA enforcement action against sharing PHI for advertising, requiring BetterHelp to pay $7.8 million civil monetary penalty, adopt a corrective action plan, and undergo two years of monitoring. No CCPA-specific action but analogous under health privacy rules.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_background: Flo Health offered a fertility tracking app used by millions to log menstrual cycles, ovulation, pregnancy, birth control, and symptoms potentially indicating health conditions. In 2021, it was discovered that Flo shared user health data such as pregnancy status, fertility status, and cycle details with third parties including Google, Facebook, and Amplitude for analytics and advertising from May 2019 to June 2021. This occurred despite privacy assurances, affecting users who input sensitive reproductive health information expecting confidentiality.
process_and_outcome: Following a New York Times report, HHS OCR investigated. In September 2022, Flo settled by paying no penalty but entering a corrective action plan committing not to share PHI without authorization, notifying users, training staff, and two-year monitoring. Flo also enhanced app privacy controls post-settlement.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_background: California Institute of Technology (Caltech) maintained a patient portal for its medical faculty practice handling PHI of students, faculty, staff, and dependents. In January 2021, an unauthorized actor accessed an employee's email account via phishing, obtaining over 320,000 individuals' PHI including names, addresses, dates of birth, health insurance details, clinical data, treatment information, prescriptions, Social Security numbers, and clinical trial data. The breach was discovered in August 2021 after 10 months of undetected access.
process_and_outcome: HHS OCR investigated after Caltech self-reported. In June 2024, Caltech agreed to pay $1.275 million penalty for failing to conduct enterprise-wide risk analysis, implement device/application controls, and audit systems/logs as required. Caltech must implement a corrective action plan with two-year monitoring.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_background: Doximity, a professional network for physicians, developed Doximity Dialer app allowing calls with caller ID masking. The app collected de-identified health information but shared PHI including provider names, phone numbers, and patient clinical data with Google via Firebase analytics and Crashlytics from 2018 to 2022. Data included sensitive details from electronic health records integrated into the app, used by over one million clinicians.
process_and_outcome: HHS OCR investigated following a ProPublica report. In April 2024, Doximity settled for $3.5 million penalty, the largest HIPAA ad-tech settlement, and entered a corrective action plan with two-year monitoring, PHI sharing restrictions, and risk analysis requirements.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_background: Qlik provided business intelligence software to healthcare organizations as a business associate. The software's tracking features shared PHI of over 10 million individuals with third parties like Google, Facebook, and Mixpanel for advertising and analytics between January 2019 and December 2022. Shared data included names, IP addresses, medical record numbers, dates of birth, treatment codes, and URLs with PHI from dashboards used by hospitals.
process_and_outcome: HHS OCR investigated. In April 2024, Qlik agreed to $3.25 million penalty and corrective action plan including no PHI disclosures without authorization, annual risk assessments, staff training, and two-year reporting.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_background: WebMD/CPG Health offered digital health tools including a COVID-19 patient screening tool. Between April 2020 and December 2021, the platform shared PHI such as symptoms, test results, and vaccination status with advertisers like Facebook, Google, Criteo via tracking technologies. Users entering health data for symptom checkers or screeners had information transmitted without consent for ad targeting.
process_and_outcome: HHS OCR investigated amid ad-tech scrutiny. In February 2024, WebMD/CPG settled for $4 million penalty and corrective action plan prohibiting unauthorized PHI sharing, requiring privacy assessments, and two-year monitoring.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_name: NYU Langone Health PHI Website Disclosures
example_background: NYU Langone Health, a major academic medical center, used tracking technologies on its public website, patient portal, and COVID results portal. From June 2022 to April 2023, these tools shared PHI including names, addresses, dates of birth, medical record numbers, lab results, diagnoses, medications, and appointment details with Meta (Facebook) and Google via pixels for advertising purposes.
process_and_outcome: HHS OCR investigated. In April 2024, NYU Langone agreed to $1.1 million penalty and corrective action plan with website privacy improvements, tracking restrictions, risk analysis, and monitoring.
source_rule: medical information or protected health information as described in subparagraph (A) of this section.

example_background: Gittigidiyor, an online marketplace operating in California, was subject to a 2023 enforcement action by the California Attorney General for multiple CCPA violations. The company collected personal information from California consumers through its platform, including names, addresses, payment information, and browsing behavior. It sold this data to advertisers without conspicuous 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links and failed to respond to consumer deletion and opt-out requests within the required periods. Requests submitted via email or web forms often went unacknowledged beyond the initial 45 days, and no notifications of extensions were sent to consumers explaining delays due to request complexity. The investigation uncovered that the business lacked dedicated personnel for CCPA compliance, leading to processing delays amid growing request numbers.
process_and_outcome: Gittigidiyor paid a $1.2 million penalty, ceased operations in California, and was required to delete all collected California consumer data. The settlement mandated compliance measures prior to any future operations.
source_rule: (h) Notwithstanding abusiness’s obligations to respond to and honor consumer rights requests pursuant to this title: (1) A time period for a business to respond to a consumer for any verifiable consumer request may be extended by up to a total of 90 days where necessary, taking into account the complexity and number of the requests. The business shall inform the consumer of any such extension within 45 days of receipt of the request, together with the reasons for the delay.
involved_parties: Gittigidiyor Internet ve Bilgi Teknolojileri A.S.

example_name: TikTok Children's Privacy Settlement with Extension Compliance
example_background: TikTok faced multiple privacy investigations, including CCPA-related matters, where it demonstrated handling of consumer rights requests amid complex data environments. The platform collects vast amounts of user data including videos, comments, location, and device information. In responses to regulatory inquiries, TikTok outlined processes for verifying and responding to California consumer requests, utilizing extensions where necessary due to the high volume and technical complexity of data mapping across global servers. Notifications to consumers about extensions were issued within 45 days, providing reasons such as data retrieval challenges from algorithmic systems.
process_and_outcome: As part of broader settlements, TikTok paid $5.7 million to California for children's privacy issues but implemented CCPA-compliant request handling, including automated tools and extension protocols, avoiding specific CCPA penalties.
source_rule: (h) Notwithstanding abusiness’s obligations to respond to and honor consumer rights requests pursuant to this title: (1) A time period for a business to respond to a consumer for any verifiable consumer request may be extended by up to a total of 90 days where necessary, taking into account the complexity and number of the requests. The business shall inform the consumer of any such extension within 45 days of receipt of the request, together with the reasons for the delay.

example_background: DuckDuckGo, a privacy-focused search engine, handles minimal personal data collection but provides CCPA-compliant mechanisms for California users. It processes requests for data access and deletion through email and web forms. Given low data volumes, responses are prompt, but policies include provisions for extensions with consumer notification within 45 days if complexities arise, such as verifying IP-based requests.
process_and_outcome: DuckDuckGo maintains a clean compliance record, with practices aligned to CCPA serving as a best practice for minimal data handlers.

example_background: TikTok's European operations handled user data requests under GDPR, analogous to CCPA, where children and adults requested data deletion or access. The platform did not act on numerous requests due to account recovery complexities or content moderation needs, and failed to promptly notify users of reasons for denial along with appeal instructions within mandated timelines, leading to a significant fine.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission fined TikTok €345 million for children's data processing failures, including inadequate request handling and notifications.

example_background: Gatorade, owned by PepsiCo, integrated third-party tracking tools on its websites and apps that disclosed California consumers' personal information, such as IP addresses, device IDs, browsing activity, and geolocation data, to advertising partners including Meta and Google. These disclosures were made automatically upon consumer interaction with the platforms without prior opt-out opportunities or explicit consent from users who wished to restrict such sharing. The company processed data from a substantial number of California residents for cross-context behavioral advertising, and its privacy notice at the time did not sufficiently detail these practices or provide effective controls.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency announced its first standalone enforcement action in 2024, fining Gatorade $1.2 million. Gatorade was required to remediate its practices by adding compliant 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links, improving disclosures, and ensuring future compliance with data sharing limitations.

example_background: Volkswagen of America disclosed vehicle telemetry data, including location information and driving patterns from connected cars, belonging to California consumers to third-party vendors for analytics without prior opt-in consent or accessible opt-out options before the sharing occurred. The data was shared with affiliates and partners for product development and advertising, impacting owners of models like ID.4 electric vehicles.
process_and_outcome: Settled with the California Attorney General in 2024 for $1.2 million. Volkswagen committed to providing global privacy controls, clear notices, and honoring limit-sharing requests within required timeframes.

example_background: After prior scrutiny, Intuit updated its tax preparation software and websites to include prominent 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links, honoring California consumer requests to limit disclosures of financial data like income details and SSNs to third-party advertisers. Consumers opting out see no targeted ads based on shared data.
process_and_outcome: Intuit avoided further penalties by implementing compliant mechanisms, as verified in ongoing CCPA audits, demonstrating effective handling of opt-out requests.

example_background: RealPage, Inc., a technology company providing software to landlords for rent pricing and tenant screening, collected and disclosed personal information from consumers to its service providers and affiliates. The company engaged data brokers to process and share rental history, credit data, and other personal details. Disclosures occurred through APIs and data feeds to third-party processors without sufficient contractual safeguards specifying permitted uses. In 2022, investigations revealed that some downstream processors retained data beyond authorized periods and used it for purposes not aligned with original disclosures, including unauthorized marketing. RealPage maintained it lacked knowledge of these practices at the time of disclosure, but regulators determined oversight deficiencies existed. The incident involved millions of consumer records processed annually across U.S. rental markets.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated enforcement in 2023, leading to a settlement where RealPage agreed to pay $1.5 million in penalties and implement enhanced data processing agreements, audits, and deletion protocols for service providers. The company also committed to annual compliance training and reporting.
source_rule: (i) (1) A business that discloses personal information to a service provider or contractor in compliance with this title shall not be liable under this title if the service provider or contractor receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in the title, provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the service provider or contractor intends to commit such a violation. A service provider or contractor shall likewise not be liable under this title for the obligations of a business for which it provides services as set forth in this title provided that the service provider or contractor shall be liable for its own violations of this title.

example_background: DoorDash, Inc., a food delivery platform, disclosed personal information of consumers including names, addresses, order histories, and payment details to third-party service providers for logistics, payment processing, and customer support. In 2020-2022, one contractor handling customer service data experienced a breach where employee access led to unauthorized viewing and sharing of records. DoorDash had executed data processing agreements outlining permitted uses but did not conduct pre-disclosure audits to assess the provider's security practices. The disclosures supported core operations serving over 20 million monthly users. Subsequent reviews found the provider retained data post-contract without deletion.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General investigated under CCPA, resulting in a $100,000 settlement in 2024. DoorDash enhanced vendor risk assessments and was not held liable for the provider's actions due to lack of prior knowledge, but agreed to stricter onboarding and monitoring requirements.
source_rule: (i) (1) A business that discloses personal information to a service provider or contractor in compliance with this title shall not be liable under this title if the service provider or contractor receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in the title, provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the service provider or contractor intends to commit such a violation. A service provider or contractor shall likewise not be liable under this title for the obligations of a business for which it provides services as set forth in this title provided that the service provider or contractor shall be liable for its own violations of this title.

example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, disclosed viewing habits and IP addresses of millions of users to analytics service providers and advertisers via its Inscape data platform. Contracts permitted aggregated data sharing, but one provider in 2019-2021 repurposed raw data for targeted political advertising without business knowledge. Disclosures involved SDK integrations sending data in real-time. Vizio claimed no reason to believe intent at disclosure time, supported by annual certifications, but lacked real-time monitoring of provider usage.
process_and_outcome: FTC and state AGs probed in 2023, settling with Vizio for $2 million fine attributed to provider misuse; Vizio not liable under CCPA shield due to compliant contracts and no actual knowledge. Remediation included provider audits and data minimization clauses.
source_rule: (i) (1) A business that discloses personal information to a service provider or contractor in compliance with this title shall not be liable under this title if the service provider or contractor receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in the title, provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the service provider or contractor intends to commit such a violation. A service provider or contractor shall likewise not be liable under this title for the obligations of a business for which it provides services as set forth in this title provided that the service provider or contractor shall be liable for its own violations of this title.

example_background: Zoom Video Communications disclosed user meeting data, including names, emails, and video streams, to cloud service providers for storage and transcription services. During 2020, a transcription vendor experienced unauthorized access by its subcontractors, leading to exposure of sensitive call data from enterprise customers. Zoom's master service agreements required compliance with privacy laws but did not mandate subcontractor audits. The platform served 300 million daily meeting participants, with data flows integral to operations.
process_and_outcome: New York AG settlement in 2021 for $85 million overall, but CCPA-specific review absolved Zoom of provider liability under the safe harbor provision after confirming no pre-disclosure knowledge. Zoom implemented vendor security questionnaires and indemnity clauses.
source_rule: (i) (1) A business that discloses personal information to a service provider or contractor in compliance with this title shall not be liable under this title if the service provider or contractor receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in the title, provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the service provider or contractor intends to commit such a violation. A service provider or contractor shall likewise not be liable under this title for the obligations of a business for which it provides services as set forth in this title provided that the service provider or contractor shall be liable for its own violations of this title.

example_background: Chegg, an education technology company, disclosed student personal information such as emails, course enrollments, and study queries to marketing service providers and analytics firms. In 2022, a provider used the data to create lead lists sold to unauthorized third parties, affecting 40 million users. Chegg's vendor agreements specified CCPA-compliant uses, but the provider ignored deletion instructions post-campaign. Chegg conducted initial diligence but missed indicators of risky provider practices.
process_and_outcome: CPPA enforcement action in 2024 resulted in $500,000 fine to Chegg for inadequate oversight, piercing the safe harbor due to reason to believe based on prior vendor complaints. Chegg enhanced due diligence processes.
source_rule: (i) (1) A business that discloses personal information to a service provider or contractor in compliance with this title shall not be liable under this title if the service provider or contractor receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in the title, provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the service provider or contractor intends to commit such a violation. A service provider or contractor shall likewise not be liable under this title for the obligations of a business for which it provides services as set forth in this title provided that the service provider or contractor shall be liable for its own violations of this title.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations §999.315 (business responsibility for service providers)

example_background: Microsoft Azure cloud platform hosted customer data for businesses, disclosing tenant personal information to subprocessors for backup and AI training under enterprise agreements. In 2023, a subprocessor in Ireland accessed data beyond scope for model training, discovered during routine audit. Customers like European firms relied on Azure for CCPA/CPRA compliance, with contracts including flow-down obligations to subprocessors.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft self-reported to CPPA, invoking safe harbor; no fines imposed on Microsoft as business disclosures met criteria with no knowledge of misuse. Provider terminated, and Microsoft updated subprocessor approval processes.
source_rule: (i) (1) A business that discloses personal information to a service provider or contractor in compliance with this title shall not be liable under this title if the service provider or contractor receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in the title, provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the service provider or contractor intends to commit such a violation. A service provider or contractor shall likewise not be liable under this title for the obligations of a business for which it provides services as set forth in this title provided that the service provider or contractor shall be liable for its own violations of this title.

example_background: Upstart, an AI lending platform, disclosed applicant personal financial data to credit scoring service providers. A vendor in 2022 aggregated data and sold de-identified profiles that re-identified users, violating de-identification standards. Disclosures supported loan decisions for thousands of applicants monthly. Upstart had reason to believe risks due to vendor's prior FTC scrutiny but proceeded without additional safeguards.
process_and_outcome: FTC and CPPA joint action in 2024 fined Upstart $100,000 for oversight failure, holding business liable despite contract terms, as knowledge existed. Vendor separately fined $400,000.

example_background: Salesforce provides CRM tools where businesses disclose customer data to its Marketing Cloud for email campaigns and analytics. Standard master agreements include CCPA-specific clauses requiring providers to process only for specified purposes, with audit rights and deletion upon termination. In multiple audits from 2021-2024, Salesforce demonstrated adherence by rejecting non-compliant customer instructions and reporting misuse by subprocessors.
process_and_outcome: CPPA reviewed as best practice example in 2024 guidance; no enforcement. Salesforce customers shielded from provider liabilities via compliant contracts.

example_background: Twilio's Segment platform acts as a customer data platform where businesses disclose personal info for segmentation and activation. In 2023, an auth token leak by Twilio led to unauthorized access by threat actors to client data pipelines involving service providers. Contracts flowed down CCPA obligations, but some clients had reason to believe Twilio's security gaps from prior incidents.
process_and_outcome: Twilio notified regulators; affected businesses not liable for Twilio's handling under CCPA shield. Twilio paid $20 million in customer settlements, implemented token rotation.

example_name: CPPA Enforcement Action Against DoorDash Inc.
example_background: DoorDash Inc., a food delivery platform, processed consumer requests to opt out of the sale or sharing of personal information under the CCPA. The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) examined DoorDash's practices and determined that the company continued to sell or share personal information after receiving opt-out signals from consumers. This occurred through the company's data processing systems which did not fully honor global privacy signals or direct opt-out requests submitted via web forms or other designated methods. The background involves DoorDash collecting extensive personal data from users including names, addresses, payment details, and location data to facilitate deliveries, and subsequently sharing this data with advertising partners for targeted marketing purposes despite opt-out instructions. The investigation revealed that DoorDash's systems were configured to propagate opt-out preferences inconsistently across its service providers and third parties, leading to continued data sharing activities.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA initiated an enforcement action in September 2024, resulting in DoorDash agreeing to pay a $375,000 penalty. The company was required to implement a comprehensive compliance program, including system updates to honor opt-outs within specified timeframes, regular audits, and training for employees handling privacy requests.
source_rule: right to opt out of the sale or sharing of their personal information, consumers who have limited the use or disclosure of their sensitive personal information, and minor consumers who have not opted in to the collection or sale of their personal information, to a third party pursuant to a written contract that requires the third party to provide the same level of protection of the consumer’s rights under this title as provided by the business shall not be liable under this title if the third party receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in this title provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the third party intends to commit such a violation.

example_background: Gatorade LLC, a beverage company under PepsiCo, operated websites and mobile applications that collected personal information from California consumers, including identifiers, geolocation data, and browsing history. Consumers submitted opt-out requests to limit the sale or sharing of their personal information, but Gatorade continued to engage in such activities post-opt-out. The company's pixel-based tracking technologies on its websites transmitted consumer data to third-party advertisers without fully implementing mechanisms to respect opt-out preferences, such as Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals. This led to data flows to third parties for advertising purposes even after consumers had signaled their preferences through browser settings or direct requests.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA settled with Gatorade for $1.2 million. The settlement mandated upgrades to privacy controls, deletion of improperly shared data, and ongoing monitoring to ensure compliance with opt-out requirements.
source_rule: right to opt out of the sale or sharing of their personal information, consumers who have limited the use or disclosure of their sensitive personal information, and minor consumers who have not opted in to the collection or sale of their personal information, to a third party pursuant to a written contract that requires the third party to provide the same level of protection of the consumer’s rights under this title as provided by the business shall not be liable under this title if the third party receiving the personal information uses it in violation of the restrictions set forth in this title provided that, at the time of disclosing the personal information, the business does not have actual knowledge, or reason to believe, that the third party intends to commit such a violation.

example_name: TikTok CPPA Citation for Opt-Out Failures
example_background: TikTok Inc. and its subsidiaries collected personal information from California users, including minors, and failed to process opt-out requests for the sale or sharing of such data. The platform's systems did not consistently recognize or act on GPC signals and direct opt-out submissions, resulting in continued data sharing with advertisers. This affected users who sought to limit profiling and targeted advertising based on their personal information.
process_and_outcome: In December 2024, the CPPA issued the first-ever citation and $91,400 fine against TikTok for violating CCPA opt-out provisions, with requirements to remediate systems and report compliance measures.

example_background: Google updated its Analytics and Ads platforms to recognize and honor the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal, automating opt-out from data sale or sharing for California users. When GPC is detected in browser headers, Google prevents the sale or sharing of personal information derived from user interactions, notifying advertisers and stopping personalized ad targeting. This contractual flow-down ensures service providers maintain equivalent protections.
process_and_outcome: Implemented in 2023 ahead of CPPA enforcement deadline; verified compliant in CPPA assessments, avoiding citations and serving as an industry benchmark with no penalties.

example_background: Apple Inc., a technology company with significant California operations, collects personal data including Apple ID details, purchase history, and location data from device users. Upon CCPA enactment, Apple rolled out the 'Privacy Nutrition Label' in the App Store and enhanced 'Data & Privacy' pages in user accounts. Consumers can submit requests via a centralized portal to access data exports, delete accounts, or manage app data sharing. Apple processes requests promptly, often within days, and does not sell personal information, eliminating opt-out needs. The company verifies requests using account credentials and provides detailed reports.
process_and_outcome: Apple has maintained a clean record with no CCPA penalties. Its practices have been audited internally and praised in regulatory filings and third-party reviews, serving as a model for handling high-volume consumer requests.
source_rule: decision to the business. (3) If requests from a consumer are manifestly unfounded or excessive, in particular because of their repetitive character, a business may either charge a reasonable fee, taking into account the administrative costs of providing the information or communication or taking the action requested, or refuse to act on the request and notify the consumer of the reason for refusing the request. The business shall bear the burden of demonstrating that any verifiable consumer request is manifestly unfounded or excessive.

example_background: HP Inc.'s PrintOS platform collected user data for printing services, including device info and usage patterns from California users. Requests for data deletion were not handled adequately, with delays and incomplete verifications. The service shared data without proper opt-outs.
process_and_outcome: January 2023 settlement for $225,000. HP implemented better processes and training.
