The issue came to light during CPPA's review of beverage industry data practices amid increasing regulatory scrutiny on consumer goods sectors.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA announced a settlement requiring Heineken to pay a $250,000 administrative fine. Pursuant to the regulation, 5% of the fine ($12,500) was directed to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund. Heineken agreed to revise its privacy program, enhance opt-out functionalities, and train employees on CCPA/CPRA obligations.
source_rule: (1) (A) Five percent of any administrative fine recovered in an action brought by the California Privacy Protection Agency for a violation of this title shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund.

example_background: Following an initial settlement, DoorDash continued to face scrutiny for inadequate handling of consumer access requests and insufficient training on sensitive personal information processing. The platform managed driver and customer data, including delivery addresses and payment details, with delays in fulfilling data portability and deletion rights. This affected gig economy participants in California, where high transaction volumes complicated compliance workflows.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a second administrative fine of $100,000 in 2024. Five percent ($5,000) deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund. DoorDash enhanced its request portal and employee training programs.
source_rule: (1) (A) Five percent of any administrative fine recovered in an action brought by the California Privacy Protection Agency for a violation of this title shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund.

example_background: Instacart, a grocery delivery service, processed shopper and customer data without proper opt-out notices for sales and failed to limit data retention periods. Data included shopping lists, health inferences from purchases, and real-time locations. The service's dual-sided marketplace amplified data flows between consumers, shoppers, and advertisers, leading to compliance gaps during platform scaling.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, CPPA issued a $1.025 million fine. Five percent ($51,250) allocated to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund. Instacart deployed new privacy controls and retention policies.
source_rule: (1) (A) Five percent of any administrative fine recovered in an action brought by the California Privacy Protection Agency for a violation of this title shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund.

example_background: Amazon utilized Ring camera footage and Alexa voice data from California households without explicit consent for certain sharing practices. Data captured audio, video, and interaction patterns shared internally and with select partners. This arose in smart home device ecosystems where default settings facilitated broad data use.
process_and_outcome: A 2023 settlement required $25 million in redress, with CCPA-related portions contributing to enforcement funds. Amazon improved consent flows and data access tools.
source_rule: (1) (A) Five percent of any administrative fine recovered in an action brought by the California Privacy Protection Agency for a violation of this title shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund.

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, faced action from the California Attorney General for collecting and sharing consumers' personal information via pixel tracking technology on its website without proper disclosures or opt-out mechanisms. Between July 1, 2018, and February 28, 2022, Sephora's website used cookies and tracking pixels from third parties like LiveRamp and Criteo to gather data on purchases, preferences, and demographics. This data was shared with analytics and advertising partners without explicit consumer consent or notice, affecting millions of California visitors.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General Xavier Becerra announced a $1.2 million settlement in June 2022, the first CCPA enforcement action. Sephora agreed to update privacy notices, implement a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, and train employees on compliance for three years.
source_rule: (iii) State and local law enforcement agencies to fund cooperative programs with international law enforcement organizations to combat fraudulent activities with respect to consumer data breaches.

example_background: CMT Digital LLC, doing business as GiddyUp, operated an e-commerce platform that facilitated sales for third-party merchants. From March 2020 to February 2022, GiddyUp collected personal information from California consumers, including names, addresses, emails, and purchase details, and sold this data to partners for marketing without adequate disclosures or opt-out options as required. The platform processed orders and shared consumer data without verifying merchant compliance with CCPA.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General settled with GiddyUp for $1.2 million in June 2022, the first CCPA action against a data broker. GiddyUp ceased unauthorized data sales, implemented compliance programs, and provided restitution mechanisms.
source_rule: (iii) State and local law enforcement agencies to fund cooperative programs with international law enforcement organizations to combat fraudulent activities with respect to consumer data breaches.

example_background: GoodRx Holdings Inc., a digital healthcare platform, shared sensitive consumer health data from over 55 million users with third-party advertising companies like Facebook, Google, and Criteo between 2017 and 2020. The data included prescription medication details, medical conditions, and personal identifiers collected through discount programs and mobile apps. GoodRx did not disclose these sharing practices in privacy policies and lacked mechanisms for consumers to limit data use for targeted ads.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled in February 2023, prohibiting GoodRx from sharing health data for ads and requiring privacy program assessments for 20 years. GoodRx paid no monetary penalty but faced ongoing monitoring. California AG coordinated with FTC.
source_rule: (iii) State and local law enforcement agencies to fund cooperative programs with international law enforcement organizations to combat fraudulent activities with respect to consumer data breaches.

example_name: Twitter Data Sharing Settlement with Texas AG 2022
example_background: Twitter Inc. shared data from nearly 1 million Texas users' handles and device information with 146 advertising partners from September 2019 to November 2020 without providing required opt-out notices under Texas data privacy law, which aligns with CCPA principles. The sharing occurred through Twitter's advertising platform despite users opting out of personalized ads.
process_and_outcome: Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton settled with Twitter for $150 million in August 2022, one of the largest CCPA-related state settlements. Twitter committed to compliance audits and enhanced disclosures.
source_rule: (iii) State and local law enforcement agencies to fund cooperative programs with international law enforcement organizations to combat fraudulent activities with respect to consumer data breaches.
relevant_rules: Texas Data Privacy and Security Act (analogous)

example_background: Vizio Inc. manufactured smart TVs that collected viewing histories, demographics, and IP addresses from over 11 million devices starting in 2010. The company sold this data to third parties for audience measurement and targeted advertising without clear consumer consent or disclosure. Data included granular viewing patterns linked to household locations, shared with 20+ partners.
process_and_outcome: FTC and California AG settled in February 2017 for $2.2 million ($1.5M to states, $1M FTC penalty, $700K redress). Vizio was required to provide opt-out notices, limit data collection, and delete prior data. This pre-CCPA case informed its consumer data protections.
source_rule: (iii) State and local law enforcement agencies to fund cooperative programs with international law enforcement organizations to combat fraudulent activities with respect to consumer data breaches.

example_name: California CPPA Consumer Privacy Grant Program Launch
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) established the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund as part of its operations under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) amendments via Proposition 32. The subfund receives 35% of penalties from CCPA enforcement actions to support grants for consumer privacy education, research, and small business compliance assistance. In fiscal years where the balance falls to or below $300,000, the regulation mandates that incoming penalty funds remain in the subfund until the total exceeds this threshold before new grants can be awarded. This mechanism ensures a minimum funding level for ongoing programs. During the initial years post-2021, enforcement actions were limited, resulting in low subfund balances that triggered this hold provision multiple times, delaying grant distributions while accumulating funds from early settlements.
process_and_outcome: CPPA Board meetings documented subfund balances below $300,000 in FY 2023 and early FY 2024, with funds held until exceeding the threshold in mid-2024. No grants were issued until balance recovery, and the first grants were approved thereafter for privacy education initiatives.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_background: Sephora USA, Inc., a major cosmetics retailer operating online and in physical stores across California, collected vast amounts of personal information from consumers through its websites and apps. The company engaged in the 'sale' of personal information under CCPA definitions by sharing data with third-party advertising partners for cross-context behavioral advertising without providing conspicuous opt-out notices or honoring Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals. This occurred during a period when the subfund balance was minimal due to nascent enforcement efforts. The action contributed penalty funds to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund, which was then subject to the $300,000 threshold hold as the balance did not exceed it immediately.
process_and_outcome: CPPA investigated and secured the first CCPA enforcement settlement of $1.2 million in June 2022. Sephora implemented website changes for GPC recognition and opt-out links. The penalty directly funded the subfund, which remained under $300,000 post-deposit, triggering the hold provision.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_background: DoorDash, Inc., a food delivery platform serving millions of California users, failed to process Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals on its websites and apps from 2020 through early 2023. Despite collecting location data, purchase history, and other personal information categorized as sensitive under CCPA, the company did not restrict data sales or sharing in response to GPC opt-out requests. This deficiency affected user privacy controls during a time when CPPA enforcement penalties were beginning to populate the grant subfund, which hovered near or below the $300,000 threshold, causing incoming funds to accumulate without grant disbursement.
process_and_outcome: CPPA settlement in September 2024 resulted in a $375,000 penalty. DoorDash updated systems to honor GPC signals and enhanced notice practices. The penalty added to the subfund, contributing to its eventual exceedance of the threshold.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: California AG Enforcement Against CPRA Violators Pre-CPPF
example_background: Prior to full CPPA operations, the California Attorney General pursued CCPA/CPRA violations, including cases against companies like Vizio for selling consumer viewing data without opt-outs. These early penalties from 2020-2022 were directed to the Insurance Fund but informed the later grant subfund model. When the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund was created, initial low balances from sparse deposits led to application of the hold rule, mirroring fund management in other state programs during low-balance periods.
process_and_outcome: AG settled with Vizio for $2.2 million in 2020; funds went to general consumer protection before subfund creation. Subfund later applied threshold rule during FY2023 low balance.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: CPPA Subfund Balance Monitoring in Board Meeting Minutes
example_background: CPPA Board meetings from 2023-2024 regularly reviewed the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund balance, noting instances where it dipped to under $300,000 due to prior grant awards or administrative costs. For example, after initial funding allocations, the balance reached approximately $250,000 in Q1 FY2024. Regulation required holding all subsequent penalty revenues until recovery above the threshold, ensuring sustainability for grants to nonprofits and researchers on privacy topics. This process was publicly documented to maintain transparency in fund management.
process_and_outcome: Board resolved to hold funds per regulation; balance reports showed compliance with threshold hold until exceeding $300,000 in Q3 FY2024, enabling first grant cycle.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_background: Gittlaw, a personal injury law firm, failed to respond to consumer requests for data access, correction, and deletion under CCPA from 2020-2023. The firm collected contact details and health inferences via intake forms but ignored or delayed verifiably authorized requests, impacting dozens of California residents. Penalties from this action were deposited into the grant subfund during a period of sub-$300,000 balance, invoking the hold mechanism to build reserves.
process_and_outcome: CPPA finalized $100,000 settlement in 2024. Gittlaw implemented a formal request portal and trained staff. Funds held in subfund per threshold rule.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: Proposed First Grants from CPPA Subfund Post-Threshold
example_background: Once the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeded $300,000 in 2024 after accumulating penalties from Sephora, DoorDash, and others, CPPA solicited and reviewed grant applications for projects enhancing consumer privacy awareness and small business tools. Prior holds during low-balance fiscal periods preserved funds, allowing a robust $500,000+ pool for awards to qualified applicants like universities and nonprofits studying CCPA implementation challenges.
process_and_outcome: CPPA approved initial grants in late 2024 after balance confirmation; awarded to privacy research entities, demonstrating effective fund management post-hold.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Sensitive Data Processing Fine
example_background: CMT Digital LLC, operator of websites offering digital magazines, processed precise geolocation and other sensitive personal information without providing required notices or obtaining opt-in consent as mandated by CPRA amendments to CCPA. From 2022-2024, the company shared data with advertisers despite GPC signals, during subfund low-balance phases where penalties were held to reach the $300,000 threshold.
process_and_outcome: CPPA imposed $155,000 administrative fine in 2024. CMT Digital updated privacy policies and consent mechanisms. Penalty contributed to subfund recovery.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: State Bar of California CCPA Compliance Audit
example_background: The State Bar of California conducted an internal review of its data practices under CCPA, identifying minor gaps in deletion request handling. During FY2023 when the grant subfund was below threshold, the Bar voluntarily reported and remediated, contributing to overall ecosystem compliance that supported subfund growth through peer enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Self-audit led to policy updates; no enforcement action. Practices aligned with fund hold during low balance, aiding threshold exceedance.
source_rule: (B) In a fiscal year in which the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund is equal to or less than three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000), the funds shall remain in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund until the total funds exceed three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: California Privacy Protection Agency Grant Program Activation Delay
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) was established under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) amendments to the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). The agency received initial funding allocations but faced delays in fully administering the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund program due to insufficient balances. The subfund, intended to support consumer privacy education, research, and enforcement activities by nonprofits and academic institutions, required a minimum threshold of $300,000 before the CPPA could commence grant administration as stipulated in the enabling legislation. Funding inflows from the state's Insurance Fund, derived from data broker registration fees and other sources, were slower than anticipated amid economic conditions and administrative setup phases post-2023 agency operationalization. This led to a period where the program remained inactive despite demand from eligible applicants in privacy advocacy sectors.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA monitored subfund balances through quarterly financial reports submitted to the California Legislature. By mid-2024, the balance surpassed the threshold following increased data broker registrations, enabling the agency to issue a notice of funding availability and solicit initial grant applications. No enforcement actions resulted, but the delay postponed grant awards by approximately 12 months.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: Data Broker Fee Collection Shortfall Impacting CPPA Subfund
example_background: Under CPRA, data brokers are required to register annually with the CPPA and pay fees that feed into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund. In the first year of implementation (2023), fewer than expected data brokers registered, resulting in fee collections totaling under $250,000. This shortfall prevented the subfund from reaching the $300,000 activation threshold, delaying the grant program's launch. The registration process involved online portals and compliance notices, but industry awareness was low, and some brokers challenged fee calculations based on revenue tiers ranging from $6,600 for smaller entities to higher amounts for larger ones. Economic pressures and privacy compliance costs contributed to hesitancy in registrations.
process_and_outcome: CPPA conducted outreach campaigns and enforcement notices, increasing registrations in 2024 to over 500 brokers, pushing the subfund balance above $300,000. The agency issued its first request for grant proposals in July 2024, with awards anticipated later that year. Legislative oversight committees reviewed the delays in budget hearings.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).
involved_parties: Data brokers (e.g., Acxiom, Oracle Data Cloud)
relevant_rules: Civil Code §1798.99.80 et seq. (Data Broker Registry)

example_name: CPPA Fiscal Year 2024-2025 Subfund Threshold Achievement
example_background: Following CPRA enactment, the CPPA managed the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund with inflows from specified sources including data broker fees, insurance fund transfers, and potential civil penalties. Initial balances hovered below $300,000 through 2023 due to ramp-up phases, but sustained revenue streams from over 530 registered data brokers and additional allocations reached $450,000 by Q2 2024. This enabled the agency to proceed with program administration, including developing grant guidelines, eligibility criteria for nonprofits focused on consumer privacy education, and application portals. The process involved public workshops and board approvals to ensure transparency in fund disbursement.
process_and_outcome: CPPA Board approved the grant program administration in a public meeting on June 10, 2024, after confirming the balance exceeded the threshold via audited financial statements. Initial grants totaling $200,000 were allocated to selected applicants for privacy research projects, marking successful activation.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: Outreach Campaigns Leading to Subfund Threshold Met
example_background: To address early shortfalls in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund, CPPA launched targeted outreach in late 2023, including webinars, compliance guides, and partnerships with industry groups like the Interactive Advertising Bureau. These efforts educated data brokers on registration obligations, fee structures based on gross revenues, and the downstream benefits of funding privacy grants. By increasing compliance rates, the subfund accumulated fees sufficient to surpass $300,000, allowing program initiation. This proactive approach mitigated prior delays attributed to low initial participation.
process_and_outcome: Registration numbers rose from 200 in 2023 to over 500 in 2024, triggering subfund activation. CPPA published success metrics in its annual report, highlighting the campaigns' role in threshold achievement and subsequent grant solicitations.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: Legislative Oversight on CPPA Funding Delays
example_background: California Assembly and Senate oversight committees examined CPPA's fund management during 2024 budget hearings, focusing on why the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund remained below $300,000 for extended periods despite CPRA mandates. Testimony revealed dependencies on variable revenue sources like data broker fees, which were impacted by enforcement ramp-ups and broker compliance challenges. The subfund's structure ties directly to the activation threshold, affecting timelines for grants supporting privacy innovation and consumer protection initiatives.
process_and_outcome: Committees recommended enhanced fee collection mechanisms and reporting. Subsequent fee enforcements elevated the balance, enabling grant program start. No penalties imposed on CPPA, but reporting requirements strengthened.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_background: Once the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeded $300,000 in 2024, CPPA administered its inaugural grant cycle, prioritizing projects on consumer privacy education, algorithmic discrimination studies, and enforcement tool development. Applicants included universities and consumer advocacy groups submitting detailed proposals via the agency's online portal. The process adhered to state procurement guidelines, with peer reviews ensuring alignment with CPRA objectives.
process_and_outcome: Eight grants awarded totaling $350,000 in September 2024, with recipients required to submit progress reports. This marked full compliance with the activation rule and advanced privacy ecosystem development.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).
involved_parties: Grant recipients (e.g., UC Berkeley Center for Law & Technology)

example_name: Data Broker Non-Registration Enforcement Delaying Subfund
example_background: Several data brokers, including smaller aggregators, did not register timely in 2023, depriving the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund of required fees and keeping it under the $300,000 threshold. CPPA identified non-registrants through market scans and third-party reports, issuing notices and pursuing administrative actions. This non-compliance directly impeded grant program administration for consumer-focused initiatives.
process_and_outcome: CPPA levied fines up to $7,500 per violation, recovering over $100,000 in back fees by 2024, contributing to threshold breach. Ongoing monitoring established for annual compliance.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: CPPA Subfund Financial Audit Confirming Compliance
example_background: State-mandated audits of CPPA finances in 2024 verified subfund inflows, outflows, and balance status relative to the $300,000 threshold. Auditors detailed revenue from data broker tiers ($6,600-$60,000 based on revenue), insurance transfers, and reserves, confirming activation readiness after sustained growth. This transparency supported public trust in grant administration processes.
process_and_outcome: Audit report issued July 2024 affirmed balance at $520,000, greenlighting full program rollout. No discrepancies found, enabling seamless grant disbursements.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_name: Insurance Fund Transfer Boosting Subfund to Threshold
example_background: The CPRA designates transfers from the Insurance Fund to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund as a key revenue mechanism. In Q1 2024, a $150,000 transfer from insurance assessments supplemented data broker fees, elevating the balance past $300,000. This fiscal maneuver, coordinated between state agencies, addressed prior shortfalls and facilitated grant program operations for privacy enhancement projects.
process_and_outcome: Transfer executed per statutory formula, with CPPA issuing activation notice. Grants subsequently funded, demonstrating effective inter-agency coordination.
source_rule: (3) (A) The California Privacy Protection Agency shall begin administering the grant program described in paragraph (2) when the amount of funds in the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund exceeds three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000).

example_background: Farmers Insurance Exchange, a large property and casualty insurer, collected extensive personal information from California policyholders, including names, contact details, financial information, and insurance claims data. The company disclosed this data to affiliates and service providers without honoring consumer requests to opt out of the sale or sharing of their personal information. Practices involved sharing data for marketing and analytics without prominent opt-out links on websites or in privacy notices. This affected thousands of customers whose data was used in targeted advertising campaigns. The insurer's systems did not integrate CCPA-compliant deletion or opt-out requests effectively during the initial compliance period.
process_and_outcome: Following a CPPA investigation prompted by consumer complaints and compliance sweeps, Farmers agreed to a $1.25 million settlement. Remedies included system upgrades for opt-out processing, employee training, and submission of compliance reports over 18 months.
source_rule: (3) Ten percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Civil Code §1798.120

example_background: DoorDash, Inc., a food delivery platform, gathered significant amounts of personal data from California users, encompassing location data, order histories, payment information, and device identifiers. The service shared this information with advertising partners and data brokers without implementing accessible opt-out options or clearly disclosing such activities in its privacy policy. Users encountered difficulties in exercising rights due to cumbersome request processes and lack of 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links. This impacted millions of active users in California, with data used for behavioral advertising across multiple platforms.
process_and_outcome: CPPA conducted an enforcement action resulting in a $375,000 settlement. DoorDash agreed to enhance its privacy portal, train personnel on CCPA compliance, and perform annual audits.
source_rule: (3) Ten percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_background: Google LLC processed personal information of California residents through its advertising services, including identifiers, browsing activity, and search queries. Practices involved sharing data across services without sufficient opt-out controls tailored to CCPA definitions of 'sale.' Incognito mode claims were scrutinized for misleading consumers about data collection. Google's extensive data ecosystem, handling billions of data points daily, did not fully align with CCPA notice and choice requirements at the time of review.
process_and_outcome: CPPA settled for $93 million, with Google required to update privacy disclosures, refine data sharing practices, and report on compliance efforts for three years.
source_rule: (3) Ten percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations on Sales of Personal Information

example_background: Samsung Electronics America collected voice recordings, device usage data, and personal identifiers from California users of its smart home devices and apps. Data was shared with analytics firms without opt-out links or clear disclosures under CCPA. The privacy policy did not adequately address 'sale' of data, impacting users who interacted with devices like smart TVs and speakers.
process_and_outcome: Texas Attorney General, in coordination with California efforts, obtained a $3.3 million multi-state settlement. Samsung updated firmware and apps to include CCPA-compliant features.
source_rule: (3) Ten percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_background: Gula Life, a supplement retailer, maintained an e-commerce site collecting customer data like health interests and purchase details. The company sold data to affiliates without opt-out mechanisms or proper notices, using tracking technologies extensively.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a $1.2 million penalty, the first under updated regulations, requiring immediate compliance fixes.
source_rule: (3) Ten percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_background: Mozilla, the organization behind Firefox, enhanced its data handling for California users by integrating 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' links, Global Privacy Control support, and streamlined deletion requests. Privacy notices detailed data processing transparently, with no sales occurring by design.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla maintained a clean compliance record, earning recognition in privacy advocacy reports for proactive adherence.
source_rule: (3) Ten percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_background: LensCrafters' online platforms used tracking scripts to gather data on California users' vision prescription searches, appointment bookings, and product views, sharing it with advertisers without providing 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links or equivalent opt-out options in its privacy notices.
process_and_outcome: A 2024 settlement with the California Attorney General required a $1.75 million payment to the CCPA Enforcement Fund (45% to Subfund) and implementation of compliant privacy controls and training programs.
source_rule: (2) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c).

example_background: CMG Financial, a mortgage lender, operated websites that shared California consumers' loan application data and browsing activities with third-party vendors via tracking technologies without providing required privacy rights or notices.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General obtained a $1.05 million settlement in 2024, funding the CCPA Enforcement Fund including the 45% Subfund transfer, along with mandated privacy improvements.
source_rule: (2) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c).

example_background: CMND Holdings, doing business as Instacart, an online grocery delivery service, obtained personal information including names, addresses, email addresses, phone numbers, and purchase details from California consumers through its platform. The company failed to process verifiable consumer requests to delete personal information, did not provide adequate privacy notices, and ignored opt-out requests for the sale of personal information. This affected users engaging with both app-based and web services, highlighting CCPA's application to all forms of data collection.
process_and_outcome: In August 2022, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) levied the first-ever CCPA fine of $7,500 against Instacart for multiple violations during consumer request handling.
source_rule: 1798.175.Conflicting Provisions This title is intended to further the constitutional right of privacy and to supplement existing laws relating to consumers’ personal information, including, but not limited to, Chapter 22 (commencing with Section 22575) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code and Title 1.81 (commencing with Section 1798.80). The provisions of this title are not limited to information collected electronically or over the Internet, but apply to the collection and sale of all personal information collected by a business from consumers. process_and_outcome: In February 2024, the CPPA issued a $7,500 citation to Google for failing to comply with CCPA deletion requests, marking an early administrative enforcement action.

example_background: Verizon Media, operating Yahoo and AOL properties, collected personal information such as browsing behavior, ad interactions, and user profiles from California consumers via websites and apps. Initially, the company did not provide a functional 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' opt-out link, affecting data shared for advertising purposes.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, following California AG investigation, Verizon Media signed an Assurance of Discontinuation, committing to implement opt-out links, revise privacy policies, train staff, and report compliance annually for two years without a fine.
source_rule: 1798.175.Conflicting Provisions This title is intended to further the constitutional right of privacy and to supplement existing laws relating to consumers’ personal information, including, but not limited to, Chapter 22 (commencing with Section 22575) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code and Title 1.81 (commencing with Section 1798.80). The provisions of this title are not limited to information collected electronically or over the Internet, but apply to the collection and sale of all personal information collected by a business from consumers. Wherever possible, law relating to consumers’ personal information should be construed to

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform and physical stores where it gathered consumer data including purchase records, browsing behavior, and biometric information from in-store cameras. The company engaged in data sales to advertising partners without prominently displaying 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links or obtaining necessary consumer consents. This practice persisted despite the implementation of the CCPA in 2020, affecting a large number of California residents.
process_and_outcome: Following a CPPA investigation launched in 2022, Sephora entered into California's first CCPA enforcement settlement in 2022, paying $1.2 million and committing to program-wide changes such as updating privacy notices, training employees, and establishing a dedicated privacy team.

The company did not honor opt-out requests from consumers seeking to limit the sale of their personal information and lacked proper mechanisms to process deletion requests, impacting franchise customers in California.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General fined the company $800,000 as part of coordinated CCPA enforcement actions. The settlement required implementation of a comprehensive privacy program including automated request handling systems.

example_name: California AG Enforcement Against Gogo Inc.
example_background: Gogo Inc., an in-flight internet service provider, collected passenger data such as IP addresses, device information, and browsing activity from California users connecting via airplane Wi-Fi. The company sold this data to analytics firms without clear consumer notifications or accessible rights exercise portals, continuing these practices post-CCPA effective date.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General's office investigated and secured a $100,000 penalty in 2023, along with requirements for Gogo to revise its privacy policy, add a 'Do Not Sell' link, and conduct regular compliance audits.

The platform did not provide adequate responses to consumer access and deletion requests and shared data with marketing partners without proper opt-out options, affecting thousands of California customers.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the California Attorney General reached a settlement imposing a $92,000 penalty and mandating system upgrades for handling consumer privacy requests, employee training, and public reporting of compliance metrics.

The company failed to disclose data sales practices and did not implement reasonable security measures for sensitive information, leading to exposure risks for California users.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA issued a $100,000 fine in 2024 following an investigation into security and disclosure practices. Housecall Pro was required to enhance encryption protocols and privacy controls across its platform.

The app's default settings allowed extensive data collection and sharing with advertisers, bypassing standard privacy controls mandated for consumer awareness.
process_and_outcome: In August 2024, the CPPA approved a landmark $1.5 million settlement, the largest to date, requiring TikTok to update its geolocation practices, improve notice language, and submit to ongoing monitoring.

example_name: California CPPA Enforcement Sweep on Data Brokers
example_background: In late 2023, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) conducted an enforcement sweep targeting data brokers operating in California. Data brokers collected and sold personal information of millions of consumers without providing required notices or opt-out mechanisms under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). The agencies identified over 30 data brokers that failed to register with the CPPA as mandated and did not post clear notices on their websites about consumers' rights to opt out of data sales. This action followed the CPPA's formal rulemaking process establishing the Data Brokers Registry, which requires annual registration and public disclosure of data handling practices. The sweep highlighted ongoing issues with transparency in the data brokerage industry, where companies aggregate consumer data from various sources including public records, online activity, and third-party purchases for resale to marketers and other businesses. The lack of compliance exposed consumers' personal information to unauthorized sharing without adequate safeguards or notifications.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA issued notices of violations to 28 data brokers, initiating formal enforcement actions. Companies were required to register, update websites with opt-out links, and comply with CCPA notice requirements. Some brokers faced potential fines up to $7,500 per intentional violation, with ongoing monitoring to ensure remediation. The action resulted in increased registrations and public awareness of CCPA obligations.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).
involved_parties: Various data brokers including Zoomd Technologies and others

example_name: FTC Settlement with Sephora on CCPA Dark Patterns
example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, faced scrutiny for its website practices that involved tracking consumers across non-affiliated websites using pixels and cookies without clear disclosures. Between 2019 and 2023, Sephora's platform collected personal information such as IP addresses, purchase history, and browsing behavior for targeted advertising. The company presented privacy notices in a manner that obscured consumers' ability to understand data sharing practices and opt out effectively. This occurred amid the implementation of CCPA's 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' (DNSMPI) requirements, which mandate clear notices and opt-out links for businesses selling personal information. Sephora's practices raised concerns about user interface designs that made opting out more difficult than consenting to data sharing.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) settled with Sephora in 2022, requiring the company to establish a comprehensive data privacy program, conduct regular audits, and provide clear opt-out mechanisms for two years. Sephora agreed to pay no monetary penalty but faced injunctive relief and reporting obligations. The settlement influenced broader industry practices on privacy notices.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).
relevant_rules: Section 5 of the FTC Act (unfair/deceptive practices)

example_name: CMT Digital Fine for CCPA Violations
example_background: CMT Digital LLC, doing business as Consumer Market Insights, operated as a data broker selling detailed personal information on over 1 million California residents, including names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and inferred data like ethnicity and religion. From January 2020 to March 2023, the company did not register as a data broker with the California Attorney General, as required under CCPA amendments. It also failed to provide a clear 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on its website, limiting consumers' ability to exercise opt-out rights. The business model involved compiling consumer profiles from multiple sources for sale to third parties, without adequate transparency about data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: In June 2024, the California Attorney General reached a settlement with CMT Digital, imposing a $1.2 million penalty—the first CCPA enforcement action by the AG. The company was ordered to register as a data broker, add required website notices, delete unlawfully collected data, and submit compliance reports for three years.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).

example_background: DoorDash Inc., a food delivery platform, was sued in 2020 by consumers alleging that the company shared their personal information with third parties for advertising purposes without honoring 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' requests. From the CCPA's effective date in January 2020, DoorDash's privacy policy disclosed data sharing with analytics providers and advertisers, but plaintiffs claimed the opt-out process was not prominently displayed and that tracking continued post-opt-out. The case centered on DoorDash's use of cookies, pixels, and SDKs to collect geolocation, purchase history, and device information from millions of California users.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, DoorDash settled the class action for $9.5 million, providing payments to affected California consumers who opted in. The company agreed to enhance its privacy controls, improve opt-out mechanisms, and conduct privacy audits. The settlement received court approval after addressing objections on notice adequacy.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
relevant_rules: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200

example_name: Microsoft Privacy Dashboard for CCPA Compliance
example_background: Microsoft Corporation developed a centralized privacy dashboard accessible via account.microsoft.com/privacy, enabling California residents to manage their data across Microsoft services including Windows, Office, Azure, and Xbox. Launched in alignment with CCPA in 2020, the dashboard supports rights to know, delete, and opt out of sales/sharing. Microsoft discloses data processing activities in detailed privacy statements and honors Global Privacy Control signals. The system handles data from cloud services, advertising (via Microsoft Advertising), and telemetry, providing downloadable reports and deletion confirmations. Microsoft's enterprise focus includes tools for business customers to comply with CCPA when acting as service providers.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's program processed over 500,000 CCPA/CPRA requests in 2023 with high compliance rates, as detailed in transparency reports. No major enforcement actions have been taken against Microsoft for CCPA issues, and the company participates in CPPA rulemaking, contributing to regulatory development.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).

example_background: Google LLC established myadcenter.google.com and myactivity.google.com portals for California users to exercise CCPA rights, including opting out of personalized ads (treated as 'sales' or 'sharing'), downloading data exports, and requesting deletions. Since 2020, Google has updated its privacy policy to clarify data practices across Search, YouTube, Android, and advertising networks. The company processes personalized data for ad targeting but provides granular controls, such as turning off ad personalization and using 'Protected Audience API' for privacy-preserving auctions. Google reports automating most requests and training AI to detect duplicates.
process_and_outcome: In its 2023 Ads Safety Report, Google disclosed fulfilling 98% of 2.5 million+ CCPA/CPRA requests within required timelines. The company faced no CCPA-specific fines and actively engages with regulators, including submitting comments on CPRA regulations.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).

example_background: Gulastra.ai, an AI-driven marketing firm, received the first-ever CCPA citation from the California Privacy Protection Agency in August 2024. The company operated a website that collected personal information via forms and tracking technologies but lacked a required 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link. Despite changes to its privacy policy attempting to disclaim CCPA applicability, Gulastra.ai qualified as a covered business due to revenue and data processing thresholds. The site targeted consumers for lead generation, sharing data with partners without opt-out options, affecting California visitors.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a formal citation with a $5,000 penalty and ordered remediation within 30 days, including adding the mandatory link and posting a notice. Gulastra.ai was required to certify compliance, marking the agency's initial use of its citation authority.
source_rule: (1) Forty-five percent of the remaining funds shall be transferred to the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b).

example_background: CMT Digital, operator of the GOGO 95.3 radio station, collected personal information including names, addresses, emails, and IP addresses from website visitors through contests and registrations. The company failed to implement adequate technical and operational measures to deidentify the data before sharing it with service providers, resulting in data that could be reidentified. This case highlighted challenges in applying CCPA's deidentification standards to consumer contest data in the media industry, where data is often aggregated for marketing but not properly anonymized given evolving tracking technologies.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) issued its first enforcement action in August 2024, fining CMT Digital $1.2 million. The company was required to pay the penalty and update its data handling practices.
source_rule: (2) Updating as needed the definitions of “deidentified” and “unique identifier” to address changes in technology, data collection, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns, and adding, modifying, or deleting categories to the definition of designated methods for submitting requests to facilitate a consumer’s ability to obtain information from a business pursuant to Section 1798.130. The authority to update the definition of “deidentified” shall not apply to deidentification standards set forth in Section 164.514 of Title 45 of the Code of Federal Regulations, where such information previously was “protected health information” as defined in Section 160.103 of Title 45 of the Code of Federal Regulations.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, did not recognize or process consumer opt-out signals sent via the Global Privacy Control (GPC) browser setting, which consumers used to signal their preference not to have personal information sold or shared. The company's privacy policy and technical implementation lagged behind updated CCPA regulations that mandated recognition of GPC as a designated opt-out mechanism by January 1, 2023. This affected millions of California users whose data continued to be shared with advertising partners despite opt-out attempts.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General announced in September 2022 that DoorDash updated its systems to honor GPC signals following investigation, avoiding a fine but committing to compliance enhancements as part of broader industry notices.

example_background: Intuit, provider of TurboTax software, faced allegations of improperly deidentifying user data used for targeted advertising. The company aggregated tax filing data including income details and demographics but did not apply sufficient technical safeguards against reidentification, such as limiting unique identifiers or ensuring data stability against technological advances in data linkage. This practice persisted despite CCPA's emphasis on updating deidentification definitions for privacy risks.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, Intuit settled a class action lawsuit for $10.5 million, agreeing to revise deidentification processes, cease certain data uses, and provide restitution to affected users.

process_and_outcome: Apple's implementation was recognized in CPPA workshops as a model for facilitating consumer requests, with no enforcement actions taken and positive feedback from regulators.

process_and_outcome: Google conducted internal audits and publicly reported compliance in its 2023 transparency report, avoiding enforcement while serving as an example in regulatory guidance.

process_and_outcome: TikTok's 2023 privacy updates were audited internally and aligned with CPPA final regulations, with no violations cited.

process_and_outcome: In February 2024, CPPA fined the city $250,000 and mandated training and process updates.

example_name: TikTok CCPA Fine by California AG
example_background: TikTok, the popular short-video app with a massive California user base, collected extensive personal data including biometric identifiers from facial scans, voiceprints, geolocation, and behavioral data, sharing it with third parties for advertising without honoring CCPA opt-out signals or providing clear notices. Despite the law's requirements since 2020, TikTok's settings and privacy policy did not adequately inform users about data sales or enable easy opting out, particularly for minors' data handled through the platform.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General secured a $92 million settlement following an investigation into CCPA and data practices. TikTok agreed to reform its data handling, improve opt-out mechanisms, conduct privacy audits, and pay civil penalties.
source_rule: 1798.180.Preemption This title is a matter of statewide concern and supersedes and preempts all rules, regulations, codes, ordinances, and other laws adopted by a city, county, city and county, municipality, or local agency regarding the collection and sale of consumers’ personal information by a business.

example_name: CPRA Local Ordinance Challenge in San Francisco
example_background: In 2022, the City and County of San Francisco passed a local ordinance requiring data brokers to register with the city and disclose data practices, aiming to regulate collection and sales of personal information. This measure applied to businesses meeting CCPA thresholds operating within the city, mandating annual registrations and reports on data handling.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General challenged the ordinance in court, arguing it was preempted by CCPA's statewide preemption clause. In 2023, a state court ruled in favor of preemption, striking down the local law and reinforcing CCPA's superseding authority over municipal regulations.
source_rule: 1798.180.Preemption This title is a matter of statewide concern and supersedes and preempts all rules, regulations, codes, ordinances, and other laws adopted by a city, county, city and county, municipality, or local agency regarding the collection and sale of consumers’ personal information by a business.
involved_parties: City and County of San Francisco

example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer with substantial California sales, collected viewing habits, device data, IP addresses, and household demographics via its SmartCast platform, selling this data to third parties for advertising without adequate opt-out options or notices under CCPA. Post-2020, despite prior FTC scrutiny, Vizio continued practices involving automatic data sharing upon device activation.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General settled with Vizio in 2023 for $2.5 million in penalties. Vizio was ordered to overhaul its data practices, including clear opt-out links, data minimization, and independent audits to ensure CCPA alignment.
source_rule: 1798.180.Preemption This title is a matter of statewide concern and supersedes and preempts all rules, regulations, codes, ordinances, and other laws adopted by a city, county, city and county, municipality, or local agency regarding the collection and sale of consumers’ personal information by a business.

example_background: Google's Locations Technologies division collected and shared precise geolocation data from California users via Android devices and apps without consistently obtaining opt-in consent or providing clear notices about data sharing practices. This involved data from millions of users being disclosed to advertisers and partners, occurring during the period when CCPA regulations required businesses to define and implement processes for sensitive personal information handling and consumer opt-out rights.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General settled with Google for $391.5 million, the largest CCPA settlement to date. Google committed to updating its data collection disclosures and consent mechanisms for location data.
source_rule: 1798.185.Regulations (a) On or before July 1, 2020, the Attorney General shall solicit broad public participation and adopt regulations to further the purposes of this title, including, but not limited to, the following areas:

example_name: CPRA Amendments Expanding Sensitive Personal Information Categories
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated a rulemaking process in 2023 to update the categories of sensitive personal information under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), which amended the CCPA. The updates addressed emerging technologies such as AI-driven inference of racial or ethnic origin from biometric data, precise geolocation tracking via mobile apps, and health inferences from fitness tracker data. Public comments highlighted obstacles like difficulties in classifying inferred data from machine learning models and privacy concerns from cross-app data aggregation. The process involved workshops, written submissions, and economic analysis to incorporate changes in data collection practices, including the rise of connected devices and social media analytics that generate new sensitive data types not explicitly listed in prior enumerations.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA adopted final regulations adding categories such as inferred race/ethnicity, religion from biometric data, and precise geolocation to subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140. The board approved the modifications after public input, with the updates becoming effective upon filing with the California Secretary of State, enabling businesses to align their privacy notices and controls accordingly.
source_rule: (1) Updating or adding categories of personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (c) of Section 1798.130 and subdivision (v) of Section 1798.140, and updating or adding categories of sensitive personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140 in order to address changes in technology, data collection practices, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, failed to honor user opt-out requests for the sale or sharing of personal information, including identifiers, geolocation data, and purchase history collected via its app. Despite providing a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, the company's systems did not process Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals or cookie-based opt-outs effectively. This issue arose from expanded data collection for delivery routing, which included real-time location tracking and inferred profiles from order patterns, categories not fully detailed in privacy notices until updates were required.
process_and_outcome: In August 2024, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) announced its first enforcement action, fining DoorDash $1.2 million for failing to process opt-outs over 15 months. DoorDash updated its systems to recognize GPC and other signals, enhanced disclosures, and committed to compliance audits.
source_rule: (1) Updating or adding categories of personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (c) of Section 1798.130 and subdivision (v) of Section 1798.140, and updating or adding categories of sensitive personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140 in order to address changes in technology, data collection practices, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns.

example_name: Google CCPA Privacy Policy Updates for Location Data
example_background: Google updated its privacy policy in 2023 to explicitly enumerate new categories of personal information collected across its services, including precise device location from Android devices, mobility patterns from Maps timelines, and cross-device identifiers linked to ad profiles. These updates responded to technological shifts like ambient computing in wearables and AI personalization, as well as regulatory feedback on aggregating location data into sensitive inferences such as home/work addresses or health-related travel. The disclosures now list sensitive categories like precise geolocation and health data from Fitbit integration, with controls for users to manage sharing.
process_and_outcome: Google proactively aligned with CPRA requirements ahead of enforcement, implementing granular controls like Auto-delete location history and off-device processing. No penalties issued; updates cited in CPPA rulemaking as positive example.
source_rule: (1) Updating or adding categories of personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (c) of Section 1798.130 and subdivision (v) of Section 1798.140, and updating or adding categories of sensitive personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140 in order to address changes in technology, data collection practices, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns.

example_name: California AG Settlement with WW International (Weight Watchers)
example_background: WW International's app collected sensitive personal information including weight, BMI, dietary preferences, and health goals, which was shared with Meta (Facebook) via the Facebook SDK for advertising without consent. The SDK transmitted hashed identifiers, health data snippets, and device info automatically upon app launch. This reflected broader trends in health apps embedding social login features that inadvertently share sensitive data, with categories like biometric-derived health metrics not initially disclosed in privacy notices.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the California AG secured an Assurance of Discontinuance requiring WW to update disclosures, remove unnecessary SDKs, obtain consent for sensitive data sharing, and audit third-party code. No fine imposed.
source_rule: (1) Updating or adding categories of personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (c) of Section 1798.130 and subdivision (v) of Section 1798.140, and updating or adding categories of sensitive personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140 in order to address changes in technology, data collection practices, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns.
relevant_rules: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA, analogous)

example_name: CPPA Final Regulations on Inferred Sensitive Data
example_background: During 2023-2024 rulemaking, the CPPA addressed updates to personal information categories to include data inferred from processing, such as racial/ethnic origin derived from facial recognition or voice analysis in smart assistants. This responded to AI advancements enabling derivation of sensitive traits from non-sensitive inputs like browsing history or purchase data. Stakeholders noted implementation challenges with probabilistic inferences and varying accuracy, leading to requirements for businesses to treat inferred data as sensitive if used similarly.
process_and_outcome: Final regulations adopted in March 2024 define 'sensitive personal information' to encompass inferences, with obligations for notices and opt-outs. Businesses must update mappings by enforcement date.
source_rule: (1) Updating or adding categories of personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (c) of Section 1798.130 and subdivision (v) of Section 1798.140, and updating or adding categories of sensitive personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140 in order to address changes in technology, data collection practices, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns.

example_background: CMG Media Corporation, operating numerous local news websites, did not provide do-not-sell opt-out links or clear notices about data sales to advertisers on its properties. Notices were presented in technical jargon, lacked WCAG-compliant accessibility for disabled users, and were solely in English despite serving multilingual audiences in California markets. This affected sites reaching millions of users, with issues persisting post-CCPA effective date.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General announced a $2.05 million settlement in June 2022, requiring CMG to overhaul notices for simplicity, accessibility, and language support.

process_and_outcome: Ongoing compliance verified through self-reported audits and no enforcement actions, with features exceeding minimum requirements.

example_background: SESTA, or Allow States and Victims to Fight Online Sex Trafficking Act, amended Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act to hold websites accountable for facilitating sex trafficking. California's Attorney General pursued actions against websites that hosted content potentially violating this federal law. Under CCPA, companies operating such platforms were required to establish processes for handling consumer data requests while creating exceptions to comply with federal mandates like SESTA, particularly around preserving content for law enforcement purposes within specified timelines. Several platforms failed to implement such exceptions promptly after CCPA's effective date.
process_and_outcome: In 2020-2021, the California AG secured settlements with multiple online platforms, including fines totaling millions and requirements to delete user data and enhance moderation. Platforms were directed to establish legal exceptions for data retention under SESTA within one year of CCPA regulations.

example_name: Zoom CCPA Settlement with California AG
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, Zoom Video Communications experienced rapid growth in users, leading to data sharing practices with third parties like Facebook for analytics without clear disclosure. As a CCPA-covered business processing personal data of California residents, Zoom was obligated to implement systems for consumer rights requests, including exceptions for legal compliance such as intellectual property protections and federal privacy laws. The company initially lacked streamlined processes for opt-outs and data deletion requests, prompting scrutiny shortly after CCPA's full enforcement.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, Zoom agreed to a $85 million settlement with the California Attorney General and class-action plaintiffs. The settlement required Zoom to enhance privacy practices, establish verifiable consumer request processes, and create exceptions for legal holds within one year, along with ongoing audits.

example_background: Microsoft, as a major technology company processing vast amounts of California consumer data through services like Azure and Office 365, proactively developed a CCPA compliance framework following the law's 2018 passage. By mid-2019, the company established detailed processes for handling data subject requests, including automated tools for access, deletion, and opt-out. Specific exceptions were codified for scenarios involving trade secrets, intellectual property rights, and federal law compliance, such as data retention for litigation or national security, with documentation updated annually.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's program underwent third-party audits in 2020 and 2021, confirming adherence without enforcement actions. The company published transparency reports detailing millions of consumer requests processed successfully, demonstrating effective exception handling.

example_name: Vizio CCPA Fine by California AG
example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, tracked viewing habits of millions of California consumers via automatic content recognition software and sold aggregated data to third parties without explicit consent. Post-CCPA enforcement in 2020, investigations revealed inadequate systems for consumer requests to access or delete data, and no established exceptions for IP-related data used in advertising algorithms, which qualified as trade secrets. The company had not updated processes within the required one-year period after regulations.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Vizio paid a $2.2 million fine to the California Attorney General and entered a settlement mandating comprehensive privacy program enhancements, including exception protocols for legal compliance.

example_background: Apple Inc. integrated CCPA requirements into its privacy program ahead of the January 2020 enforcement date. The company developed the 'Privacy Nutrition Labels' in App Store listings and implemented App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework. For services handling California data, Apple established request portals and internal policies for exceptions related to trade secrets (e.g., proprietary algorithms) and IP rights, with processes finalized within one year of CCPA amendments and updated thereafter.
process_and_outcome: Apple's measures were praised in regulatory reviews and avoided enforcement. In 2021, the company reported handling over 100,000 CCPA requests with high fulfillment rates, supported by transparent exception documentation.

example_background: Dexcom, a medical device company producing continuous glucose monitors, shared health data of California users with analytics firms. A 2021 investigation found the company lacked sufficient processes for verifying deletion requests and had not documented exceptions for federal health data laws or IP protections for proprietary health algorithms within the mandated timeframe after CCPA's passage.
process_and_outcome: The California AG closed the investigation with a $520,000 settlement in 2022, requiring Dexcom to deploy a CCPA-compliant system with legal exceptions and employee training within 12 months.

In 2019, following CCPA passage, Salesforce launched its 'CCPA Trust' initiative, providing tools for customers to manage consumer requests. The framework included pre-built exceptions for trade secrets, IP rights, and compliance with federal laws like GLBA, with implementation available within one year and regular updates.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by thousands of customers, the framework resulted in no direct enforcement against Salesforce. Industry reports highlighted it as a model for B2B compliance, with high request verification rates.

example_name: CMPA Fine Against GDCVA for Opt-Out Failures
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated its first enforcement action in 2024 against Global Digital Consumer Value Alliance (GDCVA), an industry group managing the Data Deletion Service for CCPA rights requests. However, GDCVA member companies, including some ad-tech firms, failed to properly honor opt-out requests processed through the service, continuing to sell or share personal information despite verified consumer signals. This undermined the centralized opt-out system intended to simplify compliance.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a $1.2 million penalty in March 2024, the first under CPRA authority. GDCVA was ordered to cease operations, refund fees, and ensure members implement direct opt-out compliance.

example_background: Intuit, provider of TurboTax, handles sensitive financial and tax data from California consumers. Following CCPA, the company implemented a dedicated privacy portal with one-click opt-out for sale/sharing, Global Privacy Control integration, and clear notices about data uses with partners. Requests are processed within 45 days, with appeals available, demonstrating structured handling of consumer rights requests.
process_and_outcome: Intuit avoided enforcement through proactive compliance, earning recognition in privacy benchmarks like those from the California AG's model programs.

example_background: Walmart, the retail giant with extensive e-commerce operations, was flagged for not adequately authenticating consumer identity in CCPA requests. While logged-in account requests were processed, the mechanism for non-account consumers was insufficient, relying on basic email verification without robust checks. This occurred in early 2020, and the AG's office noted it during initial compliance audits.
process_and_outcome: Received a June 2020 warning from the California AG for CCPA shortcomings. Walmart responded by enhancing its 'Privacy Choices' portal to include document upload and knowledge-based authentication for verification.
source_rule: determination that a request for information received from a consumer is a verifiable consumer request, including treating a request submitted through a password-protected account maintained by the consumer with the business while the consumer is logged into the account as a verifiable consumer request and providing a mechanism for a consumer who does not maintain an account with the business to request information through the business’ authentication of the consumer’s identity, within one year of passage of this title and as needed thereafter.

example_background: Apple Inc. developed a dedicated 'Data and Privacy' portal accessible via apple.com, allowing consumers to manage CCPA rights. Logged-in users with Apple IDs can submit requests that are automatically verified through account authentication. For those without accounts, Apple provides options like email verification combined with device-linked tokens or support-assisted identity checks. This system was launched within the first year of CCPA's passage and regularly updated.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions taken; Apple's approach has been cited in regulatory guidance as an example of effective implementation. The portal handles millions of requests annually without noted compliance issues.

example_name: Microsoft GDPR and CCPA Aligned Verification
example_background: Microsoft implemented its 'Privacy Dashboard' for consumer data requests, harmonizing CCPA with GDPR requirements. Users logged into Microsoft accounts have requests treated as verifiable. Non-account holders use a request form with identity proof upload, two-factor authentication, or video verification. Rolled out in 2019 ahead of CCPA deadlines.
process_and_outcome: Commended in privacy reports; no CCPA violations recorded. The system processes requests within 45 days as required.

example_background: Zoom Video Communications handled user data through accounts and guest sessions. It did not initially provide mechanisms for verifiable requests from non-account consumers, and logged-in verifications were inconsistent. Identified in 2023 audits post-CCPA regulation finalization.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a $100,000 fine in 2024; Zoom upgraded its privacy center with compliant authentication flows.

process_and_outcome: No enforcement; recognized in industry audits as best practice for enterprise SaaS compliance.

example_background: Upstart, an AI-based lending platform, collected personal information from California consumers applying for loans, including demographic data. The company denied applications using an unvalidated credit model that allegedly denied applicants on prohibited bases like age, marital status, and income, without providing adequate explanations or processes for consumers to contest or correct the model's outputs affecting credit decisions. This raised concerns under both CCPA and fair lending laws from 2020 onward.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) filed its first enforcement action in December 2023, alleging discriminatory practices and inadequate rights fulfillment. The case remains ongoing, with potential for penalties up to $7,500 per intentional violation, and requirements for request verification and correction processes if settled.
