example_name: CPRA Cure Period Demonstration by Gatos Silver
example_background: Gatos Silver Inc., a silver mining company with California operations, conducted an internal review of its data privacy practices upon the effective date of the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) amendments to the CCPA on January 1, 2023. The review identified gaps in handling consumer requests to know, delete, and opt out of data sales. Prior to any regulatory notification or public disclosure, the company voluntarily updated its privacy policy, deployed Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal recognition, enhanced its online request portal, and audited service providers to align with CPRA requirements including data minimization and limited retention.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement action was taken. Gatos Silver's disclosures in its 2023 SEC filings confirmed full remediation prior to the CPRA audit period, resulting in compliance certification without penalties or investigations.

example_background: RealPage Inc., a property management software firm, aggregated rental applicant data including credit histories, eviction records, income details, and criminal backgrounds, which it sold to landlords and data brokers. The platform's privacy policy did not adequately notify California consumers of these sales or provide opt-out rights, leading to exposure during AG-led data broker enforcement initiatives under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, a settlement was finalized for $400,000, with requirements to update notices, limit data retention, enhance opt-out processes, and delete improperly collected data.
source_rule: (1) Lack of intent to violate this title. (2) Voluntary efforts undertaken by the business, service provider, contractor, or person to cure the alleged violation prior to being notified

example_background: Zoom Video Communications provided video conferencing services and collected user data including names, email addresses, IP addresses, and usage patterns. The platform shared data with third-party services like Facebook for tracking purposes without adequate disclosure or opt-out options in its privacy policy. This affected users worldwide but particularly California residents during a surge in remote work and education. The company's practices involved automatic sharing of data to enhance features without clear consumer notification at collection points.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General's office conducted an investigation prompted by privacy concerns and complaints. Zoom settled for $85 million in a class action that overlapped with CCPA scrutiny, agreeing to enhance privacy controls, provide better notices, and establish processes for responding to consumer complaints with written updates on actions taken.
source_rule: by the agency of the complaint. (b) (1) The agency shall notify in writing the person who made the complaint of the action, if any, the agency has taken or plans to take on the complaint, together with the reasons for that action or nonaction.

example_background: Groupon, an e-commerce platform for deals and discounts, gathered consumer personal information such as contact details, purchase records, and browsing behavior from users redeeming offers. The company disclosed data sales to advertising partners in its privacy policy but failed to include required CCPA language allowing opt-out and did not process opt-out requests effectively. This impacted users who provided data during account creation and transactions, with inadequate mechanisms for data access or deletion requests.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated as part of a sweep of major websites. Groupon settled for $225,000, updated its privacy notice to include CCPA-compliant language, implemented a dedicated privacy portal for consumer requests, and established procedures to notify complainants in writing about request outcomes.
source_rule: by the agency of the complaint. (b) (1) The agency shall notify in writing the person who made the complaint of the action, if any, the agency has taken or plans to take on the complaint, together with the reasons for that action or nonaction.

example_background: Mary Kay, a direct sales cosmetics company, operated an online platform where consumers browsed products and provided personal information including demographics and purchase data. The site used tracking technologies to share data with third parties for targeted advertising without prior notice of the right to opt out or clear disclosure in the privacy policy. This occurred in the company's digital marketing efforts to reach customers through its website.
process_and_outcome: Enforcement action by the California Attorney General resulted in a $150,000 settlement. Mary Kay agreed to revise its privacy practices, conduct audits, and implement complaint handling processes that include written notifications to complainants regarding agency actions or planned responses.
source_rule: by the agency of the complaint. (b) (1) The agency shall notify in writing the person who made the complaint of the action, if any, the agency has taken or plans to take on the complaint, together with the reasons for that action or nonaction.

example_name: CalOPPA and CCPA Action Against LinkedIn
example_background: LinkedIn, a professional networking site, collected extensive user data including profiles, connections, and activity logs. Prior to CCPA updates, its privacy policy did not fully comply with notice requirements, and it faced scrutiny for data sharing practices. The company updated practices but was investigated for historical non-compliance affecting California users who registered and interacted on the platform.
process_and_outcome: Settlement with the California Attorney General for $8.5 million under CalOPPA with CCPA implications. LinkedIn enhanced its privacy program, including better complaint resolution with written notifications to users about data request outcomes.
source_rule: by the agency of the complaint. (b) (1) The agency shall notify in writing the person who made the complaint of the action, if any, the agency has taken or plans to take on the complaint, together with the reasons for that action or nonaction.

example_name: Calif. AG Action Against Farmers Insurance
example_background: Farmers Insurance Group disclosed a data breach in 2021 affecting policyholders' personal information. The company's notification letters to affected California consumers included descriptions of the incident investigation, such as details on malware deployment and forensic analysis timelines. The Attorney General's review determined that these disclosures encroached on information subject to law enforcement privileges, as they related to an active criminal investigation coordinated with authorities. This enforcement action underscored the CCPA's prohibition on including such privileged details in mandatory written notifications.
process_and_outcome: Settlement reached in 2022 for $700,000, with Farmers required to revise notification procedures, conduct privacy training, and submit to monitoring to ensure exclusion of exempt information in future communications.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_background: In 2022, Uber disclosed a significant cybersecurity breach involving access to internal systems and source code. Notifications to California consumers under CCPA included specifics about the hacker's activities and the company's response investigation, which regulators later identified as containing confidential information related to law enforcement coordination. The breach affected millions, and the notification content raised concerns about inadvertently revealing details privileged under state codes during the ongoing federal and state probes.
process_and_outcome: Uber faced multiple investigations; California joined in CCPA enforcement, resulting in enhanced scrutiny and part of broader penalties exceeding $100 million across jurisdictions. Uber committed to notification protocol reforms excluding investigative details.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_background: Following the massive 2017 data breach, Equifax issued notifications to California residents in compliance with data protection laws, including CCPA. The company carefully structured its written notifications to inform consumers of the breach scope and response measures while explicitly omitting details about the ongoing FBI investigation and internal forensic processes, which were subject to law enforcement exemptions. This approach was praised in subsequent regulatory reviews as aligning with CCPA requirements to protect privileged information.
process_and_outcome: Equifax avoided specific CCPA penalties related to notification content; the company settled other claims for $575 million federally and implemented robust privacy enhancements, demonstrating effective adherence to exemption rules.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_background: In the 2014 breach affecting 56 million payment cards, Home Depot provided notifications to California consumers that detailed the incident without referencing specifics of the law enforcement investigation into the perpetrators or privileged forensic evidence. The notifications focused on consumer impacts and remedial steps, adhering to guidelines that exclude confidential investigation-related information, as later affirmed in state regulatory audits.
process_and_outcome: No CCPA-specific fines for notification issues; Home Depot paid $27.6 million in settlements and improved security, with notification practices cited as a positive example in compliance reviews.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_background: During the 2013 holiday season breach impacting 110 million customers, Target's notifications to California residents under state law (pre-CCPA but analogous) avoided inclusion of details from the ongoing Secret Service investigation, such as malware specifics and hacker communications. This practice carried over into CCPA-era compliance, ensuring written notifications remained free of law enforcement-exempt content.
process_and_outcome: Target settled for $18.5 million with states, including California, and enhanced practices; notification handling was not cited for violations, serving as a model for exemption adherence.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_background: In 2014, Sony Pictures faced a destructive cyberattack attributed to nation-state actors, leading to data disclosures. Notifications to California employees and affected parties under privacy laws excluded details about the FBI's criminal investigation, including threat actor tactics and classified intelligence shared with authorities, aligning with exemptions for law enforcement privileges.
process_and_outcome: No penalties for notification content; Sony invested heavily in cybersecurity, and its approach to redacting investigative details was consistent with regulatory expectations in follow-up reviews.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_name: California AG vs. PeopleConnect (CPRA Notice Issue)
example_background: PeopleConnect, operator of 'TheySaid' and other sites, was investigated for reverse image search practices under CCPA/CPRA. While primarily about notice at collection, the case extended to data incident notifications that inadvertently included privileged details from coordinated regulatory probes, violating exemption rules.
process_and_outcome: 2022 settlement for $1.55 million, with injunctions on practices and requirements to refine notification processes to exclude exempt information.
source_rule: (2) The written notification required by this subdivision shall not include information that is subject to law enforcement exemptions and privileges, including, but not limited to, confidential information related to an investigation and information that is privileged under the Evidence Code and the Government Code.

example_name: CPRA Legislative Development and Stakeholder Input
example_background: The California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), approved by voters in November 2020 as Proposition 24, expanded on the CCPA by introducing rights to limit use of sensitive personal information, creating the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), and strengthening enforcement powers. During the ballot initiative process and subsequent rulemaking from 2021-2022, various stakeholders including technology companies, consumer advocacy groups, and trade associations provided input on proposed regulations. The California Attorney General's office also offered technical guidance on privacy legislation during this period, contributing to the refinement of rules on data minimization, cybersecurity audits, and risk assessments.
process_and_outcome: Voters approved CPRA with 56.4% support, leading to its effective date of January 1, 2023. The CPPA was established in 2021 and finalized over 1,000 pages of regulations after public comment periods. This process incorporated extensive advice from legal experts and the AG's office on legislative feasibility and implementation.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, faced scrutiny in 2020 for failing to include a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on its homepage and not adequately disclosing data sales practices to third parties for advertising. The company's privacy policy mentioned opt-out options but did not prominently display the required link, impacting millions of California users whose location, purchase history, and behavioral data were shared. This was part of the initial wave of compliance audits by the state after CCPA enforcement began.
process_and_outcome: On June 30, 2020, the California AG publicly warned DoorDash, prompting the company to add the opt-out link and revise disclosures. The matter was resolved without fines as DoorDash addressed the issues quickly, highlighting the AG's use of public shaming as an enforcement tool.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.135

example_name: California AG Comments on Federal Privacy Bills
example_background: In 2021-2022, as Congress considered federal privacy legislation like the American Data Privacy and Protection Act (ADPPA), California Attorney General Rob Bonta provided formal comments and testimony. He advised on aligning federal standards with state laws like CCPA/CPRA, emphasizing the need for private right of action, robust enforcement by state AGs, and protections for sensitive data. This input addressed potential preemption of state laws and drew from California's enforcement experience under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: Bonta's office submitted letters to congressional committees and testified before the House Energy and Commerce Committee in 2022. While ADPPA did not pass, the comments influenced discussions and amendments, demonstrating state-level technical assistance on privacy legislation.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.
relevant_rules: American Data Privacy and Protection Act (proposed)

example_background: Following CPRA's passage, the newly formed California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated formal rulemaking in 2022 to modify and expand CCPA regulations. Topics included automated decision-making, cybersecurity audits for businesses handling data of 100,000+ consumers, risk assessments for high-risk processing, and insurance contracts for data processors. The California AG's office provided input during public workshops and comment periods, sharing enforcement insights from early CCPA audits to shape practical implementation.
process_and_outcome: After 15+ public meetings and thousands of comments, final regulations were adopted in March 2024, effective for various provisions starting March 2025. This process fulfilled legislative mandates and incorporated AG expertise on privacy compliance.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.

example_background: Game publisher Activision Blizzard was flagged in the initial 2020 CCPA audits for not providing a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' opt-out link on its websites, despite selling or sharing player data for behavioral advertising based on gameplay, device info, and preferences. This affected California gamers, and the lack of notice prevented users from exercising rights under the law.
process_and_outcome: The California AG issued a public warning on June 30, 2020. Activision added the required link and updated policies shortly after, avoiding penalties through swift remediation.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.

example_name: California AG Input on Age-Appropriate Design Code Act
example_background: In 2022, California pursued the Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (AB 2273), modeled after the UK's code, requiring online services likely accessed by children to prioritize privacy by design and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments. The AG's office provided technical assistance during legislative drafting, advising on alignment with CCPA/CPRA, enforcement mechanisms, and integration with federal children's privacy laws.
process_and_outcome: The bill passed the legislature but was vetoed by Governor Newsom in September 2022 due to federal preemption concerns. AG comments helped refine the proposal and informed ongoing state-federal privacy dialogues.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.

example_name: First CCPA Penalty Against Sephora Successor Action
example_background: Building on the 2020 warning, Sephora faced further review, but more notably, the AG's early actions set precedents. In a related development, the CPPA's formation involved AG transition of enforcement duties, with advice on handling ongoing CCPA matters like opt-out compliance.
process_and_outcome: No additional penalty for Sephora, but the case contributed to over 50 warnings issued, leading to industry-wide updates. CPPA assumed CCPA enforcement in 2023.
source_rule: (g) Provide technical assistance and advice to the Legislature, upon request, with respect to privacy-related legislation.

example_background: The California Manage and Distribute Personal Information Act (CMPA, AB 1836) was introduced in 2024 to regulate data brokers more stringently, requiring registration and deletion requests. The California AG's office offered technical input during committee hearings, drawing from CCPA enforcement data on data broker practices and recommending amendments for feasibility.
process_and_outcome: The bill advanced through committees with modifications based on AG and industry feedback, positioning it for potential 2025 enactment. This exemplified direct legislative assistance on privacy policy.

example_background: The California Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) maintains records of millions of residents' personal information, including driver's licenses, vehicle registrations, and addresses. To align with privacy laws, the DMV implemented processes for notifying individuals about data usage and providing access, deletion, and opt-out rights. This was part of a broader state agency compliance program following CCPA enactment.
process_and_outcome: An internal audit and CPPA review confirmed adherence, with the DMV receiving guidance on proceedings and maintaining private probable cause reviews where needed. No enforcement actions were taken, and the agency continued operations with updated notices.

A proceeding held for the purpose of considering probable cause shall be private unless the alleged violator files with the agency a written request that the proceeding be public.

The data included IP addresses and device identifiers linked to personal profiles. After FTC actions and state scrutiny, the California Attorney General investigated ongoing practices under updated privacy frameworks.
process_and_outcome: Vizio agreed to a $2.2 million settlement with California and other states, including injunctive relief to improve disclosures and data handling. The process involved administrative review and determination of cause.

process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured a $1 million settlement, requiring Instacart to revise privacy policies, add opt-out features, and train staff. The settlement stemmed from an investigation establishing cause.

example_name: California AG v. Sephora Data Sharing Notice (2022)
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform that shared personal information of California residents with third-party advertising partners for cross-context behavioral advertising without providing the required 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or clear notice as mandated under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). The company's privacy policy did not adequately disclose these sharing practices, and users were not informed of their rights to opt out of such data sharing. This occurred during the initial enforcement period following the CCPA's effective date on January 1, 2020, when businesses were required to implement mechanisms for consumers to exercise their privacy rights. Sephora's practices involved tracking consumer behavior across websites and apps, sharing data like browsing history and purchase intent with entities such as LiveRamp and Criteo for targeted ads, without explicit consumer consent or opt-out options prominently displayed.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General initiated an enforcement action in 2022, resulting in Sephora agreeing to a settlement that included a $1.2 million payment, implementation of a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link on its website, updates to privacy notices, and ongoing compliance training for employees. No finding of probable cause was detailed publicly as bypassing the notice requirement, but the action proceeded after standard regulatory notifications.
source_rule: 1798.199.50. example_name: California AG v. DoorDash CCPA Enforcement (2024)
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, collected extensive personal data from California users including names, addresses, payment details, and location data through its app and website. The company shared this data with third parties for analytics and advertising without providing adequate notice or opt-out mechanisms as required by CCPA. From 2020 onward, DoorDash's privacy practices involved geofencing data sharing and failure to honor 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' requests promptly, affecting millions of consumers. The platform's business model relied on data-driven personalization, but disclosures were buried in lengthy policy documents without clear links or summaries.
process_and_outcome: In June 2024, the California AG announced a $375,000 settlement following an investigation into CCPA violations. DoorDash committed to enhancing privacy controls, providing clear opt-out links, and conducting regular audits. The enforcement followed notifications to DoorDash outlining alleged issues and evidence.

Consumers submitting inquiries were not informed of their rights under the law, and the company's website lacked compliant notices during the period under review.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) issued its first enforcement action in August 2023, fining GFCI $1.2 million after providing prior notice of violations and evidence summary. GFCI was required to cease noncompliant practices and update systems.

process_and_outcome: In March 2024, a $325,000 settlement was reached with the California AG after an investigation notified Farmers of deficiencies, requiring policy updates, consumer rights portals, and employee training programs.

For California users, Apple provided dashboards for accessing, deleting, and opting out of data sharing, handling billions of data points while balancing product functionality with regulatory requirements.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, collected geolocation data, purchase history, and other personal information from California users through its mobile app and website. The company shared this data with analytics providers and service partners without consistently providing or prominently displaying a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link as required for businesses that sell personal information under the CCPA. This occurred shortly after the law's effective date, during a time when DoorDash was rapidly expanding its user base and data collection practices amid the COVID-19 pandemic.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General's CCPA enforcement team detected non-compliance through proactive audits. After establishing probable cause, the agency pursued resolution via an Assurance of Discontinuation, which DoorDash signed. The agreement mandated adding the opt-out link to the footer of all pages and app screens, implementing Global Privacy Control recognition, conducting annual privacy training, and providing compliance certifications for one year.
source_rule: 1798.199.55. (a) When the agency determines there is probable cause for believing this title has been violated, it shall hold a hearing to determine if a violation has or violations have occurred. The agency shall have all the powers granted by that chapter. example_background: GTM Payload provided software services that enabled websites to deploy tracking technologies, including those that resulted in the sale of California residents' personal information without adequate opt-out mechanisms. The company's tools facilitated the transfer of data such as browsing behavior to third parties for targeted advertising. This came under scrutiny after the California Privacy Rights Act amendments expanded CCPA enforcement powers, effective January 1, 2023, which included the ability to seek injunctive relief and civil penalties through administrative processes.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), upon finding probable cause, issued its first enforcement action under the CPRA. GTM Payload agreed to an Assurance of Discontinuation and stipulated injunction, requiring cessation of violations, implementation of a CCPA/CPRA compliance program, biannual reporting, and record-keeping for five years. The case marked the agency's initial use of formal hearing powers outlined in the statute.
source_rule: 1798.199.55. (a) When the agency determines there is probable cause for believing this title has been violated, it shall hold a hearing to determine if a violation has or violations have occurred. Notice shall be given and the hearing conducted in accordance with the Administrative Procedure Act (Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code). The agency shall have all the powers granted by that chapter. If the agency determines on the basis of the hearing conducted pursuant to this subdivision that a violation or violations have occurred, it shall issue an order that may require the violator to do all or any of the following:

example_name: Microsoft Compliance with CCPA After Investigation
example_background: Microsoft faced early CCPA scrutiny for its advertising practices, including the use of tracking technologies across its properties like MSN and Outlook.com that shared user data with partners. Following public enforcement signals from the Attorney General listing potential violators, Microsoft reviewed and adjusted its practices to align with consumer rights.
process_and_outcome: Pre-litigation engagement led Microsoft to voluntarily enhance its privacy controls, adding 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links, supporting Global Privacy Control, and publishing detailed CCPA rights pages. No formal hearing or penalty ensued, demonstrating proactive remediation post-probable cause signaling.

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer operating an online platform, collected personal information from California residents through its website and mobile app. The company used tracking technologies such as pixels and cookies to share data with third-party analytics providers for advertising purposes without providing a clear notice at collection or offering consumers a mechanism to opt out of the sale of their personal information as required under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). This occurred during a period when the CCPA had recently taken effect in 2020, and Sephora continued these data-sharing practices despite the new legal requirements. The issue came to light through automated monitoring and consumer complaints about targeted advertising based on their browsing activity on the Sephora site.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) conducted an investigation and issued a notice of violation in January 2024. Sephora entered into a settlement agreement, agreeing to pay an administrative fine of $1.2 million, which represented 1,650 violations at $725 each after adjustments. Sephora also committed to implementing a comprehensive CCPA compliance program, including regular privacy audits, employee training, and updates to its privacy notices and opt-out mechanisms. The CPPA published the settlement details publicly.
source_rule: (1) Cease and desist violation of this title. (2) Subject to Section 1798.155, pay an administrative fine of up to two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) for each violation, or up to seven thousand five hundred dollars ($7,500) for each intentional violation and each violation involving the personal information of minor consumers. When the agency determines that no violation has occurred, it shall publish a declaration so stating.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_name: California AG v. Student Loan Servicer
example_background: An unnamed student loan servicer operating a platform for loan management collected sensitive personal information from California borrowers, including financial details and Social Security numbers. In 2020-2021, the servicer sold this data to marketing firms without offering opt-out rights or clear disclosures under CCPA, impacting thousands of consumers seeking loan refinancing or management services.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General settled in October 2022 for $100,000 penalty. The servicer was required to reform privacy practices, honor opt-outs, update notices, and train staff for a multi-year period.
source_rule: (1) Cease and desist violation of this title. example_background: RealPage, Inc., provided software to landlords for tenant screening and property management, collecting extensive personal data on California rental applicants including credit history, criminal records, and eviction data. From 2020 onward, RealPage disclosed this data to numerous third parties without CCPA-compliant opt-out mechanisms or notices, affecting prospective tenants across multiple housing platforms.
process_and_outcome: Settlement with California Attorney General in December 2022 for $100,000. RealPage implemented changes to data practices, including opt-out processes and compliance reporting.

When the agency determines that no violation has occurred, it shall publish a declaration so stating.

example_background: Google launched the Buzz social feature in Gmail, automatically enrolling users and exposing email contacts publicly without consent. This affected millions of Gmail users whose private contact lists were shared. Multiple parties including Google engineers, product managers, and executives were involved in the rollout. The feature violated user privacy expectations and led to complaints about unintended data exposure.
process_and_outcome: The FTC investigated and settled with Google for $22.5 million, the largest privacy settlement at the time. Google discontinued Buzz and implemented a comprehensive privacy program with biennial audits. Joint and several liability was implied across responsible parties within Google.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: CMG Stores, operator of GNC franchises, sold personal information collected from website visitors to third parties for advertising without providing opt-out rights or required notices. Data included browsing history and purchase details from health supplement consumers. The practices persisted post-CCPA effective date, involving web analytics and ad tech vendors.
process_and_outcome: Settlement reached for $250,000, requiring privacy program enhancements, notices, and audits.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: Equifax experienced a breach exposing 147 million consumers' data due to unpatched software vulnerabilities. Responsibility spanned IT, security teams, executives, and board for failing to apply patches and monitor systems. Multiple vendors and internal departments contributed to the lapses over months.
process_and_outcome: Class action and FTC settlements totaled over $700 million. Equifax paid $575 million in consumer settlement, with joint liability across corporate entities and officers. Remediation included security overhauls.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: Facebook allowed third-party app to harvest data from 87 million users without consent, shared with Cambridge Analytica for political advertising. Responsibility involved Facebook developers, API teams, and executives who approved data access policies. Data flowed to multiple entities including SCL Group.
process_and_outcome: FTC settled for $5 billion, largest privacy penalty ever, with oversight board. UK ICO fined £500,000. Joint liability across Facebook subsidiaries and partners.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: Farmers Insurance subsidiaries sold consumer data to affiliates and third parties without opt-out mechanisms or proper notices under CCPA. Practices involved sharing policyholder details for marketing, affecting thousands of California residents across multiple business units.
process_and_outcome: Joint settlement of $1.25 million with four subsidiaries, requiring compliance program implementation, training, and assessments.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: Uber suffered two breaches: one exposing 57 million users' data in 2016, and another 600,000 drivers'. Uber paid hackers to delete data and hid incidents. Multiple executives, including security chief, concealed breaches from regulators and users.
process_and_outcome: FTC and state AGs settled for $148 million in penalties and restitution. Uber enhanced security and reporting. Joint liability across officers and company.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: Microsoft established a robust privacy program post-CCPA and GDPR, including data protection officers, regular audits, and automated tools for consumer rights requests. The company handles data across cloud services, ensuring multiple teams coordinate for compliance with notice, opt-out, and deletion rights.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft publishes transparency reports and undergoes third-party audits, demonstrating adherence through certifications like ISO 27701. No major enforcement actions under CCPA to date.
source_rule: 1798.160. (b) If two or more persons are responsible for any violation or violations, they shall be jointly and severally liable.

example_background: Lens.com, an online contact lens retailer, collected consumer data such as purchase histories, contact details, and browsing patterns from California customers using its e-commerce platform. The site employed cookies and pixels from third parties to track and share data for retargeting ads, constituting a sale under CCPA, but failed to post a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or provide equivalent notices during the 2020-2022 period. Privacy policies were buried and did not clearly describe data-sharing practices at collection points. This was part of the AG's broader enforcement against e-commerce sites.
process_and_outcome: A $95,000 settlement was announced on August 25, 2022, with 95% deposited into the Consumer Privacy Fund. Lens.com implemented required notices, opt-out links, and employee training programs.
source_rule: (A) Ninety-five percent of any administrative fine assessed pursuant to this paragraph shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: CPRA Private Right of Action - California v. Marriott
example_background: Marriott International, operating hotel booking sites, experienced a data breach in 2020 exposing California residents' personal information including names, addresses, and passport numbers from over 5.2 million guests. The incident stemmed from legacy systems acquired through mergers without adequate security updates. Under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA, amending CCPA), consumers pursued claims for failure to maintain reasonable security, triggering the private right of action provision.
process_and_outcome: Marriott settled class actions for $52 million in 2023, with portions funding consumer redress. While not a direct AG fine, it referenced CCPA/CPRA penalty structures, and related AG inquiries led to compliance undertakings. Funds indirectly support privacy enforcement ecosystems.
source_rule: (A) Ninety-five percent of any administrative fine assessed pursuant to this paragraph shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: FTC v. California Choice Case Settlement
example_background: California Choice, an online lead generation service for insurance quotes, collected consumer data like phone numbers and emails from California users and sold it to affiliates without proper CCPA opt-out notices. Operating from 2020 onward, the site used aggressive data-sharing practices for marketing, lacking clear disclosures. This intersected with FTC deceptive practices scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: A joint FTC and AG settlement in 2023 imposed $1.1 million in penalties, with CCPA portions (95%) to the Consumer Privacy Subfund. The company ceased certain data practices and improved notices.
source_rule: (A) Ninety-five percent of any administrative fine assessed pursuant to this paragraph shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: DoorDash CCPA Data Processing Violation Settlement
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform serving millions of California users, processed personal information from drivers and customers by sharing it with third parties for analytics and advertising without adequate disclosures or opt-out options prior to CCPA compliance deadlines. The company collected data including location information, order histories, and device identifiers from over 100,000 consumers annually, meeting CCPA applicability criteria. This issue surfaced amid heightened scrutiny on tech platforms handling consumer data during the early enforcement period of the law.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA investigated DoorDash's practices, leading to a settlement in August 2024. DoorDash agreed to pay $375,000 in penalties, of which 5% or $18,750 was directed to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund per CCPA subdivision (d) of Section 1798.199.85. Additional remedies included enhanced privacy notices, deletion of unlawfully collected data, and a three-year monitoring period by the agency.
source_rule: (B) Five percent of any administrative fine assessed pursuant to this paragraph shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section

example_name: CPPA Action Against GFC Global Insurance
example_background: GFC Global Insurance Agency LLC failed to respond to verified consumer requests to delete personal information and did not provide required CCPA disclosures on its website regarding data collection and sales practices. As a business subject to CCPA due to handling data from California consumers in the insurance sector, the company neglected to implement necessary privacy controls, impacting customers seeking to exercise their rights.
process_and_outcome: Following a CPPA investigation prompted by consumer complaints, a settlement was reached in September 2024. GFC agreed to pay $100,000, with 5% of the fine ($5,000) deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund pursuant to CCPA Section 1798.199.85(d). Remediation included system updates for handling deletion requests and mandatory training.
source_rule: (B) Five percent of any administrative fine assessed pursuant to this paragraph shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Sections 1798.105 and 1798.130

example_background: RealPage, a software provider for landlords, shared detailed consumer data including rental histories and income information with clients without CCPA-compliant opt-out notices or transparency. This affected millions of California tenants whose sensitive financial data was processed by the company, which qualified under CCPA thresholds due to its scale and revenue.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured a $3.5 million settlement in December 2023. Per CCPA provisions, 5% of the administrative fine ($175,000) was allocated to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund. RealPage was required to update its privacy policy, provide opt-out mechanisms, and undergo independent audits for three years.
source_rule: (B) Five percent of any administrative fine assessed pursuant to this paragraph shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section

example_background: Lens.com, an online contact lens retailer, did not honor CCPA consumer requests to opt out of the sale of personal information and failed to disclose data sales to third parties. The company collected data such as purchase details and health-related information from California customers, triggering CCPA obligations.
process_and_outcome: In a February 2024 CPPA enforcement action, Lens.com settled for $92,500 in penalties, including 5% ($4,625) to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund under CCPA Section 1798.199.85(d). The settlement mandated technical fixes for opt-out processing and regular compliance reporting.

example_name: CMG Broadcast Stations CCPA Compliance Program
example_background: CMG Media, operating numerous broadcast stations in California, developed and implemented a CCPA compliance program including website privacy banners, Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal recognition, and centralized data request handling systems. This proactive approach addressed data collection from viewers and users across news and entertainment platforms, ensuring transparency for California residents.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement action was taken following voluntary disclosures and audits. The company maintained ongoing compliance, contributing to the Consumer Privacy Fund through standard operations without fines, demonstrating effective adherence to CCPA including fund allocation mechanisms.

example_background: Sirius XM Radio Inc., a satellite radio provider with significant California subscribers, integrated CCPA-compliant opt-out functionalities across its apps and websites, processing data sales disclosures and consumer rights requests efficiently. This covered listener profiles and usage data shared with advertisers.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA approved Sirius XM's modifications to its privacy practices in 2023 without penalties. Compliance efforts aligned with CCPA fund deposit requirements for any future assessments, serving as a model for media companies.

example_name: CPRA Grant Program Announcement by California Privacy Protection Agency
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), established under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) which amended the CCPA, initiated a process to solicit and review applications for grants aimed at funding projects that enhance consumer privacy education and protection. This program was enabled by funds collected from enforcement penalties under CCPA/CPRA provisions. In 2023, the CPPA announced the availability of grants pursuant to Civil Code §1798.160(b)(2), focusing on initiatives such as developing privacy curricula for schools, supporting small business compliance training, and community outreach on data rights. The agency outlined eligibility criteria, application procedures, and review standards in public notices, inviting submissions from nonprofits, educational institutions, and local governments. This marked one of the first uses of penalty-derived funds to support broader privacy ecosystem development in California.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA opened the application period in late 2023, reviewed submissions based on merit and alignment with statutory goals, and awarded initial grants totaling several million dollars to selected recipients. Ongoing monitoring ensures grant-funded projects deliver measurable outcomes in privacy awareness.
source_rule: (k) Solicit, review, and approve applications for grants to the extent funds are available pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_background: Gushcloud Inc., a social media influencer marketing firm based in California, processed personal information of consumers without implementing required safeguards under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), an amendment to CCPA. Between March 2022 and February 2023, the company sold or shared data of up to 238,000 California consumers to third parties without honoring opt-out requests or providing privacy notices detailing data processing activities. The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) identified these issues through complaint reviews and audits, noting failures in data mapping, risk assessments, and consumer request handling.
process_and_outcome: In August 2024, the CPPA imposed California's first CPRA administrative fine of $1.2 million. Gushcloud was ordered to cease non-compliant processing, delete affected data, and implement a CPRA compliance program with regular reporting.
source_rule: (k) Solicit, review, and approve applications for grants to the extent funds are available pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: CPPA Consumer Privacy Education Grant Awards
example_background: Under the grant authority in CCPA §1798.160(b)(2), the California Privacy Protection Agency solicited applications in 2024 for projects promoting consumer awareness of CCPA/CPRA rights. Funds from prior enforcement actions were allocated to support multilingual outreach, digital literacy programs, and tools for exercising data rights. The agency published detailed requests for proposals (RFPs), established review committees with criteria on impact, feasibility, and equity, and prioritized applicants serving underserved communities. This initiative built on earlier rounds, demonstrating structured use of available funds to fulfill legislative intent for public education.
process_and_outcome: Applications were reviewed and grants awarded to organizations like the California Health Care Foundation and others for specific projects, with performance metrics tracked through annual reports. Total funding exceeded $3 million across recipients.
source_rule: (k) Solicit, review, and approve applications for grants to the extent funds are available pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: CPPA Grant Solicitation for Small Business Compliance
example_background: The CPPA issued a formal solicitation for grant applications under §1798.160(b)(2)(k) specifically targeting resources for small businesses to achieve CCPA/CPRA compliance. This included funding for compliance software, training workshops, and consultant services. The process involved public notices, webinars for applicants, and a multi-stage review by agency staff and external experts to ensure funds supported accessible tools for entities under revenue thresholds but still subject to the laws.
process_and_outcome: Grants approved and disbursed in 2024 to qualified small businesses and support organizations, with funds monitored for effective deployment.
source_rule: (k) Solicit, review, and approve applications for grants to the extent funds are available pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: California Public Utilities Commission v. Administrative Law Judge Decision Rejection (2019)
example_background: In a rate case involving Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E), the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) reviewed a proposed decision issued by an Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) following formal hearings conducted under Government Code Section 11517. The ALJ's decision addressed PG&E's application for recovery of costs associated with wildfire mitigation and infrastructure investments. The CPUC commissioners conducted a de novo review of the record, including evidentiary transcripts, exhibits, and party briefs submitted during the proceeding. Specific issues included the appropriate rate of return on equity, capital structure adjustments, and allocation of vegetation management expenses. The agency identified discrepancies between the ALJ's findings and the evidentiary record, particularly regarding the prudence of certain expenditures and their alignment with statutory mandates for cost recovery. This process unfolded amid broader scrutiny of PG&E's operations following devastating wildfires in Northern California, which had prompted legislative reforms to utility regulation. The CPUC's handling of the ALJ decision became part of a larger examination of procedural fairness in agency adjudications.
process_and_outcome: The CPUC rejected portions of the ALJ's proposed decision in its final decision issued on December 19, 2019. The agency provided a detailed 15-page statement of reasons explaining the rejection, citing specific evidence from the record that contradicted the ALJ's conclusions on cost prudence and rate calculations. Modifications were made to authorize $2.5 billion in revenue requirements, lower than PG&E's request but higher than the ALJ's recommendation. PG&E was directed to implement the approved rates, and the decision was appealed to state courts but upheld on procedural grounds.
source_rule: 1798.199.60. Whenever the agency rejects the decision of an administrative law judge made pursuant to Section 11517 of the Government Code, the agency shall state the reasons in writing for rejecting the decision.

example_name: Department of Social Services Rejection of ALJ Decision in Medi-Cal Provider Appeal (2021)
example_background: A Medi-Cal provider appealed an audit determination by the California Department of Health Care Services (DHCS) that resulted in a $1.2 million overpayment assessment for improper billing practices over a three-year period. The administrative hearing was conducted pursuant to Government Code Section 11517, where the ALJ reviewed documentation including patient records, billing codes, and compliance with federal Medicaid requirements. The ALJ issued a proposed decision reducing the overpayment to $450,000 based on partial acceptance of the provider's documentation and reinterpretation of billing guidelines. DHCS staff argued that the ALJ overlooked key regulatory definitions and patterns in the billing data that indicated systemic issues. The case arose from routine post-payment audits aimed at ensuring fiscal integrity in California's Medicaid program, which serves over 13 million enrollees. The agency's review focused on consistency with established policies and prior precedents.
process_and_outcome: DHCS rejected the ALJ's proposed decision on March 15, 2021, issuing a 10-page written statement specifying reasons, including citation to specific evidence pages where the ALJ's findings deviated from the record and regulatory standards. The original overpayment amount was reinstated with interest, and the provider was ordered to remit payment within 30 days. The provider pursued judicial review, but the superior court denied the writ petition, affirming the agency's procedures.
source_rule: 1798.199.60. Whenever the agency rejects the decision of an administrative law judge made pursuant to Section 11517 of the Government Code, the agency shall state the reasons in writing for rejecting the decision.
involved_parties: California Department of Health Care Services (DHCS)
Administrative Law Judge (Office of Administrative Hearings)

example_name: Fair Political Practices Commission v. ALJ in Conflict of Interest Case (2018)
example_background: The California Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) investigated a local government official for failing to disclose economic interests in a contract award process. The administrative proceeding under Government Code Section 11517 involved testimony from the official, city staff, and ethics experts, with exhibits including disclosure forms and meeting minutes. The ALJ's proposed decision dismissed the violation, finding insufficient evidence of knowing intent and interpreting disclosure thresholds narrowly. FPPC staff contended that the ALJ misapplied statutory definitions of 'economic interest' and disregarded circumstantial evidence from the record. This case emerged from increased enforcement following high-profile public corruption scandals in California municipalities, prompting stricter adherence to political reform laws.
process_and_outcome: On November 8, 2018, the FPPC rejected the ALJ decision with a comprehensive written explanation detailing evidentiary inconsistencies and legal errors. The official was found in violation, resulting in a $25,000 fine and a one-year disqualification from similar roles. The decision set a precedent for intent standards in disclosure cases.
source_rule: 1798.199.60. Whenever the agency rejects the decision of an administrative law judge made pursuant to Section 11517 of the Government Code, the agency shall state the reasons in writing for rejecting the decision.

example_name: Department of Motor Vehicles License Revocation Appeal (2020)
example_background: A commercial driver's license was revoked following multiple violations documented in Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) records. The hearing under Government Code Section 11517 examined logs, witness statements, and violation reports. The ALJ proposed reinstating the license with probationary conditions, citing mitigating factors like training completion. DMV review identified overlooked prior convictions and inconsistencies with safety regulations. The case was part of broader efforts to enhance road safety through rigorous licensing standards.
process_and_outcome: DMV rejected the proposed decision on July 22, 2020, providing a 7-page statement referencing specific record evidence and statutory criteria. The revocation was upheld, with no appeal success.
source_rule: 1798.199.60. Whenever the agency rejects the decision of an administrative law judge made pursuant to Section 11517 of the Government Code, the agency shall state the reasons in writing for rejecting the decision.

The Section 11517 hearing included expert testimony on code compliance. The ALJ recommended revocation but CSLB found the penalty disproportionate to findings.
process_and_outcome: CSLB modified the ALJ decision on September 14, 2022, issuing written reasons for reducing to suspension and probation, citing precedent and rehabilitation evidence.

example_name: California AG v. Sephora Data Practices Settlement
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform that collected personal information from California residents through its website and mobile app. The company implemented a 'Customer Data Platform' that tracked consumer behavior across multiple websites without providing clear notice or opt-out mechanisms at the point of collection. This involved sharing data with third-party vendors for advertising purposes. The practices came under scrutiny as part of broader enforcement efforts following the implementation of the California Consumer Privacy Act in 2020. Sephora's methods included monitoring user interactions such as browsing history, purchase patterns, and location data, which were aggregated and sold or shared without adequate disclosures required under the law.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General initiated an enforcement action in 2022, alleging failure to provide notice and opt-out rights. Sephora entered into a settlement agreement in June 2022, agreeing to pay $1.2 million in penalties and implement a comprehensive privacy program including automated opt-out tools, annual audits, and employee training for three years. No court ruling was issued as the matter resolved through settlement.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120, § 1798.135

example_name: California AG v. DoorDash CCPA Enforcement
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, collected extensive personal data from users including names, addresses, payment information, order histories, and device identifiers through its app and website. The company shared this data with third parties for analytics and advertising without prominently displaying a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or providing accessible opt-out options as mandated. This occurred amid increased regulatory focus on tech platforms post-CCPA effective date of January 1, 2020. DoorDash's practices involved over 30 million monthly active users in California, amplifying the scope of data handling.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the California Attorney General announced a settlement after investigation, with DoorDash paying $375,000 in penalties and committing to 30-month corrective measures including adding visible opt-out links, data retention policies, and compliance certifications. The action was resolved without litigation.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_name: FTC v. Flo Health Data Sharing Case
example_background: Flo Health, a menstrual cycle tracking app, gathered sensitive health data from millions of users including California residents, such as cycle dates, symptoms, and fertility indicators. Between 2019 and 2021, Flo shared this data with third-party analytics firms like Google and Facebook despite user privacy assurances. The sharing occurred through SDK integrations that transmitted data without explicit consent or opt-out provisions. This case gained attention after a New York Times report in 2021 highlighted the risks to reproductive privacy, especially relevant under CCPA for California users.
process_and_outcome: The FTC investigated starting in 2021 and reached a settlement in 2021 requiring Flo to prohibit future sharing of sensitive health data, undergo independent audits for two years, and delete prior shared data. No monetary penalty was imposed, but compliance obligations extended forward.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
relevant_rules: CCPA health data provisions, Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140

example_name: Texas AG v. Google CCPA/CPRA Notice Violations
example_background: Google's advertising platform collected and processed personal data from California users across its services including Search, YouTube, and Android devices. A multi-state investigation revealed that from 2018 to 2022, Google failed to adequately inform users about data sales/sharing for targeted ads and did not provide functional 'Do Not Sell' opt-out links until after CPRA amendments. Data included browsing activity, location, and interests, affecting over 300 million users. The inquiry focused on pre- and post-CCPA implementation periods.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Texas AG and seven other states settled with Google for $391.5 million total, with Texas receiving $85 million. Google agreed to update privacy notices and disclosures for five years without admitting liability.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.

example_name: California AG v. Gittigidiyor CCPA Settlement
example_background: Gittigidiyor, a Turkish e-commerce platform operating internationally, maintained a US-facing website that collected personal information from California consumers such as names, emails, IP addresses, and browsing data. From 2020 to 2022, the site did not post required CCPA privacy notices, including details on collected data categories, purposes, and third-party sharing. It also lacked 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links despite engaging in data sales for advertising. The platform processed transactions and user registrations from California users.
process_and_outcome: The California AG settled in October 2023 for $1.2 million in civil penalties, marking the first enforcement against a non-US company under CCPA. Gittigidiyor was required to implement compliant notices and retain records.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
involved_parties: Gittigidiyor Internet ve Bilişim Teknolojileri A.Ş.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.130

example_name: CMT Blues v. Crunchbase CCPA Litigation Dismissal
example_background: CMT Blues Digital, a music licensing firm, alleged that Crunchbase, a business information platform, collected and sold personal data scraped from public sources including LinkedIn profiles of California residents without consent. The complaint, filed in 2022, claimed Crunchbase failed to provide CCPA-required notices and opt-outs for data sales used in its database services. Crunchbase maintained the data was publicly available and not subject to CCPA sales definitions.
process_and_outcome: In July 2023, the Northern District of California dismissed the case with prejudice, ruling that Crunchbase's practices did not constitute 'sales' under CCPA as no monetary consideration flowed directly for personal information. The decision clarified CCPA scope for public data.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140 (sale definition)

example_name: FTC v. GoodRx CCPA/CPRA Health Data Enforcement
example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount platform, collected health data including prescription records, conditions, and biometric identifiers from California users via its apps and websites from 2017 to 2023. The company shared this data with advertising networks like Facebook, Google, and Criteo for targeted ads without user authorization or proper safeguards. Users expected confidentiality, but SDKs transmitted hashed data enabling re-identification.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled in February 2023, imposing a $1.5 million civil penalty, bans on sharing health data for ads, requirements for HIPAA-level protections, and triennial audits for 20 years.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
relevant_rules: CCPA/CPRA sensitive data rules, Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140

example_name: California AG v. Farmers Insurance CCPA Settlement
example_background: Farmers Insurance Group maintained websites and apps that collected personal and sensitive data from California policyholders, including driver behavior via telematics devices, health inferences, and geolocation. From 2020 onward, the company sold or shared this data with affiliates and vendors without clear CCPA disclosures or opt-out links, despite tracking over 1 million customers.
process_and_outcome: Settled in August 2024 for $190,000 penalty plus injunctive relief including privacy program enhancements, notice updates, and monitoring for two years.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.

example_name: In re Zoom Video Communications CCPA Class Action
example_background: Zoom Video Communications provided video conferencing services and collected user data including meeting metadata, device info, and IP addresses from California users during the 2020 pandemic surge. Plaintiffs alleged Zoom shared data with Facebook and Google for analytics without opt-out notices, classifying it as a 'sale' under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Northern District of California dismissed CCPA claims, ruling no 'sale' occurred as sharing was for service provision, not monetary exchange. Plaintiffs appealed but claims were severed, upholding compliance interpretation.
source_rule: 1798.199.70. No administrative action brought pursuant to this title alleging a violation of any of the provisions of this title shall be commenced more than five years after the date on which the violation occurred.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140

example_name: California AG Enforcement Against Gatos Silver
example_background: Gatos Silver Inc., a publicly traded mining company focused on silver production in Mexico, maintained a website that collected personal data from California visitors, including names, contact details, and investor information. The firm did not post a privacy notice, provide opt-out links for data sales, or process consumer rights requests during the period under review. This lack of CCPA infrastructure persisted despite operating in California markets.
process_and_outcome: Investigated by the Attorney General, Gatos Silver settled in June 2022 via Assurance of Discontinuance, agreeing to implement a full CCPA program with notices, opt-outs, and request handling procedures. No fine was assessed.

example_name: FTC Enforcement Against California Choice Data Brokers
example_background: In a landmark action, the Federal Trade Commission targeted nine data brokers, several based in California including Tower Research Capital and others, for collecting and selling sensitive personal data such as health, financial, and location information from millions of consumers without consent or proper safeguards. The brokers aggregated data from public records, apps, and websites, selling it for profiling purposes. This overlapped with CCPA jurisdiction as many operated in California.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled in December 2024, requiring the brokers to honor opt-out signals from tools like Global Privacy Control (GPC), delete collected data, and undergo privacy assessments. No direct CCPA citation, but it reinforced consumer control mechanisms.
source_rule: 1798.199.65. example_name: TikTok Children's Privacy Settlement with California AG
example_background: TikTok collected personal information from children under 16, including biometric data from videos, location data, and behavioral signals, without verifiable parental consent. The platform's algorithm-driven content sharing exposed this data internally and to advertisers. California joined a multi-state action highlighting CCPA overlaps with child privacy.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, TikTok agreed to pay $92 million to affected states, including California, and implement stricter age verification, data deletion for minors, and parental controls. Additional injunctive relief mandated compliance enhancements.

The company provided detailed privacy notices, accessible 'My Activity' dashboards for data access and deletion, Global Privacy Control support, and opt-out tools for personalized ads. Regular third-party audits verified adherence, with public transparency reports detailing request volumes and fulfillment rates exceeding 90%.
process_and_outcome: Google voluntarily submitted to CCPA audits and published annual privacy reports showing high compliance rates. No enforcement actions were taken, and the company received recognition in regulatory guidance for best practices in handling consumer requests.

Over a multi-year period, the platform shared user data with advertisers without opt-out links, impacting California subscribers.
process_and_outcome: Settlement in October 2022 for $500,000, mandating updates to privacy practices, request processing systems, and training for three years.

example_name: California AG v. Gatorade Data Sharing
example_background: Gatorade, a beverage company under PepsiCo, maintained a mobile app called Gatorade Gx Sweat Patch App that collected biometric data, including sweat composition metrics, from users tracking hydration and fitness. The app shared this sensitive personal information with third-party vendors without explicit consent or clear disclosure to California users. Data collection occurred during app usage sessions, involving transmission to analytics and marketing partners. This raised issues regarding the handling of biometric identifiers in consumer products.
process_and_outcome: Following an investigation by the California Attorney General, Gatorade settled in September 2022 by paying $915,000 in penalties. The company agreed to cease unauthorized sharing of biometric data, provide prominent privacy notices, and conduct regular compliance audits for two years.

The practices involved tracking user locations in real-time and sharing with analytics firms. Following audits and complaints, the Attorney General examined compliance with CCPA disclosure requirements.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, DoorDash settled CCPA violations for $375,000, agreeing to enhance privacy notices, implement opt-out tools, and conduct regular audits. The settlement addressed failures in notifying consumers about data sharing practices.

example_background: Google collected and shared precise location data of California residents through its Android and services ecosystem without always providing clear notice of secondary uses or obtaining necessary consents. This involved location history data shared with advertisers. The case highlighted notification gaps in user agreements and privacy policies affecting millions of users.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC) fined Google $392 million for location tracking practices that paralleled CCPA concerns, with California regulators referencing it in CCPA audits. Google updated its notice practices and data controls in response.
source_rule: (2) That the defendant or defendants in the action were notified, by actual or constructive notice, of the imposition of the administrative fines.

example_background: Meta Platforms (Facebook) updated its privacy policies and user interfaces to provide detailed notices about data collection and sharing practices for California users following CCPA implementation. This included prominent opt-out links for data sales and granular control panels for privacy settings. The changes were part of broader global compliance efforts post-GDPR.
process_and_outcome: California regulators reviewed and accepted Meta's updated notices during routine audits, with no enforcement actions taken. The company demonstrated adherence through transparent disclosures and functional opt-out mechanisms, avoiding penalties.
source_rule: (2) That the defendant or defendants in the action were notified, by actual or constructive notice, of the imposition of the administrative fines.

example_background: Apple introduced App Privacy Labels and system-wide privacy nutrition labels in iOS, providing California users with clear, upfront notices about data collection practices by apps. This included details on data types collected and purposes, integrated into the App Store and settings. The initiative exceeded CCPA requirements by standardizing notices across the ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: CPPA and Attorney General commended Apple's approach in public statements, using it as a model for compliance. No violations were cited, and it facilitated consumer awareness without regulatory intervention.
source_rule: (2) That the defendant or defendants in the action were notified, by actual or constructive notice, of the imposition of the administrative fines.

example_background: Clearview AI, a facial recognition technology company, collected billions of facial images from public websites without consent, raising privacy concerns under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). In 2022, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement action alleging violations of CCPA data practices. During the administrative proceeding, a subpoena was issued for documents related to data collection and consumer opt-out mechanisms. Clearview AI did not fully produce the requested documents, prompting the CPPA to seek a superior court order compelling production. The company continued to delay compliance post-order, affecting the statute of limitations calculations under CCPA enforcement provisions.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA obtained a court order in Alameda County Superior Court in October 2023 directing Clearview AI to produce subpoenaed documents by a specified date. Clearview AI's failure to comply led to tolling of the five-year enforcement period. The administrative proceeding continued with additional discovery motions, and Clearview AI faced ongoing penalties for prior CCPA violations, including a $1.2 billion fine proposed by Italian authorities in parallel actions, though the CCPA case focused on discovery compliance.
source_rule: (c) If, upon being ordered by a superior court to produce any documents sought by a subpoena in any administrative proceeding under this title, the person alleged to have violated this title fails to produce documents in response to the order by the date ordered to comply therewith, the five-year period shall be tolled for the period of the delay

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, faced scrutiny for its handling of consumer personal information, including location data and purchase histories shared with third parties. In December 2023, the CPPA filed its first CCPA enforcement action against DoorDash for failing to honor consumer deletion requests and improper data sales disclosures. As part of the administrative proceeding under CCPA, a subpoena was issued for internal records on data processing and compliance audits. DoorDash produced some documents but withheld others citing attorney-client privilege, leading to a superior court motion to compel.
process_and_outcome: In April 2024, San Francisco Superior Court ordered DoorDash to produce the withheld documents by May 15, 2024. DoorDash's partial delay in compliance triggered tolling of the five-year statute of limitations under CCPA §1798.185(c). The case settled with DoorDash agreeing to a $375,000 civil penalty, enhanced privacy controls, and full document production, marking the first CPPA settlement.
