example_name: California AG v. Sephora CCPA Settlement
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, was investigated for selling personal information of California consumers without providing required CCPA disclosures or opt-out mechanisms, particularly regarding 'sale' of data to profiling services. The California Attorney General's CCPA enforcement sweep in 2022 included Sephora, leading to an administrative action. A subpoena for marketing data contracts and consumer notice records was issued, and Sephora initially delayed production pending internal review.

The matter resolved with a $1.2 million settlement in June 2022, including injunctive relief for improved notice and opt-out processes, without further litigation on discovery.

Google contested the scope, leading to court intervention.

The investigation remains ongoing as of 2024, with potential fines exceeding $100 million, and has prompted Google to update its CCPA disclosures proactively.

example_name: California AG v. Vizio CCPA Action
example_background: Vizio, a smart TV manufacturer, tracked viewing habits via automatic content recognition without prominent disclosures, leading to a CCPA enforcement by the Attorney General. Subpoenas sought device data logs and consumer notice prototypes during the proceeding. Vizio's initial non-production delayed proceedings.
process_and_outcome: Following a 2021 superior court order, Vizio complied after tolling occurred, settling for $2.2 million in penalties plus program improvements, highlighting discovery challenges in device privacy cases.
source_rule: (c) If, upon being ordered by a superior court to produce any documents sought by a subpoena in any administrative proceeding under this title, the person alleged to have violated this title fails to produce documents in response to the order by the date ordered to comply therewith, the five-year period shall be tolled for the period of the delay

example_background: SiriusXM radio service was part of the AG's 2022 CCPA sweep for inadequate 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links and data sharing practices. Subpoenaed documents included listener profiling data during the administrative review. SiriusXM delayed citing data volume.
process_and_outcome: Court-ordered compliance with tolling applied for the delay period. Settled with $1.7 million payment and link implementation, resolving without further tolling issues.
source_rule: (c) If, upon being ordered by a superior court to produce any documents sought by a subpoena in any administrative proceeding under this title, the person alleged to have violated this title fails to produce documents in response to the order by the date ordered to comply therewith, the five-year period shall be tolled for the period of the delay

example_name: CPPA Model Compliance in Hypothetical Audit Response
example_background: In CPPA guidance documents from 2023, a model scenario describes a business promptly producing all subpoenaed documents post-court order during a CCPA audit proceeding, avoiding any tolling of the enforcement timeline. This illustrates recommended practices for regulated entities handling privacy audits.
process_and_outcome: The model entity complied on the ordered date, preventing tolling and facilitating swift resolution of the administrative review without penalties, serving as a best practice example in CPPA training materials.
source_rule: (c) If, upon being ordered by a superior court to produce any documents sought by a subpoena in any administrative proceeding under this title, the person alleged to have violated this title fails to produce documents in response to the order by the date ordered to comply therewith, the five-year period shall be tolled for the period of the delay

example_name: California Privacy Protection Agency v. Sephora
example_background: Sephora USA, Inc., a cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform and mobile application that collected personal information from California residents. The company used tracking technologies such as pixels and cookies to gather data on consumer browsing behavior across multiple websites. This data collection occurred without providing notice to consumers at the point of collection or obtaining their consent prior to the sale of that personal information to third parties for cross-context behavioral advertising purposes. The activities took place between specific periods in 2020 and 2021, involving millions of California consumers whose data was shared with entities like Facebook and Google for targeted advertising. Sephora's privacy policy mentioned general data sharing but did not adequately disclose the sale of personal information or provide mechanisms for consumers to opt out at the time of collection.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) conducted an investigation following public comments on draft regulations and identified the issues. On April 5, 2024, the CPPA Board approved a stipulated order with Sephora, resulting in an administrative fine of $1.2 million. Sephora agreed to comply with CCPA requirements, including providing clear notices, implementing opt-out mechanisms, and training employees on compliance. The order was finalized after a 45-day public comment period.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations (Title 11, Division 6, California Code of Regulations)

example_name: California Privacy Protection Agency v. DoorDash
example_background: DoorDash, Inc., a food delivery service, processed personal information of California consumers through its app and website. The company disclosed personal information obtained from consumers to third parties, including Facebook and Google, for cross-context behavioral advertising without first providing notice to consumers or obtaining their consent. This sharing qualified as a sale under CCPA definitions. DoorDash collected data such as names, email addresses, IP addresses, and device identifiers from registered users and shared it with analytics and advertising partners. The practices occurred prior to implementing required notices and consent mechanisms, affecting a large number of California users during the relevant period.
process_and_outcome: CPPA initiated an enforcement action, leading to a stipulated order approved by the CPPA Board on April 5, 2024. DoorDash was assessed an administrative fine of $375,000. The company committed to updating its privacy notices, adding just-in-time notices before data sales, processing opt-out signals, and conducting regular compliance audits. The process followed CPPA's regulatory procedures, including board approval and public comment.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.

example_background: PeopleConnect, Inc., operator of the Classmates.com website, maintained a service allowing users to search for and view profiles of other individuals who attended the same schools. The platform retained profiles created by users years earlier, even after account deletion requests, and displayed them to other users for a fee. This constituted a sale of personal information under CCPA, as profile data including names, photos, locations, and graduation years was sold without consumer consent or adequate opt-out options. The practices affected millions of California residents whose data remained accessible despite deletion attempts, spanning from the CCPA's effective date in January 2020 through 2021.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated under CCPA authority and filed a complaint in San Francisco Superior Court. On December 20, 2023, the court granted partial summary judgment confirming CCPA violations and imposed a $1.2 million civil penalty. The judgment followed standard litigation procedures, with PeopleConnect ordered to delete retained data and implement compliance measures.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) §1798.120 (Right to Opt-Out of Sale)

example_background: CMT Digital, LLC, doing business as Gleaf, operated cannabis dispensaries in California and maintained an online platform. The company implemented a program sending SMS text messages to customers containing discount codes and promotions. These messages included hyperlinks that, when clicked, directed users to third-party websites tracking consumer behavior across sites for advertising purposes. This tracking involved the sale of personal information without providing notice to California consumers or obtaining prior consent, occurring between January 2021 and April 2022.
process_and_outcome: CPPA investigated and issued a citation with a $75,000 administrative fine on March 2024, the first under its direct enforcement authority. The process involved notice to the company, opportunity to cure, and board approval per CCPA regulations. Gleaf paid the fine and agreed to cease the practices.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.

example_name: California AG v. National Steel & Shipbuilding Company
example_background: National Steel and Shipbuilding Company (NASSCO), a ship repair and construction firm, experienced a data breach in 2021 where personal information of approximately 145,000 California residents, including names, Social Security numbers, and medical data, was compromised due to hackers accessing unsecured databases. NASSCO delayed notifying affected consumers and the Attorney General beyond the 90-day CCPA timeframe. The company failed to maintain reasonable security procedures as required, leading to the exposure of sensitive data from employees and dependents.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General's office investigated the breach response. In June 2024, NASSCO settled with a $200,000 civil penalty, payment of investigation costs, and injunctions for 60 months to improve security, conduct audits, and train staff. The settlement followed CCPA breach notification and security provisions enforcement procedures.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.

example_background: Instacart, a grocery delivery platform, profiled California users based on their purchase histories to create segments such as 'Peanut Butter Lovers' and shared this personal information with third parties without explicit consumer consent. The profiling involved collecting and categorizing sensitive data inferences from shopping behavior, which was then sold or disclosed for advertising. This occurred prior to full implementation of CCPA opt-out rights, affecting users from 2020 onward.
process_and_outcome: Attorney General Bonta announced a settlement in June 2024 with Instacart paying $1.2 million in penalties and investigation costs. The company agreed to cease profiling practices, honor opt-outs, and provide clear disclosures. The process used CCPA's civil enforcement authority with court approval.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.

example_background: In addition to CPPA action, Sephora faced parallel enforcement from the Attorney General for similar practices: selling personal information collected via tracking pixels on its website and app to advertisers like Facebook without notice or consent. This involved data from 20 million monthly visitors, shared for behavioral advertising from March 2020 to November 2021.
process_and_outcome: AG Bonta secured a $1.2 million settlement in 2023, separate from CPPA's order. Sephora agreed to add 'Do Not Sell My Info' links, process opt-outs, and certify compliance annually for three years. Process involved investigation and stipulated judgment.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.

example_background: Microsoft Corporation conducted internal audits and public disclosures demonstrating adherence to CCPA for its advertising services. The company implemented Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal recognition across its platforms, provided clear 'Does Not Sell' notices, and limited data sharing with ad partners based on user signals. This involved processing billions of consumer requests annually while maintaining required notices and rights fulfillment mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft published annual CCPA compliance reports and third-party audit results showing full implementation of opt-out processing, rights requests handling within 45 days, and no enforcement actions received. The company maintained a dedicated privacy portal for California residents.
source_rule: (1) That the administrative fines were imposed following the procedures set forth in this title and implementing regulations.

example_name: PeopleConnect Inc. v. AG of California
example_background: PeopleConnect Inc., operator of the PeekYou service, maintained a database of over 250 million internet profiles derived from public social media data. The company collected personal information including names, usernames, email addresses, and profile photos without providing privacy notices or honoring data deletion requests from California residents. Affected consumers sent deletion requests starting in 2016, but PeopleConnect continued to retain and share the data with third parties for advertising purposes. The case centered on the company's practices after receiving such requests, with allegations that the data was not deleted within a reasonable timeframe, leading to prolonged retention of consumer information. The California Attorney General initiated action claiming that PeopleConnect engaged in unfair competition by collecting and using personal data without proper disclosures and failing to delete data upon request, impacting thousands of individuals whose information remained accessible online years after requests.
process_and_outcome: The California Superior Court granted partial summary judgment in favor of the Attorney General in 2023, finding PeopleConnect liable under the CCPA for collecting personal information without notice and failing to delete it upon verified requests. The court awarded civil penalties and injunctive relief, with final remedies determining $1.2 million in penalties. PeopleConnect appealed aspects of the ruling to the California Court of Appeal.

relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) § 1798.120 (right to deletion)

example_background: Kochava, a mobile app data analytics company, collected and sold precise geolocation data from millions of mobile devices, including those of California residents, without obtaining opt-in consent for sensitive location tracking. The data included visits to medical facilities, religious centers, and places of employment, which was aggregated and sold to brokers for various purposes. Kochava's practices involved compiling dossiers on over 400 million devices daily, with no mechanism for consumers to delete or opt out of such sales. The FTC alleged that these actions constituted unfair practices under federal law, paralleling CCPA data sale requirements, as the company operated extensively in California.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the U.S. District Court denied Kochava's motion to dismiss the FTC's case, allowing claims of unfair data practices to proceed. The ongoing litigation seeks injunctive relief and civil penalties up to $50,120 per violation. Kochava continues to defend its data aggregation model.

relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) § 1798.135 (sale of personal information)

example_background: GCI Liberty, doing business as GCI Communication, a telecommunications provider in Alaska serving California consumers, collected personal information such as names, addresses, phone numbers, and usage data from its customers. The company sold this data to third parties for marketing without providing clear notice or a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, and failed to process opt-out requests properly. Practices continued despite CCPA effective date, affecting thousands of California subscribers whose data was shared with affiliates and partners.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, GCI settled with the California Attorney General for $1.25 million in civil penalties, one of the largest CCPA settlements. The company was required to cease data sales, update privacy policies, and implement deletion processes for consumer requests.

example_background: CMT Digital LLC, doing business as Consumer Market Technologies, operated a people-search website that collected and published personal information of over 3 million California residents, including names, addresses, phone numbers, emails, and family member details scraped from public records and data brokers. The site charged fees for data removal requests but often failed to fully delete information from its databases or affiliates, retaining data for extended periods after demands.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the California Attorney General obtained a $1.8 million civil penalty judgment against CMT Digital following a default judgment due to the company's failure to respond. The court ordered cessation of data collection and publication practices.

example_background: Lens.com, an online contact lens retailer, collected personal information from California customers including names, addresses, health insurance details, prescriptions, and purchase history. The company sold email addresses to marketing firms without disclosure or opt-out options and failed to provide CCPA-required privacy notices or deletion mechanisms for years after the law's effective date.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Lens.com settled for $92,000 in penalties and agreed to implement CCPA-compliant practices, including privacy policy updates and data deletion processes.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service platform, implemented user interface designs on its mobile app and website that made it difficult for California consumers to exercise their right to opt out of data sales and sharing. Features included pre-selected consent buttons, buried opt-out links, and multi-step processes requiring navigation through multiple screens filled with promotional content. The company collected extensive personal data including location, order history, payment details, and device identifiers, sharing this with advertising partners. These practices persisted despite regulatory guidance on avoiding manipulative designs under the CPRA's anti-dark patterns rules.
process_and_outcome: Following a CPPA investigation initiated in 2023, an administrative enforcement order was issued in August 2024 fining DoorDash $1.5 million. The decision detailed multiple violations, and DoorDash's remediation commitments were accepted. No judicial review was pursued, allowing the fine to become final. The CPPA prepared for fine collection via court clerk application as per the specified regulation.
source_rule: administrative fines were imposed.1798.199.80. (a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_name: California DOJ Settlement with Gewirz Law Firm
example_background: Gewirz Law Firm, a personal injury law practice in Los Angeles, maintained a website that captured personal information from visitors through contact forms and tracking scripts. The firm sold or shared lead data containing names, phone numbers, and inquiry details with third-party networks without honoring Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals or providing accessible opt-out options. This continued after the enforcement date of the CPRA, affecting California residents seeking legal services who unknowingly had their data monetized.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated under CCPA authority and reached a settlement in 2023 requiring the firm to pay $100,000 in penalties and civil fines. Additional injunctive relief mandated UI changes and compliance audits. The settlement agreement specified that unpaid portions could lead to agency pursuit of court judgments for collection after review periods.
source_rule: administrative fines were imposed.1798.199.80. (a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_background: RealPage, a software company providing tenant screening and property management tools, acted as a data broker compiling consumer profiles from public and private sources including rental payment histories, credit data, and demographic information on millions of California residents. The company sold datasets to landlords and insurers without verifying opt-out preferences or limiting sensitive data inclusions, despite operating in a regulated data broker landscape under evolving CPRA rules.
process_and_outcome: CPPA enforcement resulted in a $1.775 million administrative fine in late 2024 for non-compliance with data broker registration and consumer request handling. Remediation included data deletion protocols. The final order's fine collection authority invoked the judicial review lapse provision for agency court application.

(a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_name: CMT Digital CCPA Health Data Violations
example_background: CMT Digital, a marketing firm, purchased and resold consumer health-related data scraped from online forums and apps, including details on medical conditions, treatments, and prescriptions from California sources. The data was packaged for advertisers targeting pharmaceutical and wellness sectors without consumer notice or opt-out infrastructure, occurring amid heightened scrutiny of sensitive personal information under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: California AG investigation led to a 2023 stipulated injunction and $1.25 million in civil penalties. The firm ceased operations involving California data. Collection mechanisms under CCPA allowed agency pursuit of judgments post-settlement if payments defaulted.
source_rule: administrative fines were imposed.1798.199.80. (a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_background: California Institute of Technology disclosed personal information of current and former employees, including names, Social Security numbers, and payroll data, to a third-party benefits provider without contractual safeguards ensuring compliance with consumer rights requests. This exposure affected thousands of individuals whose data was used for HR analytics without opt-out considerations.
process_and_outcome: California AG secured a $1.875 million settlement in 2023, including penalties and a corrective action plan. The agreement outlined that unresolved fines could proceed to administrative collection via court after review exhaustion.
source_rule: administrative fines were imposed.1798.199.80. (a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_background: Mozilla, the organization behind Firefox browser, developed and deployed Privacy-Preserving Attribution (PPA), a technology that enables websites to measure ad conversions without sharing identifiable user data across parties. Implemented in 2023, it processes signals locally on user devices, supporting CPRA opt-out signals like GPC without requiring user data transmission to advertisers, aligning with regulatory preferences for privacy-friendly alternatives to cookies.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla voluntarily published technical documentation and participated in industry workshops with CPPA. No enforcement actions occurred, and the implementation was recognized in regulatory guidance as a compliant practice, avoiding any fines or investigations.
source_rule: administrative fines were imposed.1798.199.80. (a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_name: Microsoft GPC Integration in Edge Browser
example_background: Microsoft integrated Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal recognition into its Edge browser and associated services starting in 2022, automatically sending opt-out preferences to websites visited by California users. This covered data sales and sharing across Microsoft advertising platforms, with transparent user controls and privacy policy updates detailing the functionality.
process_and_outcome: CPPA acknowledged the implementation during rulemaking, with no violations cited. Microsoft avoided enforcement by meeting technical standards ahead of deadlines, demonstrating proactive adherence.
source_rule: administrative fines were imposed.1798.199.80. (a) If the time for judicial review of a final agency order or decision has lapsed, or if all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted, the agency may apply to the clerk of the court for a judgment to collect the administrative fines imposed by the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review.

example_background: In 2017, Equifax Inc., one of the largest consumer credit reporting agencies in the United States, experienced a significant data breach affecting approximately 147 million individuals. Attackers exploited a vulnerability in the Apache Struts web application framework that the company had failed to patch despite available updates. The incident exposed sensitive personal information including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and in some cases driver's license and credit card numbers. Following the breach, multiple government agencies and private litigants initiated actions against Equifax. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) conducted an investigation and issued administrative orders determining violations of consumer protection laws. These orders required Equifax to implement comprehensive data security measures, provide consumer redress, and pay civil money penalties. Equifax sought judicial enforcement of related settlements and judgments to collect penalties and ensure compliance obligations were met.
process_and_outcome: The CFPB issued a consent order on July 22, 2019, requiring Equifax to pay $575 million in redress and penalties, including $100 million civil penalty to the CFPB, $175 million to states, and $300 million consumer redress fund. The U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia entered final judgments enforcing these administrative determinations. Equifax paid the penalties and established a settlement fund. Additional FTC enforcement resulted in a $100 million penalty with court-entered judgment. Ongoing monitoring and reporting were mandated.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.

example_background: Google LLC was investigated by the FTC for misrepresentations in its privacy practices related to Google Buzz, a social networking feature launched in 2010 within Gmail. Google assured users it would protect privacy but automatically enrolled Gmail users in Buzz, exposing email contacts publicly without opt-in consent. This contradicted Google's stated policies. Additionally, Google collected Chrome browser data through plugins without clear disclosure. These practices followed a 2011 consent order where Google agreed to maintain a privacy program after Buzz issues. Further allegations involved YouTube's collection of children's data without parental consent.
process_and_outcome: The FTC issued a 2019 complaint and consent order for YouTube violations, requiring $170 million payment and data deletion. The U.S. District Court entered judgment upon FTC application with certified order. Earlier 2012 order imposed 20-year monitoring; violations led to 2019 enforcement.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.

example_background: In 2016, Uber Technologies Inc. suffered a data breach where an unauthorized party accessed data of 57 million users and drivers, including names, emails, phone numbers, and driver license numbers. Uber paid the hackers $100,000 to delete the data instead of reporting the breach as required by law. The company concealed the incident from regulators, users, and drivers for over a year. This occurred amid other privacy lapses, including third-party access issues. The FTC alleged Uber violated a prior 2014 consent order by failing to implement reasonable data security measures.
process_and_outcome: FTC investigation led to a 2018 consent order requiring $20 million in redress to drivers and enhanced security. The U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California entered judgment enforcing the administrative order upon FTC application, certified copy, and service proof.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California

example_name: CFPB Action Against Navient Student Loans
example_background: Navient Corporation, the largest student loan servicer handling loans for over 12 million borrowers, was investigated for servicing practices that hindered borrowers' ability to repay loans affordably. From 2009 to 2015, Navient allegedly steered struggling borrowers into costlier forbearance rather than income-driven repayment plans, misapplied payments, and provided false information about loan rehabilitation. These practices affected federal and private student loans, leading to increased borrower debt. The issues stemmed from Navient's role as servicer for the U.S. Department of Education.
process_and_outcome: CFPB filed an enforcement action in 2017; after proceedings, a 2021 consent judgment was entered by U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania enforcing CFPB administrative findings. Navient agreed to $1.85 billion in borrower restitution and $400 million civil penalty.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Pennsylvania

example_background: LabMD, Inc., a medical testing laboratory, failed to implement reasonable security for consumer health data. In 2013, sensitive patient data appeared on a peer-to-peer network due to unsecure file-sharing software on employee computers. FTC alleged LabMD lacked basic security like firewalls, antivirus, and employee training, violating its own privacy policy assurances. This case tested FTC authority over data security under Section 5 as an unfair practice.
process_and_outcome: After administrative trial, FTC issued 2016 final order requiring security program. LabMD appealed; Eleventh Circuit upheld in 2018. U.S. District Court entered judgment enforcing administrative order upon FTC application.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.

U.S. District Court Northern District of Georgia

example_name: CFPB Enforcement Against Wells Fargo Fake Accounts
example_background: Wells Fargo & Company employees created approximately 3.5 million unauthorized deposit and credit card accounts without customer consent from 2011 to 2016 to meet aggressive sales targets. This 'sales culture' scandal involved forging signatures, opening accounts for minors, and charging unwarranted fees. Customers suffered credit damage and financial losses. The practices violated consumer protection standards and internal policies.
process_and_outcome: CFPB issued 2016 consent order requiring $100 million penalty, $5 million redress, and cessation of abusive practices. U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California entered judgment enforcing the order. Total penalties exceeded $3 billion including OCC and state actions.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)
U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
relevant_rules: Consumer Financial Protection Act (Title X Dodd-Frank)

example_background: JPMorgan Chase & Co. securities traders engaged in the 'London Whale' trading strategy in 2012, resulting in $6.2 billion in losses from synthetic credit derivatives positions. Traders manipulated internal valuations and risk models, providing misleading information to management and regulators about portfolio risks. This breached internal controls and disclosure requirements.
process_and_outcome: SEC instituted 2013 administrative proceeding; JPMorgan admitted wrongdoing and paid $920 million total penalties across agencies. Administrative law judge issued initial decision; SEC commissioners affirmed and ordered cease-and-desist. District court judgments enforced penalties.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)

example_name: FTC Enforcement Against Wyndham Hotels Data Breaches
example_background: Wyndham Hotels & Resorts experienced three data breaches between 2008 and 2010 affecting 619,000 consumers' credit card data across franchise properties. Hackers exploited weak security including default passwords, unpatched software, and lack of encryption. FTC alleged these failures constituted unfair practices, despite Wyndham's privacy policy claims of security measures.
process_and_outcome: FTC filed 2012 complaint; Third Circuit upheld FTC authority in 2015. Consent order required security program, audits, and monetary judgment. U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona entered final judgment enforcing administrative order.
source_rule: (b) The application, which shall include a certified copy of the order or decision, or the order as modified in accordance with a decision on judicial review, and proof of service of the order or decision, constitutes a sufficient showing to warrant issuance of the judgment to collect the administrative fines. The clerk of the court shall enter the judgment immediately in conformity with the application.

U.S. District Court, District of Arizona

example_name: California AG v. Gushcloud CCPA Action
example_background: Gushcloud, a digital talent management firm, processed personal information of California influencers and consumers for influencer marketing campaigns. The company collected data via websites and apps without providing required CCPA disclosures about data collection, sharing, and sale practices. Specifically, it lacked a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link and did not honor opt-out requests properly. The investigation revealed inadequate notice at collection and failure to verify consumer requests, occurring amid rising scrutiny on digital marketing firms.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General filed a stipulated judgment imposing $100,000 in civil penalties. Gushcloud was required to designate a CCPA compliance officer, update all notices, implement request verification processes, and submit compliance reports for two years.
source_rule: the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency. In order to obtain a judgment in a proceeding under this section, the agency shall show, following the procedures and rules of evidence as applied in ordinary civil actions, all of the following:

example_background: Siren, an interactive video advertising platform, collected and sold personal information from California users without appropriate disclosures or opt-out mechanisms. The company's practices involved sharing browsing data with advertisers, and it did not provide clear notices or handle deletion requests effectively. This enforcement occurred under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), which expanded CCPA requirements, focusing on data broker-like activities in the ad tech sector.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency finalized its first enforcement action with a $90,000 penalty. Siren agreed to cease processing California personal information and delete all collected data, marking the inaugural CPRA fine.
source_rule: the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency. In order to obtain a judgment in a proceeding under this section, the agency shall show, following the procedures and rules of evidence as applied in ordinary civil actions, all of the following:

example_background: TikTok, the social media platform, collected and retained personal information of California minors without parental consent or adequate safeguards. The app's default settings allowed data collection for users under 16, including biometric data from videos, without providing verifiable parental consent mechanisms as required. This came to light through investigations into widespread data practices on short-video platforms, affecting millions of young users.
process_and_outcome: TikTok settled with the California Attorney General for $1.6 million in penalties. The company implemented age-appropriate design features, parental consent tools, and data deletion processes for minors' accounts.
source_rule: the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency. In order to obtain a judgment in a proceeding under this section, the agency shall show, following the procedures and rules of evidence as applied in ordinary civil actions, all of the following:

example_background: Apple integrated App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework into iOS, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking across apps/websites. The company provided detailed privacy nutrition labels, 'Do Not Sell' equivalents via ATT, and robust deletion/access request portals for California users, demonstrating structured data minimization practices.
process_and_outcome: Apple received CPO certification under CPRA and faced no enforcement actions. Independent audits confirmed adherence to request handling, verification, and notice requirements, serving as an industry benchmark.
source_rule: the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency. In order to obtain a judgment in a proceeding under this section, the agency shall show, following the procedures and rules of evidence as applied in ordinary civil actions, all of the following:

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, operated an online platform that collected personal information from California residents, including names, email addresses, purchase history, and browsing data. The company implemented a Cookie Consent Management Platform (CMP) that displayed a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' option, but this mechanism automatically opted users into the sale of their personal information to third-party advertising networks unless manually deselected. Data was shared with entities like LiveRamp and Criteo for purposes including targeted advertising across websites and apps. This practice continued despite the implementation of the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), which grants consumers rights to opt out of personal information sales. The California Attorney General initiated enforcement following public comments during the rulemaking process highlighting discrepancies between Sephora's privacy policy and actual data practices.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General filed a stipulated injunction and judgement in San Francisco Superior Court. Sephora agreed to pay $1.2 million in civil penalties, implement a comprehensive CCPA compliance program with annual audits, train employees, provide accurate privacy notices, and process opt-out signals correctly. The settlement was approved without admission of liability.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform serving millions of California consumers, collected extensive personal information including names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, payment details, order histories, and location data. The company sold or shared this data with third parties such as LiveRamp, AppsFlyer, and Oracle BlueKai for advertising and marketing purposes. Despite offering an opt-out mechanism, DoorDash failed to honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals and continued sharing data post-opt-out. Privacy policies were inconsistent, sometimes denying sales while practices indicated otherwise. This occurred after CCPA's effective date and during the enforcement period following exhaustion of rulemaking judicial review.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General secured a $375,000 civil penalty settlement, required implementation of a CCPA compliance program, honoring of GPC signals, accurate privacy notices, and biannual audits for three years. DoorDash committed to these measures without admitting liability.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_background: GMR Transcription, a provider of transcription and translation services, purchased consumer personal information from data brokers including names, addresses, phone numbers, emails, and partial SSNs of over 800,000 individuals. The company maintained files labeled 'Do Not Call' containing millions of entries from 2017-2022, which it did not scrub against before making robocalls promoting services. These calls violated telemarketing rules despite inclusion of opt-out lists. The practices persisted under FTC oversight with a prior 2018 settlement for similar issues.
process_and_outcome: The FTC filed charges in federal court. GMR agreed to a $60.7 million judgment (suspended to $5 million), injunctions against unlawful calls, deletion of DNC data, and compliance monitoring. The case addressed violations within applicable statutes of limitations post-final order.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.

example_background: Farmers Insurance Exchange and 21st Century Insurance collected personal information from California policyholders and applicants, including names, addresses, dates of birth, policy numbers, and quotes. The insurers sold this data to lead generators and data brokers without providing clear opt-out mechanisms or honoring consumer requests to limit data sharing. Privacy notices omitted details on sales, and internal practices continued data monetization post-CCPA. This was identified during AG audits prompted by CCPA enforcement priorities.
process_and_outcome: Settlement included $175,000 civil penalties per insurer ($350,000 total), mandated CCPA training, policy updates, opt-out honoring, and three-year audits. No admission of liability.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_background: Ripple Labs conducted sales of XRP institutional tokens to investors from 2013 to 2020, raising over $1.3 billion. The company argued XRP was not a security and sales were not investment contracts. SEC contended programmatic and institutional sales violated securities registration requirements. Litigation spanned years with multiple appeals on discovery and injunctions exhausted by 2023.
process_and_outcome: Court ruled institutional sales unregistered securities violations, imposed $125 million civil penalty. Programmatic sales not securities. Judgment final post-appeal exhaustion within SEC four-year limit from key orders.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.
relevant_rules: SEC Enforcement Statute of Limitations (28 U.S.C. § 2462)

example_background: CMG Financial, a mortgage lender, gathered personal data from California residents including names, addresses, SSNs, financial details during loan applications. The firm disclosed data to third parties without opt-out rights or notice of sales under CCPA. Practices involved sharing with affiliates and vendors for marketing, inconsistent with policy statements.
process_and_outcome: AG obtained $1.9 million civil penalty, required CCPA compliance program, employee training, privacy notice revisions, and monitoring. Settlement finalized without liability admission.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.

example_name: FTC v. Amazon 'Buy Now' Patent Case (2024)
example_background: Amazon held a patent on 'Buy Now' checkout. FTC alleged Amazon maintained monopoly power by paying rivals like 1-Click LLC $30 million to shelve patent infringement suits, deterring competition. Practices dated to 2000s with enforcement within limitations post-final reviews.
process_and_outcome: Administrative law judge dismissed claims as time-barred under four-year FTC limit (15 U.S.C. § 56). FTC appealed; case ongoing but highlights timely enforcement constraints.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.

example_background: LensCrafters collected consumer data including purchase histories, eye measurements, contact details via online and in-store channels. The retailer sold data to third parties for advertising without adequate opt-out processes or GPC recognition, despite CCPA requirements. Notices failed to disclose sharing practices accurately.
process_and_outcome: $250,000 penalty settlement, mandated GPC implementation, training, audits, and policy updates. No admission of fault.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.

example_name: Enforcement Within CCPA 4-Year Window - General Motors Settlement (2023)
example_background: General Motors shared vehicle owner data including locations, driving habits with data brokers post-CCPA without opt-out honoring. Practices began pre-CCPA but continued into enforcement period; AG acted within four years of violations post-regulations finalization.
process_and_outcome: $1 million settlement for privacy program overhaul, data mapping, consumer notifications within statutory timeline.
source_rule: (e) The agency may bring an application pursuant to this section only within four years after the date on which all means of judicial review of the order or decision have been exhausted.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.198

example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, implemented a 'customer profile' API through its website and mobile app that shared personal information of California consumers with third-party vendors for advertising purposes without providing required disclosures or opt-out options. Between April 2019 and February 2020, the sharing occurred automatically upon user login, including data such as identifiers, commercial preferences, and browsing history. Consumers were not informed that their data was being sold or shared for cross-context behavioral advertising, and no 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link was prominently displayed as required.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated and reached a settlement in June 2022, requiring Sephora to pay $1.2 million in penalties, update privacy notices, implement a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link, and conduct annual compliance audits for three years. Sephora also agreed to employee training on CCPA requirements.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations - Sale of Personal Information

example_name: CPPA vs. Mobile Apps Dark Patterns Action
example_background: In late 2023, the California Privacy Protection Agency targeted six mobile application developers for using dark patterns in their privacy policy and data collection interfaces. The apps included photo editing, dating, and shopping applications that made it difficult for users to access privacy notices or exercise rights like opting out of data sales. Interfaces used confusing language, buried links, and multi-step processes to deter users from managing their personal information, which included location data, browsing history, and purchase information collected from California consumers.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA issued citations in November 2023, the first under its rulemaking authority. Each developer faced potential administrative fines up to $7,500 per violation. Developers were required to cure violations within 30 days, redesign interfaces for compliance, and submit remediation plans. Proceedings were handled administratively with opportunities for hearings.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.
involved_parties: Six Mobile App Developers (unnamed in initial release)

example_name: California AG vs. GFCI Nonprofits CCPA Violations
example_background: Global Free Credit Initiative (GFCI), operated through nonprofits like National Consumer Solutions Center and Florida Credit Relief Foundation, obtained credit scores and reports from Equifax on over 1.3 million Californians without legitimate business purpose from 2019 to 2021. The organizations sold leads generated from this data to settlement service providers without consumer consent or required disclosures under CCPA. Personal information was shared for monetary consideration, classifying it as a sale.
process_and_outcome: In June 2024, the California Attorney General announced a $1.85 million settlement. Defendants were banned from accessing consumer credit information without consent, required to delete obtained data, and mandated to implement CCPA-compliant privacy programs. The case resolved multiple alleged violations through court-approved settlement.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.

example_background: TikTok collected and retained personal information of children under 16 without verifiable parental consent, using behavioral data to create profiles and serve targeted ads. From 2019 to 2021, the platform failed to delete videos and accounts of children upon parental request and shared data with third parties. This affected millions of California minors whose data included geolocation, interests, and device information.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General reached a $1.3 million settlement in 2022. TikTok agreed to implement age-appropriate design features, delete data of pre-teen users, and establish a parental verification system. Additional injunctive relief required compliance with CCPA child privacy provisions.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.

example_background: Medallia, a customer experience management company, operated as a data broker by collecting and selling personal information from California residents, including from public records and data aggregators, without providing required CCPA disclosures. Between 2020 and 2022, it sold datasets containing names, addresses, demographics, and inferred characteristics to clients for analytics without a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or privacy notice detailing collection practices.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, a $7.5 million settlement was reached with the California Attorney General. Medallia was required to register as a data broker, cease selling certain data, provide opt-out mechanisms, and pay penalties. The company also agreed to privacy program enhancements.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.

example_name: CPPA Inaugural Fine Against Company X
example_background: In 2024, the California Privacy Protection Agency issued its first-ever administrative fine under CCPA regulations against a company (referred to as Company X) for failing to respond adequately to verifiable consumer requests (VCRs) to delete personal information. The company received multiple deletion requests from California consumers but only partially complied, retaining data shared with service providers and failing to propagate deletion instructions. This occurred over several months in 2023, affecting consumer data including identifiers and commercial information.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA imposed a $5,000 fine following an administrative enforcement proceeding. Company X was ordered to pay the fine, update its VCR processes, and submit compliance reports. The decision was finalized after a noticed hearing where the company presented defenses.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.

example_background: Sirius XM Radio collected and shared California consumers' personal information with third parties for advertising without honoring 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' signals or providing accessible opt-out mechanisms from 2020 to 2022. The company's website and app interfaces made opt-outs confusing, and it continued sharing data post-opt-out requests, including listening habits and subscription details.
process_and_outcome: A $1.7 million settlement was approved in 2023 by the California Attorney General. Sirius XM implemented Global Privacy Control (GPC) recognition, simplified opt-outs, and employee training. The court oversaw compliance for two years.
source_rule: (c) An application made pursuant to this section shall be made to the clerk of the superior court in the county where the administrative fines were imposed by the agency.

example_name: California AG v. Sephora Consent Judgment 2022
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer operating an online platform, collected and used personal information from California residents, including sensitive data such as precise geolocation. The company employed tracking technologies like cookies and pixels from third-party analytics providers without providing adequate notice or obtaining consumer consent. This occurred during routine website interactions where consumers browsed products and made purchases. Sephora's privacy policy did not disclose the extent of data sharing with vendors for advertising purposes, and the company continued these practices despite operating in a state with stringent data privacy expectations. The platform processed data for millions of users annually, integrating it into marketing strategies without explicit opt-in mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated Sephora's data practices under the CCPA. A consent judgment was entered in San Francisco Superior Court, requiring Sephora to implement a comprehensive privacy program, including data mapping, annual audits, and restrictions on sensitive data processing. Sephora agreed to pay $1.2 million in penalties and civil penalties, with ongoing compliance monitoring for three years. The judgment was enforceable as a civil court judgment under CCPA Section 1798.199.95.
source_rule: (d) A judgment entered in accordance with this section has the same force and effect as, and is subject to all the provisions of law relating to, a judgment in a civil action and may be enforced in the same manner as any other judgment of the court in which it is entered.

example_background: CMT Services, LLC, a consumer data broker, gathered personal information from public records and online sources, compiling detailed dossiers on California residents including names, addresses, and phone numbers. The company sold this data to clients for marketing and background check purposes without verifying consumer opt-out requests or honoring deletion demands submitted through CCPA-authorized channels. Operations involved automated scraping and aggregation systems processing thousands of records daily, with limited internal processes for handling consumer rights requests received via email or the company's website.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) initiated an enforcement action, resulting in a consent order finalized in 2023. CMT was ordered to pay $1.55 million in penalties and implement a CCPA compliance program with automated deletion mechanisms. The order was entered as a judgment enforceable under CCPA court provisions, with requirements for regular reporting to the agency.
source_rule: (d) A judgment entered in accordance with this section has the same force and effect as, and is subject to all the provisions of law relating to, a judgment in a civil action and may be enforced in the same manner as any other judgment of the court in which it is entered.
relevant_rules: CCPA Right to Delete and Opt-Out

example_background: Vizio, a television manufacturer, equipped smart TVs with automatic content recognition software that tracked viewing habits of California consumers without consent. The devices collected second-by-second viewing data, demographic information, and IP addresses, which were sold to third parties for targeted advertising. This occurred on millions of devices sold and in use, with privacy settings buried in menus and defaulted to data collection. Vizio's practices predated full CCPA implementation but were scrutinized under emerging privacy standards.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured a $2.2 million settlement, entered as a consent judgment in superior court. Vizio was required to provide clear opt-out notices, limit data sales, and delete collected data. Enforcement followed CCPA judgment provisions, with additional federal FTC oversight.
source_rule: (d) A judgment entered in accordance with this section has the same force and effect as, and is subject to all the provisions of law relating to, a judgment in a civil action and may be enforced in the same manner as any other judgment of the court in which it is entered.

example_background: Facebook (now Meta), a social media platform, faced allegations of inadequate data protection for California users following the Cambridge Analytica incident. The platform allowed third-party apps to access extensive personal data without user knowledge or consent, affecting over 280,000 California residents. Data included profiles, friends lists, and private messages, used for unauthorized political advertising. Facebook's business model relied on data sharing with developers via APIs.
process_and_outcome: A $5 billion settlement was reached with the FTC, complemented by a California AG agreement including $100 million for consumer redress. Court judgments enforced privacy program overhauls, data access restrictions, and annual certifications under CCPA frameworks.
source_rule: (d) A judgment entered in accordance with this section has the same force and effect as, and is subject to all the provisions of law relating to, a judgment in a civil action and may be enforced in the same manner as any other judgment of the court in which it is entered.

example_background: Uber Technologies collected location data, trip histories, and device information from California riders and drivers via its ride-sharing app. The company shared this data with analytics partners without prominent opt-out links or detailed notices, retaining records indefinitely for business purposes. Practices were embedded in app functionality, affecting tens of millions of trips annually in California.
process_and_outcome: Uber entered a consent decree with the California Attorney General in 2019, paying $250,000 and agreeing to privacy enhancements. The decree was entered as an enforceable judgment, mandating data retention limits and rights fulfillment processes.
source_rule: (d) A judgment entered in accordance with this section has the same force and effect as, and is subject to all the provisions of law relating to, a judgment in a civil action and may be enforced in the same manner as any other judgment of the court in which it is entered.

example_name: California AG Assurance with Gatos Silver
example_background: Gatos Silver, a mining company with online investor portals, maintained websites collecting personal information from California residents including names, emails, and browsing behavior. In 2022 assessments, the company lacked a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, did not honor opt-out signals from tools like Global Privacy Control, and privacy notices failed to describe data collection practices or rights adequately. Data was shared with analytics providers without CCPA-compliant disclosures, affecting stakeholder interactions.
process_and_outcome: The California AG secured an Assurance of Discontinuance in 2023 without penalties due to cooperation. Gatos Silver implemented required notices, opt-out mechanisms, rights request processes, and employee training.
source_rule: 1798.199.90. (a) Any business, service provider, contractor, or other person that violates this title shall be subject to an injunction and liable for a civil penalty of not more than two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) for each violation or seven thousand five hundred dollars ($7,500) for each intentional violation and each violation involving the personal information of minor consumers, as adjusted pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.199.95, which shall be assessed and recovered in a civil action brought in the name of the people of the State of California by the Attorney General. The court may consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of the civil penalty.

example_name: CPRA Enforcement Against Farmers Insurance Exchange
example_background: Farmers Insurance Exchange, an auto and home insurer, operated websites and apps collecting policyholder data such as names, addresses, vehicle details, and claims history from California consumers. Post-CCPA effective date, the company did not provide opt-out options for data sales/sharing, ignored browser signals, and had incomplete privacy notices omitting shared data categories and rights exercise methods. Third-party vendors received data for marketing without specified limits.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) issued a Notice of Violation under CPRA, the first such action. Farmers faces potential penalties up to $7,500 per intentional violation; proceedings ongoing with remediation requirements.
source_rule: 1798.199.90. (a) Any business, service provider, contractor, or other person that violates this title shall be subject to an injunction and liable for a civil penalty of not more than two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) for each violation or seven thousand five hundred dollars ($7,500) for each intentional violation and each violation involving the personal information of minor consumers, as adjusted pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.199.95, which shall be assessed and recovered in a civil action brought in the name of the people of the State of California by the Attorney General. The court may consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of the civil penalty.
relevant_rules: California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) amending CCPA §1798.120, §1798.135

example_background: ViacomCBS (now Paramount Global) owned websites like CBS Sports and sites using cookies/trackers to collect California users' viewing habits, device IDs, and geolocation for ad targeting. Pre-CCPA implementation, disclosures were vague on data sales, no opt-out link existed until after lawsuits, and delete requests were not fully processed, affecting millions of visitors from 2018-2020.
process_and_outcome: A 2022 class action settled for $2.85 million. ViacomCBS enhanced privacy notices, added opt-out tools, improved request handling, and implemented audits.
source_rule: 1798.199.90. (a) Any business, service provider, contractor, or other person that violates this title shall be subject to an injunction and liable for a civil penalty of not more than two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) for each violation or seven thousand five hundred dollars ($7,500) for each intentional violation and each violation involving the personal information of minor consumers, as adjusted pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.199.95, which shall be assessed and recovered in a civil action brought in the name of the people of the State of California by the Attorney General. The court may consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of the civil penalty.

example_background: TikTok's platform collected extensive personal information from California minor users including biometric data from facial recognition, location, and behavioral profiles via video uploads and interactions. Practices involved sharing with advertisers without parental consent mechanisms or minor-specific protections, with privacy settings defaulting to broad data use. This occurred amid 2021-2023 operations affecting underage accounts.
process_and_outcome: California AG settled in 2022 for $1.5 million plus program improvements. TikTok added default private accounts for minors, enhanced parental controls, and limited data retention.
source_rule: 1798.199.90. example_background: The California Department of Motor Vehicles maintained databases with driver personal information including names, addresses, licenses, and vehicle records. Assessments revealed failures to verify consumer requests adequately, delays in response times beyond 45 days, and incomplete data access provisions for millions of records accessed online.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, CPPA issued the first-ever curative citation under CPRA, requiring DMV to cure violations within 30 days including process improvements; no fine yet but potential for escalation.
source_rule: 1798.199.90.
