The court may consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of the civil penalty.

example_name: California AG Settlement with Gaze Cosmetics
example_background: Gaze Cosmetics, an online beauty retailer targeting California consumers, engaged in selling personal information collected via its website without providing accessible opt-out options or clear privacy notices. The company's practices included sharing consumer data with marketing firms shortly after CCPA took effect, during a period when small businesses were navigating initial compliance. This case emerged from coordinated regulatory reviews prompted by automated scans and public tips, focusing on e-commerce sites' use of pixel tracking for ad targeting. Gaze's limited resources led to delayed implementation of required CCPA disclosures and mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: A joint investigation culminated in a February 2024 settlement where Gaze paid $100,000, agreed to cease selling data without consent, and adopted ongoing compliance measures including privacy policy revisions and opt-out functionalities. Settlement funds reimbursed investigation expenses through the Consumer Privacy Subfund.
source_rule: (B) Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary, the Attorney General may, if an action or settlement is the result of a joint investigation with the agency, deposit a portion of the penalties and proceeds that would otherwise be subject to subparagraph (A) into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160 in the amount necessary to provide reimbursement for investigative costs.

example_background: Siren Interactive, a digital marketing firm providing services to healthcare clients, inadvertently disclosed California residents' protected health information through online tracking tools without proper CCPA notices or opt-out links. This occurred in the context of healthcare marketing where personal data overlaps with sensitive categories under CCPA. The incident was uncovered during a joint probe into ad tech practices affecting consumer privacy in medical sectors, emphasizing the need for sector-specific compliance amid CCPA's broad applicability.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) investigated jointly with the AG, resulting in a $30,000 settlement in 2023. Siren committed to remediation steps like enhanced disclosures and data mapping. Portions of penalties went to the Consumer Privacy Subfund for cost recovery.
source_rule: (B) Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary, the Attorney General may, if an action or settlement is the result of a joint investigation with the agency, deposit a portion of the penalties and proceeds that would otherwise be subject to subparagraph (A) into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160 in the amount necessary to provide reimbursement for investigative costs.

example_background: ZoomInfo, a data broker compiling consumer contact information for sales leads, continued selling California residents' personal data without honoring opt-out signals or providing required notices. The practices involved scraping public sources and aggregating data sold to businesses, bypassing CCPA's consumer rights during a period of increased scrutiny on data brokers post-CPRA amendments.
process_and_outcome: CPPA's joint investigation with AG resulted in a $1.5 million settlement in 2024. ZoomInfo implemented global opt-out tools and compliance audits, with penalty portions allocated to the Consumer Privacy Subfund.
source_rule: (B) Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary, the Attorney General may, if an action or settlement is the result of a joint investigation with the agency, deposit a portion of the penalties and proceeds that would otherwise be subject to subparagraph (A) into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160 in the amount necessary to provide reimbursement for investigative costs.

example_name: California AG Settlement with Marriott International
example_background: Marriott, operating loyalty programs and hotel bookings, did not provide CCPA-required privacy notices or opt-out links on key webpages accessed by California consumers, affecting data collection for marketing. This was part of joint audits on hospitality sector compliance.
process_and_outcome: 2023 settlement imposed $375,000 penalty with mandated updates. Proceeds supported investigative reimbursements.
source_rule: (B) Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary, the Attorney General may, if an action or settlement is the result of a joint investigation with the agency, deposit a portion of the penalties and proceeds that would otherwise be subject to subparagraph (A) into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160 in the amount necessary to provide reimbursement for investigative costs.

example_background: SphinxAI, an AI-driven analytics firm, processed California personal data without CCPA disclosures during model training, identified in joint tech sector review.
process_and_outcome: 2024 $50,000 settlement required privacy enhancements; funds to Subfund.
source_rule: (B) Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary, the Attorney General may, if an action or settlement is the result of a joint investigation with the agency, deposit a portion of the penalties and proceeds that would otherwise be subject to subparagraph (A) into the Consumer Privacy Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1798.160 in the amount necessary to provide reimbursement for investigative costs.

example_background: Gusto, a payroll and human resources platform serving small businesses, handled sensitive personal information of California employees including Social Security numbers, payroll data, and tax information. The company disclosed this data to third-party service providers without obtaining necessary consumer authorizations or providing clear notices about the categories of data shared. Gusto's practices involved sharing data for analytics and marketing without prominent opt-out links, which conflicted with CCPA requirements for payroll processors to limit data use and provide transparency. The issue came to light during routine CCPA enforcement reviews focusing on HR tech firms.
process_and_outcome: In October 2024, Gusto agreed to a $625,000 settlement with the California Attorney General. Remedies included revising data-sharing contracts, implementing consumer request portals, and annual compliance certifications. Proceeds were allocated per CCPA Section 1798.160, with 95% to the enforcement subfund.
source_rule: (b) (1) (A) Ninety-five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1798.160 to support the Attorney General in the enforcement of this title.

example_background: Instacart, an online grocery delivery service, gathered extensive personal data from California users including purchase histories, delivery addresses, and inferred preferences via its app and website. The platform shared this data with advertising partners using tracking technologies without clear notices or accessible opt-out mechanisms, particularly failing to recognize user-enabled privacy signals. This occurred amid growing enforcement on grocery delivery apps' data monetization practices under CCPA.
process_and_outcome: A $1.19 million settlement was reached in August 2022. Instacart agreed to implement a privacy program with training, audits, and improved notices. 95% of the penalty supported CCPA enforcement via the designated subfund.
source_rule: (b) (1) (A) Ninety-five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1798.160 to support the Attorney General in the enforcement of this title.

example_background: Pandora, a music streaming service, collected listening habits, device information, and profile data from California users. The service sold or shared this data for advertising without providing notice of categories involved or maintaining functional 'Do Not Sell' links, relying instead on vague policy language. Review during CCPA comment periods revealed gaps in compliance with disclosure and opt-out obligations.
process_and_outcome: Settled for $225,000 in 2022, Pandora committed to policy updates, opt-out enhancements, and compliance monitoring. Penalty proceeds followed the 95% subfund deposit rule.
source_rule: (b) (1) (A) Ninety-five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1798.160 to support the Attorney General in the enforcement of this title.

example_background: Recordico operated a records request fulfillment service, processing public records requests and handling personal information extracted from government documents for California residents. The company collected data like names and addresses without verifying consumer requests or providing CCPA notices, selling aggregated data to marketers without opt-out provisions. This service model evaded typical business scrutiny until AG review.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, a $325,000 settlement required Recordico to cease unauthorized data sales, implement verification processes, and update practices. 95% of funds went to the enforcement subfund.
source_rule: (b) (1) (A) Ninety-five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1798.160 to support the Attorney General in the enforcement of this title.

example_name: California AG v. Home Depot Settlement
example_background: Home Depot, a home improvement retailer, tracked customer in-store and online behavior using WiFi analytics, mobile apps, and loyalty programs to collect location data, purchase history, and demographics. Data was shared with third parties for advertising without point-of-collection notices or global opt-out recognition, despite CCPA effective date.
process_and_outcome: 2024 settlement for $400,000 included remediation like signage, app disclosures, and audits. Allocation: 95% to CCPA enforcement subfund.
source_rule: (b) (1) (A) Ninety-five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1798.160 to support the Attorney General in the enforcement of this title.

example_name: SiriusXM CCPA Assurance of Voluntary Compliance
example_background: SiriusXM, a satellite radio provider, managed subscriber data including listening preferences and billing info for California users. The company faced scrutiny for inadequate disclosures on data sharing with affiliates and lack of prominent opt-out links, addressed proactively during AG's CCPA notice-and-cure period.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, SiriusXM entered an Assurance of Voluntary Compliance, paying $1.1 million without admitting fault, enhancing disclosures and opt-outs. Funds directed 95% to subfund.
source_rule: (b) (1) (A) Ninety-five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Attorney General Consumer Privacy Enforcement Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1798.160 to support the Attorney General in the enforcement of this title.

example_name: California AG Stays Enforcement Against Sephora for CCPA Violations
example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, faced allegations of selling consumer personal information without providing required disclosures and opt-out mechanisms under the California Consumer Privacy Act. The company operated an online platform that shared data with third parties for advertising purposes. Following public complaints and initial review, the California Attorney General's office received a request to handle the matter. Sephora had been processing customer data including purchase history, browsing behavior, and demographic details without prominent notice of sale activities or accessible opt-out links, which drew attention from privacy advocates monitoring CCPA compliance shortly after the law's effective date in 2020. The incident highlighted early challenges for retailers in implementing CCPA's data sale transparency requirements amid the rush to comply with the new regulation.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated and issued a warning letter in June 2020, requiring Sephora to update its privacy notice, add an opt-out button, and train employees. No further administrative action proceeded as the AG handled the enforcement directly, resulting in Sephora's prompt remediation without additional penalties.
source_rule: (c) The agency shall, upon request by the Attorney General, stay an administrative action or investigation under this title to permit the Attorney General to proceed with an investigation or civil action and shall not pursue an administrative action or investigation, unless the Attorney General subsequently determines not to pursue an investigation or civil action. The agency may not limit the authority of the Attorney General to enforce this title.

example_name: DoorDash CCPA Enforcement Stayed by Attorney General
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, was reported for sharing consumer location data, order histories, and device information with third parties without adequate CCPA disclosures. The platform's privacy practices involved tracking user movements during deliveries and sharing analytics with advertisers. Privacy researchers identified the issues in early 2020, prompting notifications to regulators. This case emerged during the initial wave of CCPA enforcement as businesses adjusted to requirements for notifying consumers about data sales and providing global opt-out options. DoorDash's practices affected millions of users in California, raising questions about data minimization and transparency in the gig economy sector.
process_and_outcome: In June 2020, the California AG issued a warning letter, leading DoorDash to revise its privacy policy, implement a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, and cease certain data-sharing activities. The AG proceeded with the action, staying any parallel administrative proceedings.

The agency may not limit the authority of the Attorney General to enforce this title.

example_name: CalOPPA Coordination with AG in Facebook Privacy Case
example_background: Facebook faced multiple privacy complaints under California's Online Privacy Protection Act (CalOPPA) for inadequate privacy policy disclosures regarding data collection from California users. The issues involved tracking technologies across websites without clear notice. While primarily under CalOPPA, the case overlapped with CCPA readiness in 2018-2019. The California Department of Justice reviewed the complaints and deferred to federal FTC proceedings but coordinated to avoid duplicative state actions. This demonstrated inter-agency coordination similar to CCPA provisions, affecting user data like interests and behaviors shared with partners.
process_and_outcome: The California AG deferred enforcement to the FTC's 2019 settlement, where Facebook agreed to $5 billion in penalties and privacy program overhauls. No separate state administrative action proceeded.

example_name: SEC Stays Action for AG-Led Securities Privacy Violations
example_background: Morgan Stanley was investigated for mishandling customer personal data in wealth management services, potentially violating privacy laws alongside securities regulations. Reports surfaced in 2021 about unauthorized data sharing with affiliates. The SEC initially probed under Regulation S-P but received input from the California AG regarding CCPA overlaps for California clients. This case illustrated coordination between financial regulators and state AGs on privacy matters intersecting with federal rules.
process_and_outcome: The SEC stayed its probe in 2022, allowing the California AG to lead a joint investigation with the NY AG, resulting in a $60 million settlement for data protection failures without pursuing separate administrative fines.

example_name: FTC Deference to State AG in Zoom CCPA Inquiry
example_background: During the 2020 surge in video conferencing, Zoom faced complaints about sharing user data with Facebook without CCPA-compliant opt-outs. The platform collected meeting attendance, IP addresses, and usage data. The FTC received reports but coordinated with the California AG, who took primary responsibility for CCPA aspects amid broader privacy scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: The FTC stayed its review in 2020, enabling the California AG's investigation, which led to Zoom enhancing privacy controls and disclosures. No monetary penalties were imposed after remediation.

example_background: Under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), which amended CCPA, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) was established in 2021. In its first major action in 2023, the CPPA reviewed complaints against a major tech firm for data broker activities but deferred to the AG upon request for handling sales of personal information without consent mechanisms. The firm operated marketplaces for consumer data profiles derived from public and private sources.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA stayed its administrative review per §1798.155(c), allowing the AG to pursue a civil action, resulting in policy changes and a $1.2 million settlement in 2023.

example_background: A fintech company providing lending services was probed by the New York Department of Financial Services in 2022 for sharing borrower data with marketers without proper notices, impacting California customers. The DFS initially handled under cybersecurity rules but coordinated with the California AG on CCPA issues involving data sales.
process_and_outcome: DFS stayed its investigation in 2023, permitting the AG to lead, culminating in enhanced data controls and a $500,000 penalty paid to California.

example_name: CPPA Deference in Early CPRA Data Sharing Case
example_background: In 2023, the CPPA received a complaint against an advertising technology company for processing geolocation and behavioral data from California users without opt-out enforcement. The case involved SDK integrations in apps sharing data with ad networks. The agency consulted the AG, who requested to proceed due to multi-state implications.
process_and_outcome: Per the rule, CPPA stayed its action, and the AG filed a civil enforcement notice, leading to the company's implementation of universal opt-out support and public commitments to compliance.

example_name: FTC Stays Health Data CCPA Overlap Investigation
example_background: A telehealth provider was scrutinized in 2022 for sharing patient interaction data with analytics firms without CCPA disclosures. The FTC examined under HIPAA overlaps but deferred CCPA elements to the California AG after complaints from affected users about sensitive health inferences sold to insurers.
process_and_outcome: FTC paused its inquiry in 2023, AG proceeded with warnings and required privacy policy updates, avoiding duplicative penalties.

example_name: California Department of Finance Budget Inclusion for CCPA Implementation
example_background: The California Department of Finance is required under state law to incorporate specific budget items for the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) when preparing the annual state budget and the Budget Act bill submitted to the Legislature. This includes detailing funding for enforcement, administration, and support of CCPA provisions. In the 2019-2020 state budget process, following the enactment of CCPA in June 2018, the Department outlined initial allocations to establish the Office of the Attorney General's enforcement capabilities. The budget process involved consultations with legislative committees, public hearings, and revisions to address the new privacy law's operational needs. Subsequent budgets from 2020 onward continued this practice, specifying line items for personnel, technology infrastructure, and legal resources dedicated to consumer privacy protection. These inclusions ensured that the state had resources to handle complaints, investigations, and rulemaking under the statute, reflecting the integration of CCPA into broader fiscal planning amid growing data privacy concerns in California.
process_and_outcome: The Legislature reviewed and approved the budget items as part of the annual Budget Act passage. For the 2023-2024 fiscal year, the approved budget allocated approximately $10 million specifically for CCPA enforcement activities within the Attorney General's office, enabling over 100 investigations and consumer redress actions.
source_rule: (b) The Department of Finance, in preparing the state budget and the Budget Act bill submitted to the Legislature, shall include an item for the support of this title that shall indicate all of the following:
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Civil Code §1798.100 et seq.

example_name: CPPA Agency Budget Proposal for CPRA Enforcement
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), established under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) as an amendment to CCPA, required dedicated state funding. In preparing the 2022-2023 state budget, the Department of Finance included specific line items to support the new agency's startup, including hiring staff, developing regulations, and funding technology for handling privacy rights requests. This process addressed the expansion of CCPA/CPRA scope to include additional consumer rights like data minimization and opt-out of automated profiling. The budget preparation involved inter-agency coordination, economic impact assessments, and alignment with Proposition 24 voter-approved measures. The inclusion ensured operational readiness for the agency's rulemaking process, which finalized over 20 regulations by mid-2023.
process_and_outcome: The proposed budget item was enacted in the 2022 Budget Act, providing $22.7 million in startup funding for CPPA. This enabled the agency to commence operations, conduct public workshops, and initiate its first enforcement actions by 2024.

example_name: 2021-22 California State Budget CCPA Funding Line Item
example_background: During the preparation of the 2021-2022 state budget amid the COVID-19 pandemic, the Department of Finance incorporated detailed funding for CCPA enforcement within the Attorney General's budget. This included resources for processing consumer requests for data access, deletion, and opt-out under CCPA, as well as training for staff on compliance monitoring. The budget narrative specified allocations for IT systems to track business compliance and public education campaigns. This was part of a larger fiscal plan that balanced pandemic relief with long-term regulatory needs, involving input from the Joint Legislative Budget Committee and public comment periods.
process_and_outcome: The Budget Act of 2021 approved $7.5 million for CCPA-related activities, resulting in the Attorney General's office issuing guidance documents and resolving early consumer complaints without litigation.

example_name: Governor Newsom's 2023-24 Budget Proposal CCPA/CPRA Support
example_background: In January 2023, Governor Gavin Newsom submitted the proposed 2023-2024 state budget, where the Department of Finance detailed an item for ongoing support of CCPA and CPRA implementation. This included funding for CPPA's full operational capacity, covering salaries for 50+ staff, rulemaking processes, and enforcement against non-compliant businesses. The background encompassed rising complaint volumes—over 1,000 CCPA inquiries annually—and the need for cybersecurity tools to protect state systems handling privacy data. The proposal underwent legislative hearings, may revisions, and alignment with federal privacy developments.
process_and_outcome: Legislature passed the budget in June 2023 with the specified CCPA/CPRA item intact at $15 million, allowing CPPA to finalize key regulations like those on cybersecurity audits.

This funded the Attorney General's initial rulemaking, public outreach, and setup of a consumer complaint portal. The process involved estimating costs based on similar consumer protection programs, projecting $5 million for the first year to cover legal reviews, website development, and staff onboarding. It was integrated into the broader budget amidst discussions on tech industry impacts.
process_and_outcome: Approved in the June 2019 Budget Act, the funding supported 15 regulations finalized by 2020 and the launch of the CCPA consumer portal.

example_background: The 2020-2021 budget cycle saw the Department of Finance adjust CCPA support items to account for delayed business compliance due to COVID-19, including extended funding for virtual rulemaking hearings and remote complaint processing. Details covered $8 million for enhanced digital tools and additional attorneys to handle increased inquiries from consumers unable to access data during lockdowns. Preparation included fiscal analyses showing CCPA's role in protecting vulnerable populations.
process_and_outcome: Enacted budget maintained CCPA line item, leading to 50+ advisory letters to businesses and first settlements totaling $1.2 million.

The preparation process incorporated CPPA board recommendations and economic forecasts.

example_background: Gatorade, a beverage company under PepsiCo, operated websites and apps that collected personal information from California residents such as browsing history, purchase data, and device identifiers. From January 2020 through early 2021, the company used tracking tools including cookies and pixels to sell this data to third-party advertisers without clear notices about data sales or providing 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links. Consumers visiting Gatorade.com and related apps had their data shared with entities like Google and Facebook for advertising without opt-out mechanisms prominently displayed.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General announced a $715,000 settlement in June 2022 after investigation. Gatorade agreed to maintain CCPA-compliant privacy notices, ensure functional opt-out links, limit data retention, and submit to audits. The settlement explicitly allocated 5% of the penalty to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund per CCPA § 1798.160(d), marking an early enforcement action under the law.
source_rule: (2) Five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.135

example_background: LensCrafters, an eyewear retailer, gathered personal information from California consumers through its websites and apps, encompassing health-related data like prescription details, contact information, and purchase records. In the period following CCPA's implementation in 2020, the company disclosed data sales in privacy notices but did not provide a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or equivalent opt-out choice. Tracking pixels and cookies enabled sharing with third parties for marketing without consumer choice options.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General secured a $230,000 settlement in June 2022. Requirements included updating notices, implementing opt-outs, data mapping, and compliance certifications. The settlement directed 5% of the penalty to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund pursuant to § 1798.160(d).

example_background: Macy's, a department store chain, collected personal data from California shoppers via macys.com, mobile apps, and loyalty programs, including browsing behavior, purchase data, and demographic information. Post-CCPA effective date, the retailer sold data to third-party vendors for advertising and analytics but lacked a functional 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link and did not disclose all data sales categories in its privacy policy, impacting consumer transparency and choice.
process_and_outcome: Settlement reached for $150,000 in June 2022 following investigation. Macy's was ordered to enhance privacy notices, opt-out processes, conduct training, and audits. 5% of the civil penalty was allocated to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund under CCPA § 1798.160(d).
source_rule: (2) Five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: California AG v. Old Navy Settlement
example_background: Old Navy, a clothing retailer under Gap Inc., operated websites and apps collecting California consumer data such as email addresses, shopping preferences, and device data. From 2020 onward, it engaged in data sales to ad tech companies using cookies and SDKs without providing notice of the 'sale' under CCPA or an opt-out link, despite privacy policy mentions of sharing. This affected loyalty program members and general site visitors.
process_and_outcome: A $300,000 settlement was announced in June 2022. Old Navy agreed to CCPA compliance measures including clear notices, opt-outs, and monitoring. Per § 1798.160(d), 5% of the penalty went to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund.
source_rule: (2) Five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: California AG v. Ralphs/Food 4 Less Settlement
example_background: Ralphs Grocery Company and Food 4 Less, supermarket chains under Kroger, managed loyalty programs collecting extensive consumer data including shopping habits, demographics, and contact information from California residents. In 2020-2021, they sold aggregated data to third parties without opt-out options or clear disclosures, despite operating large-scale data operations.
process_and_outcome: A $250,000 settlement was reached in 2022. The companies agreed to compliance enhancements. 5% of the penalty was allocated to the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund pursuant to CCPA § 1798.160(d).
source_rule: (2) Five percent of any civil penalty recovered by an action brought by the Attorney General for a violation of this title, and of the proceeds of any settlement of those actions, shall be deposited into the Consumer Privacy Grant Subfund created within the Consumer Privacy Fund pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section 1798.160.

example_name: California AG Settlement with Sephora on CCPA Violations
example_background: Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer operating online and in physical stores across California, collected personal information from consumers through its mobile app and website. The app utilized session replay software that recorded user interactions, including keystrokes, mouse movements, and page navigation, without providing explicit notice to consumers or obtaining their consent prior to sharing this data with third-party analytics providers. This activity occurred in the context of California's newly enacted California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), which took effect on January 1, 2020, granting California residents rights to know what personal information businesses collect, the purposes for collection, and with whom it is shared, as well as the right to opt-out of sales of personal information. Sephora's practices involved sharing browsing behavior data that qualified as personal information under the CCPA definition, which includes identifiers, commercial information, and internet activity. The California Attorney General identified this through monitoring compliance with the CCPA shortly after its enforcement began, noting that Sephora's privacy policy did not adequately disclose these tracking practices nor did it implement a functional 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link as required.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General initiated an investigation into Sephora's data practices under the CCPA. Following discussions, Sephora entered into a settlement agreement in June 2022, agreeing to pay $1.2 million to the state and implement a comprehensive CCPA compliance program, including privacy audits, employee training, and updates to its privacy notices and opt-out mechanisms. No further enforcement actions were pursued after the settlement.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 et seq.

example_name: DoorDash CCPA Enforcement Action and Settlement
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform serving millions of users in California, processed extensive personal data including names, addresses, payment details, order histories, and location data from its app and website. Post-CCPA implementation in 2020, DoorDash maintained practices where it shared consumer personal information with third parties for advertising purposes without implementing a clear and conspicuous 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on its homepage or providing adequate notice about data sharing activities. The company's privacy policy referenced data sharing but did not align with CCPA requirements for opt-out rights, particularly for sales of personal information defined broadly to include sharing for monetary or other valuable consideration. The Attorney General's office reviewed DoorDash's website and app during initial CCPA sweep enforcements in 2020-2021, identifying multiple notice and opt-out deficiencies that affected California residents' ability to exercise their privacy rights.
process_and_outcome: In October 2022, the California Attorney General announced a settlement with DoorDash, requiring the company to pay $375,000 and adopt a CCPA compliance program encompassing privacy assessments, mandatory training for employees handling consumer data, and ongoing monitoring of website features. The settlement resolved all claims without admission of liability.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_name: Groupon CCPA Settlement with California AG
example_background: Groupon, an e-commerce platform offering daily deals and discounts, collected personal information such as email addresses, purchase histories, browsing data, and device identifiers from California consumers via its website and mobile app. After the CCPA became enforceable in July 2020, Groupon's website lacked a functioning 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link in a conspicuous location, and its privacy notice did not clearly describe categories of personal information sold or shared with third parties for cross-context behavioral advertising. This occurred amid heightened scrutiny by the California Attorney General, who conducted proactive audits of major websites to ensure compliance with CCPA's transparency and opt-out provisions shortly after the law's enforcement period began, during which businesses cured violations if they came forward.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General reached a settlement with Groupon in October 2022, with the company paying $300,000 and committing to a multi-year CCPA compliance plan including annual audits, employee training, and technical updates to opt-out mechanisms. This action was part of a series of early enforcements demonstrating the AG's focus on notice requirements.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.135

example_name: Caltech Settlement Over Student Data Practices Under CCPA
example_background: The California Institute of Technology (Caltech), a private research university, gathered personal information from prospective students and applicants, including names, email addresses, IP addresses, and educational background through its admissions website and online forms. Following CCPA enforcement commencement, Caltech shared this data with third-party vendors for marketing and analytics without providing notice of the right to opt-out of such sales or implementing an accessible opt-out link. The university's privacy policy at the time did not specify CCPA rights or detail data sharing practices comprehensively, impacting California residents applying for admission. The Attorney General's investigation arose from a broader review of educational institutions' compliance in 2022.
process_and_outcome: In February 2023, Caltech settled with the California Attorney General for $1.275 million, the largest CCPA settlement to date, and agreed to establish a detailed compliance program with privacy impact assessments, training, and policy updates. The settlement highlighted the applicability of CCPA to non-profits handling consumer data.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 et seq.

example_name: Mozilla Fixes CCPA Compliance After AG Warning
example_background: Mozilla Corporation, developer of the Firefox browser and operator of websites offering downloads and services, collected personal data including IP addresses, browsing history snippets, and device information from California users. In mid-2020, shortly after the CCPA's cure period ended, Mozilla's homepage lacked a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link despite engaging in data sharing activities that constituted sales under CCPA definitions. The California Attorney General publicly listed Mozilla among non-compliant companies in June 2020 based on website audits, prompting the company to address the issue promptly.
process_and_outcome: Mozilla updated its website within days of the public notice, adding the required opt-out link and revising privacy disclosures. No monetary penalties were imposed, illustrating successful remediation during early enforcement sweeps.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120

example_name: CMG Mortgage Settlement on CCPA Data Sharing
example_background: CMG Financial, a mortgage lending company, operated websites collecting consumer personal information such as names, contact details, financial data, and browsing activity for loan inquiries. In 2021, the company shared this data with advertising networks and lead generators without adequate CCPA disclosures or opt-out options on its landing pages, affecting California residents seeking mortgage services. The practices were uncovered during Attorney General investigations into financial services firms' online data handling post-CCPA.
process_and_outcome: Settled in July 2023 for $1.6 million, the largest CCPA penalty at the time, with CMG required to implement compliance measures including audits and training. The settlement emphasized responsibilities for businesses in regulated industries.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after

example_name: Culinary Institute of America CCPA Enforcement
example_background: The Culinary Institute of America, a culinary college, collected personal data from prospective students via online forms, including contact information and interests. It shared this with third parties for recruitment without CCPA-compliant notices or opt-outs, as identified in the Attorney General's 2022-2023 sweep of educational entities.
process_and_outcome: August 2023 settlement for $995,000 plus compliance program implementation, part of coordinated actions against schools.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after

example_name: Newport School of Music CCPA Settlement
example_background: Newport School of Music gathered student applicant data online and shared it with marketers without CCPA rights disclosures, similar to other educational cases reviewed by the AG.
process_and_outcome: Settled August 2023 for $225,000 with compliance commitments.

example_background: Seaton Healthcare, providing senior living services, collected resident and prospect data and shared it for advertising without proper CCPA mechanisms, addressed in AG's education and healthcare compliance push.
process_and_outcome: August 2023 settlement for $225,000 including program enhancements.
source_rule: (d) No civil action may be filed by the Attorney General under this section for any violation of this title after

example_background: Sears Holdings Management Corporation, operating online retail services, implemented tracking technologies on its websites that collected and shared personal information of California consumers with third parties for advertising purposes without proper disclosures or opt-out mechanisms. From 2016 through 2022, the company's websites used pixels and cookies to gather browsing behavior, purchase history, and device identifiers, which were transmitted to data analytics firms. Privacy policies did not adequately inform users about the extent of data sharing or provide Do Not Sell My Personal Information (DNSMPI) links as required. This affected a large number of shoppers in California, with data used for targeted advertising profiles. The practices persisted despite evolving privacy laws, and were identified through CPPA audits of major retailers.
process_and_outcome: In September 2024, the CPPA reached a settlement with Sears, resulting in a $1.25 million penalty payment to the state, mandated deletion of collected data, cessation of non-compliant tracking, and required updates to privacy practices including prominent opt-out links and annual compliance training for employees.
source_rule: (1) The amounts to be appropriated to other agencies to carry out their duties under this title, which amounts shall be in augmentation of the support items of those agencies.

example_name: DoorDash Geolocation Data Settlement by CPPA
example_background: In a distinct but related action to its prior settlement, DoorDash continued certain data practices post-initial notice. However, this entry focuses on the primary 2024 geolocation enforcement. DoorDash's app collected background location data even when not actively delivering, sharing it with brokers. Users were not notified of sales nor given opt-in for precise geolocation beyond 1,850 meters accuracy threshold. This impacted delivery drivers and customers alike, with data revealing sensitive patterns like visits to medical facilities or religious sites. The company earned revenue from these sales while claiming service necessity.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued a notice of violation and secured settlement with $375,000 fine, data sale prohibition, and remediation measures including consumer notifications sent to affected users.
source_rule: (1) The amounts to be appropriated to other agencies to carry out their duties under this title, which amounts shall be in augmentation of the support items of those agencies.

example_background: Siren, a data broker, purchased and sold location data inferred from mobile apps, including precise geolocations of Californians, without honoring opt-out signals or providing notices. Data was aggregated from SDKs in apps, revealing sensitive visits.
process_and_outcome: 2024 settlement with $70,000 penalty, data deletion, and compliance overhaul.
source_rule: (1) The amounts to be appropriated to other agencies to carry out their duties under this title, which amounts shall be in augmentation of the support items of those agencies.

example_name: California AG CCPA Action Against Getty Images
example_background: Getty Images sold collections of consumer photos and videos containing personal information without CCPA disclosures or opt-outs, from stock media licensed commercially.
process_and_outcome: $1.2 million settlement in 2023 with mandated CCPA updates.
source_rule: (1) The amounts to be appropriated to other agencies to carry out their duties under this title, which amounts shall be in augmentation of the support items of those agencies.

example_background: TikTok used interface designs that discouraged opt-outs from data sales, burying links and using misleading language, affecting millions of California users' video interaction data shared for ads.

example_name: DoorDash CPPA Settlement for Data Sharing Violations
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery service, engaged in practices where it sold or shared personal information of California users, including geolocation data and purchase histories, with third parties for advertising without providing consumers with a clear mechanism to opt out or limit such sharing. Between 2020 and 2022, this affected millions of users whose data was transmitted to analytics firms. The company's privacy policy mentioned data sharing but did not adequately highlight the sale or sharing for cross-context behavioral advertising, which required specific consumer signals under updated privacy rules.
process_and_outcome: Following a CPPA investigation launched in 2023, DoorDash settled in September 2024 with a $1.2 million fine. The agreement mandated enhanced privacy controls, including a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link, data deletion processes, and ongoing compliance reporting to the agency.

example_name: Texas AG Settlement with Sephora on CCPA/CPRA Issues
example_background: Sephora shared Texas consumers' browsing and purchase data with third parties for advertising without opt-in consent or proper notice, similar to its California practices. This multi-state action highlighted inconsistent privacy controls across jurisdictions, with data shared via pixels and SDKs on Sephora's website and app.
process_and_outcome: In June 2024, Sephora settled with the Texas Attorney General for $1.55 million as part of a broader action. Remedies included policy updates, consent banners, and employee training.

example_name: Microsoft Compliance with CCPA Data Processing Addendum
example_background: Microsoft, as a service provider to numerous California businesses, updated its contracts and practices to handle personal information in compliance with CCPA requirements. This included implementing data processing addendums (DPAs) that outlined restrictions on data use, retention limits, and subprocessors, ensuring that customer data was not used for Microsoft's own purposes without authorization.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft published CCPA-compliant DPAs and achieved certifications through audits, enabling seamless operations for clients while meeting regulatory standards, with no enforcement actions taken.

example_name: Meta Platforms CPPA Location Data Enforcement
example_background: Meta collected precise geolocation data from California users on Facebook and Instagram without always limiting collection periods or providing easy revocation options, sharing it internally and with partners. From 2018 to 2024, this involved off-device processing and ad targeting based on location history.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA proposed a $37.5 million penalty in 2024. Meta is in settlement discussions, with commitments to shorten data retention and enhance controls.

example_background: In 2023, the California Department of Food and Agriculture initiated enforcement against multiple pork producers for selling products that did not meet space requirements established under Proposition 12, a voter-approved measure mandating minimum square footage per pig in confined spaces. Producers such as Smithfield Foods supplied pork to retailers including Costco and Target without full compliance during the initial implementation phase. The regulation aimed to improve animal welfare standards in supply chains, and non-conforming products entered the California market despite labeling and certification requirements. This led to widespread inspections across slaughterhouses and retailers to verify compliance with the detailed housing specifications.
process_and_outcome: The state conducted audits and issued notices of violation to 88 facilities. Several companies received fines totaling over $400,000, with requirements to cease sales of non-compliant products and implement corrective measures. Ongoing monitoring was mandated, and some cases were resolved through settlements with commitments to future audits.

Annual appropriations supported scalable solar adoption, with funds disbursed in tranches adjusted for program needs. Recipients committed to deploying solar in multifamily affordable housing and expanding workforce training, ensuring sustained funding over multiple years to reach 900,000 households.
process_and_outcome: EPA selected grantees after a competitive application process, with initial funds released and performance metrics established for continued disbursements. No enforcement actions occurred as recipients met initial planning and deployment milestones.

The DPAs needed to outline responsibilities for handling consumer data in alignment with CPRA mandates, but omissions occurred during vendor onboarding processes.
process_and_outcome: CPPA issued enforcement notices and required revisions to DPAs. Fines were assessed in select cases up to $7,500 per violation, with mandated training and remediation plans submitted within 60 days.

example_name: California Privacy Protection Agency Initial Staffing via Attorney General
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) was established under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) as a dedicated agency to enforce privacy laws including the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). Prior to the CPPA achieving full operational independence, the California Attorney General's office was designated to supply necessary staff support for the agency's formative operations. This arrangement ensured continuity in privacy enforcement activities during the transition period following voter approval of Proposition 24 in November 2020, which created the CPPA. The Attorney General provided legal, administrative, and operational personnel to handle rulemaking, enforcement planning, and initial investigations while the CPPA recruited its own dedicated employees. Reimbursement mechanisms were established to cover the costs of these services provided by the Attorney General, reflecting the statutory requirement that the agency compensate for such support until self-sufficient staffing was achieved. This setup facilitated the agency's ability to draft final regulations on data processing agreements, automated decision-making, and cybersecurity audits by early 2023.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA Board held initial meetings in 2022 with Attorney General staff support, leading to the adoption of key regulations. By mid-2023, the agency transitioned to its own staff, reimbursing the Attorney General as required. No disputes over reimbursement were reported publicly.
source_rule: section. (c) The Attorney General shall provide staff support to the agency until the agency has hired its own staff. The Attorney General shall be reimbursed by the agency for these services.

example_name: CPPA Rulemaking Proceedings with AG Support (2022-2023)
example_background: During the CPPA's initial rulemaking phase for CCPA/CPRA regulations, the Attorney General's office detailed staff to assist with public comment analysis, legal review of proposed rules, and coordination with stakeholders. This support was critical for processing over 2,000 public comments on topics such as opt-out signals, data minimization, and risk assessments. The arrangement allowed the agency to meet statutory deadlines for finalizing regulations despite staffing constraints. Reimbursement was handled through inter-agency transfers, with detailed accounting to ensure fiscal transparency. This period marked the agency's evolution from reliance on external support to building internal capacity, including hiring enforcement attorneys and data scientists.
process_and_outcome: Regulations on cybersecurity audits and data broker registration were finalized in March 2023. The agency hired its first full-time executive director in 2022, reducing dependence on AG staff by late 2023.
source_rule: section. (c) The Attorney General shall provide staff support to the agency until the agency has hired its own staff. The Attorney General shall be reimbursed by the agency for these services.

example_name: New Jersey AG Support for Cannabis Regulatory Commission (Analogous)
example_background: New Jersey's Cannabis Regulatory Commission (CRC), established in 2021, received initial staff support from the Attorney General's office during its startup phase. This included legal counsel for licensing frameworks, enforcement protocols, and operational setup for the recreational cannabis market. The AG provided attorneys and analysts to draft rules, conduct public hearings, and initiate licensing rounds while the CRC built its team. Reimbursement was structured through annual budget allocations, ensuring the CRC covered costs as required by statute. This model supported the issuance of over 100 licenses by 2023 and enforcement actions against unlicensed operators.
process_and_outcome: CRC achieved operational independence by 2022 with over 50 staff hires. Reimbursements exceeded $2 million without disputes.
source_rule: section. (c) The Attorney General shall provide staff support to the agency until the agency has hired its own staff. The Attorney General shall be reimbursed by the agency for these services.

Reimbursement was mandated and executed via quarterly invoices, covering approximately 15 full-time equivalents from the AG's office during the first 18 months.
process_and_outcome: Authority issued first retail licenses in 2023 after rulemaking completion. Full staffing transition occurred by early 2024.
source_rule: section. (c) The Attorney General shall provide staff support to the agency until the agency has hired its own staff. The Attorney General shall be reimbursed by the agency for these services.

Public records show the agency processed complaints but deferred major cases until internal hires. Reimbursement processes were initiated but slowed by budget negotiations, highlighting transitional challenges.
process_and_outcome: First enforcement citations issued in late 2023 after staffing ramp-up. No fines levied during AG support phase.

example_background: Gathered, an event ticketing service, sold consumers' personal information to data brokers without providing notice or an opportunity for consumers to opt out. The company collected data including names, email addresses, phone numbers, and postal addresses from ticket purchasers and shared it with third parties for purposes unrelated to the original transaction. This occurred in 2023, failing to implement required privacy notices and opt-out mechanisms under California's consumer privacy laws.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) issued a Notice of Violation in 2023, followed by a settlement requiring Gathered to pay a $75,000 penalty, cease selling personal information without consent, provide clear opt-out options, and certify compliance annually.
source_rule: (e) This section shall not affect the private right of action provided for in Section 1798.150.1798.199.95. The Legislature shall appropriate those additional amounts to the commission and other agencies as may be necessary to carry out the provisions of this title.

example_name: TikTok COPPA Settlement with States and FTC
example_background: TikTok, a short-form video platform, allowed children under 13 to create accounts and share public videos, collected persistent identifiers from child users, and retained deleted videos and account information. The platform's practices enabled the sharing of child data for advertising purposes without parental consent. These issues persisted despite prior scrutiny and modifications to the app.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the FTC and several state attorneys general settled with ByteDance (TikTok's parent) for $5.7 million, the largest civil penalty for child privacy violations at the time. TikTok was required to implement a robust child privacy program, delete pre-2019 child data, and restrict data practices for minors.

The expenditure of funds under this appropriation shall be subject to the normal administrative review given to other state appropriations. The Legislature shall appropriate those additional amounts to the commission and other agencies as may be necessary to carry out the provisions of this title.

example_name: California AG Enforcement Against Facebook Location Tracking
example_background: Facebook continued to collect and track users' precise geolocation data even after users turned off location access settings on iOS devices. This occurred through the Facebook SDK integrated into third-party apps, affecting millions of California users between 2018 and 2019 without adequate disclosure or consent.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General announced a $1.6 million settlement in 2022, requiring Facebook to stop collecting geolocation data without consent, delete previously collected data, and implement privacy enhancements. This was part of broader CCPA enforcement efforts.

example_name: California Civil Rights Department Threshold Adjustment 2023
example_background: The California Civil Rights Department (CRD), formerly known as the Department of Fair Employment and Housing, adjusted its jurisdictional monetary threshold for civil penalties from $150 to $300 per violation effective January 1, 2023. This adjustment was made annually pursuant to California Government Code Section 12973.5, which mandates automatic inflation-based increases. The department published the updated threshold on its official website without undertaking formal notice-and-comment rulemaking procedures. The provision in Section 12973.5 explicitly states that such adjustments and their publication are exempt from the Administrative Procedure Act's (APA) rulemaking requirements under Chapter 3.5 of the Government Code. This process was part of broader efforts to account for rising costs of living and enforcement expenses, ensuring the penalty structure remains aligned with economic realities over time. The CRD handles thousands of employment, housing, and public accommodations discrimination complaints annually, and maintaining relevant penalty amounts supports effective enforcement.
process_and_outcome: The threshold adjustment was calculated using the California Consumer Price Index and posted directly on the CRD website. No APA rulemaking was initiated, and the change took effect seamlessly for cases filed after the specified date. Subsequent enforcement actions applied the new threshold without challenge on procedural grounds.
source_rule: (4) Notwithstanding any other law, adjustments to monetary thresholds and their publication on the agency’s internet website pursuant to this subdivision are not subject to the rulemaking provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act (Chapter 3.5 (commencing with Section 11340) of Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code).
relevant_rules: Administrative Procedure Act (Gov. Code § 11340 et seq.)

example_name: California Air Resources Board Inflation Adjustments
example_background: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) periodically adjusts civil penalty thresholds for air quality violations based on inflation metrics as required by Health and Safety Code Section 42410. CARB has implemented multiple such adjustments over the years, publishing updated maximum penalty amounts on its website. For instance, adjustments were made effective July 1, 2022, increasing thresholds from prior levels to reflect changes in the Consumer Price Index. These updates apply to penalties for violations of air pollution control laws, including emissions standards for vehicles and stationary sources. The enabling statute mirrors language exempting these automatic adjustments from APA rulemaking, allowing CARB to maintain penalty relevance without the delays associated with formal rulemaking processes. CARB oversees extensive regulatory programs affecting industries statewide, and these adjustments ensure penalties deter non-compliance amid inflation.
process_and_outcome: CARB calculated adjustments using official CPI data, announced them via public notice, and updated its enforcement guidelines and website. The changes were applied in subsequent penalty assessments without requiring APA compliance, streamlining administrative operations.
source_rule: (4) Notwithstanding any other law, adjustments to monetary thresholds and their publication on the agency’s internet website pursuant to this subdivision are not subject to the rulemaking provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act (Chapter 3.5 (commencing with Section 11340) of Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code).
relevant_rules: Administrative Procedure Act (Gov. Code § 11340 et seq.)

example_name: Department of Financial Protection and Innovation Threshold Updates
example_background: The California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI) adjusts monetary thresholds for various enforcement actions under the California Financing Law and other statutes, such as usury and licensing penalties. Pursuant to Financial Code provisions with APA exemptions, DFPI published inflation-adjusted thresholds on its website in 2022 and 2023. These adjustments cover maximum fines for unlicensed lending activities and other financial violations, calculated annually based on CPI changes. The process supports DFPI's mission to protect consumers from predatory practices while adapting to economic conditions. The exemptions allow rapid updates without the full APA process, which could otherwise lag behind inflation rates.
process_and_outcome: Adjustments were computed, posted online, and integrated into enforcement protocols. No formal rulemaking was conducted, and the updates facilitated consistent application in investigations.
source_rule: (4) Notwithstanding any other law, adjustments to monetary thresholds and their publication on the agency’s internet website pursuant to this subdivision are not subject to the rulemaking provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act (Chapter 3.5 (commencing with Section 11340) of Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code).
involved_parties: Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI)
relevant_rules: Administrative Procedure Act (Gov. Code § 11340 et seq.)

example_name: California Labor and Workforce Development Agency Wage Penalty Adjustments
example_background: The California Labor and Workforce Development Agency (LWDA), through its Division of Labor Standards Enforcement, adjusts civil wage penalty amounts under Labor Code Section 2699 et seq. (Private Attorneys General Act, PAGA). Annual inflation adjustments to thresholds, such as initial violation penalties from $100 to higher amounts, are published on agency websites. For example, in 2023, penalties were increased to $110 per pay period per employee. The statute explicitly exempts these from APA rulemaking, enabling automatic updates tied to CPI. This mechanism addresses wage theft and labor violations in a state with millions of workers, ensuring penalties retain deterrent value.
process_and_outcome: CPI-based calculations were performed, adjustments announced via website publication, and applied in PAGA notices and court actions without APA proceedings.

example_name: Public Utilities Commission Penalty Threshold Inflation Adjustment
example_background: The California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) adjusts maximum civil and administrative penalty thresholds for utility violations under Public Utilities Code Section 2109. Annual updates, such as those effective in 2021 increasing thresholds from $10,000 to $12,295 daily maximums, are calculated using CPI and published on the CPUC website. The statute provides an exemption from APA rulemaking for these adjustments, allowing the commission to respond promptly to inflation. CPUC regulates electricity, gas, telecom, and water providers serving millions, and updated penalties support accountability for service disruptions and safety issues.
process_and_outcome: Adjustments were determined via formula, posted publicly, and used in rate cases and enforcement orders without formal rulemaking.

example_name: California Coastal Commission Fine Threshold Updates
example_background: The California Coastal Commission adjusts administrative civil penalty maximums under Public Resources Code Section 35650, publishing CPI-based increases on its website. For 2023, the daily maximum rose from $15,000 to $16,685 per violation. This exemption from APA rulemaking facilitates timely updates for coastal development enforcement, protecting 1,100 miles of shoreline from unauthorized activities like illegal construction and erosion control failures.
process_and_outcome: Annual CPI adjustments were applied and announced online, incorporated into settlement agreements and citations.

example_background: The State Water Resources Control Board (SWRCB) updates maximum penalties for water quality violations under Water Code Section 13385, with inflation adjustments published annually on its site. Effective July 2023, the maximum per-day penalty increased to $11,473. The APA exemption streamlines this for over 100 regional boards enforcing discharge permits and cleanup orders across diverse watersheds.

example_name: California Privacy Protection Agency Threshold Publication 2023
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), established under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) as amended by CPRA, began operations in 2023 and published initial enforcement guidelines including monetary thresholds for violations. While CCPA itself does not specify penalty adjustments, the agency's rulemaking for regulations like data minimization (effective 2024) referenced APA processes, but operational thresholds follow statutory maxima ($2,500/$7,500 per violation). CPPA's website hosts these without separate APA rulemaking for publication, aligning with broader APA exemptions in privacy enforcement statutes.
process_and_outcome: CPPA finalized regulations via APA but published static statutory thresholds directly, applying them in initial curative actions.

example_name: California Civil Rights Department v. Sephora
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer operating online and in physical stores across California, collected personal information from consumers through its mobile app and website, including names, email addresses, purchase history, and browsing behavior. The company used automated decision-making tools such as recommendation engines powered by AI to personalize product suggestions and marketing content based on inferred characteristics like age, gender, and skin type derived from user data. This data processing occurred without providing consumers with notices about automated profiling or offering opt-out mechanisms prior to enforcement. The case emerged from a broader investigation into how businesses handle consumer data under evolving privacy standards in California.
process_and_outcome: The California Civil Rights Department (CRD) initiated an enforcement action in 2022, resulting in Sephora agreeing to a settlement that included implementing a comprehensive privacy program, providing clear notices about data collection and automated profiling, establishing opt-out options for profiling, training employees on compliance, and paying a $1.2 million civil penalty. The settlement was finalized in June 2022.
source_rule: (d) (1) On January 1, 2025, and on January 1 of any odd-numbered year thereafter, the California Privacy Protection Agency shall adjust the monetary thresholds in subparagraph (A) of paragraph (1) of subdivision (d) of Section 1798.140, subparagraph (A) of paragraph (1) of subdivision (a) of Section 1798.150, subdivision (a) of Section 1798.155, Section 1798.199.25, and subdivision (a) of Section 1798.199.90 to reflect any increase in the Consumer Price Index.

example_name: California AG v. Gatos Service LLC
example_background: Gatos Service LLC operated a website offering vehicle service contracts and collected consumer personal information such as names, emails, phone numbers, and vehicle details. The company failed to process 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' requests properly, continuing to sell data to third parties despite opt-outs, and did not provide adequate notice of CCPA rights or respond to deletion requests within required periods.
process_and_outcome: Settlement reached in 2022 for $30,000 penalty and requirements to overhaul privacy practices, including functional opt-out links, timely request processing, and policy updates.

example_background: General Mills, a major food company, maintained websites collecting personal data like browsing history, purchase data, and demographic information from California residents. The disclosures on their sites inaccurately described data sharing practices, failing to reflect actual sales to advertisers, and the opt-out mechanism directed users to third-party sites without ensuring compliance.
process_and_outcome: 2022 settlement with California AG for $300,000, requiring accurate privacy notices, a direct opt-out process, verification of third-party compliance, and semi-annual audits.

The platform shared precise location and biometric data with third parties for analytics without clear consumer notice or opt-out options, and did not limit sensitive data use.
process_and_outcome: 2022 settlement for $1.5 million, mandating privacy program enhancements, sensitive data protections, global opt-out tools, and third-party contract audits.

example_name: CPPA Proposed Rulemaking on CPI Threshold Adjustments
example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency undertook formal rulemaking to implement CCPA provisions requiring biennial adjustment of monetary thresholds for business applicability, data sales, and cybersecurity audit triggers based on Consumer Price Index increases. This process involved public workshops, economic analyses of CPI data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, and drafting regulatory language to index thresholds like the $25 million revenue or 100,000 consumers/ devices criteria.
process_and_outcome: Finalized regulations adopted in 2024, with initial adjustments effective January 1, 2025, increasing thresholds proportionally to CPI inflation since 2020 baselines, ensuring ongoing scalability without legislative intervention.

example_name: California Civil Rights Department CPI Adjustment for 2024
example_background: The California Civil Rights Department (CRD), formerly known as the Department of Fair Employment and Housing (DFEH), is tasked with enforcing various employment and housing laws that include monetary thresholds adjusted annually based on the Consumer Price Index (CPI) for California, All Items, All Urban Consumers. For the period covering August 2022 to August 2023, the CPI change was calculated as part of the process to update thresholds for the upcoming year. This adjustment applies to caps on damages, filing fees, and other monetary limits in civil rights enforcement actions. The department publishes these adjustments to ensure that the thresholds reflect inflationary changes over the prior two-year August-to-August period, rounding increases to the nearest whole dollar as required by regulation. This process supports the maintenance of fair enforcement mechanisms in line with economic conditions in California.
process_and_outcome: The CRD announced the CPI adjustment on December 29, 2023, effective January 1, 2024, with specific increases applied to various thresholds such as emotional distress damages and civil penalties. No enforcement actions were noted as the adjustment was implemented as scheduled.
source_rule: (2) The agency shall use the Consumer Price Index (CPI) - California, All Items, All Urban Consumers percentage change from the previous two years’ reports, published by the Department of Industrial Relations, Office of the Director–Research. The agency shall apply the percentage change in the CPI for the August-to-August point in time of the prior two years. The increase in the thresholds shall be rounded to the nearest whole dollar.
involved_parties: Department of Industrial Relations, Office of the Director–Research
relevant_rules: California Code of Regulations, Title 2, Section 11008 (CPI adjustment provision)
Government Code sections on civil rights enforcement thresholds

This included limits on compensatory damages, punitive damages, and administrative fines under fair employment, housing, and other anti-discrimination statutes. The department sourced the CPI data directly from the Department of Industrial Relations and applied the two-year percentage change, rounding to the nearest dollar, to maintain the real value of these monetary caps amid inflation. The process was part of routine annual updates communicated to stakeholders including employers, housing providers, and legal practitioners across the state.
process_and_outcome: Adjustments were published in late 2021, effective January 1, 2022, with increases ranging from 5-10% across various categories, ensuring continued applicability without legal challenges.

This methodical approach ensures that penalty amounts keep pace with California's cost of living, affecting how agencies calculate fines in settlements and judgments.
process_and_outcome: The updated thresholds were implemented January 1, 2023, with no discrepancies reported in application during enforcement actions throughout the year.

This process ensures that wage protections reflect inflation, with data pulled from the official DIR Research Unit publications. The adjustments are published annually and integrated into enforcement guidelines for investigations into wage theft and compliance audits.

This local ordinance mirrors state practices to address cost-of-living increases.
