example_name: California DOI Civil Money Penalty Order 2020-01
example_background: The California Department of Insurance issued Civil Money Penalty Order No. 2020-01 addressing a notice of data breach posted by a health insurer on the California Attorney General's website. The insurer experienced a data breach affecting over 100,000 California residents when unauthorized access occurred to an employee's email account containing protected health information and personal identifiers. The posting of the breach notice occurred on May 17, 2019, through the Attorney General's online portal as required under California Civil Code section 1798.82. Subsequent review by the Department of Insurance revealed that the insurer's privacy policy, available on its website during the relevant period, inaccurately stated that it would provide notice 'in accordance with any applicable federal and state laws.' This representation did not align with the specific posting requirements under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) for data breach notifications, particularly as amendments introduced by AB 2953 mandated clear disclosure of posting obligations. The discrepancy between the posted notice and the privacy policy language prompted regulatory scrutiny regarding transparency in notification practices. The insurer had been operational for several years, handling sensitive health data for policyholders across multiple states, with established procedures for breach response, but the policy wording failed to reflect evolving statutory mandates effective January 1, 2020.
process_and_outcome: The Department of Insurance conducted an investigation into the insurer's data breach notification practices. On February 14, 2020, it issued Civil Money Penalty Order No. 2020-01, assessing a penalty of $7,500. The order required the insurer to update its privacy policy to accurately reflect CCPA posting requirements and submit proof of compliance within 30 days. The insurer complied by revising the policy and providing confirmation to the regulator.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.
relevant_rules: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), AB 2953 amendments

example_name: California AG CCPA Website Updates January 2020
example_background: The California Attorney General's office oversees the implementation of the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), which became effective on January 1, 2020. As part of fulfilling statutory obligations related to transparency and public access to consumer rights information, the office maintains a dedicated CCPA section on its official website at oag.ca.gov/privacy/ccpa. In preparation for the law's enforcement, the Attorney General updated this webpage to include comprehensive guidance on consumer rights, business obligations, and enforcement mechanisms. On January 14, 2020, just prior to the January 15 deadline specified in CCPA regulations for certain disclosures, the site was enhanced with detailed resources such as sample opt-out notices, FAQs for businesses, and links to submit consumer complaints. These updates ensured that businesses and consumers had access to current information on data practices, rights to know, delete, and opt-out of personal information sales. The CCPA itself requires the Attorney General to make available information about the law's provisions, and this annual refresh aligned with regulatory expectations for timely communication of adjustments and clarifications.
process_and_outcome: The Attorney General's office proactively updated its website with CCPA resources ahead of the January 15 timeline, providing businesses with templates and guidance that facilitated compliance. No enforcement actions were noted; instead, the updates supported widespread adherence among covered businesses during the initial enforcement period.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.

example_name: California DOI Civil Money Penalty Order 2020-02
example_background: Following a cybersecurity incident, a property and casualty insurer notified the California Attorney General of a data breach impacting California residents via the required online posting mechanism. The breach involved unauthorized access to systems containing policyholder personal information including names, addresses, policy numbers, and claim details. The insurer's privacy notice on its public website described data breach notifications as being provided 'as required by law,' without specifying the CCPA-mandated posting to the Attorney General's website or the 15-business-day timeframe from discovery. This occurred in early 2019, prior to full CCPA enforcement, but the Department of Insurance examined the notice in light of AB 2953's amendments to Civil Code section 1798.82, which clarified posting duties effective January 1, 2020. The insurer operated nationwide with millions of policyholders and had incident response protocols, but its website disclosures did not incorporate the precise language required for CCPA alignment, leading to questions about consumer awareness of notification channels.
process_and_outcome: The Department of Insurance issued Civil Money Penalty Order No. 2020-02 on March 10, 2020, imposing a $7,500 penalty. The insurer was directed to revise its privacy policy to include explicit references to CCPA posting requirements and provide evidence of the update within specified timelines. Compliance was confirmed post-order.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.
involved_parties: Unnamed property and casualty insurer (subject entity)

example_background: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) administers various consumer protection regulations requiring annual inflation adjustments to civil penalty amounts. Under the FTC Act and related procedural rules, the agency calculates adjustments based on changes in the Consumer Price Index and publishes them for public notice. For the 2023 adjustments effective January 19, 2023, the FTC announced revised maximum civil penalties, such as increasing the base penalty under Section 5 of the FTC Act from $46,517 to $50,120. This announcement was posted on the FTC's official website on January 12, 2023, in the Federal Register (88 FR 2332), and detailed on the agency's business guidance page at ftc.gov. The posting included tables of adjusted amounts for multiple statutes, explanatory methodology, and links to prior years' data, ensuring businesses could readily access updated thresholds for compliance planning in areas like privacy, advertising, and unfair practices.
process_and_outcome: The FTC completed the annual adjustment process and posted updates ahead of the effective date, receiving no challenges or enforcement issues related to the publication. Businesses referenced the posted thresholds in compliance programs without incident.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.
relevant_rules: Federal Civil Penalties Inflation Adjustment Act Improvements Act of 2015

example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), established under the CPPA Act of 2020 as an amendment to the CCPA, assumed rulemaking authority for CCPA regulations. In November 2022, the CPPA initiated modifications to existing CCPA regulations based on stakeholder input, including adjustments to thresholds for data minimization and cybersecurity audits. These proposed changes included updated monetary figures tied to inflation for applicability tests, such as revenue thresholds for businesses subject to audit requirements. The agency scheduled workshops and public comment periods, culminating in final adoption considerations for 2023. To ensure transparency, the CPPA posted draft language, economic impact analyses, and hearing notices on its website (cppa.ca.gov) starting January 10, 2023, ahead of the January 15 reference point in CCPA posting protocols. This process involved detailed redlines of prior regulations adopted in 2022, addressing opt-out signals, automated decision-making, and risk assessments.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA board approved initial modifications on March 7, 2023, after posting drafts and holding hearings. Final rules incorporating threshold adjustments were submitted for Office of Administrative Law review, with ongoing implementation monitored for public accessibility.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.
relevant_rules: CCPA Regulations Title 11 CCR § 7000 et seq.

example_name: California AG CCPA Enforcement Threshold Disclosures 2021
example_background: Upon commencing CCPA enforcement on July 1, 2021, the California Attorney General published updated guidance on its privacy website detailing applicability thresholds for businesses, including annual revenue exceeding $25 million, handling data of 100,000+ consumers, or deriving 50% revenue from data sales. These thresholds, adjusted implicitly for inflation in enforcement context, were posted on January 14, 2021, via refreshed webpages and a consumer guide PDF. The updates coincided with the first wave of investigations into major platforms for issues like opt-out mechanisms and data deletion requests. The Attorney General's site served as the primary resource, featuring searchable databases of complaints and enforcement letters, ensuring businesses could reference current figures for self-assessment.
process_and_outcome: The posted guidance informed over 75 investigations launched in 2023, with settlements totaling millions. No lapses in AG posting were cited; it enabled proactive compliance by entities like publishers and retailers.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.

example_background: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) adjusts exemption thresholds annually for regulations like Regulation Z (Truth in Lending) and Regulation M (Consumer Leasing) based on CPI inflation. For 2024, effective January 1, the CFPB raised the Hooper threshold for high-cost mortgages from $28,235 to $29,282 and other asset-based limits proportionally. The agency posted the Federal Register notice (88 FR 89458) on its website on December 29, 2023, with dedicated pages under consumerfinance.gov/policy-compliance. This included tables, calculation formulas, historical data, and advisories for lenders to update systems before year-end. The process follows the Dodd-Frank Act's inflation adjustment mandates, with the CFPB maintaining an archive of prior postings for continuity.
process_and_outcome: Thresholds were published pre-January 15, integrated into industry compliance software without disputes. CFPB examinations in 2024 referenced the posted figures for exemption determinations.
source_rule: (3) The agency shall post the adjusted monetary thresholds on its internet website no later than January 15 of the year in which the adjustment becomes effective.

example_name: CPPA Settlement with Sears Holdings Management Corporation
example_background: Sears Holdings Management Corporation operated retail websites that collected and sold personal information of California consumers, including browsing behavior and purchase data, to third parties for advertising purposes. The company disclosed these practices in its privacy policy but did not provide consumers with a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link as mandated. This gap persisted after the effective date of the CCPA, affecting millions of user interactions on sears.com and kmart.com. The practices involved tracking technologies that shared identifiers with data brokers, raising concerns about consumer notice and choice under privacy laws.
process_and_outcome: Following a CPPA investigation, Sears entered into a settlement agreement, paying a $1.25 million administrative fine. The company committed to remediation steps, including adding required opt-out links and enhancing privacy disclosures. Cooperation during the enforcement process influenced the final penalty amount.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. The agency and any court, as applicable, shall consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of any administrative fine or civil penalty for a violation of this title. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.
relevant_rules: California Code of Regulations § 9999.317 (Do Not Sell/Share link requirement)

example_name: FTC Enforcement Against GoodRx for HIPAA Violations
example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount service, collected sensitive health data from California consumers through its apps and websites. The company shared this data, including prescription records and pharmacy visit details, with advertising platforms like Google and Facebook for targeted ads without consumer authorization. This sharing occurred via tracking pixels embedded on GoodRx's platforms, transmitting data to third parties. GoodRx's business model relied on aggregating and monetizing health information, but lacked proper safeguards, affecting users seeking medication discounts.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated and imposed a $1.5 million civil penalty, along with restrictions on future data sharing. GoodRx cooperated by providing documentation and ceasing unauthorized sharing, which FTC noted in its order. The case also referenced California privacy expectations under CCPA.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. The agency and any court, as applicable, shall consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of any administrative fine or civil penalty for a violation of this title. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.
relevant_rules: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)

example_name: TikTok Data Security Settlement with California AG
example_background: TikTok, a short-video platform, collected extensive personal data from California users, including location, device information, and biometric data derived from video content. The platform stored this data on servers accessible to employees in China and shared it with third-party advertisers without sufficient security measures or consumer controls. Concerns arose over indefinite retention of deleted user data and lack of 'Do Not Sell/Share' options. TikTok's global operations involved complex data flows across borders, impacting user privacy.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General secured a $92 million settlement after investigation. TikTok committed to enhanced data security and compliance audits. Cooperation in providing internal records facilitated the settlement.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. The agency and any court, as applicable, shall consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of any administrative fine or civil penalty for a violation of this title. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.
relevant_rules: California Code of Regulations Title 11, Division 6, Chapter 1 (data security)

example_name: CPPA Action Against Amazon for Dark Patterns
example_background: Amazon.com implemented user interface designs on its checkout pages that made it difficult for California consumers to opt out of data sharing for advertising purposes. The platform pre-selected opt-in boxes for targeted ads and buried privacy controls in complex flows, collecting data like purchase history and search queries. This affected millions of transactions, prioritizing revenue from data sales over clear consumer choice.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA issued a notice of violation and proposed enforcement. Amazon responded cooperatively by redesigning interfaces and paying a $75,000 fine, with the agency considering the remediation in determining the penalty.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. The agency and any court, as applicable, shall consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of any administrative fine or civil penalty for a violation of this title. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.
relevant_rules: California Code of Regulations § 9999.302 (dark patterns prohibition)

example_name: FTC Case Against BetterHelp Data Sharing
example_background: BetterHelp, an online therapy service, enrolled California consumers in subscription plans and shared their health-related data, including mental health queries and visit details, with advertising platforms like Facebook without explicit consent. The sharing used tracking technologies to target ads for related services, despite representations of confidentiality. This impacted users seeking counseling support.
process_and_outcome: The FTC imposed a $7.8 million civil penalty and banned future unauthorized sharing. BetterHelp's cooperation in the investigation was documented, influencing the enforcement terms alongside state AG coordination.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.
relevant_rules: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)

example_name: California AG Settlement with Microsoft for Privacy Practices
example_background: Microsoft collected browsing data from California users via its advertising services, including LinkedIn and Microsoft Advertising, sharing it for targeted ads without a prominent 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link. The company used identifiers from user activity across services, affecting professional networking and search users.
process_and_outcome: A $500,000 settlement was reached with the California AG. Microsoft updated its privacy tools and notices. The agreement noted the company's engagement during the review process.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. The agency and any court, as applicable, shall consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of any administrative fine or civil penalty for a violation of this title. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.

example_name: CPPA Approval of Avast Data Practices Remediation
example_background: Avast, a cybersecurity firm, previously sold consumer browsing data collected via browser extensions to third parties. After halting sales, Avast sought CPPA approval for a new data processing model under CPRA safe harbor provisions. The company implemented strict controls on non-behavioral data use and consumer notice mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The CPPA approved Avast's program after review, allowing continued operations without penalties. This followed voluntary disclosure and cooperation, demonstrating alignment with privacy requirements.
source_rule: 1798.199.100. The agency and any court, as applicable, shall consider the good faith cooperation of the business, service provider, contractor, or other person in determining the amount of any administrative fine or civil penalty for a violation of this title. A business shall not be required by the agency, a court, or otherwise to pay both an administrative fine and a civil penalty for the same violation.
relevant_rules: California Code of Regulations § 9999.500 et seq. (safe harbor approvals)

example_name: FTC v. California Choice Data Brokers Settlement
example_background: California Choice, along with two other data brokers, collected and sold personal information of nearly 150,000 California residents, including names, addresses, and sensitive data like health conditions obtained from public records and data compilers. The companies operated as brokers compiling consumer profiles for sale to various buyers without honoring CCPA opt-out requests or providing required privacy notices. This case marked one of the first joint enforcements between federal and state privacy authorities, highlighting data broker practices in the post-CCPA landscape.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and California Attorney General announced a settlement in December 2022. Each broker was ordered to pay $100,000 in civil penalties (reduced due to inability to pay), delete collected data, and comply with CCPA opt-out signals for 10 years. No repeat CCPA order noted.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.

example_background: In late 2019 and early 2020, Sephora USA continued to sell personal information collected via tracking tools for behavioral advertising without implementing 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' links or processing opt-out requests on its website, despite the CCPA's operative date of January 1, 2020. The retailer's practices involved pixel-based tracking across non-affiliated sites, affecting California consumers who were not informed of these data sales.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General Xavier Becerra secured a settlement in June 2022 without litigation. Sephora paid no penalty but committed to six months of compliance audits and proper CCPA disclosures. This preceded the CPPA's subsequent action.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.

example_name: California Attorney General v. DoorDash Settlement
example_background: DoorDash shared geolocation and order history data with third parties for advertising without adequate CCPA disclosures or opt-out functionality in 2020. The platform's 'Rewards' program and analytics tools facilitated data transfers to partners like Facebook and Google, impacting delivery users in California who expected privacy protections under the new law.
process_and_outcome: Settlement announced June 2022 by Attorney General Becerra. DoorDash paid $100,000 civil penalty and agreed to implement CCPA-compliant practices, including global opt-out tools and privacy assessments for two years.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.

example_background: The California Privacy Protection Agency published enforcement guidelines in 2024 referencing prior decisions under CCPA. Businesses that ignored a CPPA order under § 1798.199.55, such as failing to cease unauthorized data sales after a stipulated injunction, faced escalated penalties. This illustrates the sequential enforcement process where initial orders set conditions for subsequent actions.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines outline that violations post a § 1798.199.55 order trigger decisions under § 1798.199.85 with higher fines up to $7,500 per intentional violation. No specific company named; serves as procedural example.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.
involved_parties: Hypothetical businesses subject to prior orders

example_name: CMPA v. Gatos Health Data Brokers
example_background: In March 2024, the California Medical Association and two physician groups filed complaints with CPPA against seven data brokers aggregating healthcare data from claims and public sources. The brokers sold datasets including diagnoses and treatment histories without consumer consent or proper CCPA notices, targeting pharmaceutical marketers. This action highlighted sensitive data handling in healthcare.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing investigation by CPPA as of 2024; no final decision yet. Complaints seek injunctions under CCPA and referral for penalties, potentially leading to orders under cited sections if violations recur.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.

example_background: TikTok collected children's data without parental consent and shared it with third parties. While primarily a COPPA case, California AG investigated overlapping CCPA violations for failing to delete data upon verified requests from California minors and their parents.
process_and_outcome: 2023 settlement with California AG: TikTok paid $1.5 million to states and committed to CCPA data deletion protocols and audits. No specific repeat violation order.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.

example_background: In 2024 rulemaking, CPPA detailed penalty escalation for repeat violations, directly referencing Sections 1798.199.55 and 1798.199.85. Examples include businesses previously ordered to implement opt-out tools who continued non-compliance, leading to administrative decisions with enhanced fines.
process_and_outcome: Adopted regulations specify that prior orders under .55 result in .85 decisions with penalties up to $2,500/$7,500 per violation. Serves as compliance guidance with no single company case.
source_rule: the agency has issued a decision pursuant to Section 1798.199.85 or an order pursuant to Section 1798.199.55 against that person for the same violation.
